Topology (Munkres)

Table of Contents · Previous section · Next section

§27 Compact Subspaces of the Real Line

Definition 151
immediate_successor

$y$ is an immediate successor of $x$ in $X$ with respect to $R$ iff $x\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} X$ and $y\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} X$ and $x\mathrel{R}y$ and $\intervalclosedrel{R}{X}{x}{y} = \{x,y\}$.

LaTeX source 
\begin{definition}
\label{immediate_successor}
    $y$ is an immediate successor of $x$ in $X$ with respect to $R$ iff
        $x\in X$ and $y\in X$ and $x\mathrel{R}y$ and
        $\intervalclosedrel{R}{X}{x}{y} = \{x,y\}$.
\end{definition}
Lemma 254
immediate_successor_interval

Assume $R$ is asymmetric.
Suppose $y$ is an immediate successor of $x$ in $X$ with respect to $R$.
Let $I = \intervalclosedrel{R}{X}{x}{y}$.
Then $I = \{x,y\}$.

LaTeX source 
\begin{lemma}
\label{immediate_successor_interval}
    Assume $R$ is asymmetric.
    Suppose $y$ is an immediate successor of $x$ in $X$ with respect to $R$.
    Let $I = \intervalclosedrel{R}{X}{x}{y}$.
    Then $I = \{x,y\}$.
\end{lemma}
Proof

Follows by Definition 151.

LaTeX source 
\begin{proof}
    Follows by \cref{immediate_successor}.
\end{proof}
Lemma 255
no_immediate_successor_between

Suppose $x\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} X$.
Suppose $y\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} X$.
Suppose $x\mathrel{R}y$.
Suppose it is wrong that $y$ is an immediate successor of $x$ in $X$ with respect to $R$.
Then there exists $z\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} X$ such that $x\mathrel{R}z$ and $z\mathrel{R}y$.

LaTeX source 
\begin{lemma}
\label{no_immediate_successor_between}
    Suppose $x\in X$.
    Suppose $y\in X$.
    Suppose $x\mathrel{R}y$.
    Suppose it is wrong that $y$ is an immediate successor of $x$ in $X$ with respect to $R$.
    Then there exists $z\in X$ such that $x\mathrel{R}z$ and $z\mathrel{R}y$.
\end{lemma}
Proof

Let $I = \intervalclosedrel{R}{X}{x}{y}$.
Suppose not.

Subclaim: Show that $\{x,y\}\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-subseteq}{\subseteq} I$.

Follows by Proposition 18, Definition 34, Definition 188.

Subclaim: Show that for all $z$ such that $z\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} I$ we have $z\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\{x,y\}$.

Fix $z$ such that $z\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} I$.
Thus $(x\mathrel{R}z \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-lor}{\lor} z = x)$ and $(z\mathrel{R}y \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-lor}{\lor} z = y)$ by Definition 34.

Case. $z = x$.

Thus $z\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\{x,y\}$ by Proposition 16.

Case. $z = y$.

Thus $z\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\{x,y\}$ by Proposition 17.

Case. $x\mathrel{R}z$ and $z\mathrel{R}y$.

Contradiction.

LaTeX source 
\begin{byCase}
        \caseOf{$z = x$.}
            Thus $z\in\{x,y\}$ by \cref{upair_intro_left}.
        \caseOf{$z = y$.}
            Thus $z\in\{x,y\}$ by \cref{upair_intro_right}.
        \caseOf{$x\mathrel{R}z$ and $z\mathrel{R}y$.}
            Contradiction.
        
\end{byCase}

Hence $y$ is an immediate successor of $x$ in $X$ with respect to $R$ by Definition 188, Proposition 3, Definition 151.
Contradiction.

LaTeX source 
\begin{proof}
    Let $I = \intervalclosedrel{R}{X}{x}{y}$.
    Suppose not.
    Show that $\{x,y\}\subseteq I$.
    \begin{subproof}
        Follows by \cref{upair_elim,intervalclosed_rel,subseteq}.
    \end{subproof}
    Show that for all $z$ such that $z\in I$ we have $z\in\{x,y\}$.
    \begin{subproof}
        Fix $z$ such that $z\in I$.
        Thus $(x\mathrel{R}z \lor z = x)$ and $(z\mathrel{R}y \lor z = y)$
            by \cref{intervalclosed_rel}.
        \begin{byCase}
        \caseOf{$z = x$.}
            Thus $z\in\{x,y\}$ by \cref{upair_intro_left}.
        \caseOf{$z = y$.}
            Thus $z\in\{x,y\}$ by \cref{upair_intro_right}.
        \caseOf{$x\mathrel{R}z$ and $z\mathrel{R}y$.}
            Contradiction.
        \end{byCase}
    \end{subproof}
    Hence $y$ is an immediate successor of $x$ in $X$ with respect to $R$
        by \cref{subseteq,subseteq_antisymmetric,immediate_successor}.
    Contradiction.
\end{proof}
Theorem 60
ordered_closed_interval_compact

Assume $R$ is a simple order relation on $X$.
Assume $X$ has the least upper bound property with respect to $R$.
Assume $T$ is the order topology on $X$ with respect to $R$.
Suppose $a\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} X$.
Suppose $b\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} X$.
Suppose $a\mathrel{R}b$ or $a = b$.
Let $Y = \intervalclosedrel{R}{X}{a}{b}$.
Let $T_Y = \subspaceTopology{T}{Y}$.
Then $Y$ is compact with respect to $T_Y$.

LaTeX source 
\begin{theorem}
\label{ordered_closed_interval_compact}
    Assume $R$ is a simple order relation on $X$.
    Assume $X$ has the least upper bound property with respect to $R$.
    Assume $T$ is the order topology on $X$ with respect to $R$.
    Suppose $a\in X$.
    Suppose $b\in X$.
    Suppose $a\mathrel{R}b$ or $a = b$.
    Let $Y = \intervalclosedrel{R}{X}{a}{b}$.
    Let $T_Y = \subspaceTopology{T}{Y}$.
    Then $Y$ is compact with respect to $T_Y$.
\end{theorem}
Proof

$R$ is transitive and $R$ is asymmetric and $R$ is connex on $X$ by Definition 28.

Subclaim: Show that for all $A$ such that $A$ is an open covering of $Y$ with respect to $T_Y$ there exists $B$ such that $B\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-subseteq}{\subseteq} A$ and $B$ is finite and $B$ is a covering of $Y$.

Fix $A$ such that $A$ is an open covering of $Y$ with respect to $T_Y$.

Case. $a = b$.

Take $U\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} A$ such that $a\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} U$ by Definition 148, Definition 147, Definition 34, Definition 188, Axiom 5.
Let $B = \{U\}$.
Hence $B\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-subseteq}{\subseteq} A$ and $B$ is finite by Proposition 25, Lemma 394, Proposition 16, Lemma 395.
Thus for all $x$ such that $x\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} Y$ we have $x\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\unions{B}$ by Definition 34, Definition 226, Proposition 20, Axiom 5.
Therefore $B$ is a covering of $Y$ by Definition 188, Definition 147.
Hence $B\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-subseteq}{\subseteq} A$ and $B$ is finite and $B$ is a covering of $Y$.

Case. $a\mathrel{R}b$.

$a\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} Y$ and $b\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} Y$ by Definition 34.
Hence $a\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-neq}{\neq} b$ by Definition 226.
Therefore $X$ has more than one element and $Y$ has more than one element by Definition 27.
$Y$ is convex in $X$ with respect to $R$ by Lemma 209.
For all $y$ such that $y\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} Y$ we have $y\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} X$ by Definition 34.
Hence $Y\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-subseteq}{\subseteq} X$ by Definition 188.
Then $a$ is a smallest element of $Y$ with respect to $R$ by Definition 34, Definition 29.
Finally $b$ is a largest element of $Y$ with respect to $R$ by Definition 34, Definition 30.
$T = \basisTopology{\orderBasis{R}{X}}{X}$ by Definition 36.
Therefore $T$ is a topology on $X$ by Lemma 38, Lemma 1.
Hence $T_Y$ is a topology on $Y$ by Lemma 51, Definition 49.

Subclaim: Show that for all $x$ such that $x\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} Y$ and $x\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-neq}{\neq} b$ there exists $y\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} Y$ such that $x\mathrel{R}y$ and there exists $B$ such that $B\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-subseteq}{\subseteq} A$ and $B$ is finite and $B$ is a covering of $\intervalclosedrel{R}{X}{x}{y}$.

Fix $x$ such that $x\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} Y$ and $x\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-neq}{\neq} b$.
Take $U\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} A$ such that $x\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} U$ by Definition 148, Definition 147, Definition 188, Axiom 5.
Take $V\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} T$ such that $U = Y\inter V$ by Definition 148, Definition 3, Definition 48.
Thus $x\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} V$ by Proposition 63.
Take $B_1\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\orderBasis{R}{X}$ such that $x\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} B_1$ and $B_1\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-subseteq}{\subseteq} V$ by Definition 14.

Subclaim: Show that there exists $c\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} Y$ such that $x\mathrel{R}c$ and $\intervalclosedopenrel{R}{X}{x}{c} \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-subseteq}{\subseteq} V$.

$B_1\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\pow{X}$ and $((\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-exists}{\exists} u, v\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} X. u\mathrel{R}v \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-land}{\land} B_1 = \intervalopenrel{R}{X}{u}{v}) \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-lor}{\lor} (\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-exists}{\exists} v\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} X. \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-exists}{\exists} a_0\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} X. (\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-forall}{\forall} z\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} X. a_0\mathrel{R}z \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-lor}{\lor} a_0 = z) \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-land}{\land} B_1 = \intervalclosedopenrel{R}{X}{a_0}{v}) \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-lor}{\lor} (\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-exists}{\exists} u\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} X. \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-exists}{\exists} b_0\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} X. (\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-forall}{\forall} z\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} X. z\mathrel{R}b_0 \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-lor}{\lor} z = b_0) \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-land}{\land} B_1 = \intervalopenclosedrel{R}{X}{u}{b_0}))$ by Definition 35.

Case. $\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-exists}{\exists} u, v\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} X. u\mathrel{R}v \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-land}{\land} B_1 = \intervalopenrel{R}{X}{u}{v}$.

Take $u, v\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} X$ such that $u\mathrel{R}v$ and $B_1 = \intervalopenrel{R}{X}{u}{v}$.
Thus $u\mathrel{R}x$ and $x\mathrel{R}v$ by Definition 31.

Case. $v\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} Y$.

Let $c = v$.
Thus $\intervalclosedopenrel{R}{X}{x}{c} \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-subseteq}{\subseteq} B_1$ by Definition 33, Definition 227 [Transitivity], Definition 31, Definition 188.

Case. it is wrong that $v\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} Y$.

Thus $a\mathrel{R}v$ by Definition 34, Definition 227 [Transitivity].
Thus $b\mathrel{R}v$ by Definition 230, Definition 34.
Thus $\intervalclosedopenrel{R}{X}{x}{b} \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-subseteq}{\subseteq} B_1$ by Definition 33, Definition 227 [Transitivity], Definition 31, Definition 188.
Thus there exists $c\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} Y$ such that $x\mathrel{R}c$ and $\intervalclosedopenrel{R}{X}{x}{c} \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-subseteq}{\subseteq} B_1$ by Definition 34.

LaTeX source 
\begin{byCase}
                        \caseOf{$v\in Y$.}
                            Let $c = v$.
                            Thus $\intervalclosedopenrel{R}{X}{x}{c} \subseteq B_1$
                                by \cref{intervalclosedopen_rel,transitive,intervalopen_rel,subseteq}.
                        \caseOf{it is wrong that $v\in Y$.}
                            Thus $a\mathrel{R}v$ by \cref{intervalclosed_rel,transitive}.
                            Thus $b\mathrel{R}v$ by \cref{connex,intervalclosed_rel}.
                            Thus $\intervalclosedopenrel{R}{X}{x}{b} \subseteq B_1$
                                by \cref{intervalclosedopen_rel,transitive,intervalopen_rel,subseteq}.
                            Thus there exists $c\in Y$ such that $x\mathrel{R}c$ and
                                $\intervalclosedopenrel{R}{X}{x}{c} \subseteq B_1$ by \cref{intervalclosed_rel}.
                        
\end{byCase}
Case. $\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-exists}{\exists} v\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} X. \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-exists}{\exists} a_0\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} X. (\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-forall}{\forall} z\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} X. a_0\mathrel{R}z \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-lor}{\lor} a_0 = z) \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-land}{\land} B_1 = \intervalclosedopenrel{R}{X}{a_0}{v}$.

Take $a_0, v\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} X$ such that $(\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-forall}{\forall} z\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} X. a_0\mathrel{R}z \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-lor}{\lor} a_0 = z)$ and $B_1 = \intervalclosedopenrel{R}{X}{a_0}{v}$.
Thus $x\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} X$ and $(a_0\mathrel{R}x \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-lor}{\lor} x = a_0)$ and $x\mathrel{R}v$ by Definition 33.

Case. $v\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} Y$.

Let $c = v$.
Thus $\intervalclosedopenrel{R}{X}{x}{c} \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-subseteq}{\subseteq} B_1$ by Definition 33, Definition 29, Definition 34, Definition 188.

Case. it is wrong that $v\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} Y$.

Thus $a\mathrel{R}v$ by Definition 34, Definition 227 [Transitivity].
Thus $b\mathrel{R}v$ by Definition 230, Definition 34.
Thus $\intervalclosedopenrel{R}{X}{x}{b} \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-subseteq}{\subseteq} B_1$ by Definition 33, Definition 29, Definition 227 [Transitivity], Definition 188.
Thus there exists $c\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} Y$ such that $x\mathrel{R}c$ and $\intervalclosedopenrel{R}{X}{x}{c} \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-subseteq}{\subseteq} B_1$ by Definition 34.

\begin{byCase}
                        \caseOf{$v\in Y$.}
                            Let $c = v$.
                            Thus $\intervalclosedopenrel{R}{X}{x}{c} \subseteq B_1$
                                by \cref{intervalclosedopen_rel,smallest_element,intervalclosed_rel,subseteq}.
                        \caseOf{it is wrong that $v\in Y$.}
                            Thus $a\mathrel{R}v$ by \cref{intervalclosed_rel,transitive}.
                            Thus $b\mathrel{R}v$ by \cref{connex,intervalclosed_rel}.
                            Thus $\intervalclosedopenrel{R}{X}{x}{b} \subseteq B_1$

                            Thus there exists $c\in Y$ such that $x\mathrel{R}c$ and

\end{byCase}
Case. $\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-exists}{\exists} u\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} X. \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-exists}{\exists} b_0\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} X. (\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-forall}{\forall} z\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} X. z\mathrel{R}b_0 \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-lor}{\lor} z = b_0) \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-land}{\land} B_1 = \intervalopenclosedrel{R}{X}{u}{b_0}$.

Take $u, b_0\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} X$ such that $(\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-forall}{\forall} z\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} X. z\mathrel{R}b_0 \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-lor}{\lor} z = b_0)$ and $B_1 = \intervalopenclosedrel{R}{X}{u}{b_0}$.
Thus $u\mathrel{R}x$ and $(x\mathrel{R}b_0 \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-lor}{\lor} x = b_0)$ by Definition 32.
Thus $\intervalclosedopenrel{R}{X}{x}{b} \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-subseteq}{\subseteq} B_1$ by Definition 33, Definition 227 [Transitivity], Definition 32, Definition 188.
Thus there exists $c\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} Y$ such that $x\mathrel{R}c$ and $\intervalclosedopenrel{R}{X}{x}{c} \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-subseteq}{\subseteq} B_1$ by Definition 34.

LaTeX source 
\begin{byCase}
                    \caseOf{$\exists u, v\in X. u\mathrel{R}v \land B_1 = \intervalopenrel{R}{X}{u}{v}$.}
                        Take $u, v\in X$ such that $u\mathrel{R}v$ and $B_1 = \intervalopenrel{R}{X}{u}{v}$.
                        Thus $u\mathrel{R}x$ and $x\mathrel{R}v$ by \cref{intervalopen_rel}.
                        \begin{byCase}
                        \caseOf{$v\in Y$.}
                            Let $c = v$.
                            Thus $\intervalclosedopenrel{R}{X}{x}{c} \subseteq B_1$
                                by \cref{intervalclosedopen_rel,transitive,intervalopen_rel,subseteq}.
                        \caseOf{it is wrong that $v\in Y$.}
                            Thus $a\mathrel{R}v$ by \cref{intervalclosed_rel,transitive}.
                            Thus $b\mathrel{R}v$ by \cref{connex,intervalclosed_rel}.
                            Thus $\intervalclosedopenrel{R}{X}{x}{b} \subseteq B_1$
                                by \cref{intervalclosedopen_rel,transitive,intervalopen_rel,subseteq}.
                            Thus there exists $c\in Y$ such that $x\mathrel{R}c$ and
                                $\intervalclosedopenrel{R}{X}{x}{c} \subseteq B_1$ by \cref{intervalclosed_rel}.
                        \end{byCase}
                    \caseOf{$\exists v\in X. \exists a_0\in X. (\forall z\in X. a_0\mathrel{R}z \lor a_0 = z) \land
                        B_1 = \intervalclosedopenrel{R}{X}{a_0}{v}$.}
                        Take $a_0, v\in X$ such that
                            $(\forall z\in X. a_0\mathrel{R}z \lor a_0 = z)$ and
                            $B_1 = \intervalclosedopenrel{R}{X}{a_0}{v}$.
                        Thus $x\in X$ and $(a_0\mathrel{R}x \lor x = a_0)$ and $x\mathrel{R}v$
                            by \cref{intervalclosedopen_rel}.
                        \begin{byCase}
                        \caseOf{$v\in Y$.}
                            Let $c = v$.
                            Thus $\intervalclosedopenrel{R}{X}{x}{c} \subseteq B_1$
                                by \cref{intervalclosedopen_rel,smallest_element,intervalclosed_rel,subseteq}.
                        \caseOf{it is wrong that $v\in Y$.}
                            Thus $a\mathrel{R}v$ by \cref{intervalclosed_rel,transitive}.
                            Thus $b\mathrel{R}v$ by \cref{connex,intervalclosed_rel}.
                            Thus $\intervalclosedopenrel{R}{X}{x}{b} \subseteq B_1$
                                by \cref{intervalclosedopen_rel,smallest_element,transitive,subseteq}.
                            Thus there exists $c\in Y$ such that $x\mathrel{R}c$ and
                                $\intervalclosedopenrel{R}{X}{x}{c} \subseteq B_1$ by \cref{intervalclosed_rel}.
                        \end{byCase}
                    \caseOf{$\exists u\in X. \exists b_0\in X. (\forall z\in X. z\mathrel{R}b_0 \lor z = b_0) \land
                        B_1 = \intervalopenclosedrel{R}{X}{u}{b_0}$.}
                        Take $u, b_0\in X$ such that
                            $(\forall z\in X. z\mathrel{R}b_0 \lor z = b_0)$ and
                            $B_1 = \intervalopenclosedrel{R}{X}{u}{b_0}$.
                        Thus $u\mathrel{R}x$ and $(x\mathrel{R}b_0 \lor x = b_0)$ by \cref{intervalopenclosed_rel}.
                        Thus $\intervalclosedopenrel{R}{X}{x}{b} \subseteq B_1$
                            by \cref{intervalclosedopen_rel,transitive,intervalopenclosed_rel,subseteq}.
                        Thus there exists $c\in Y$ such that $x\mathrel{R}c$ and
                            $\intervalclosedopenrel{R}{X}{x}{c} \subseteq B_1$ by \cref{intervalclosed_rel}.
                    
\end{byCase}

Take $c\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} Y$ such that $x\mathrel{R}c$ and $\intervalclosedopenrel{R}{X}{x}{c} \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-subseteq}{\subseteq} V$.

Case. there exists $y\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} X$ such that $y$ is an immediate successor of $x$ in $X$ with respect to $R$.

Take $y\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} X$ such that $y$ is an immediate successor of $x$ in $X$ with respect to $R$.

Subclaim: Show that $y\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} Y$.
Subclaim: Show that $y\mathrel{R}b$ or $y = b$.

Thus $y\mathrel{R}b$ or $y = b$ or $b\mathrel{R}y$ by Definition 230.

Case. $b\mathrel{R}y$.

Then $b\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\intervalclosedrel{R}{X}{x}{y}$ by Definition 34.
Thus $b\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\{x,y\}$.
Thus $b = x$ or $b = y$ by Proposition 18.
Thus $y = b$.

LaTeX source 
\begin{byCase}
                            \caseOf{$b\mathrel{R}y$.}
                                Then $b\in\intervalclosedrel{R}{X}{x}{y}$ by \cref{intervalclosed_rel}.
                                Thus $b\in\{x,y\}$.
                                Thus $b = x$ or $b = y$ by \cref{upair_elim}.
                                Thus $y = b$.
                            \caseOf{$y\mathrel{R}b$ or $y = b$.}
                            
\end{byCase}

Thus $y\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} Y$ by Definition 151, Definition 34, Definition 227 [Transitivity].

Take $U_1\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} A$ such that $y\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} U_1$ by Definition 148, Definition 147, Definition 188, Axiom 5.
Let $B = \{U,U_1\}$.
Hence $B\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-subseteq}{\subseteq} A$ and $B$ is finite by Proposition 18, Definition 188, Lemma 394.
For all $z$ such that $z\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\intervalclosedrel{R}{X}{x}{y}$ we have $z\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\unions{B}$ by Lemma 254, Proposition 18, Proposition 16, Proposition 17, Axiom 5.
Therefore $B$ is a covering of $\intervalclosedrel{R}{X}{x}{y}$ by Definition 188, Definition 147.

Case. it is wrong that there exists $y\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} X$ such that $y$ is an immediate successor of $x$ in $X$ with respect to $R$.

Therefore it is wrong that $c$ is an immediate successor of $x$ in $X$ with respect to $R$ by Definition 188.
Take $y\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} X$ such that $x\mathrel{R}y$ and $y\mathrel{R}c$ by Lemma 255.
Hence $y\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} Y$ by Definition 50.
Let $B = \{U\}$.
Thus $B\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-subseteq}{\subseteq} A$ and $B$ is finite by Proposition 25, Lemma 394, Proposition 16, Lemma 395.
For all $z$ such that $z\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\intervalclosedrel{R}{X}{x}{y}$ we have $z\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} X$ and $(x\mathrel{R}z \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-lor}{\lor} z = x)$ and $(z\mathrel{R}y \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-lor}{\lor} z = y)$ by Definition 34.
For all $z$ such that $z\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\intervalclosedrel{R}{X}{x}{y}$ we have $z\mathrel{R}c$ by \hyperref[transitive]{transitivity}.
Thus for all $z$ such that $z\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\intervalclosedrel{R}{X}{x}{y}$ we have $z\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\unions{B}$ by Definition 33, Definition 188, Definition 50, Proposition 61, Proposition 20, Axiom 5.
Therefore $B$ is a covering of $\intervalclosedrel{R}{X}{x}{y}$ by Definition 188, Definition 147.

LaTeX source 
\begin{byCase}
                \caseOf{there exists $y\in X$ such that $y$ is an immediate successor of $x$ in $X$ with respect to $R$.}
                    Take $y\in X$ such that $y$ is an immediate successor of $x$ in $X$ with respect to $R$.
                    Show that $y\in Y$.
                    \begin{subproof}
                        Show that $y\mathrel{R}b$ or $y = b$.
                        \begin{subproof}
                            Thus $y\mathrel{R}b$ or $y = b$ or $b\mathrel{R}y$ by \cref{connex}.
                            \begin{byCase}
                            \caseOf{$b\mathrel{R}y$.}
                                Then $b\in\intervalclosedrel{R}{X}{x}{y}$ by \cref{intervalclosed_rel}.
                                Thus $b\in\{x,y\}$.
                                Thus $b = x$ or $b = y$ by \cref{upair_elim}.
                                Thus $y = b$.
                            \caseOf{$y\mathrel{R}b$ or $y = b$.}
                            \end{byCase}
                        \end{subproof}
                        Thus $y\in Y$ by \cref{immediate_successor,intervalclosed_rel,transitive}.
                    \end{subproof}
                    Take $U_1\in A$ such that $y\in U_1$
                        by \cref{open_covering,covering,subseteq,unions_iff}.
                    Let $B = \{U,U_1\}$.
                    Hence $B\subseteq A$ and $B$ is finite by \cref{upair_elim,subseteq,pair_is_finite}.
                    For all $z$ such that $z\in\intervalclosedrel{R}{X}{x}{y}$ we have $z\in\unions{B}$
                        by \cref{immediate_successor_interval,upair_elim,upair_intro_left,upair_intro_right,unions_iff}.
                    Therefore $B$ is a covering of $\intervalclosedrel{R}{X}{x}{y}$ by \cref{subseteq,covering}.
                \caseOf{it is wrong that there exists $y\in X$ such that $y$ is an immediate successor of $x$ in $X$ with respect to $R$.}
                    Therefore it is wrong that $c$ is an immediate successor of $x$ in $X$ with respect to $R$
                        by \cref{subseteq}.
                    Take $y\in X$ such that $x\mathrel{R}y$ and $y\mathrel{R}c$ by \cref{no_immediate_successor_between}.
                    Hence $y\in Y$ by \cref{convex_in}.
                    Let $B = \{U\}$.
                    Thus $B\subseteq A$ and $B$ is finite
                        by \cref{singleton_subset_intro,pair_is_finite,upair_intro_left,subseteq_finite_is_finite}.
                    For all $z$ such that $z\in\intervalclosedrel{R}{X}{x}{y}$ we have
                        $z\in X$ and $(x\mathrel{R}z \lor z = x)$ and $(z\mathrel{R}y \lor z = y)$
                        by \cref{intervalclosed_rel}.
                    For all $z$ such that $z\in\intervalclosedrel{R}{X}{x}{y}$ we have $z\mathrel{R}c$
                        by \hyperref[transitive]{transitivity}.
                    Thus for all $z$ such that $z\in\intervalclosedrel{R}{X}{x}{y}$ we have $z\in\unions{B}$
                        by \cref{intervalclosedopen_rel,subseteq,convex_in,inter_intro,singleton_intro,unions_iff}.
                    Therefore $B$ is a covering of $\intervalclosedrel{R}{X}{x}{y}$ by \cref{subseteq,covering}.
                
\end{byCase}

Let $C = \{y\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} Y \mid \text{$a\mathrel{R}y$ and there exists $B\subseteq A$ such that $B$ is finite and $B$ is a covering of $\intervalclosedrel{R}{X}{a}{y}$}\}$.
Now $a\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} Y$.
Take $y\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} Y$ such that $a\mathrel{R}y$ and there exists $B$ such that $B\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-subseteq}{\subseteq} A$ and $B$ is finite and $B$ is a covering of $\intervalclosedrel{R}{X}{a}{y}$.
Hence $y\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} C$.
Therefore $C$ is inhabited.
Hence $C\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-subseteq}{\subseteq} X$ by Definition 34, Definition 188.
For all $y\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} C$ we have $y\mathrel{R}b$ or $y = b$ by Definition 34.
Therefore $b$ is an upper bound of $C$ in $X$ with respect to $R$ by Definition 130.
Take $c$ such that $c$ is a least upper bound of $C$ in $X$ with respect to $R$ by Definition 132.

Subclaim: Show that $a\mathrel{R}c$.

Take $y_0\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} C$ such that $a\mathrel{R}y_0$ by Proposition 10.
Suppose not.

Case. $c = a$.

Thus $y_0\mathrel{R}c$ or $y_0 = c$ by Definition 131, Definition 130.
Thus $y_0\mathrel{R}c$ by Definition 131, Definition 130, Definition 226.
Thus $y_0\mathrel{R}a$.
Contradiction by Definition 226.

Case. $c\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-neq}{\neq} a$.

Thus $c\mathrel{R}a$ or $a\mathrel{R}c$ by Definition 131, Definition 130, Definition 230.
Thus $c\mathrel{R}a$ by Definition 131, Definition 130, Definition 230.
Thus $c\mathrel{R}y$ by \hyperref[transitive]{transitivity}.
Thus $y\mathrel{R}c$ or $y = c$ by Definition 131, Definition 130.
Contradiction by Definition 226.

LaTeX source 
\begin{byCase}
                \caseOf{$c = a$.}
                    Thus $y_0\mathrel{R}c$ or $y_0 = c$ by \cref{least_upper_bound_rel,upper_bound_rel}.
                    Thus $y_0\mathrel{R}c$ by \cref{least_upper_bound_rel,upper_bound_rel,asymmetric}.
                    Thus $y_0\mathrel{R}a$.
                    Contradiction by \cref{asymmetric}.
                \caseOf{$c\neq a$.}
                    Thus $c\mathrel{R}a$ or $a\mathrel{R}c$ by \cref{least_upper_bound_rel,upper_bound_rel,connex}.
                    Thus $c\mathrel{R}a$ by \cref{least_upper_bound_rel,upper_bound_rel,connex}.
                    Thus $c\mathrel{R}y$ by \hyperref[transitive]{transitivity}.
                    Thus $y\mathrel{R}c$ or $y = c$ by \cref{least_upper_bound_rel,upper_bound_rel}.
                    Contradiction by \cref{asymmetric}.
                
\end{byCase}

Thus $c\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} X$ and $c\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} Y$ by Definition 131, Definition 130, Definition 34.

Subclaim: Show that $c\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} C$.

Take $U\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} A$ such that $c\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} U$ by Definition 148, Definition 147, Definition 188, Axiom 5.
Take $V\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} T$ such that $U = Y\inter V$ by Definition 148, Definition 3, Definition 48.
Thus $c\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} V$ by Proposition 63.
Take $B_1\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\orderBasis{R}{X}$ such that $c\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} B_1$ and $B_1\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-subseteq}{\subseteq} V$ by Definition 14.

Subclaim: Show that there exists $d\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} Y$ such that $d\mathrel{R}c$ and $\intervalopenclosedrel{R}{X}{d}{c} \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-subseteq}{\subseteq} V$.

$B_1\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\pow{X}$ and $((\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-exists}{\exists} u, v\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} X. u\mathrel{R}v \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-land}{\land} B_1 = \intervalopenrel{R}{X}{u}{v}) \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-lor}{\lor} (\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-exists}{\exists} v\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} X. \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-exists}{\exists} a_0\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} X. (\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-forall}{\forall} z\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} X. a_0\mathrel{R}z \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-lor}{\lor} a_0 = z) \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-land}{\land} B_1 = \intervalclosedopenrel{R}{X}{a_0}{v}) \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-lor}{\lor} (\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-exists}{\exists} u\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} X. \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-exists}{\exists} b_0\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} X. (\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-forall}{\forall} z\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} X. z\mathrel{R}b_0 \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-lor}{\lor} z = b_0) \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-land}{\land} B_1 = \intervalopenclosedrel{R}{X}{u}{b_0}))$ by Definition 35.

Case. $\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-exists}{\exists} u, v\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} X. u\mathrel{R}v \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-land}{\land} B_1 = \intervalopenrel{R}{X}{u}{v}$.

Take $u, v\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} X$ such that $u\mathrel{R}v$ and $B_1 = \intervalopenrel{R}{X}{u}{v}$.
Thus $u\mathrel{R}c$ and $c\mathrel{R}v$ by Definition 31.

Case. $u\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} Y$.

Let $d = u$.
Thus $\intervalopenclosedrel{R}{X}{d}{c} \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-subseteq}{\subseteq} B_1$ by Definition 32, Definition 227 [Transitivity], Definition 31, Definition 188.

Case. it is wrong that $u\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} Y$.

Thus $u\mathrel{R}a$ by Definition 230, Definition 131, Definition 227 [Transitivity], Definition 34.
Let $d = a$.
Thus $\intervalopenclosedrel{R}{X}{d}{c} \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-subseteq}{\subseteq} B_1$ by Definition 32, Definition 227 [Transitivity], Definition 31, Definition 188.

\begin{byCase}
                        \caseOf{$u\in Y$.}
                            Let $d = u$.
                            Thus $\intervalopenclosedrel{R}{X}{d}{c} \subseteq B_1$
                                by \cref{intervalopenclosed_rel,transitive,intervalopen_rel,subseteq}.
                        \caseOf{it is wrong that $u\in Y$.}
                            Thus $u\mathrel{R}a$ by \cref{connex,least_upper_bound_rel,transitive,intervalclosed_rel}.
                            Let $d = a$.
                            Thus $\intervalopenclosedrel{R}{X}{d}{c} \subseteq B_1$
                                by \cref{intervalopenclosed_rel,transitive,intervalopen_rel,subseteq}.
                        
\end{byCase}
Case. $\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-exists}{\exists} v\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} X. \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-exists}{\exists} a_0\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} X. (\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-forall}{\forall} z\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} X. a_0\mathrel{R}z \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-lor}{\lor} a_0 = z) \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-land}{\land} B_1 = \intervalclosedopenrel{R}{X}{a_0}{v}$.

Take $a_0, v\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} X$ such that $(\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-forall}{\forall} z\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} X. a_0\mathrel{R}z \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-lor}{\lor} a_0 = z)$ and $B_1 = \intervalclosedopenrel{R}{X}{a_0}{v}$.
Thus $c\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} X$ and $(a_0\mathrel{R}c \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-lor}{\lor} c = a_0)$ and $c\mathrel{R}v$ by Definition 33.
Let $d = a$.
Thus $\intervalopenclosedrel{R}{X}{d}{c} \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-subseteq}{\subseteq} B_1$ by Definition 32, Definition 29, Definition 227 [Transitivity], Definition 33, Definition 188.

Case. $\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-exists}{\exists} u\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} X. \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-exists}{\exists} b_0\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} X. (\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-forall}{\forall} z\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} X. z\mathrel{R}b_0 \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-lor}{\lor} z = b_0) \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-land}{\land} B_1 = \intervalopenclosedrel{R}{X}{u}{b_0}$.

Take $u, b_0\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} X$ such that $(\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-forall}{\forall} z\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} X. z\mathrel{R}b_0 \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-lor}{\lor} z = b_0)$ and $B_1 = \intervalopenclosedrel{R}{X}{u}{b_0}$.
Thus $u\mathrel{R}c$ and $(c\mathrel{R}b_0 \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-lor}{\lor} c = b_0)$ by Definition 32.

Case. $u\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} Y$.

Let $d = u$.
Thus $\intervalopenclosedrel{R}{X}{d}{c} \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-subseteq}{\subseteq} B_1$ by Definition 32, Definition 227 [Transitivity], Definition 32, Definition 188.

Case. it is wrong that $u\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} Y$.

Thus $u\mathrel{R}a$ by Definition 230, Definition 131, Definition 227 [Transitivity], Definition 34.
Let $d = a$.
Thus $\intervalopenclosedrel{R}{X}{d}{c} \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-subseteq}{\subseteq} B_1$ by Definition 32, Definition 227 [Transitivity], Definition 32, Definition 188.

LaTeX source 
\begin{byCase}
                        \caseOf{$u\in Y$.}
                            Let $d = u$.
                            Thus $\intervalopenclosedrel{R}{X}{d}{c} \subseteq B_1$
                                by \cref{intervalopenclosed_rel,transitive,intervalopenclosed_rel,subseteq}.
                        \caseOf{it is wrong that $u\in Y$.}
                            Thus $u\mathrel{R}a$ by \cref{connex,least_upper_bound_rel,transitive,intervalclosed_rel}.
                            Let $d = a$.
                            Thus $\intervalopenclosedrel{R}{X}{d}{c} \subseteq B_1$
                                by \cref{intervalopenclosed_rel,transitive,intervalopenclosed_rel,subseteq}.
                        
\end{byCase}
LaTeX source 
\begin{byCase}
                    \caseOf{$\exists u, v\in X. u\mathrel{R}v \land B_1 = \intervalopenrel{R}{X}{u}{v}$.}
                        Take $u, v\in X$ such that $u\mathrel{R}v$ and $B_1 = \intervalopenrel{R}{X}{u}{v}$.
                        Thus $u\mathrel{R}c$ and $c\mathrel{R}v$ by \cref{intervalopen_rel}.
                        \begin{byCase}
                        \caseOf{$u\in Y$.}
                            Let $d = u$.
                            Thus $\intervalopenclosedrel{R}{X}{d}{c} \subseteq B_1$
                                by \cref{intervalopenclosed_rel,transitive,intervalopen_rel,subseteq}.
                        \caseOf{it is wrong that $u\in Y$.}
                            Thus $u\mathrel{R}a$ by \cref{connex,least_upper_bound_rel,transitive,intervalclosed_rel}.
                            Let $d = a$.
                            Thus $\intervalopenclosedrel{R}{X}{d}{c} \subseteq B_1$
                                by \cref{intervalopenclosed_rel,transitive,intervalopen_rel,subseteq}.
                        \end{byCase}
                    \caseOf{$\exists v\in X. \exists a_0\in X. (\forall z\in X. a_0\mathrel{R}z \lor a_0 = z) \land
                        B_1 = \intervalclosedopenrel{R}{X}{a_0}{v}$.}
                        Take $a_0, v\in X$ such that
                            $(\forall z\in X. a_0\mathrel{R}z \lor a_0 = z)$ and
                            $B_1 = \intervalclosedopenrel{R}{X}{a_0}{v}$.
                        Thus $c\in X$ and $(a_0\mathrel{R}c \lor c = a_0)$ and $c\mathrel{R}v$
                            by \cref{intervalclosedopen_rel}.
                        Let $d = a$.
                        Thus $\intervalopenclosedrel{R}{X}{d}{c} \subseteq B_1$
                            by \cref{intervalopenclosed_rel,smallest_element,transitive,intervalclosedopen_rel,subseteq}.
                    \caseOf{$\exists u\in X. \exists b_0\in X. (\forall z\in X. z\mathrel{R}b_0 \lor z = b_0) \land
                        B_1 = \intervalopenclosedrel{R}{X}{u}{b_0}$.}
                        Take $u, b_0\in X$ such that
                            $(\forall z\in X. z\mathrel{R}b_0 \lor z = b_0)$ and
                            $B_1 = \intervalopenclosedrel{R}{X}{u}{b_0}$.
                        Thus $u\mathrel{R}c$ and $(c\mathrel{R}b_0 \lor c = b_0)$ by \cref{intervalopenclosed_rel}.
                        \begin{byCase}
                        \caseOf{$u\in Y$.}
                            Let $d = u$.
                            Thus $\intervalopenclosedrel{R}{X}{d}{c} \subseteq B_1$
                                by \cref{intervalopenclosed_rel,transitive,intervalopenclosed_rel,subseteq}.
                        \caseOf{it is wrong that $u\in Y$.}
                            Thus $u\mathrel{R}a$ by \cref{connex,least_upper_bound_rel,transitive,intervalclosed_rel}.
                            Let $d = a$.
                            Thus $\intervalopenclosedrel{R}{X}{d}{c} \subseteq B_1$
                                by \cref{intervalopenclosed_rel,transitive,intervalopenclosed_rel,subseteq}.
                        \end{byCase}
                    
\end{byCase}

Take $d\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} Y$ such that $d\mathrel{R}c$ and $\intervalopenclosedrel{R}{X}{d}{c} \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-subseteq}{\subseteq} V$.
Suppose not.

Subclaim: Show that there exists $z\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} C$ such that $d\mathrel{R}z$.

Suppose not.
For all $y\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} C$ we have $y\mathrel{R}d$ or $y = d$ by Definition 188, Definition 230.
Thus $c\mathrel{R}d$ or $c = d$ by Definition 188, Definition 130, Definition 131.
Contradiction by Definition 226.

Take $z\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} C$ such that $d\mathrel{R}z$.
Thus $z\mathrel{R}c$ by Definition 131, Definition 130.
Take $B_0$ such that $B_0\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-subseteq}{\subseteq} A$ and $B_0$ is finite and $B_0$ is a covering of $\intervalclosedrel{R}{X}{a}{z}$.
Let $B = B_0\union\{U\}$.
Hence $B$ is finite by Lemma 394, Proposition 16, Proposition 25, Lemma 395, Lemma 396.

Subclaim: Show that for all $w$ such that $w\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\intervalclosedrel{R}{X}{a}{c}$ we have $w\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\unions{B}$.

Fix $w$ such that $w\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\intervalclosedrel{R}{X}{a}{c}$.
Thus $w\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} X$ and $(a\mathrel{R}w \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-lor}{\lor} w = a)$ and $(w\mathrel{R}c \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-lor}{\lor} w = c)$ by Definition 34.
Thus $w\mathrel{R}z$ or $w = z$ or $z\mathrel{R}w$ by Definition 230.

Case. $w\mathrel{R}z$ or $w = z$.

Thus $w\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\unions{B}$ by Definition 188, Definition 34, Definition 147, Axiom 5, Proposition 38.

Case. $z\mathrel{R}w$.

Thus $w\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} V$ by Definition 227 [Transitivity], Definition 32, Definition 188.
Thus $w\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} Y$ by Definition 50.
Thus $w\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} Y\inter V$ by Proposition 61.
Thus $w\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} U$.
Thus $w\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\unions{B}$ by Proposition 20, Proposition 39, Axiom 5.

LaTeX source 
\begin{byCase}
                    \caseOf{$w\mathrel{R}z$ or $w = z$.}
                        Thus $w\in\unions{B}$ by \cref{subseteq,intervalclosed_rel,covering,unions_iff,union_intro_left}.
                    \caseOf{$z\mathrel{R}w$.}
                        Thus $w\in V$ by \cref{transitive,intervalopenclosed_rel,subseteq}.
                        Thus $w\in Y$ by \cref{convex_in}.
                        Thus $w\in Y\inter V$ by \cref{inter_intro}.
                        Thus $w\in U$.
                        Thus $w\in\unions{B}$ by \cref{singleton_intro,union_intro_right,unions_iff}.
                    
\end{byCase}

Therefore $B$ is a covering of $\intervalclosedrel{R}{X}{a}{c}$ by Definition 188, Definition 147.
Hence $B\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-subseteq}{\subseteq} A$ and $B$ is finite and $B$ is a covering of $\intervalclosedrel{R}{X}{a}{c}$.
Therefore $c\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} C$.
Contradiction.

Subclaim: Show that $c = b$.

Suppose not.
Thus $c\mathrel{R}b$ by Definition 131.
Take $y_1,B_1$ such that $y_1\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} Y$ and $c\mathrel{R}y_1$ and $B_1\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-subseteq}{\subseteq} A$ and $B_1$ is finite and $B_1$ is a covering of $\intervalclosedrel{R}{X}{c}{y_1}$.
Thus $a\mathrel{R}y_1$ by \hyperref[transitive]{transitivity}.
Take $B_0$ such that $B_0\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-subseteq}{\subseteq} A$ and $B_0$ is finite and $B_0$ is a covering of $\intervalclosedrel{R}{X}{a}{c}$.
Let $B = B_0\union B_1$.
Hence $B$ is finite by Lemma 396.
For all $w$ such that $w\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\intervalclosedrel{R}{X}{a}{y_1}$ we have $w\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\unions{B}$ by Definition 34, Definition 147, Definition 188, Axiom 5, Proposition 38, Proposition 39, Definition 230.
Therefore $B$ is a covering of $\intervalclosedrel{R}{X}{a}{y_1}$ by Definition 188, Definition 147.
Hence $y_1\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} C$.
Therefore $y_1\mathrel{R}c$ or $y_1 = c$ by Definition 131, Definition 130.
Contradiction by Definition 226.

Take $B$ such that $B\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-subseteq}{\subseteq} A$ and $B$ is finite and $B$ is a covering of $\intervalclosedrel{R}{X}{a}{b}$.

LaTeX source 
\begin{byCase}
        \caseOf{$a = b$.}
            Take $U\in A$ such that $a\in U$
                by \cref{open_covering,covering,intervalclosed_rel,subseteq,unions_iff}.
            Let $B = \{U\}$.
            Hence $B\subseteq A$ and $B$ is finite
                by \cref{singleton_subset_intro,pair_is_finite,upair_intro_left,subseteq_finite_is_finite}.
            Thus for all $x$ such that $x\in Y$ we have $x\in\unions{B}$
                by \cref{intervalclosed_rel,asymmetric,singleton_intro,unions_iff}.
            Therefore $B$ is a covering of $Y$ by \cref{subseteq,covering}.
            Hence $B\subseteq A$ and $B$ is finite and $B$ is a covering of $Y$.
        \caseOf{$a\mathrel{R}b$.}
            $a\in Y$ and $b\in Y$ by \cref{intervalclosed_rel}.
            Hence $a\neq b$ by \cref{asymmetric}.
            Therefore $X$ has more than one element and $Y$ has more than one element by \cref{more_than_one_element}.
            $Y$ is convex in $X$ with respect to $R$ by \cref{intervalclosedrel_convex}.
            For all $y$ such that $y\in Y$ we have $y\in X$ by \cref{intervalclosed_rel}.
            Hence $Y\subseteq X$ by \cref{subseteq}.
            Then $a$ is a smallest element of $Y$ with respect to $R$ by \cref{intervalclosed_rel,smallest_element}.
            Finally $b$ is a largest element of $Y$ with respect to $R$ by \cref{intervalclosed_rel,largest_element}.
            $T = \basisTopology{\orderBasis{R}{X}}{X}$ by \cref{order_topology}.
            Therefore $T$ is a topology on $X$ by \cref{order_basis_is_basis,basis_topology_is_topology}.
            Hence $T_Y$ is a topology on $Y$ by \cref{subspace_topology_is_topology,subspace_of}.

            Show that for all $x$ such that $x\in Y$ and $x\neq b$ there exists $y\in Y$ such that
                $x\mathrel{R}y$ and
                there exists $B$ such that $B\subseteq A$ and $B$ is finite and
                    $B$ is a covering of $\intervalclosedrel{R}{X}{x}{y}$.
            \begin{subproof}
                Fix $x$ such that $x\in Y$ and $x\neq b$.
                Take $U\in A$ such that $x\in U$ by \cref{open_covering,covering,subseteq,unions_iff}.
                Take $V\in T$ such that $U = Y\inter V$ by \cref{open_covering,open_in,subspace_topology}.
                Thus $x\in V$ by \cref{inter_elim_right}.
                Take $B_1\in\orderBasis{R}{X}$ such that $x\in B_1$ and $B_1\subseteq V$
                    by \cref{topology_generated_by_basis}.
                Show that there exists $c\in Y$ such that $x\mathrel{R}c$ and
                    $\intervalclosedopenrel{R}{X}{x}{c} \subseteq V$.
                \begin{subproof}
                    $B_1\in\pow{X}$ and
                        $((\exists u, v\in X. u\mathrel{R}v \land B_1 = \intervalopenrel{R}{X}{u}{v}) \lor
                        (\exists v\in X. \exists a_0\in X. (\forall z\in X. a_0\mathrel{R}z \lor a_0 = z) \land
                            B_1 = \intervalclosedopenrel{R}{X}{a_0}{v}) \lor
                        (\exists u\in X. \exists b_0\in X. (\forall z\in X. z\mathrel{R}b_0 \lor z = b_0) \land
                            B_1 = \intervalopenclosedrel{R}{X}{u}{b_0}))$
                        by \cref{order_basis}.
                    \begin{byCase}
                    \caseOf{$\exists u, v\in X. u\mathrel{R}v \land B_1 = \intervalopenrel{R}{X}{u}{v}$.}
                        Take $u, v\in X$ such that $u\mathrel{R}v$ and $B_1 = \intervalopenrel{R}{X}{u}{v}$.
                        Thus $u\mathrel{R}x$ and $x\mathrel{R}v$ by \cref{intervalopen_rel}.
                        \begin{byCase}
                        \caseOf{$v\in Y$.}
                            Let $c = v$.
                            Thus $\intervalclosedopenrel{R}{X}{x}{c} \subseteq B_1$
                                by \cref{intervalclosedopen_rel,transitive,intervalopen_rel,subseteq}.
                        \caseOf{it is wrong that $v\in Y$.}
                            Thus $a\mathrel{R}v$ by \cref{intervalclosed_rel,transitive}.
                            Thus $b\mathrel{R}v$ by \cref{connex,intervalclosed_rel}.
                            Thus $\intervalclosedopenrel{R}{X}{x}{b} \subseteq B_1$
                                by \cref{intervalclosedopen_rel,transitive,intervalopen_rel,subseteq}.
                            Thus there exists $c\in Y$ such that $x\mathrel{R}c$ and
                                $\intervalclosedopenrel{R}{X}{x}{c} \subseteq B_1$ by \cref{intervalclosed_rel}.
                        \end{byCase}
                    \caseOf{$\exists v\in X. \exists a_0\in X. (\forall z\in X. a_0\mathrel{R}z \lor a_0 = z) \land
                        B_1 = \intervalclosedopenrel{R}{X}{a_0}{v}$.}
                        Take $a_0, v\in X$ such that
                            $(\forall z\in X. a_0\mathrel{R}z \lor a_0 = z)$ and
                            $B_1 = \intervalclosedopenrel{R}{X}{a_0}{v}$.
                        Thus $x\in X$ and $(a_0\mathrel{R}x \lor x = a_0)$ and $x\mathrel{R}v$
                            by \cref{intervalclosedopen_rel}.
                        \begin{byCase}
                        \caseOf{$v\in Y$.}
                            Let $c = v$.
                            Thus $\intervalclosedopenrel{R}{X}{x}{c} \subseteq B_1$
                                by \cref{intervalclosedopen_rel,smallest_element,intervalclosed_rel,subseteq}.
                        \caseOf{it is wrong that $v\in Y$.}
                            Thus $a\mathrel{R}v$ by \cref{intervalclosed_rel,transitive}.
                            Thus $b\mathrel{R}v$ by \cref{connex,intervalclosed_rel}.
                            Thus $\intervalclosedopenrel{R}{X}{x}{b} \subseteq B_1$
                                by \cref{intervalclosedopen_rel,smallest_element,transitive,subseteq}.
                            Thus there exists $c\in Y$ such that $x\mathrel{R}c$ and
                                $\intervalclosedopenrel{R}{X}{x}{c} \subseteq B_1$ by \cref{intervalclosed_rel}.
                        \end{byCase}
                    \caseOf{$\exists u\in X. \exists b_0\in X. (\forall z\in X. z\mathrel{R}b_0 \lor z = b_0) \land
                        B_1 = \intervalopenclosedrel{R}{X}{u}{b_0}$.}
                        Take $u, b_0\in X$ such that
                            $(\forall z\in X. z\mathrel{R}b_0 \lor z = b_0)$ and
                            $B_1 = \intervalopenclosedrel{R}{X}{u}{b_0}$.
                        Thus $u\mathrel{R}x$ and $(x\mathrel{R}b_0 \lor x = b_0)$ by \cref{intervalopenclosed_rel}.
                        Thus $\intervalclosedopenrel{R}{X}{x}{b} \subseteq B_1$
                            by \cref{intervalclosedopen_rel,transitive,intervalopenclosed_rel,subseteq}.
                        Thus there exists $c\in Y$ such that $x\mathrel{R}c$ and
                            $\intervalclosedopenrel{R}{X}{x}{c} \subseteq B_1$ by \cref{intervalclosed_rel}.
                    \end{byCase}
                \end{subproof}
                Take $c\in Y$ such that $x\mathrel{R}c$ and $\intervalclosedopenrel{R}{X}{x}{c} \subseteq V$.
                \begin{byCase}
                \caseOf{there exists $y\in X$ such that $y$ is an immediate successor of $x$ in $X$ with respect to $R$.}
                    Take $y\in X$ such that $y$ is an immediate successor of $x$ in $X$ with respect to $R$.
                    Show that $y\in Y$.
                    \begin{subproof}
                        Show that $y\mathrel{R}b$ or $y = b$.
                        \begin{subproof}
                            Thus $y\mathrel{R}b$ or $y = b$ or $b\mathrel{R}y$ by \cref{connex}.
                            \begin{byCase}
                            \caseOf{$b\mathrel{R}y$.}
                                Then $b\in\intervalclosedrel{R}{X}{x}{y}$ by \cref{intervalclosed_rel}.
                                Thus $b\in\{x,y\}$.
                                Thus $b = x$ or $b = y$ by \cref{upair_elim}.
                                Thus $y = b$.
                            \caseOf{$y\mathrel{R}b$ or $y = b$.}
                            \end{byCase}
                        \end{subproof}
                        Thus $y\in Y$ by \cref{immediate_successor,intervalclosed_rel,transitive}.
                    \end{subproof}
                    Take $U_1\in A$ such that $y\in U_1$
                        by \cref{open_covering,covering,subseteq,unions_iff}.
                    Let $B = \{U,U_1\}$.
                    Hence $B\subseteq A$ and $B$ is finite by \cref{upair_elim,subseteq,pair_is_finite}.
                    For all $z$ such that $z\in\intervalclosedrel{R}{X}{x}{y}$ we have $z\in\unions{B}$
                        by \cref{immediate_successor_interval,upair_elim,upair_intro_left,upair_intro_right,unions_iff}.
                    Therefore $B$ is a covering of $\intervalclosedrel{R}{X}{x}{y}$ by \cref{subseteq,covering}.
                \caseOf{it is wrong that there exists $y\in X$ such that $y$ is an immediate successor of $x$ in $X$ with respect to $R$.}
                    Therefore it is wrong that $c$ is an immediate successor of $x$ in $X$ with respect to $R$
                        by \cref{subseteq}.
                    Take $y\in X$ such that $x\mathrel{R}y$ and $y\mathrel{R}c$ by \cref{no_immediate_successor_between}.
                    Hence $y\in Y$ by \cref{convex_in}.
                    Let $B = \{U\}$.
                    Thus $B\subseteq A$ and $B$ is finite
                        by \cref{singleton_subset_intro,pair_is_finite,upair_intro_left,subseteq_finite_is_finite}.
                    For all $z$ such that $z\in\intervalclosedrel{R}{X}{x}{y}$ we have
                        $z\in X$ and $(x\mathrel{R}z \lor z = x)$ and $(z\mathrel{R}y \lor z = y)$
                        by \cref{intervalclosed_rel}.
                    For all $z$ such that $z\in\intervalclosedrel{R}{X}{x}{y}$ we have $z\mathrel{R}c$
                        by \hyperref[transitive]{transitivity}.
                    Thus for all $z$ such that $z\in\intervalclosedrel{R}{X}{x}{y}$ we have $z\in\unions{B}$
                        by \cref{intervalclosedopen_rel,subseteq,convex_in,inter_intro,singleton_intro,unions_iff}.
                    Therefore $B$ is a covering of $\intervalclosedrel{R}{X}{x}{y}$ by \cref{subseteq,covering}.
                \end{byCase}
            \end{subproof}

            Let $C = \{y\in Y \mid \text{$a\mathrel{R}y$ and there exists $B\subseteq A$ such that
                $B$ is finite and $B$ is a covering of $\intervalclosedrel{R}{X}{a}{y}$}\}$.
            Now $a\in Y$.
            Take $y\in Y$ such that $a\mathrel{R}y$ and
                there exists $B$ such that $B\subseteq A$ and $B$ is finite and
                    $B$ is a covering of $\intervalclosedrel{R}{X}{a}{y}$.
            Hence $y\in C$.
            Therefore $C$ is inhabited.
            Hence $C\subseteq X$ by \cref{intervalclosed_rel,subseteq}.
            For all $y\in C$ we have $y\mathrel{R}b$ or $y = b$ by \cref{intervalclosed_rel}.
            Therefore $b$ is an upper bound of $C$ in $X$ with respect to $R$ by \cref{upper_bound_rel}.
            Take $c$ such that $c$ is a least upper bound of $C$ in $X$ with respect to $R$
                by \cref{least_upper_bound_property}.
            Show that $a\mathrel{R}c$.
            \begin{subproof}
                Take $y_0\in C$ such that $a\mathrel{R}y_0$ by \cref{nonempty_inhabited}.
                Suppose not.
                \begin{byCase}
                \caseOf{$c = a$.}
                    Thus $y_0\mathrel{R}c$ or $y_0 = c$ by \cref{least_upper_bound_rel,upper_bound_rel}.
                    Thus $y_0\mathrel{R}c$ by \cref{least_upper_bound_rel,upper_bound_rel,asymmetric}.
                    Thus $y_0\mathrel{R}a$.
                    Contradiction by \cref{asymmetric}.
                \caseOf{$c\neq a$.}
                    Thus $c\mathrel{R}a$ or $a\mathrel{R}c$ by \cref{least_upper_bound_rel,upper_bound_rel,connex}.
                    Thus $c\mathrel{R}a$ by \cref{least_upper_bound_rel,upper_bound_rel,connex}.
                    Thus $c\mathrel{R}y$ by \hyperref[transitive]{transitivity}.
                    Thus $y\mathrel{R}c$ or $y = c$ by \cref{least_upper_bound_rel,upper_bound_rel}.
                    Contradiction by \cref{asymmetric}.
                \end{byCase}
            \end{subproof}
            Thus $c\in X$ and $c\in Y$ by \cref{least_upper_bound_rel,upper_bound_rel,intervalclosed_rel}.

            Show that $c\in C$.
            \begin{subproof}
                Take $U\in A$ such that $c\in U$ by \cref{open_covering,covering,subseteq,unions_iff}.
                Take $V\in T$ such that $U = Y\inter V$ by \cref{open_covering,open_in,subspace_topology}.
                Thus $c\in V$ by \cref{inter_elim_right}.
                Take $B_1\in\orderBasis{R}{X}$ such that $c\in B_1$ and $B_1\subseteq V$
                    by \cref{topology_generated_by_basis}.
                Show that there exists $d\in Y$ such that $d\mathrel{R}c$ and
                    $\intervalopenclosedrel{R}{X}{d}{c} \subseteq V$.
                \begin{subproof}
                    $B_1\in\pow{X}$ and
                        $((\exists u, v\in X. u\mathrel{R}v \land B_1 = \intervalopenrel{R}{X}{u}{v}) \lor
                        (\exists v\in X. \exists a_0\in X. (\forall z\in X. a_0\mathrel{R}z \lor a_0 = z) \land
                            B_1 = \intervalclosedopenrel{R}{X}{a_0}{v}) \lor
                        (\exists u\in X. \exists b_0\in X. (\forall z\in X. z\mathrel{R}b_0 \lor z = b_0) \land
                            B_1 = \intervalopenclosedrel{R}{X}{u}{b_0}))$
                        by \cref{order_basis}.
                    \begin{byCase}
                    \caseOf{$\exists u, v\in X. u\mathrel{R}v \land B_1 = \intervalopenrel{R}{X}{u}{v}$.}
                        Take $u, v\in X$ such that $u\mathrel{R}v$ and $B_1 = \intervalopenrel{R}{X}{u}{v}$.
                        Thus $u\mathrel{R}c$ and $c\mathrel{R}v$ by \cref{intervalopen_rel}.
                        \begin{byCase}
                        \caseOf{$u\in Y$.}
                            Let $d = u$.
                            Thus $\intervalopenclosedrel{R}{X}{d}{c} \subseteq B_1$
                                by \cref{intervalopenclosed_rel,transitive,intervalopen_rel,subseteq}.
                        \caseOf{it is wrong that $u\in Y$.}
                            Thus $u\mathrel{R}a$ by \cref{connex,least_upper_bound_rel,transitive,intervalclosed_rel}.
                            Let $d = a$.
                            Thus $\intervalopenclosedrel{R}{X}{d}{c} \subseteq B_1$
                                by \cref{intervalopenclosed_rel,transitive,intervalopen_rel,subseteq}.
                        \end{byCase}
                    \caseOf{$\exists v\in X. \exists a_0\in X. (\forall z\in X. a_0\mathrel{R}z \lor a_0 = z) \land
                        B_1 = \intervalclosedopenrel{R}{X}{a_0}{v}$.}
                        Take $a_0, v\in X$ such that
                            $(\forall z\in X. a_0\mathrel{R}z \lor a_0 = z)$ and
                            $B_1 = \intervalclosedopenrel{R}{X}{a_0}{v}$.
                        Thus $c\in X$ and $(a_0\mathrel{R}c \lor c = a_0)$ and $c\mathrel{R}v$
                            by \cref{intervalclosedopen_rel}.
                        Let $d = a$.
                        Thus $\intervalopenclosedrel{R}{X}{d}{c} \subseteq B_1$
                            by \cref{intervalopenclosed_rel,smallest_element,transitive,intervalclosedopen_rel,subseteq}.
                    \caseOf{$\exists u\in X. \exists b_0\in X. (\forall z\in X. z\mathrel{R}b_0 \lor z = b_0) \land
                        B_1 = \intervalopenclosedrel{R}{X}{u}{b_0}$.}
                        Take $u, b_0\in X$ such that
                            $(\forall z\in X. z\mathrel{R}b_0 \lor z = b_0)$ and
                            $B_1 = \intervalopenclosedrel{R}{X}{u}{b_0}$.
                        Thus $u\mathrel{R}c$ and $(c\mathrel{R}b_0 \lor c = b_0)$ by \cref{intervalopenclosed_rel}.
                        \begin{byCase}
                        \caseOf{$u\in Y$.}
                            Let $d = u$.
                            Thus $\intervalopenclosedrel{R}{X}{d}{c} \subseteq B_1$
                                by \cref{intervalopenclosed_rel,transitive,intervalopenclosed_rel,subseteq}.
                        \caseOf{it is wrong that $u\in Y$.}
                            Thus $u\mathrel{R}a$ by \cref{connex,least_upper_bound_rel,transitive,intervalclosed_rel}.
                            Let $d = a$.
                            Thus $\intervalopenclosedrel{R}{X}{d}{c} \subseteq B_1$
                                by \cref{intervalopenclosed_rel,transitive,intervalopenclosed_rel,subseteq}.
                        \end{byCase}
                    \end{byCase}
                \end{subproof}
                Take $d\in Y$ such that $d\mathrel{R}c$ and $\intervalopenclosedrel{R}{X}{d}{c} \subseteq V$.
                Suppose not.
                Show that there exists $z\in C$ such that $d\mathrel{R}z$.
                \begin{subproof}
                    Suppose not.
                    For all $y\in C$ we have $y\mathrel{R}d$ or $y = d$ by \cref{subseteq,connex}.
                    Thus $c\mathrel{R}d$ or $c = d$ by \cref{subseteq,upper_bound_rel,least_upper_bound_rel}.
                    Contradiction by \cref{asymmetric}.
                \end{subproof}
                Take $z\in C$ such that $d\mathrel{R}z$.
                Thus $z\mathrel{R}c$ by \cref{least_upper_bound_rel,upper_bound_rel}.
                Take $B_0$ such that $B_0\subseteq A$ and $B_0$ is finite and
                    $B_0$ is a covering of $\intervalclosedrel{R}{X}{a}{z}$.
                Let $B = B_0\union\{U\}$.
                Hence $B$ is finite by \cref{pair_is_finite,upair_intro_left,singleton_subset_intro,subseteq_finite_is_finite,union_of_finite_is_finite}.
                Show that for all $w$ such that $w\in\intervalclosedrel{R}{X}{a}{c}$ we have $w\in\unions{B}$.
                \begin{subproof}
                    Fix $w$ such that $w\in\intervalclosedrel{R}{X}{a}{c}$.
                    Thus $w\in X$ and $(a\mathrel{R}w \lor w = a)$ and $(w\mathrel{R}c \lor w = c)$
                        by \cref{intervalclosed_rel}.
                    Thus $w\mathrel{R}z$ or $w = z$ or $z\mathrel{R}w$ by \cref{connex}.
                    \begin{byCase}
                    \caseOf{$w\mathrel{R}z$ or $w = z$.}
                        Thus $w\in\unions{B}$ by \cref{subseteq,intervalclosed_rel,covering,unions_iff,union_intro_left}.
                    \caseOf{$z\mathrel{R}w$.}
                        Thus $w\in V$ by \cref{transitive,intervalopenclosed_rel,subseteq}.
                        Thus $w\in Y$ by \cref{convex_in}.
                        Thus $w\in Y\inter V$ by \cref{inter_intro}.
                        Thus $w\in U$.
                        Thus $w\in\unions{B}$ by \cref{singleton_intro,union_intro_right,unions_iff}.
                    \end{byCase}
                \end{subproof}
                Therefore $B$ is a covering of $\intervalclosedrel{R}{X}{a}{c}$ by \cref{subseteq,covering}.
                Hence $B\subseteq A$ and $B$ is finite and
                    $B$ is a covering of $\intervalclosedrel{R}{X}{a}{c}$.
                Therefore $c\in C$.
                Contradiction.
            \end{subproof}

            Show that $c = b$.
            \begin{subproof}
                Suppose not.
                Thus $c\mathrel{R}b$ by \cref{least_upper_bound_rel}.
                Take $y_1,B_1$ such that $y_1\in Y$ and $c\mathrel{R}y_1$ and $B_1\subseteq A$ and
                    $B_1$ is finite and $B_1$ is a covering of $\intervalclosedrel{R}{X}{c}{y_1}$.
                Thus $a\mathrel{R}y_1$ by \hyperref[transitive]{transitivity}.
                Take $B_0$ such that $B_0\subseteq A$ and $B_0$ is finite and
                    $B_0$ is a covering of $\intervalclosedrel{R}{X}{a}{c}$.
                Let $B = B_0\union B_1$.
                Hence $B$ is finite by \cref{union_of_finite_is_finite}.
                For all $w$ such that $w\in\intervalclosedrel{R}{X}{a}{y_1}$ we have $w\in\unions{B}$
                    by \cref{intervalclosed_rel,covering,subseteq,unions_iff,union_intro_left,union_intro_right,connex}.
                Therefore $B$ is a covering of $\intervalclosedrel{R}{X}{a}{y_1}$ by \cref{subseteq,covering}.
                Hence $y_1\in C$.
                Therefore $y_1\mathrel{R}c$ or $y_1 = c$ by \cref{least_upper_bound_rel,upper_bound_rel}.
                Contradiction by \cref{asymmetric}.
            \end{subproof}
            Take $B$ such that $B\subseteq A$ and $B$ is finite and
                $B$ is a covering of $\intervalclosedrel{R}{X}{a}{b}$.
        
\end{byCase}

Therefore $Y$ is compact with respect to $T_Y$ by Definition 149.

LaTeX source 
\begin{proof}
    $R$ is transitive and $R$ is asymmetric and $R$ is connex on $X$ by \cref{simple_order_relation}.
    Show that for all $A$ such that $A$ is an open covering of $Y$ with respect to $T_Y$
        there exists $B$ such that $B\subseteq A$ and $B$ is finite and $B$ is a covering of $Y$.
    \begin{subproof}
        Fix $A$ such that $A$ is an open covering of $Y$ with respect to $T_Y$.
        \begin{byCase}
        \caseOf{$a = b$.}
            Take $U\in A$ such that $a\in U$
                by \cref{open_covering,covering,intervalclosed_rel,subseteq,unions_iff}.
            Let $B = \{U\}$.
            Hence $B\subseteq A$ and $B$ is finite
                by \cref{singleton_subset_intro,pair_is_finite,upair_intro_left,subseteq_finite_is_finite}.
            Thus for all $x$ such that $x\in Y$ we have $x\in\unions{B}$
                by \cref{intervalclosed_rel,asymmetric,singleton_intro,unions_iff}.
            Therefore $B$ is a covering of $Y$ by \cref{subseteq,covering}.
            Hence $B\subseteq A$ and $B$ is finite and $B$ is a covering of $Y$.
        \caseOf{$a\mathrel{R}b$.}
            $a\in Y$ and $b\in Y$ by \cref{intervalclosed_rel}.
            Hence $a\neq b$ by \cref{asymmetric}.
            Therefore $X$ has more than one element and $Y$ has more than one element by \cref{more_than_one_element}.
            $Y$ is convex in $X$ with respect to $R$ by \cref{intervalclosedrel_convex}.
            For all $y$ such that $y\in Y$ we have $y\in X$ by \cref{intervalclosed_rel}.
            Hence $Y\subseteq X$ by \cref{subseteq}.
            Then $a$ is a smallest element of $Y$ with respect to $R$ by \cref{intervalclosed_rel,smallest_element}.
            Finally $b$ is a largest element of $Y$ with respect to $R$ by \cref{intervalclosed_rel,largest_element}.
            $T = \basisTopology{\orderBasis{R}{X}}{X}$ by \cref{order_topology}.
            Therefore $T$ is a topology on $X$ by \cref{order_basis_is_basis,basis_topology_is_topology}.
            Hence $T_Y$ is a topology on $Y$ by \cref{subspace_topology_is_topology,subspace_of}.

            Show that for all $x$ such that $x\in Y$ and $x\neq b$ there exists $y\in Y$ such that
                $x\mathrel{R}y$ and
                there exists $B$ such that $B\subseteq A$ and $B$ is finite and
                    $B$ is a covering of $\intervalclosedrel{R}{X}{x}{y}$.
            \begin{subproof}
                Fix $x$ such that $x\in Y$ and $x\neq b$.
                Take $U\in A$ such that $x\in U$ by \cref{open_covering,covering,subseteq,unions_iff}.
                Take $V\in T$ such that $U = Y\inter V$ by \cref{open_covering,open_in,subspace_topology}.
                Thus $x\in V$ by \cref{inter_elim_right}.
                Take $B_1\in\orderBasis{R}{X}$ such that $x\in B_1$ and $B_1\subseteq V$
                    by \cref{topology_generated_by_basis}.
                Show that there exists $c\in Y$ such that $x\mathrel{R}c$ and
                    $\intervalclosedopenrel{R}{X}{x}{c} \subseteq V$.
                \begin{subproof}
                    $B_1\in\pow{X}$ and
                        $((\exists u, v\in X. u\mathrel{R}v \land B_1 = \intervalopenrel{R}{X}{u}{v}) \lor
                        (\exists v\in X. \exists a_0\in X. (\forall z\in X. a_0\mathrel{R}z \lor a_0 = z) \land
                            B_1 = \intervalclosedopenrel{R}{X}{a_0}{v}) \lor
                        (\exists u\in X. \exists b_0\in X. (\forall z\in X. z\mathrel{R}b_0 \lor z = b_0) \land
                            B_1 = \intervalopenclosedrel{R}{X}{u}{b_0}))$
                        by \cref{order_basis}.
                    \begin{byCase}
                    \caseOf{$\exists u, v\in X. u\mathrel{R}v \land B_1 = \intervalopenrel{R}{X}{u}{v}$.}
                        Take $u, v\in X$ such that $u\mathrel{R}v$ and $B_1 = \intervalopenrel{R}{X}{u}{v}$.
                        Thus $u\mathrel{R}x$ and $x\mathrel{R}v$ by \cref{intervalopen_rel}.
                        \begin{byCase}
                        \caseOf{$v\in Y$.}
                            Let $c = v$.
                            Thus $\intervalclosedopenrel{R}{X}{x}{c} \subseteq B_1$
                                by \cref{intervalclosedopen_rel,transitive,intervalopen_rel,subseteq}.
                        \caseOf{it is wrong that $v\in Y$.}
                            Thus $a\mathrel{R}v$ by \cref{intervalclosed_rel,transitive}.
                            Thus $b\mathrel{R}v$ by \cref{connex,intervalclosed_rel}.
                            Thus $\intervalclosedopenrel{R}{X}{x}{b} \subseteq B_1$
                                by \cref{intervalclosedopen_rel,transitive,intervalopen_rel,subseteq}.
                            Thus there exists $c\in Y$ such that $x\mathrel{R}c$ and
                                $\intervalclosedopenrel{R}{X}{x}{c} \subseteq B_1$ by \cref{intervalclosed_rel}.
                        \end{byCase}
                    \caseOf{$\exists v\in X. \exists a_0\in X. (\forall z\in X. a_0\mathrel{R}z \lor a_0 = z) \land
                        B_1 = \intervalclosedopenrel{R}{X}{a_0}{v}$.}
                        Take $a_0, v\in X$ such that
                            $(\forall z\in X. a_0\mathrel{R}z \lor a_0 = z)$ and
                            $B_1 = \intervalclosedopenrel{R}{X}{a_0}{v}$.
                        Thus $x\in X$ and $(a_0\mathrel{R}x \lor x = a_0)$ and $x\mathrel{R}v$
                            by \cref{intervalclosedopen_rel}.
                        \begin{byCase}
                        \caseOf{$v\in Y$.}
                            Let $c = v$.
                            Thus $\intervalclosedopenrel{R}{X}{x}{c} \subseteq B_1$
                                by \cref{intervalclosedopen_rel,smallest_element,intervalclosed_rel,subseteq}.
                        \caseOf{it is wrong that $v\in Y$.}
                            Thus $a\mathrel{R}v$ by \cref{intervalclosed_rel,transitive}.
                            Thus $b\mathrel{R}v$ by \cref{connex,intervalclosed_rel}.
                            Thus $\intervalclosedopenrel{R}{X}{x}{b} \subseteq B_1$
                                by \cref{intervalclosedopen_rel,smallest_element,transitive,subseteq}.
                            Thus there exists $c\in Y$ such that $x\mathrel{R}c$ and
                                $\intervalclosedopenrel{R}{X}{x}{c} \subseteq B_1$ by \cref{intervalclosed_rel}.
                        \end{byCase}
                    \caseOf{$\exists u\in X. \exists b_0\in X. (\forall z\in X. z\mathrel{R}b_0 \lor z = b_0) \land
                        B_1 = \intervalopenclosedrel{R}{X}{u}{b_0}$.}
                        Take $u, b_0\in X$ such that
                            $(\forall z\in X. z\mathrel{R}b_0 \lor z = b_0)$ and
                            $B_1 = \intervalopenclosedrel{R}{X}{u}{b_0}$.
                        Thus $u\mathrel{R}x$ and $(x\mathrel{R}b_0 \lor x = b_0)$ by \cref{intervalopenclosed_rel}.
                        Thus $\intervalclosedopenrel{R}{X}{x}{b} \subseteq B_1$
                            by \cref{intervalclosedopen_rel,transitive,intervalopenclosed_rel,subseteq}.
                        Thus there exists $c\in Y$ such that $x\mathrel{R}c$ and
                            $\intervalclosedopenrel{R}{X}{x}{c} \subseteq B_1$ by \cref{intervalclosed_rel}.
                    \end{byCase}
                \end{subproof}
                Take $c\in Y$ such that $x\mathrel{R}c$ and $\intervalclosedopenrel{R}{X}{x}{c} \subseteq V$.
                \begin{byCase}
                \caseOf{there exists $y\in X$ such that $y$ is an immediate successor of $x$ in $X$ with respect to $R$.}
                    Take $y\in X$ such that $y$ is an immediate successor of $x$ in $X$ with respect to $R$.
                    Show that $y\in Y$.
                    \begin{subproof}
                        Show that $y\mathrel{R}b$ or $y = b$.
                        \begin{subproof}
                            Thus $y\mathrel{R}b$ or $y = b$ or $b\mathrel{R}y$ by \cref{connex}.
                            \begin{byCase}
                            \caseOf{$b\mathrel{R}y$.}
                                Then $b\in\intervalclosedrel{R}{X}{x}{y}$ by \cref{intervalclosed_rel}.
                                Thus $b\in\{x,y\}$.
                                Thus $b = x$ or $b = y$ by \cref{upair_elim}.
                                Thus $y = b$.
                            \caseOf{$y\mathrel{R}b$ or $y = b$.}
                            \end{byCase}
                        \end{subproof}
                        Thus $y\in Y$ by \cref{immediate_successor,intervalclosed_rel,transitive}.
                    \end{subproof}
                    Take $U_1\in A$ such that $y\in U_1$
                        by \cref{open_covering,covering,subseteq,unions_iff}.
                    Let $B = \{U,U_1\}$.
                    Hence $B\subseteq A$ and $B$ is finite by \cref{upair_elim,subseteq,pair_is_finite}.
                    For all $z$ such that $z\in\intervalclosedrel{R}{X}{x}{y}$ we have $z\in\unions{B}$
                        by \cref{immediate_successor_interval,upair_elim,upair_intro_left,upair_intro_right,unions_iff}.
                    Therefore $B$ is a covering of $\intervalclosedrel{R}{X}{x}{y}$ by \cref{subseteq,covering}.
                \caseOf{it is wrong that there exists $y\in X$ such that $y$ is an immediate successor of $x$ in $X$ with respect to $R$.}
                    Therefore it is wrong that $c$ is an immediate successor of $x$ in $X$ with respect to $R$
                        by \cref{subseteq}.
                    Take $y\in X$ such that $x\mathrel{R}y$ and $y\mathrel{R}c$ by \cref{no_immediate_successor_between}.
                    Hence $y\in Y$ by \cref{convex_in}.
                    Let $B = \{U\}$.
                    Thus $B\subseteq A$ and $B$ is finite
                        by \cref{singleton_subset_intro,pair_is_finite,upair_intro_left,subseteq_finite_is_finite}.
                    For all $z$ such that $z\in\intervalclosedrel{R}{X}{x}{y}$ we have
                        $z\in X$ and $(x\mathrel{R}z \lor z = x)$ and $(z\mathrel{R}y \lor z = y)$
                        by \cref{intervalclosed_rel}.
                    For all $z$ such that $z\in\intervalclosedrel{R}{X}{x}{y}$ we have $z\mathrel{R}c$
                        by \hyperref[transitive]{transitivity}.
                    Thus for all $z$ such that $z\in\intervalclosedrel{R}{X}{x}{y}$ we have $z\in\unions{B}$
                        by \cref{intervalclosedopen_rel,subseteq,convex_in,inter_intro,singleton_intro,unions_iff}.
                    Therefore $B$ is a covering of $\intervalclosedrel{R}{X}{x}{y}$ by \cref{subseteq,covering}.
                \end{byCase}
            \end{subproof}

            Let $C = \{y\in Y \mid \text{$a\mathrel{R}y$ and there exists $B\subseteq A$ such that
                $B$ is finite and $B$ is a covering of $\intervalclosedrel{R}{X}{a}{y}$}\}$.
            Now $a\in Y$.
            Take $y\in Y$ such that $a\mathrel{R}y$ and
                there exists $B$ such that $B\subseteq A$ and $B$ is finite and
                    $B$ is a covering of $\intervalclosedrel{R}{X}{a}{y}$.
            Hence $y\in C$.
            Therefore $C$ is inhabited.
            Hence $C\subseteq X$ by \cref{intervalclosed_rel,subseteq}.
            For all $y\in C$ we have $y\mathrel{R}b$ or $y = b$ by \cref{intervalclosed_rel}.
            Therefore $b$ is an upper bound of $C$ in $X$ with respect to $R$ by \cref{upper_bound_rel}.
            Take $c$ such that $c$ is a least upper bound of $C$ in $X$ with respect to $R$
                by \cref{least_upper_bound_property}.
            Show that $a\mathrel{R}c$.
            \begin{subproof}
                Take $y_0\in C$ such that $a\mathrel{R}y_0$ by \cref{nonempty_inhabited}.
                Suppose not.
                \begin{byCase}
                \caseOf{$c = a$.}
                    Thus $y_0\mathrel{R}c$ or $y_0 = c$ by \cref{least_upper_bound_rel,upper_bound_rel}.
                    Thus $y_0\mathrel{R}c$ by \cref{least_upper_bound_rel,upper_bound_rel,asymmetric}.
                    Thus $y_0\mathrel{R}a$.
                    Contradiction by \cref{asymmetric}.
                \caseOf{$c\neq a$.}
                    Thus $c\mathrel{R}a$ or $a\mathrel{R}c$ by \cref{least_upper_bound_rel,upper_bound_rel,connex}.
                    Thus $c\mathrel{R}a$ by \cref{least_upper_bound_rel,upper_bound_rel,connex}.
                    Thus $c\mathrel{R}y$ by \hyperref[transitive]{transitivity}.
                    Thus $y\mathrel{R}c$ or $y = c$ by \cref{least_upper_bound_rel,upper_bound_rel}.
                    Contradiction by \cref{asymmetric}.
                \end{byCase}
            \end{subproof}
            Thus $c\in X$ and $c\in Y$ by \cref{least_upper_bound_rel,upper_bound_rel,intervalclosed_rel}.

            Show that $c\in C$.
            \begin{subproof}
                Take $U\in A$ such that $c\in U$ by \cref{open_covering,covering,subseteq,unions_iff}.
                Take $V\in T$ such that $U = Y\inter V$ by \cref{open_covering,open_in,subspace_topology}.
                Thus $c\in V$ by \cref{inter_elim_right}.
