                Take $B_1\in\orderBasis{R}{X}$ such that $c\in B_1$ and $B_1\subseteq V$
                    by \cref{topology_generated_by_basis}.
                Show that there exists $d\in Y$ such that $d\mathrel{R}c$ and
                    $\intervalopenclosedrel{R}{X}{d}{c} \subseteq V$.
                \begin{subproof}
                    $B_1\in\pow{X}$ and
                        $((\exists u, v\in X. u\mathrel{R}v \land B_1 = \intervalopenrel{R}{X}{u}{v}) \lor
                        (\exists v\in X. \exists a_0\in X. (\forall z\in X. a_0\mathrel{R}z \lor a_0 = z) \land
                            B_1 = \intervalclosedopenrel{R}{X}{a_0}{v}) \lor
                        (\exists u\in X. \exists b_0\in X. (\forall z\in X. z\mathrel{R}b_0 \lor z = b_0) \land
                            B_1 = \intervalopenclosedrel{R}{X}{u}{b_0}))$
                        by \cref{order_basis}.
                    \begin{byCase}
                    \caseOf{$\exists u, v\in X. u\mathrel{R}v \land B_1 = \intervalopenrel{R}{X}{u}{v}$.}
                        Take $u, v\in X$ such that $u\mathrel{R}v$ and $B_1 = \intervalopenrel{R}{X}{u}{v}$.
                        Thus $u\mathrel{R}c$ and $c\mathrel{R}v$ by \cref{intervalopen_rel}.
                        \begin{byCase}
                        \caseOf{$u\in Y$.}
                            Let $d = u$.
                            Thus $\intervalopenclosedrel{R}{X}{d}{c} \subseteq B_1$
                                by \cref{intervalopenclosed_rel,transitive,intervalopen_rel,subseteq}.
                        \caseOf{it is wrong that $u\in Y$.}
                            Thus $u\mathrel{R}a$ by \cref{connex,least_upper_bound_rel,transitive,intervalclosed_rel}.
                            Let $d = a$.
                            Thus $\intervalopenclosedrel{R}{X}{d}{c} \subseteq B_1$
                                by \cref{intervalopenclosed_rel,transitive,intervalopen_rel,subseteq}.
                        \end{byCase}
                    \caseOf{$\exists v\in X. \exists a_0\in X. (\forall z\in X. a_0\mathrel{R}z \lor a_0 = z) \land
                        B_1 = \intervalclosedopenrel{R}{X}{a_0}{v}$.}
                        Take $a_0, v\in X$ such that
                            $(\forall z\in X. a_0\mathrel{R}z \lor a_0 = z)$ and
                            $B_1 = \intervalclosedopenrel{R}{X}{a_0}{v}$.
                        Thus $c\in X$ and $(a_0\mathrel{R}c \lor c = a_0)$ and $c\mathrel{R}v$
                            by \cref{intervalclosedopen_rel}.
                        Let $d = a$.
                        Thus $\intervalopenclosedrel{R}{X}{d}{c} \subseteq B_1$
                            by \cref{intervalopenclosed_rel,smallest_element,transitive,intervalclosedopen_rel,subseteq}.
                    \caseOf{$\exists u\in X. \exists b_0\in X. (\forall z\in X. z\mathrel{R}b_0 \lor z = b_0) \land
                        B_1 = \intervalopenclosedrel{R}{X}{u}{b_0}$.}
                        Take $u, b_0\in X$ such that
                            $(\forall z\in X. z\mathrel{R}b_0 \lor z = b_0)$ and
                            $B_1 = \intervalopenclosedrel{R}{X}{u}{b_0}$.
                        Thus $u\mathrel{R}c$ and $(c\mathrel{R}b_0 \lor c = b_0)$ by \cref{intervalopenclosed_rel}.
                        \begin{byCase}
                        \caseOf{$u\in Y$.}
                            Let $d = u$.
                            Thus $\intervalopenclosedrel{R}{X}{d}{c} \subseteq B_1$
                                by \cref{intervalopenclosed_rel,transitive,intervalopenclosed_rel,subseteq}.
                        \caseOf{it is wrong that $u\in Y$.}
                            Thus $u\mathrel{R}a$ by \cref{connex,least_upper_bound_rel,transitive,intervalclosed_rel}.
                            Let $d = a$.
                            Thus $\intervalopenclosedrel{R}{X}{d}{c} \subseteq B_1$
                                by \cref{intervalopenclosed_rel,transitive,intervalopenclosed_rel,subseteq}.
                        \end{byCase}
                    \end{byCase}
                \end{subproof}
                Take $d\in Y$ such that $d\mathrel{R}c$ and $\intervalopenclosedrel{R}{X}{d}{c} \subseteq V$.
                Suppose not.
                Show that there exists $z\in C$ such that $d\mathrel{R}z$.
                \begin{subproof}
                    Suppose not.
                    For all $y\in C$ we have $y\mathrel{R}d$ or $y = d$ by \cref{subseteq,connex}.
                    Thus $c\mathrel{R}d$ or $c = d$ by \cref{subseteq,upper_bound_rel,least_upper_bound_rel}.
                    Contradiction by \cref{asymmetric}.
                \end{subproof}
                Take $z\in C$ such that $d\mathrel{R}z$.
                Thus $z\mathrel{R}c$ by \cref{least_upper_bound_rel,upper_bound_rel}.
                Take $B_0$ such that $B_0\subseteq A$ and $B_0$ is finite and
                    $B_0$ is a covering of $\intervalclosedrel{R}{X}{a}{z}$.
                Let $B = B_0\union\{U\}$.
                Hence $B$ is finite by \cref{pair_is_finite,upair_intro_left,singleton_subset_intro,subseteq_finite_is_finite,union_of_finite_is_finite}.
                Show that for all $w$ such that $w\in\intervalclosedrel{R}{X}{a}{c}$ we have $w\in\unions{B}$.
                \begin{subproof}
                    Fix $w$ such that $w\in\intervalclosedrel{R}{X}{a}{c}$.
                    Thus $w\in X$ and $(a\mathrel{R}w \lor w = a)$ and $(w\mathrel{R}c \lor w = c)$
                        by \cref{intervalclosed_rel}.
                    Thus $w\mathrel{R}z$ or $w = z$ or $z\mathrel{R}w$ by \cref{connex}.
                    \begin{byCase}
                    \caseOf{$w\mathrel{R}z$ or $w = z$.}
                        Thus $w\in\unions{B}$ by \cref{subseteq,intervalclosed_rel,covering,unions_iff,union_intro_left}.
                    \caseOf{$z\mathrel{R}w$.}
                        Thus $w\in V$ by \cref{transitive,intervalopenclosed_rel,subseteq}.
                        Thus $w\in Y$ by \cref{convex_in}.
                        Thus $w\in Y\inter V$ by \cref{inter_intro}.
                        Thus $w\in U$.
                        Thus $w\in\unions{B}$ by \cref{singleton_intro,union_intro_right,unions_iff}.
                    \end{byCase}
                \end{subproof}
                Therefore $B$ is a covering of $\intervalclosedrel{R}{X}{a}{c}$ by \cref{subseteq,covering}.
                Hence $B\subseteq A$ and $B$ is finite and
                    $B$ is a covering of $\intervalclosedrel{R}{X}{a}{c}$.
                Therefore $c\in C$.
                Contradiction.
            \end{subproof}

            Show that $c = b$.
            \begin{subproof}
                Suppose not.
                Thus $c\mathrel{R}b$ by \cref{least_upper_bound_rel}.
                Take $y_1,B_1$ such that $y_1\in Y$ and $c\mathrel{R}y_1$ and $B_1\subseteq A$ and
                    $B_1$ is finite and $B_1$ is a covering of $\intervalclosedrel{R}{X}{c}{y_1}$.
                Thus $a\mathrel{R}y_1$ by \hyperref[transitive]{transitivity}.
                Take $B_0$ such that $B_0\subseteq A$ and $B_0$ is finite and
                    $B_0$ is a covering of $\intervalclosedrel{R}{X}{a}{c}$.
                Let $B = B_0\union B_1$.
                Hence $B$ is finite by \cref{union_of_finite_is_finite}.
                For all $w$ such that $w\in\intervalclosedrel{R}{X}{a}{y_1}$ we have $w\in\unions{B}$
                    by \cref{intervalclosed_rel,covering,subseteq,unions_iff,union_intro_left,union_intro_right,connex}.
                Therefore $B$ is a covering of $\intervalclosedrel{R}{X}{a}{y_1}$ by \cref{subseteq,covering}.
                Hence $y_1\in C$.
                Therefore $y_1\mathrel{R}c$ or $y_1 = c$ by \cref{least_upper_bound_rel,upper_bound_rel}.
                Contradiction by \cref{asymmetric}.
            \end{subproof}
            Take $B$ such that $B\subseteq A$ and $B$ is finite and
                $B$ is a covering of $\intervalclosedrel{R}{X}{a}{b}$.
        \end{byCase}
    \end{subproof}
    Therefore $Y$ is compact with respect to $T_Y$ by \cref{compact}.
\end{proof}
Corollary 3
real_closed_interval_compact

Suppose $a\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\reals$.
Suppose $b\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\reals$.
Suppose $a < b$ or $a = b$.
Let $R = \realOrderRel$.
Let $T = \realOrderTopology$.
Let $Y = \intervalclosedrel{R}{\reals}{a}{b}$.
Let $T_Y = \subspaceTopology{T}{Y}$.
Then $Y$ is compact with respect to $T_Y$.

LaTeX source 
\begin{corollary}
\label{real_closed_interval_compact}
    Suppose $a\in\reals$.
    Suppose $b\in\reals$.
    Suppose $a < b$ or $a = b$.
    Let $R = \realOrderRel$.
    Let $T = \realOrderTopology$.
    Let $Y = \intervalclosedrel{R}{\reals}{a}{b}$.
    Let $T_Y = \subspaceTopology{T}{Y}$.
    Then $Y$ is compact with respect to $T_Y$.
\end{corollary}
Proof

Let $X = \reals$.
$R$ is a simple order relation on $X$ and $X$ has the least upper bound property with respect to $R$ and $X$ has more than one element by Lemma 218, Lemma 220, Definition 133.
Hence $Y$ is compact with respect to $T_Y$ by Definition 135, Definition 36, Lemma 216, Theorem 60.

LaTeX source 
\begin{proof}
    Let $X = \reals$.
    $R$ is a simple order relation on $X$ and $X$ has the least upper bound property with respect to $R$ and
        $X$ has more than one element by \cref{real_order_relation_simple,reals_linear_continuum,linear_continuum}.
    Hence $Y$ is compact with respect to $T_Y$
        by \cref{real_order_topology,order_topology,real_order_relation_intro,ordered_closed_interval_compact}.
\end{proof}
Lemma 256
real_four_sum_bound

Suppose $r_1,r_2,r_3,r_4,M_0\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\reals$.
Suppose $r_1 \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-leq}{\leq} 1$ and $r_4 \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-leq}{\leq} 1$ and $r_2 \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-leq}{\leq} M_0$ and $r_3 \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-leq}{\leq} M_0$.
Then $(r_1 + r_2) + (r_3 + r_4) \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-leq}{\leq} (M_0 + 1) + (M_0 + 1)$.

LaTeX source 
\begin{lemma}
\label{real_four_sum_bound}
    Suppose $r_1,r_2,r_3,r_4,M_0\in\reals$.
    Suppose $r_1 \leq 1$ and $r_4 \leq 1$ and $r_2 \leq M_0$ and $r_3 \leq M_0$.
    Then $(r_1 + r_2) + (r_3 + r_4) \leq (M_0 + 1) + (M_0 + 1)$.
\end{lemma}
Proof

$1\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\reals$ and $r_2 + 1\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\reals$ and $r_3 + 1\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\reals$ and $M_0 + 1\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\reals$ by Axiom 31, Axiom 32, Definition 188, Axiom 20.
$r_1 + r_2\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\reals$ and $r_3 + r_4\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\reals$ and $r_4 + r_3\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\reals$ and $(r_1 + r_2) + (r_3 + r_4)\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\reals$ and $(M_0 + 1) + (r_3 + r_4)\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\reals$ and $(r_3 + r_4) + (M_0 + 1)\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\reals$ and $(M_0 + 1) + (M_0 + 1)\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\reals$ by Axiom 20.
Therefore $(r_1 + r_2) + (r_3 + r_4) \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-leq}{\leq} (M_0 + 1) + (M_0 + 1)$ by Lemma 408, Axiom 23, Lemma 407.

LaTeX source 
\begin{proof}
    $1\in\reals$ and $r_2 + 1\in\reals$ and $r_3 + 1\in\reals$ and $M_0 + 1\in\reals$
        by \cref{real_naturals_subset_reals,real_one_in_naturals,subseteq,real_add_closed}.
    $r_1 + r_2\in\reals$ and $r_3 + r_4\in\reals$ and $r_4 + r_3\in\reals$ and
        $(r_1 + r_2) + (r_3 + r_4)\in\reals$ and $(M_0 + 1) + (r_3 + r_4)\in\reals$ and
        $(r_3 + r_4) + (M_0 + 1)\in\reals$ and $(M_0 + 1) + (M_0 + 1)\in\reals$
        by \cref{real_add_closed}.
    Therefore $(r_1 + r_2) + (r_3 + r_4) \leq (M_0 + 1) + (M_0 + 1)$
        by \cref{real_leq_add,real_add_comm,real_leq_trans}.
\end{proof}
Lemma 257
real_intervalclosed_compact_standard

Suppose $a\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\reals$.
Suppose $b\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\reals$.
Suppose $a \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-leq}{\leq} b$.
Let $I = \intervalclosed{a}{b}$.
Then $I$ is compact with respect to $\subspaceTopology{\standardTopologyR}{I}$.

LaTeX source 
\begin{lemma}
\label{real_intervalclosed_compact_standard}
    Suppose $a\in\reals$.
    Suppose $b\in\reals$.
    Suppose $a \leq b$.
    Let $I = \intervalclosed{a}{b}$.
    Then $I$ is compact with respect to $\subspaceTopology{\standardTopologyR}{I}$.
\end{lemma}
Proof

Let $R = \realOrderRel$.
Hence $I$ is compact with respect to $\subspaceTopology{\standardTopologyR}{I}$ by Definition 34, Lemma 217, Definition 275, Definition 188, Lemma 216, Proposition 3, Corollary 3, Lemma 230.

LaTeX source 
\begin{proof}
    Let $R = \realOrderRel$.
    Hence $I$ is compact with respect to $\subspaceTopology{\standardTopologyR}{I}$
        by \cref{intervalclosed_rel,real_order_relation_elim,intervalclosed,subseteq,real_order_relation_intro,subseteq_antisymmetric,real_closed_interval_compact,real_order_topology_eq_standard}.
\end{proof}
Lemma 258
projection_map_indexed_continuous

Assume $X$ is a function.
Assume $T$ is a function.
Assume $\dom{T} = \dom{X}$.
Assume $\dom{X}$ is inhabited.
Assume that for all $\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-alpha}{\alpha}\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\dom{X}$ we have $\apply{T}{\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-alpha}{\alpha}}$ is a topology on $\apply{X}{\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-alpha}{\alpha}}$.
Let $T_0 = \productTopologyIndexed{T}{X}$.
Then for all $\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-alpha}{\alpha}\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\dom{X}$ we have $\piIndex{X}{\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-alpha}{\alpha}}$ is continuous from $\productFamily{X}$ to $\apply{X}{\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-alpha}{\alpha}}$ with respect to $T_0$ and $\apply{T}{\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-alpha}{\alpha}}$.

LaTeX source 
\begin{lemma}
\label{projection_map_indexed_continuous}
    Assume $X$ is a function.
    Assume $T$ is a function.
    Assume $\dom{T} = \dom{X}$.
    Assume $\dom{X}$ is inhabited.
    Assume that for all $\alpha\in\dom{X}$ we have $\apply{T}{\alpha}$ is a topology on $\apply{X}{\alpha}$.
    Let $T_0 = \productTopologyIndexed{T}{X}$.
    Then for all $\alpha\in\dom{X}$ we have $\piIndex{X}{\alpha}$ is continuous from $\productFamily{X}$ to $\apply{X}{\alpha}$
        with respect to $T_0$ and $\apply{T}{\alpha}$.
\end{lemma}
Proof

Hence $T_0$ is a topology on $\productFamily{X}$ by Lemma 93, Lemma 94, Lemma 1, Definition 15.
Let $j = \identity{\productFamily{X}}$.
$j\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\funs{\productFamily{X}}{\productFamily{X}}$ and $j$ is continuous from $\productFamily{X}$ to $\productFamily{X}$ with respect to $T_0$ and $T_0$ by Proposition 418, Lemma 204.
Therefore for all $\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-alpha}{\alpha}\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\dom{X}$ we have $\piIndex{X}{\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-alpha}{\alpha}}\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-circ}{\circ} j$ is continuous from $\productFamily{X}$ to $\apply{X}{\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-alpha}{\alpha}}$ with respect to $T_0$ and $\apply{T}{\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-alpha}{\alpha}}$ by Lemma 110.
Fix $\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-alpha}{\alpha}$ such that $\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-alpha}{\alpha}\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\dom{X}$.
For all $x,z$ we have $x\mathrel{\piIndex{X}{\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-alpha}{\alpha}}\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-circ}{\circ} j} z$ iff $x\mathrel{\piIndex{X}{\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-alpha}{\alpha}}} z$ by Proposition 269, Proposition 305, Definition 72, Axiom 7.
Therefore $\piIndex{X}{\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-alpha}{\alpha}}$ is continuous from $\productFamily{X}$ to $\apply{X}{\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-alpha}{\alpha}}$ with respect to $T_0$ and $\apply{T}{\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-alpha}{\alpha}}$ by Lemma 110, Proposition 266, Lemma 108, Proposition 418, Proposition 367, Proposition 189.

LaTeX source 
\begin{proof}
    Hence $T_0$ is a topology on $\productFamily{X}$
        by \cref{product_subbasis_is_subbasis,product_subbasis_finite_intersections_basis,basis_topology_is_topology,basis_for_topology}.
    Let $j = \identity{\productFamily{X}}$.
    $j\in\funs{\productFamily{X}}{\productFamily{X}}$ and
        $j$ is continuous from $\productFamily{X}$ to $\productFamily{X}$ with respect to $T_0$ and $T_0$
        by \cref{id_elem_funs,identity_continuous}.
    Therefore for all $\alpha\in\dom{X}$ we have $\piIndex{X}{\alpha}\circ j$ is continuous from $\productFamily{X}$
        to $\apply{X}{\alpha}$ with respect to $T_0$ and $\apply{T}{\alpha}$
        by \cref{continuous_map_into_indexed_product}.
    Fix $\alpha$ such that $\alpha\in\dom{X}$.
    For all $x,z$ we have $x\mathrel{\piIndex{X}{\alpha}\circ j} z$ iff $x\mathrel{\piIndex{X}{\alpha}} z$
        by \cref{circ_iff,id_iff,projection_map_indexed,pair_eq_iff}.
    Therefore $\piIndex{X}{\alpha}$ is continuous from $\productFamily{X}$ to $\apply{X}{\alpha}$
        with respect to $T_0$ and $\apply{T}{\alpha}$
        by \cref{continuous_map_into_indexed_product,circ_is_relation,projection_map_indexed_function,id_elem_funs,funs_is_relation,relext}.
\end{proof}
Definition 152
indexed_product_compact_index

A natural number $m$ is indexed-product-compact iff for all $Y_1,T_1$ such that $Y_1$ is a function and $T_1$ is a function and $\dom{Y_1} = \initialSegment{m}$ and $\dom{T_1} = \initialSegment{m}$, if for all $i\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\initialSegment{m}$ we have $\apply{T_1}{i}$ is a topology on $\apply{Y_1}{i}$ then if for all $i\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\initialSegment{m}$ we have $\apply{Y_1}{i}$ is compact with respect to $\apply{T_1}{i}$ then $\productFamily{Y_1}$ is compact with respect to $\productTopologyIndexed{T_1}{Y_1}$.

LaTeX source 
\begin{definition}
\label{indexed_product_compact_index}
    A natural number $m$ is indexed-product-compact iff
        for all $Y_1,T_1$ such that $Y_1$ is a function and $T_1$ is a function and
            $\dom{Y_1} = \initialSegment{m}$ and $\dom{T_1} = \initialSegment{m}$,
            if for all $i\in\initialSegment{m}$ we have $\apply{T_1}{i}$ is a topology on $\apply{Y_1}{i}$ then
            if for all $i\in\initialSegment{m}$ we have $\apply{Y_1}{i}$ is compact with respect to $\apply{T_1}{i}$ then
            $\productFamily{Y_1}$ is compact with respect to $\productTopologyIndexed{T_1}{Y_1}$.
\end{definition}
Definition 153
finite_indexed_product_compact_property

$\finiteIndexedProductCompactProp{m}$ iff for all $Y_1,T_1$ such that $Y_1$ is a function and $T_1$ is a function and $\dom{Y_1} = \initialSegment{m}$ and $\dom{T_1} = \initialSegment{m}$ and for all $i\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\dom{Y_1}$ we have $\apply{T_1}{i}$ is a topology on $\apply{Y_1}{i}$ and $\apply{Y_1}{i}$ is compact with respect to $\apply{T_1}{i}$, we have $\productFamily{Y_1}$ is compact with respect to $\productTopologyIndexed{T_1}{Y_1}$.

LaTeX source 
\begin{definition}
\label{finite_indexed_product_compact_property}
    $\finiteIndexedProductCompactProp{m}$ iff
        for all $Y_1,T_1$ such that
            $Y_1$ is a function and $T_1$ is a function and
            $\dom{Y_1} = \initialSegment{m}$ and $\dom{T_1} = \initialSegment{m}$ and
            for all $i\in\dom{Y_1}$ we have $\apply{T_1}{i}$ is a topology on $\apply{Y_1}{i}$ and
                $\apply{Y_1}{i}$ is compact with respect to $\apply{T_1}{i}$,
        we have $\productFamily{Y_1}$ is compact with respect to $\productTopologyIndexed{T_1}{Y_1}$.
\end{definition}
Lemma 259
continuous_eq_subst

Let $f,g$ be functions.
Suppose $f = g$.
Suppose $g$ is continuous from $X$ to $Y$ with respect to $T$ and $T_1$.
Then $f$ is continuous from $X$ to $Y$ with respect to $T$ and $T_1$.

LaTeX source 
\begin{lemma}
\label{continuous_eq_subst}
    Let $f,g$ be functions.
    Suppose $f = g$.
    Suppose $g$ is continuous from $X$ to $Y$ with respect to $T$ and $T_1$.
    Then $f$ is continuous from $X$ to $Y$ with respect to $T$ and $T_1$.
\end{lemma}
Proof

Trivial.

LaTeX source 
\begin{proof}
    Trivial.
\end{proof}
Lemma 260
continuous_eq_codomain

Suppose $Y = Y_2$.
Suppose $T_1 = T_2$.
Suppose $f$ is continuous from $X$ to $Y$ with respect to $T$ and $T_1$.
Then $f$ is continuous from $X$ to $Y_2$ with respect to $T$ and $T_2$.

LaTeX source 
\begin{lemma}
\label{continuous_eq_codomain}
    Suppose $Y = Y_2$.
    Suppose $T_1 = T_2$.
    Suppose $f$ is continuous from $X$ to $Y$ with respect to $T$ and $T_1$.
    Then $f$ is continuous from $X$ to $Y_2$ with respect to $T$ and $T_2$.
\end{lemma}
Proof

Trivial.

LaTeX source 
\begin{proof}
    Trivial.
\end{proof}
Lemma 261
finite_indexed_product_compact_property_succ

Assume $k\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\naturalsPlus$.
Assume $\finiteIndexedProductCompactProp{k}$.
Then $\finiteIndexedProductCompactProp{k + 1}$.

LaTeX source 
\begin{lemma}
\label{finite_indexed_product_compact_property_succ}
    Assume $k\in\naturalsPlus$.
    Assume $\finiteIndexedProductCompactProp{k}$.
    Then $\finiteIndexedProductCompactProp{k + 1}$.
\end{lemma}
Proof
Subclaim: Show that for all $Y_1,T_1$ such that $Y_1$ is a function and $T_1$ is a function and $\dom{Y_1} = \initialSegment{k + 1}$ and $\dom{T_1} = \initialSegment{k + 1}$ and for all $i\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\dom{Y_1}$ we have $\apply{T_1}{i}$ is a topology on $\apply{Y_1}{i}$ and $\apply{Y_1}{i}$ is compact with respect to $\apply{T_1}{i}$, we have $\productFamily{Y_1}$ is compact with respect to $\productTopologyIndexed{T_1}{Y_1}$.

Fix $Y_1$.
Fix $T_1$.
Assume $Y_1$ is a function.
Assume $T_1$ is a function.
Assume $\dom{Y_1} = \initialSegment{k + 1}$.
Assume $\dom{T_1} = \initialSegment{k + 1}$.
Assume that for all $i\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\dom{Y_1}$ we have $\apply{T_1}{i}$ is a topology on $\apply{Y_1}{i}$ and $\apply{Y_1}{i}$ is compact with respect to $\apply{T_1}{i}$.
Let $J_1 = \initialSegment{k}$.
Let $J = \initialSegment{k + 1}$.
For all $i$ such that $i\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} J_1$ we have $i\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} J$ by Definition 281, Axiom 31, Definition 188, Axiom 35, Axiom 24, Axiom 32, Theorem 114, Axiom 45, Axiom 23, Lemma 7, Lemma 146, Lemma 407.
Hence $J_1\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-subseteq}{\subseteq} J$ by Definition 188.
$k\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\reals$ by Axiom 31, Definition 188.
$k + 1\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\naturalsPlus$ and $k + 1\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\reals$ by Axiom 35, Axiom 31, Definition 188.
Thus $k < k + 1$ by Theorem 114, Axiom 24, Axiom 31, Axiom 32, Definition 188, Axiom 45, Axiom 23, Lemma 7.

Subclaim: Show that $k \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-neq}{\neq} k + 1$.

Suppose not.
Thus $k = k + 1$.
Thus $k < k$ by Lemma 7.
Contradiction by Axiom 47.

Let $Y_0 = \{(i,\apply{Y_1}{i}) \mid i\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} J_1\}$.
Let $T_0 = \{(i,\apply{T_1}{i}) \mid i\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} J_1\}$.
$\dom{Y_0} = J_1$ by Proposition 212, Proposition 211, Axiom 7, Definition 188, Proposition 3.
Then $\dom{T_0} = J_1$ by Proposition 212, Proposition 211, Axiom 7, Definition 188, Proposition 3.
$Y_0$ is a relation by Definition 202.
For all $a,b_1,b_2$ such that $(a,b_1)\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} Y_0$ and $(a,b_2)\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} Y_0$ we have $b_1 = b_2$ by Axiom 7.
Then $Y_0$ is a function by Definition 241.
$T_0$ is a relation by Definition 202.
For all $a,b_1,b_2$ such that $(a,b_1)\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} T_0$ and $(a,b_2)\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} T_0$ we have $b_1 = b_2$ by Axiom 7.
Then $T_0$ is a function by Definition 241.
For all $i$ such that $i\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} J_1$ we have $\apply{T_0}{i}$ is a topology on $\apply{Y_0}{i}$ by Definition 188, Proposition 358.
For all $i$ such that $i\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} J_1$ we have $\apply{Y_0}{i}$ is compact with respect to $\apply{T_0}{i}$ by Definition 188, Proposition 358.
Therefore $\productFamily{Y_0}$ is compact with respect to $\productTopologyIndexed{T_0}{Y_0}$ by Definition 153.
Let $X_0 = \productFamily{Y_0}$.
Let $T_X = \productTopologyIndexed{T_0}{Y_0}$.
Let $X_1 = \apply{Y_1}{k + 1}$.
Let $T_{1p} = \apply{T_1}{k + 1}$.
Then $k + 1\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} J$ by Axiom 35, Definition 281.

Subclaim: Show that $T_{1p}$ is a topology on $X_1$ and $X_1$ is compact with respect to $T_{1p}$.

Follows by Definition 188.

Now $J_1$ is inhabited by Axiom 33, Axiom 32, Definition 281.
Hence $T_X$ is a topology on $X_0$ by Lemma 94, Definition 15, Lemma 1.
Let $T_P = \productTopology{T_X}{T_{1p}}{X_0}{X_1}$.
Therefore $X_0\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-times}{\times} X_1$ is compact with respect to $T_P$ by Theorem 58.
Let $g = \{(p,\fst{p}\union\{(k + 1,\snd{p})\}) \mid p\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} X_0\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-times}{\times} X_1\}$.
$g$ is a relation by Definition 202.
For all $p,u,v$ such that $(p,u)\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} g$ and $(p,v)\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} g$ we have $u = v$ by Axiom 7.
Hence $g$ is a function by Definition 241.
Thus $\dom{g} = X_0\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-times}{\times} X_1$ by Proposition 212, Proposition 211, Axiom 7, Definition 188, Proposition 3.

Subclaim: Show that for all $p\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\dom{g}$ we have $g(p)\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\productFamily{Y_1}$.

Fix $p$ such that $p\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\dom{g}$.
Take $v$ such that $(p,v)\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} g$ by Proposition 211.
Take $p_0\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} X_0\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-times}{\times} X_1$ such that $(p,v) = (p_0,\fst{p_0}\union\{(k + 1,\snd{p_0})\})$.
Thus $p = p_0$ and $v = \fst{p}\union\{(k + 1,\snd{p})\}$ by Axiom 7.
Let $x = \fst{p}$.
Let $y = \snd{p}$.
Then $x\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} X_0$ and $y\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} X_1$ by Definition 188, Proposition 122.
Hence $x\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\funs{\dom{Y_0}}{\unions{\ran{Y_0}}}$ and $x$ is a function and $\dom{x} = \dom{Y_0}$ by Definition 188, Definition 69, Proposition 371.
Then $x\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\rels{\dom{Y_0}}{\unions{\ran{Y_0}}}$ and $x$ is a relation by Proposition 369, Definition 188, Proposition 297.

Subclaim: Show that $v\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\funs{\dom{Y_1}}{\unions{\ran{Y_1}}}$.

For all $w$ such that $w\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} v$ there exist $q_1,q_2$ such that $w = (q_1,q_2)$ by Axiom 6, Proposition 22, Definition 202.
Hence $v$ is a relation by Definition 202.

Subclaim: Show that for all $a,b_1,b_2$ such that $(a,b_1)\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} v$ and $(a,b_2)\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} v$ we have $b_1 = b_2$.

Fix $a$.
Fix $b_1$.
Fix $b_2$ such that $(a,b_1)\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} v$ and $(a,b_2)\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} v$.

Case. $a = k + 1$.

Thus $(a,b_1)\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} x$ or $(a,b_1) = (k + 1,y)$ by Axiom 6, Proposition 22.

Subclaim: Show that $(a,b_1)\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-notin}{\notin} x$.

Suppose not.
Thus $(a,b_1)\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} x$.
Thus $a\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\dom{x}$ by Proposition 212.
Thus $\dom{x} = \dom{Y_0}$ by Proposition 371.
Thus $a\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} J_1$ by Definition 188.
Thus $a\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\naturalsPlus$ and $a \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-leq}{\leq} k$ by Definition 281.
Then $k + 1 \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-leq}{\leq} k$.
Hence $k + 1 < k$ or $k + 1 = k$ by Lemma 6.
Contradiction by Axiom 43, Lemma 7, Axiom 47.

Hence $(a,b_1) = (k + 1,y)$.
Then $(a,b_2)\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} x$ or $(a,b_2) = (k + 1,y)$ by Axiom 6, Proposition 22.

Subclaim: Show that $(a,b_2)\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-notin}{\notin} x$.

Suppose not.
Thus $(a,b_2)\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} x$.
Thus $a\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\dom{x}$ by Proposition 212.
Thus $\dom{x} = \dom{Y_0}$ by Proposition 371.
Thus $a\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} J_1$ by Definition 188.
Thus $a\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\naturalsPlus$ and $a \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-leq}{\leq} k$ by Definition 281.
Then $k + 1 \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-leq}{\leq} k$.
Hence $k + 1 < k$ or $k + 1 = k$ by Lemma 6.
Contradiction by Axiom 43, Lemma 7, Axiom 47.

Hence $(a,b_2) = (k + 1,y)$.
Then $b_1 = y$ and $b_2 = y$ by Axiom 7.
Hence $b_1 = b_2$.

Case. $a \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-neq}{\neq} k + 1$.

Then $(a,b_1)\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} x$ by Axiom 6, Proposition 22, Axiom 7.
Then $(a,b_2)\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} x$ by Axiom 6, Proposition 22, Axiom 7.
Thus $b_1 = b_2$ by Definition 241, Definition 202.

LaTeX source 
\begin{byCase}
                                \caseOf{$a = k + 1$.}
                                    Thus $(a,b_1)\in x$ or $(a,b_1) = (k + 1,y)$ by \cref{union_iff,singleton_iff}.
                                    Show that $(a,b_1)\notin x$.
                                    \begin{subproof}
                                        Suppose not.
                                        Thus $(a,b_1)\in x$.
                                        Thus $a\in\dom{x}$ by \cref{dom_intro}.
                                        Thus $\dom{x} = \dom{Y_0}$ by \cref{funs_elim}.
                                        Thus $a\in J_1$ by \cref{subseteq}.
                                        Thus $a\in\naturalsPlus$ and $a \leq k$ by \cref{initial_segment_naturalsplus}.
                                        Then $k + 1 \leq k$.
                                        Hence $k + 1 < k$ or $k + 1 = k$ by \cref{real_leq_split}.
                                        Contradiction by \cref{real_lt_asym,real_lt_subst_left,real_lt_irreflexive}.
                                    \end{subproof}
                                    Hence $(a,b_1) = (k + 1,y)$.
                                    Then $(a,b_2)\in x$ or $(a,b_2) = (k + 1,y)$ by \cref{union_iff,singleton_iff}.
                                    Show that $(a,b_2)\notin x$.
                                    \begin{subproof}
                                        Suppose not.
                                        Thus $(a,b_2)\in x$.
                                        Thus $a\in\dom{x}$ by \cref{dom_intro}.
                                        Thus $\dom{x} = \dom{Y_0}$ by \cref{funs_elim}.
                                        Thus $a\in J_1$ by \cref{subseteq}.
                                        Thus $a\in\naturalsPlus$ and $a \leq k$ by \cref{initial_segment_naturalsplus}.
                                        Then $k + 1 \leq k$.
                                        Hence $k + 1 < k$ or $k + 1 = k$ by \cref{real_leq_split}.
                                        Contradiction by \cref{real_lt_asym,real_lt_subst_left,real_lt_irreflexive}.
                                    \end{subproof}
                                    Hence $(a,b_2) = (k + 1,y)$.
                                    Then $b_1 = y$ and $b_2 = y$ by \cref{pair_eq_iff}.
                                    Hence $b_1 = b_2$.
                                \caseOf{$a \neq k + 1$.}
                                    Then $(a,b_1)\in x$ by \cref{union_iff,singleton_iff,pair_eq_iff}.
                                    Then $(a,b_2)\in x$ by \cref{union_iff,singleton_iff,pair_eq_iff}.
                                    Thus $b_1 = b_2$ by \cref{rightunique,relation}.
                                
\end{byCase}

Then $v$ is a function by Definition 241.
For all $i$ such that $i\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\dom{Y_1}$ we have $i\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\dom{v}$ by Definition 188, Axiom 6, Proposition 22, Proposition 212, Lemma 417, Definition 281, Definition 69, Proposition 371, Proposition 211.
Hence $\dom{Y_1}\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-subseteq}{\subseteq}\dom{v}$ by Definition 188.

Subclaim: Show that for all $i$ such that $i\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\dom{v}$ we have $i\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\dom{Y_1}$.

Fix $i$ such that $i\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\dom{v}$.
Take $b$ such that $(i,b)\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} v$ by Proposition 211.
Thus $(i,b)\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} x$ or $(i,b) = (k + 1,y)$ by Axiom 6, Proposition 22.

Case. $(i,b) = (k + 1,y)$.

Thus $i = k + 1$ by Axiom 7.
Thus $i\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} J$.
Thus $i\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\dom{Y_1}$ by Definition 188.

Case. $(i,b)\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} x$.

Thus $i\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\dom{x}$ by Proposition 212.
Thus $i\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} J_1$ by Definition 69, Proposition 371.
Thus $i\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} J$ by Definition 188, Definition 281.
Thus $i\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\dom{Y_1}$ by Definition 188.

LaTeX source 
\begin{byCase}
                                \caseOf{$(i,b) = (k + 1,y)$.}
                                    Thus $i = k + 1$ by \cref{pair_eq_iff}.
                                    Thus $i\in J$.
                                    Thus $i\in\dom{Y_1}$ by \cref{subseteq}.
                                \caseOf{$(i,b)\in x$.}
                                    Thus $i\in\dom{x}$ by \cref{dom_intro}.
                                    Thus $i\in J_1$ by \cref{indexed_product,funs_elim}.
                                    Thus $i\in J$ by \cref{subseteq,initial_segment_naturalsplus}.
                                    Thus $i\in\dom{Y_1}$ by \cref{subseteq}.
                                
\end{byCase}

Therefore $\dom{v}\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-subseteq}{\subseteq}\dom{Y_1}$ by Definition 188.
Hence $\dom{v} = \dom{Y_1}$ by Proposition 3.

Subclaim: Show that for all $i\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\dom{Y_1}$ we have $v(i)\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\unions{\ran{Y_1}}$.

Fix $i$ such that $i\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\dom{Y_1}$.
Thus $i\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} J$ by Definition 188.

Case. $i = k + 1$.

Then $(k + 1,y)\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} v$ by Axiom 6, Proposition 22.
Hence $v(k + 1) = y$ by Proposition 358.
Thus $(k + 1,X_1)\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} Y_1$ by Proposition 360, Definition 188.
Thus $X_1\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\ran{Y_1}$ by Proposition 221.
Thus $y\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\unions{\ran{Y_1}}$ by Axiom 5.
Therefore $v(i)\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\unions{\ran{Y_1}}$.

Case. $i \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-neq}{\neq} k + 1$.

Thus $i\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} J_1$ by Lemma 417, Definition 281.
Thus $i\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\dom{x}$ by Definition 188, Definition 69, Proposition 371.
Thus $x(i)\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\apply{Y_0}{i}$ by Definition 69.
Thus $(i,\apply{Y_1}{i})\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} Y_0$.
Then $x(i)\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\apply{Y_1}{i}$ by Proposition 358.
Thus $(i,\apply{Y_1}{i})\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} Y_1$ by Proposition 360.
Thus $\apply{Y_1}{i}\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\ran{Y_1}$ by Proposition 221.
Thus $x(i)\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\unions{\ran{Y_1}}$ by Axiom 5.
Thus $(i,x(i))\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} v$ by Proposition 360, Axiom 6.
Thus $v(i) = x(i)$ by Proposition 358.
Hence $v(i)\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\unions{\ran{Y_1}}$.

LaTeX source 
\begin{byCase}
                            \caseOf{$i = k + 1$.}
                                Then $(k + 1,y)\in v$ by \cref{union_iff,singleton_iff}.
                                Hence $v(k + 1) = y$ by \cref{function_apply_intro}.
                                Thus $(k + 1,X_1)\in Y_1$ by \cref{function_apply_elim,subseteq}.
                                Thus $X_1\in\ran{Y_1}$ by \cref{ran_intro}.
                                Thus $y\in\unions{\ran{Y_1}}$ by \cref{unions_iff}.
                                Therefore $v(i)\in\unions{\ran{Y_1}}$.
                            \caseOf{$i \neq k + 1$.}
                                Thus $i\in J_1$ by \cref{real_naturals_leq_succ_elim,initial_segment_naturalsplus}.
                                Thus $i\in\dom{x}$ by \cref{subseteq,indexed_product,funs_elim}.
                                Thus $x(i)\in\apply{Y_0}{i}$ by \cref{indexed_product}.
                                Thus $(i,\apply{Y_1}{i})\in Y_0$.
                                Then $x(i)\in\apply{Y_1}{i}$ by \cref{function_apply_intro}.
                                Thus $(i,\apply{Y_1}{i})\in Y_1$ by \cref{function_apply_elim}.
                                Thus $\apply{Y_1}{i}\in\ran{Y_1}$ by \cref{ran_intro}.
                                Thus $x(i)\in\unions{\ran{Y_1}}$ by \cref{unions_iff}.
                                Thus $(i,x(i))\in v$ by \cref{function_apply_elim,union_iff}.
                                Thus $v(i) = x(i)$ by \cref{function_apply_intro}.
                                Hence $v(i)\in\unions{\ran{Y_1}}$.
                            
\end{byCase}

Therefore $v\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\funs{\dom{Y_1}}{\unions{\ran{Y_1}}}$ by Proposition 370.

Then $v$ is right-unique and $v$ is a relation by Definition 245, Proposition 297.

Subclaim: Show that for all $i\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\dom{Y_1}$ we have $v(i)\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\apply{Y_1}{i}$.

Fix $i$ such that $i\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\dom{Y_1}$.
Thus $i\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} J$ by Definition 188.

Case. $i = k + 1$.

Thus $(k + 1,y)\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} v$ by Axiom 6, Proposition 22.
Thus $v(k + 1) = y$ by Proposition 358.
Thus $y\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} X_1$ by Definition 188.
Hence $v(i)\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\apply{Y_1}{i}$.

Case. $i \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-neq}{\neq} k + 1$.

Thus $i\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} J_1$ by Lemma 417, Definition 281.
Thus $x(i)\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\apply{Y_0}{i}$ by Definition 69.
Then $x(i)\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\apply{Y_1}{i}$ by Proposition 358.
Thus $(i,x(i))\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} v$ by Proposition 360, Axiom 6.
Thus $v(i) = x(i)$ by Proposition 358.
Then $v(i)\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\apply{Y_1}{i}$.

LaTeX source 
\begin{byCase}
                        \caseOf{$i = k + 1$.}
                            Thus $(k + 1,y)\in v$ by \cref{union_iff,singleton_iff}.
                            Thus $v(k + 1) = y$ by \cref{function_apply_intro}.
                            Thus $y\in X_1$ by \cref{subseteq}.
                            Hence $v(i)\in\apply{Y_1}{i}$.
                        \caseOf{$i \neq k + 1$.}
                            Thus $i\in J_1$ by \cref{real_naturals_leq_succ_elim,initial_segment_naturalsplus}.
                            Thus $x(i)\in\apply{Y_0}{i}$ by \cref{indexed_product}.
                            Then $x(i)\in\apply{Y_1}{i}$ by \cref{function_apply_intro}.
                            Thus $(i,x(i))\in v$ by \cref{function_apply_elim,union_iff}.
                            Thus $v(i) = x(i)$ by \cref{function_apply_intro}.
                            Then $v(i)\in\apply{Y_1}{i}$.
                        
\end{byCase}

Therefore $v\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\productFamily{Y_1}$ by Definition 69.
Hence $g(p)\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\productFamily{Y_1}$ by Proposition 358.

Therefore $g\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\funs{X_0\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-times}{\times} X_1}{\productFamily{Y_1}}$ by Proposition 370.
Then $g$ is right-unique and $g$ is a relation by Definition 245, Proposition 297.

Subclaim: Show that for all $i\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} J$ we have $\piIndex{Y_1}{i}\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-circ}{\circ} g$ is continuous from $X_0\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-times}{\times} X_1$ to $\apply{Y_1}{i}$ with respect to $T_P$ and $\apply{T_1}{i}$.

Fix $i$ such that $i\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} J$.

Case. $i = k + 1$.

Let $h = \piTwo{X_0}{X_1}$.
$h$ is continuous from $X_0\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-times}{\times} X_1$ to $X_1$ with respect to $T_P$ and $T_{1p}$ by Lemma 89.

Subclaim: Show that for all $p\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} X_0\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-times}{\times} X_1$ we have $\apply{\piIndex{Y_1}{i}\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-circ}{\circ} g}{p} = \apply{h}{p}$.

Fix $p$ such that $p\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} X_0\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-times}{\times} X_1$.
Thus $g$ is a function to $\productFamily{Y_1}$ and $g$ is a function by Proposition 371, Proposition 368.
Thus $g(p)\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\productFamily{Y_1}$ by Definition 245, Proposition 360, Proposition 221, Proposition 366, Definition 188.
Thus $(p,\fst{p}\union\{(k + 1,\snd{p})\})\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} g$.
Thus $g(p) = \fst{p}\union\{(k + 1,\snd{p})\}$ by Proposition 358.
Thus $(i,\snd{p})\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} g(p)$ by Axiom 6, Proposition 22.
Thus $g(p)\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\funs{\dom{Y_1}}{\unions{\ran{Y_1}}}$ and $g(p)$ is a function by Definition 69, Proposition 368.
Thus $\apply{g(p)}{i} = \snd{p}$ by Proposition 358.
Thus $(g(p),\snd{p})\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\piIndex{Y_1}{i}$ by Axiom 7, Definition 72.
Thus $(p,\snd{p})\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\piIndex{Y_1}{i}\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-circ}{\circ} g$ by Proposition 269.
Thus $\piIndex{Y_1}{i}\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\funs{\productFamily{Y_1}}{\apply{Y_1}{i}}$ and $\piIndex{Y_1}{i}$ is a function by Lemma 108, Proposition 368.
Thus $\piIndex{Y_1}{i}\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-circ}{\circ} g$ is a function by Proposition 384.
Thus $\apply{\piIndex{Y_1}{i}\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-circ}{\circ} g}{p} = \snd{p}$ by Proposition 358.
Thus $h\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\funs{X_0\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-times}{\times} X_1}{X_1}$ and $h$ is a function by Definition 62, Proposition 368.
Take $x,y$ such that $p = (x,y)$ by Proposition 121.
Thus $(x,y)\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} X_0\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-times}{\times} X_1$.
Thus $x\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} X_0$ and $y\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} X_1$ by Proposition 114.
Thus $\snd{p} = y$ by Axiom 12.
Thus $(p,\snd{p})\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} h$ by Axiom 7, Definition 45.
Therefore $\apply{\piIndex{Y_1}{i}\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-circ}{\circ} g}{p} = \apply{h}{p}$ by Proposition 358.

$\piIndex{Y_1}{i}\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-circ}{\circ} g$ is a function and $\dom{\piIndex{Y_1}{i}\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-circ}{\circ} g} = X_0\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-times}{\times} X_1$ by Lemma 108, Proposition 368, Proposition 384, Proposition 211, Proposition 269, Proposition 212, Definition 245, Definition 188, Proposition 360, Proposition 221, Proposition 371, Proposition 366, Definition 72, Proposition 3.
$h$ is a function and $\dom{h} = X_0\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-times}{\times} X_1$ by Definition 62, Proposition 368, Proposition 371.
Hence $\piIndex{Y_1}{i}\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-circ}{\circ} g = h$ by Proposition 371, Definition 188, Proposition 362, Proposition 3.
Thus $\piIndex{Y_1}{i}\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-circ}{\circ} g$ is continuous from $X_0\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-times}{\times} X_1$ to $\apply{Y_1}{i}$ with respect to $T_P$ and $\apply{T_1}{i}$ by Lemma 259.

Case. $i \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-neq}{\neq} k + 1$.

Then $i\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} J_1$ by Lemma 417, Definition 281.
Let $h_0 = \piOne{X_0}{X_1}$.
$h_0$ is continuous from $X_0\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-times}{\times} X_1$ to $X_0$ with respect to $T_P$ and $T_X$ by Lemma 88.
$\piIndex{Y_0}{i}$ is continuous from $X_0$ to $\apply{Y_0}{i}$ with respect to $T_X$ and $\apply{T_0}{i}$ by Lemma 258, Definition 188, Proposition 3.
Let $h = \piIndex{Y_0}{i}\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-circ}{\circ} h_0$.
$h$ is continuous from $X_0\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-times}{\times} X_1$ to $\apply{Y_0}{i}$ with respect to $T_P$ and $\apply{T_0}{i}$ by Lemma 82.

Subclaim: Show that for all $p\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} X_0\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-times}{\times} X_1$ we have $\apply{\piIndex{Y_1}{i}\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-circ}{\circ} g}{p} = \apply{h}{p}$.

Fix $p$ such that $p\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} X_0\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-times}{\times} X_1$.
Thus $(p,\fst{p}\union\{(k + 1,\snd{p})\})\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} g$.
Thus $g(p) = \fst{p}\union\{(k + 1,\snd{p})\}$ by Proposition 358.
Let $x = \fst{p}$.
Take $a,b$ such that $p = (a,b)$ and $a\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} X_0$ and $b\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} X_1$ by Proposition 121.
Thus $\fst{p} = a$ by Axiom 11.
Thus $x = a$.
Thus $x\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} X_0$.
Thus $(p,x)\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} h_0$ by Axiom 7, Definition 44.
Thus $(x,\apply{x}{i})\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\piIndex{Y_0}{i}$ by Definition 72.
Thus $(p,\apply{x}{i})\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\piIndex{Y_0}{i}\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-circ}{\circ} h_0$ by Proposition 269.
Thus $h\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\funs{X_0\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-times}{\times} X_1}{\apply{Y_0}{i}}$ and $h$ is a function by Definition 62, Proposition 368.
Thus $\apply{h}{p} = \apply{x}{i}$ by Proposition 358.
Thus $g$ is a function to $\productFamily{Y_1}$ and $g$ is a function by Proposition 371, Proposition 368.
Thus $g(p)\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\productFamily{Y_1}$ by Definition 245, Proposition 360, Proposition 221, Proposition 366, Definition 188.
Thus $g(p)\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\funs{\dom{Y_1}}{\unions{\ran{Y_1}}}$ and $g(p)$ is a function by Definition 69, Proposition 368.
Thus $x\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\funs{\dom{Y_0}}{\unions{\ran{Y_0}}}$ and $x$ is a function by Definition 69, Proposition 368.
Thus $i\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\dom{x}$ by Proposition 371, Definition 188.
Thus $(i,\apply{x}{i})\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} x$ by Proposition 360.
Thus $(i,\apply{x}{i})\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} g(p)$ by Axiom 6.
Thus $\apply{g(p)}{i} = \apply{x}{i}$ by Proposition 358.
Thus $(g(p),\apply{x}{i})\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\piIndex{Y_1}{i}$ by Definition 72.
Thus $(p,\apply{x}{i})\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\piIndex{Y_1}{i}\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-circ}{\circ} g$ by Proposition 269.
Thus $\piIndex{Y_1}{i}\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\funs{\productFamily{Y_1}}{\apply{Y_1}{i}}$ and $\piIndex{Y_1}{i}$ is a function by Lemma 108, Proposition 368.
Thus $\piIndex{Y_1}{i}\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-circ}{\circ} g$ is a function by Proposition 384.
Therefore $\apply{\piIndex{Y_1}{i}\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-circ}{\circ} g}{p} = \apply{h}{p}$ by Proposition 358.

$\piIndex{Y_1}{i}\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-circ}{\circ} g$ is a function and $\dom{\piIndex{Y_1}{i}\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-circ}{\circ} g} = X_0\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-times}{\times} X_1$ by Lemma 108, Proposition 368, Proposition 384, Proposition 211, Proposition 269, Proposition 212, Definition 245, Definition 188, Proposition 360, Proposition 221, Proposition 371, Proposition 366, Definition 72, Proposition 3.
$h$ is a function and $\dom{h} = X_0\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-times}{\times} X_1$ by Definition 62, Proposition 368, Proposition 371.
Hence $\piIndex{Y_1}{i}\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-circ}{\circ} g = h$ by Proposition 371, Definition 188, Proposition 362, Proposition 3.
Hence $h$ is continuous from $X_0\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-times}{\times} X_1$ to $\apply{Y_1}{i}$ with respect to $T_P$ and $\apply{T_1}{i}$ by Proposition 358, Lemma 260.
Then $h\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\funs{X_0\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-times}{\times} X_1}{\apply{Y_1}{i}}$ and $h$ is a function by Definition 62, Proposition 368.
Thus $\piIndex{Y_1}{i}\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\funs{\productFamily{Y_1}}{\apply{Y_1}{i}}$ and $\piIndex{Y_1}{i}$ is a function by Lemma 108, Proposition 368.
Hence $\piIndex{Y_1}{i}\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-circ}{\circ} g$ is a function by Proposition 384.
Therefore $\piIndex{Y_1}{i}\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-circ}{\circ} g$ is continuous from $X_0\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-times}{\times} X_1$ to $\apply{Y_1}{i}$ with respect to $T_P$ and $\apply{T_1}{i}$ by Lemma 259.

LaTeX source 
\begin{byCase}
            \caseOf{$i = k + 1$.}
                    Let $h = \piTwo{X_0}{X_1}$.
                    $h$ is continuous from $X_0\times X_1$ to $X_1$ with respect to $T_P$ and $T_{1p}$
                        by \cref{projection_two_continuous}.
                    Show that for all $p\in X_0\times X_1$ we have $\apply{\piIndex{Y_1}{i}\circ g}{p} = \apply{h}{p}$.
                    \begin{subproof}
                        Fix $p$ such that $p\in X_0\times X_1$.
                        Thus $g$ is a function to $\productFamily{Y_1}$ and $g$ is a function
                            by \cref{funs_elim,funs_is_function}.
                        Thus $g(p)\in\productFamily{Y_1}$
                            by \cref{funs,function_apply_elim,ran_intro,function_to_ran,subseteq}.
                        Thus $(p,\fst{p}\union\{(k + 1,\snd{p})\})\in g$.
                        Thus $g(p) = \fst{p}\union\{(k + 1,\snd{p})\}$ by \cref{function_apply_intro}.
                        Thus $(i,\snd{p})\in g(p)$ by \cref{union_iff,singleton_iff}.
                        Thus $g(p)\in\funs{\dom{Y_1}}{\unions{\ran{Y_1}}}$
                            and $g(p)$ is a function by \cref{indexed_product,funs_is_function}.
                        Thus $\apply{g(p)}{i} = \snd{p}$ by \cref{function_apply_intro}.
                        Thus $(g(p),\snd{p})\in\piIndex{Y_1}{i}$ by \cref{pair_eq_iff,projection_map_indexed}.
                        Thus $(p,\snd{p})\in\piIndex{Y_1}{i}\circ g$ by \cref{circ_iff}.
                        Thus $\piIndex{Y_1}{i}\in\funs{\productFamily{Y_1}}{\apply{Y_1}{i}}$
                            and $\piIndex{Y_1}{i}$ is a function by \cref{projection_map_indexed_function,funs_is_function}.
                        Thus $\piIndex{Y_1}{i}\circ g$ is a function by \cref{function_circ}.
                        Thus $\apply{\piIndex{Y_1}{i}\circ g}{p} = \snd{p}$ by \cref{function_apply_intro}.
                        Thus $h\in\funs{X_0\times X_1}{X_1}$ and $h$ is a function by \cref{continuous,funs_is_function}.
                        Take $x,y$ such that $p = (x,y)$ by \cref{times_elem_is_tuple}.
                        Thus $(x,y)\in X_0\times X_1$.
                        Thus $x\in X_0$ and $y\in X_1$ by \cref{times_tuple_elim}.
                        Thus $\snd{p} = y$ by \cref{snd_eq}.
                        Thus $(p,\snd{p})\in h$ by \cref{pair_eq_iff,projection_second}.
                        Therefore $\apply{\piIndex{Y_1}{i}\circ g}{p} = \apply{h}{p}$
                            by \cref{function_apply_intro}.
                    \end{subproof}
                    $\piIndex{Y_1}{i}\circ g$ is a function and $\dom{\piIndex{Y_1}{i}\circ g} = X_0\times X_1$
                        by \cref{projection_map_indexed_function,funs_is_function,function_circ,dom_iff,circ_iff,dom_intro,funs,subseteq,function_apply_elim,ran_intro,funs_elim,function_to_ran,projection_map_indexed,subseteq_antisymmetric}.
                    $h$ is a function and $\dom{h} = X_0\times X_1$ by \cref{continuous,funs_is_function,funs_elim}.
                    Hence $\piIndex{Y_1}{i}\circ g = h$ by \cref{funs_elim,subseteq,fun_subseteq,subseteq_antisymmetric}.
                    Thus $\piIndex{Y_1}{i}\circ g$ is continuous from $X_0\times X_1$ to $\apply{Y_1}{i}$ with respect to $T_P$ and $\apply{T_1}{i}$
                        by \cref{continuous_eq_subst}.
                \caseOf{$i \neq k + 1$.}
                    Then $i\in J_1$ by \cref{real_naturals_leq_succ_elim,initial_segment_naturalsplus}.
                    Let $h_0 = \piOne{X_0}{X_1}$.
                    $h_0$ is continuous from $X_0\times X_1$ to $X_0$ with respect to $T_P$ and $T_X$
                        by \cref{projection_one_continuous}.
                    $\piIndex{Y_0}{i}$ is continuous from $X_0$ to $\apply{Y_0}{i}$ with respect to $T_X$ and $\apply{T_0}{i}$
                        by \cref{projection_map_indexed_continuous,subseteq,subseteq_antisymmetric}.
                    Let $h = \piIndex{Y_0}{i}\circ h_0$.
                    $h$ is continuous from $X_0\times X_1$ to $\apply{Y_0}{i}$ with respect to $T_P$ and $\apply{T_0}{i}$
                        by \cref{continuous_composite}.
                    Show that for all $p\in X_0\times X_1$ we have $\apply{\piIndex{Y_1}{i}\circ g}{p} = \apply{h}{p}$.
                    \begin{subproof}
                            Fix $p$ such that $p\in X_0\times X_1$.
                            Thus $(p,\fst{p}\union\{(k + 1,\snd{p})\})\in g$.
                            Thus $g(p) = \fst{p}\union\{(k + 1,\snd{p})\}$ by \cref{function_apply_intro}.
                            Let $x = \fst{p}$.
                            Take $a,b$ such that $p = (a,b)$ and $a\in X_0$ and $b\in X_1$ by \cref{times_elem_is_tuple}.
                            Thus $\fst{p} = a$ by \cref{fst_eq}.
                            Thus $x = a$.
                            Thus $x\in X_0$.
                            Thus $(p,x)\in h_0$ by \cref{pair_eq_iff,projection_first}.
                            Thus $(x,\apply{x}{i})\in\piIndex{Y_0}{i}$ by \cref{projection_map_indexed}.
                            Thus $(p,\apply{x}{i})\in\piIndex{Y_0}{i}\circ h_0$ by \cref{circ_iff}.
                            Thus $h\in\funs{X_0\times X_1}{\apply{Y_0}{i}}$ and $h$ is a function
                                by \cref{continuous,funs_is_function}.
                            Thus $\apply{h}{p} = \apply{x}{i}$ by \cref{function_apply_intro}.
                            Thus $g$ is a function to $\productFamily{Y_1}$ and $g$ is a function
                                by \cref{funs_elim,funs_is_function}.
                            Thus $g(p)\in\productFamily{Y_1}$
                                by \cref{funs,function_apply_elim,ran_intro,function_to_ran,subseteq}.
                            Thus $g(p)\in\funs{\dom{Y_1}}{\unions{\ran{Y_1}}}$
                                and $g(p)$ is a function by \cref{indexed_product,funs_is_function}.
                            Thus $x\in\funs{\dom{Y_0}}{\unions{\ran{Y_0}}}$ and $x$ is a function
                                by \cref{indexed_product,funs_is_function}.
                            Thus $i\in\dom{x}$ by \cref{funs_elim,subseteq}.
                            Thus $(i,\apply{x}{i})\in x$ by \cref{function_apply_elim}.
                            Thus $(i,\apply{x}{i})\in g(p)$ by \cref{union_iff}.
                            Thus $\apply{g(p)}{i} = \apply{x}{i}$ by \cref{function_apply_intro}.
                            Thus $(g(p),\apply{x}{i})\in\piIndex{Y_1}{i}$ by \cref{projection_map_indexed}.
                            Thus $(p,\apply{x}{i})\in\piIndex{Y_1}{i}\circ g$ by \cref{circ_iff}.
                            Thus $\piIndex{Y_1}{i}\in\funs{\productFamily{Y_1}}{\apply{Y_1}{i}}$ and
                                $\piIndex{Y_1}{i}$ is a function by \cref{projection_map_indexed_function,funs_is_function}.
                            Thus $\piIndex{Y_1}{i}\circ g$ is a function by \cref{function_circ}.
                            Therefore $\apply{\piIndex{Y_1}{i}\circ g}{p} = \apply{h}{p}$
                                by \cref{function_apply_intro}.
                    \end{subproof}
                    $\piIndex{Y_1}{i}\circ g$ is a function and $\dom{\piIndex{Y_1}{i}\circ g} = X_0\times X_1$
                        by \cref{projection_map_indexed_function,funs_is_function,function_circ,dom_iff,circ_iff,dom_intro,funs,subseteq,function_apply_elim,ran_intro,funs_elim,function_to_ran,projection_map_indexed,subseteq_antisymmetric}.
                    $h$ is a function and $\dom{h} = X_0\times X_1$ by \cref{continuous,funs_is_function,funs_elim}.
                    Hence $\piIndex{Y_1}{i}\circ g = h$ by \cref{funs_elim,subseteq,fun_subseteq,subseteq_antisymmetric}.
                    Hence $h$ is continuous from $X_0\times X_1$ to $\apply{Y_1}{i}$ with respect to $T_P$ and $\apply{T_1}{i}$
                        by \cref{function_apply_intro,continuous_eq_codomain}.
                    Then $h\in\funs{X_0\times X_1}{\apply{Y_1}{i}}$ and $h$ is a function
                        by \cref{continuous,funs_is_function}.
                    Thus $\piIndex{Y_1}{i}\in\funs{\productFamily{Y_1}}{\apply{Y_1}{i}}$ and
                        $\piIndex{Y_1}{i}$ is a function by \cref{projection_map_indexed_function,funs_is_function}.
                    Hence $\piIndex{Y_1}{i}\circ g$ is a function by \cref{function_circ}.
                    Therefore $\piIndex{Y_1}{i}\circ g$ is continuous from $X_0\times X_1$ to $\apply{Y_1}{i}$ with respect to $T_P$ and $\apply{T_1}{i}$
                        by \cref{continuous_eq_subst}.
            
\end{byCase}

$J$ is inhabited.
$T_P$ is a topology on $X_0\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-times}{\times} X_1$.
Hence $g$ is continuous from $X_0\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-times}{\times} X_1$ to $\productFamily{Y_1}$ with respect to $T_P$ and $\productTopologyIndexed{T_1}{Y_1}$ by Lemma 110.

Subclaim: Show that for all $z\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\productFamily{Y_1}$ there exists $p\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} X_0\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-times}{\times} X_1$ such that $g(p) = z$.

Fix $z$ such that $z\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\productFamily{Y_1}$.
Thus $z$ is a function and $\dom{z} = \dom{Y_1}$ by Definition 69, Proposition 371.
Let $x = \{(i,z(i)) \mid i\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} J_1\}$.
$x$ is a relation by Definition 202.
For all $a,b_1,b_2$ such that $(a,b_1)\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} x$ and $(a,b_2)\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} x$ we have $b_1 = b_2$ by Axiom 7.
Thus $x$ is a function by Definition 241.
Thus $\dom{x} = \dom{Y_0}$ by Proposition 212, Proposition 211, Axiom 7, Definition 188, Proposition 3.
For all $i$ such that $i\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\dom{Y_0}$ we have $x(i)\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\unions{\ran{Y_0}}$ by Definition 188, Proposition 358, Definition 69, Proposition 221, Axiom 5.
Thus $x\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\funs{\dom{Y_0}}{\unions{\ran{Y_0}}}$ and $x$ is a function by Proposition 370, Proposition 368.
For all $i\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\dom{Y_0}$ we have $x(i)\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\apply{Y_0}{i}$ by Definition 188, Proposition 358, Definition 69.
Thus $x\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\productFamily{Y_0}$ by Definition 69.
Hence $x\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} X_0$ by Definition 188.
Let $y = z(k + 1)$.
Then $y\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} X_1$ by Definition 69, Definition 188.
Let $p = (x,y)$.
Hence $p\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} X_0\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-times}{\times} X_1$ by Proposition 115.

Subclaim: Show that for all $i\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\dom{z}$ we have $\apply{g(p)}{i} = z(i)$.

Fix $i$ such that $i\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\dom{z}$.
Thus $i\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} J$ by Definition 188.
Thus $p\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\dom{g}$ by Definition 245.
Hence $g(p)\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\productFamily{Y_1}$ by Proposition 371, Definition 245, Proposition 360, Proposition 221, Proposition 366, Definition 188.
Thus $g(p)\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\funs{\dom{Y_1}}{\unions{\ran{Y_1}}}$ and $g(p)$ is a function by Definition 69, Proposition 368.
Thus $(p,\fst{p}\union\{(k + 1,\snd{p})\})\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} g$.
Thus $g(p) = \fst{p}\union\{(k + 1,\snd{p})\}$ by Proposition 358.
Thus $g(p) = x\union\{(k + 1,y)\}$ by Axiom 11, Axiom 12.

Case. $i = k + 1$.

Thus $(k + 1,y)\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\{(k + 1,y)\}$ by Proposition 20.
Thus $(k + 1,y)\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} g(p)$ by Axiom 6.
Thus $\apply{g(p)}{k + 1} = y$ by Proposition 358.
Thus $\apply{g(p)}{i} = z(i)$.

Case. $i \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-neq}{\neq} k + 1$.

Thus $i\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} J_1$ by Lemma 417, Definition 281.
Thus $(i,z(i))\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} x$.
Thus $(i,z(i))\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} g(p)$ by Axiom 6.
Thus $\apply{g(p)}{i} = z(i)$ by Proposition 358.

LaTeX source 
\begin{byCase}
                        \caseOf{$i = k + 1$.}
                            Thus $(k + 1,y)\in\{(k + 1,y)\}$ by \cref{singleton_intro}.
                            Thus $(k + 1,y)\in g(p)$ by \cref{union_iff}.
                            Thus $\apply{g(p)}{k + 1} = y$ by \cref{function_apply_intro}.
                            Thus $\apply{g(p)}{i} = z(i)$.
                        \caseOf{$i \neq k + 1$.}
                            Thus $i\in J_1$ by \cref{real_naturals_leq_succ_elim,initial_segment_naturalsplus}.
                            Thus $(i,z(i))\in x$.
                            Thus $(i,z(i))\in g(p)$ by \cref{union_iff}.
                            Thus $\apply{g(p)}{i} = z(i)$ by \cref{function_apply_intro}.
                        
\end{byCase}

Thus $g$ is a function to $\productFamily{Y_1}$ by Proposition 371.
Thus $p\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\dom{g}$ by Definition 245.
Hence $g(p)\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\productFamily{Y_1}$ by Proposition 371, Definition 245, Proposition 360, Proposition 221, Proposition 366, Definition 188.
Thus $g(p)$ is a function and $\dom{g(p)} = \dom{Y_1}$ by Definition 69, Proposition 371.
Therefore there exists $q\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} X_0\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-times}{\times} X_1$ such that $g(q) = z$ by Definition 188, Proposition 362, Proposition 3.

Therefore $g\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\surj{X_0\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-times}{\times} X_1}{\productFamily{Y_1}}$ by Definition 247.
Hence $\img{g}{X_0\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-times}{\times} X_1} = \productFamily{Y_1}$ by Definition 247, Proposition 371, Proposition 250, Corollary 15.
Let $A = \img{g}{X_0\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-times}{\times} X_1}$.
Let $T_Q = \productTopologyIndexed{T_1}{Y_1}$.
Let $T_A = \subspaceTopology{T_Q}{A}$.
Then $T_A = \subspaceTopology{T_Q}{\productFamily{Y_1}}$.
$T_Q$ is a topology on $\productFamily{Y_1}$ by Lemma 94, Definition 15, Lemma 1.
Therefore $T_A = T_Q$ by Definition 48, Definition 1, Definition 188, Proposition 156, Proposition 70, Proposition 3.
Hence $\productFamily{Y_1}$ is compact with respect to $T_Q$ by Theorem 56.

Hence $\finiteIndexedProductCompactProp{k + 1}$ by Definition 153.

LaTeX source 
\begin{proof}
    Show that for all $Y_1,T_1$ such that $Y_1$ is a function and $T_1$ is a function and
        $\dom{Y_1} = \initialSegment{k + 1}$ and $\dom{T_1} = \initialSegment{k + 1}$ and
        for all $i\in\dom{Y_1}$ we have $\apply{T_1}{i}$ is a topology on $\apply{Y_1}{i}$ and
            $\apply{Y_1}{i}$ is compact with respect to $\apply{T_1}{i}$,
        we have $\productFamily{Y_1}$ is compact with respect to $\productTopologyIndexed{T_1}{Y_1}$.
    \begin{subproof}
        Fix $Y_1$.
        Fix $T_1$.
        Assume $Y_1$ is a function.
        Assume $T_1$ is a function.
        Assume $\dom{Y_1} = \initialSegment{k + 1}$.
        Assume $\dom{T_1} = \initialSegment{k + 1}$.
        Assume that for all $i\in\dom{Y_1}$ we have $\apply{T_1}{i}$ is a topology on $\apply{Y_1}{i}$ and
            $\apply{Y_1}{i}$ is compact with respect to $\apply{T_1}{i}$.
        Let $J_1 = \initialSegment{k}$.
        Let $J = \initialSegment{k + 1}$.
        For all $i$ such that $i\in J_1$ we have $i\in J$
            by \cref{initial_segment_naturalsplus,real_naturals_subset_reals,subseteq,real_naturals_closed_succ,real_add_ident,real_one_in_naturals,real_zero_lt_one,real_lt_add,real_add_comm,real_lt_subst_left,real_lt_imp_leq,real_leq_trans}.
        Hence $J_1\subseteq J$ by \cref{subseteq}.
        $k\in\reals$ by \cref{real_naturals_subset_reals,subseteq}.
        $k + 1\in\naturalsPlus$ and $k + 1\in\reals$
            by \cref{real_naturals_closed_succ,real_naturals_subset_reals,subseteq}.
        Thus $k < k + 1$
            by \cref{real_zero_lt_one,real_add_ident,real_naturals_subset_reals,real_one_in_naturals,subseteq,real_lt_add,real_add_comm,real_lt_subst_left}.
        Show that $k \neq k + 1$.
        \begin{subproof}
            Suppose not.
            Thus $k = k + 1$.
            Thus $k < k$ by \cref{real_lt_subst_left}.
            Contradiction by \cref{real_lt_irreflexive}.
        \end{subproof}
        Let $Y_0 = \{(i,\apply{Y_1}{i}) \mid i\in J_1\}$.
        Let $T_0 = \{(i,\apply{T_1}{i}) \mid i\in J_1\}$.
        $\dom{Y_0} = J_1$ by \cref{dom_intro,dom_iff,pair_eq_iff,subseteq,subseteq_antisymmetric}.
        Then $\dom{T_0} = J_1$ by \cref{dom_intro,dom_iff,pair_eq_iff,subseteq,subseteq_antisymmetric}.
        $Y_0$ is a relation by \cref{relation}.
        For all $a,b_1,b_2$ such that $(a,b_1)\in Y_0$ and $(a,b_2)\in Y_0$ we have $b_1 = b_2$
            by \cref{pair_eq_iff}.
        Then $Y_0$ is a function by \cref{rightunique}.
        $T_0$ is a relation by \cref{relation}.
        For all $a,b_1,b_2$ such that $(a,b_1)\in T_0$ and $(a,b_2)\in T_0$ we have $b_1 = b_2$
            by \cref{pair_eq_iff}.
        Then $T_0$ is a function by \cref{rightunique}.
        For all $i$ such that $i\in J_1$ we have $\apply{T_0}{i}$ is a topology on $\apply{Y_0}{i}$
            by \cref{subseteq,function_apply_intro}.
        For all $i$ such that $i\in J_1$ we have $\apply{Y_0}{i}$ is compact with respect to $\apply{T_0}{i}$
            by \cref{subseteq,function_apply_intro}.
        Therefore $\productFamily{Y_0}$ is compact with respect to $\productTopologyIndexed{T_0}{Y_0}$
            by \cref{finite_indexed_product_compact_property}.
        Let $X_0 = \productFamily{Y_0}$.
        Let $T_X = \productTopologyIndexed{T_0}{Y_0}$.
        Let $X_1 = \apply{Y_1}{k + 1}$.
        Let $T_{1p} = \apply{T_1}{k + 1}$.
        Then $k + 1\in J$ by \cref{real_naturals_closed_succ,initial_segment_naturalsplus}.
        Show that $T_{1p}$ is a topology on $X_1$ and $X_1$ is compact with respect to $T_{1p}$.
        \begin{subproof}
            Follows by \cref{subseteq}.
        \end{subproof}
        Now $J_1$ is inhabited by \cref{real_one_leq_naturalsplus,real_one_in_naturals,initial_segment_naturalsplus}.
        Hence $T_X$ is a topology on $X_0$
            by \cref{product_subbasis_finite_intersections_basis,basis_for_topology,basis_topology_is_topology}.
        Let $T_P = \productTopology{T_X}{T_{1p}}{X_0}{X_1}$.
        Therefore $X_0\times X_1$ is compact with respect to $T_P$ by \cref{product_compact}.
        Let $g = \{(p,\fst{p}\union\{(k + 1,\snd{p})\}) \mid p\in X_0\times X_1\}$.
        $g$ is a relation by \cref{relation}.
        For all $p,u,v$ such that $(p,u)\in g$ and $(p,v)\in g$ we have $u = v$ by \cref{pair_eq_iff}.
        Hence $g$ is a function by \cref{rightunique}.
        Thus $\dom{g} = X_0\times X_1$ by \cref{dom_intro,dom_iff,pair_eq_iff,subseteq,subseteq_antisymmetric}.
            Show that for all $p\in\dom{g}$ we have $g(p)\in\productFamily{Y_1}$.
            \begin{subproof}
                Fix $p$ such that $p\in\dom{g}$.
                Take $v$ such that $(p,v)\in g$ by \cref{dom_iff}.
                Take $p_0\in X_0\times X_1$ such that $(p,v) = (p_0,\fst{p_0}\union\{(k + 1,\snd{p_0})\})$.
                Thus $p = p_0$ and $v = \fst{p}\union\{(k + 1,\snd{p})\}$ by \cref{pair_eq_iff}.
                Let $x = \fst{p}$.
                    Let $y = \snd{p}$.
                    Then $x\in X_0$ and $y\in X_1$ by \cref{subseteq,times_proj_elim}.
                    Hence $x\in\funs{\dom{Y_0}}{\unions{\ran{Y_0}}}$ and $x$ is a function and $\dom{x} = \dom{Y_0}$
                        by \cref{subseteq,indexed_product,funs_elim}.
                    Then $x\in\rels{\dom{Y_0}}{\unions{\ran{Y_0}}}$ and $x$ is a relation
                        by \cref{funs_subseteq_rels,subseteq,rels_is_relation}.
                    Show that $v\in\funs{\dom{Y_1}}{\unions{\ran{Y_1}}}$.
                    \begin{subproof}
                            For all $w$ such that $w\in v$ there exist $q_1,q_2$ such that $w = (q_1,q_2)$
                                by \cref{union_iff,singleton_iff,relation}.
                            Hence $v$ is a relation by \cref{relation}.
                            Show that for all $a,b_1,b_2$ such that $(a,b_1)\in v$ and $(a,b_2)\in v$ we have $b_1 = b_2$.
                            \begin{subproof}
                                Fix $a$.
                                Fix $b_1$.
                                Fix $b_2$ such that $(a,b_1)\in v$ and $(a,b_2)\in v$.
                                \begin{byCase}
                                \caseOf{$a = k + 1$.}
                                    Thus $(a,b_1)\in x$ or $(a,b_1) = (k + 1,y)$ by \cref{union_iff,singleton_iff}.
                                    Show that $(a,b_1)\notin x$.
                                    \begin{subproof}
                                        Suppose not.
                                        Thus $(a,b_1)\in x$.
                                        Thus $a\in\dom{x}$ by \cref{dom_intro}.
                                        Thus $\dom{x} = \dom{Y_0}$ by \cref{funs_elim}.
                                        Thus $a\in J_1$ by \cref{subseteq}.
                                        Thus $a\in\naturalsPlus$ and $a \leq k$ by \cref{initial_segment_naturalsplus}.
                                        Then $k + 1 \leq k$.
                                        Hence $k + 1 < k$ or $k + 1 = k$ by \cref{real_leq_split}.
                                        Contradiction by \cref{real_lt_asym,real_lt_subst_left,real_lt_irreflexive}.
                                    \end{subproof}
                                    Hence $(a,b_1) = (k + 1,y)$.
                                    Then $(a,b_2)\in x$ or $(a,b_2) = (k + 1,y)$ by \cref{union_iff,singleton_iff}.
                                    Show that $(a,b_2)\notin x$.
                                    \begin{subproof}
                                        Suppose not.
                                        Thus $(a,b_2)\in x$.
                                        Thus $a\in\dom{x}$ by \cref{dom_intro}.
                                        Thus $\dom{x} = \dom{Y_0}$ by \cref{funs_elim}.
                                        Thus $a\in J_1$ by \cref{subseteq}.
                                        Thus $a\in\naturalsPlus$ and $a \leq k$ by \cref{initial_segment_naturalsplus}.
                                        Then $k + 1 \leq k$.
                                        Hence $k + 1 < k$ or $k + 1 = k$ by \cref{real_leq_split}.
                                        Contradiction by \cref{real_lt_asym,real_lt_subst_left,real_lt_irreflexive}.
                                    \end{subproof}
                                    Hence $(a,b_2) = (k + 1,y)$.
                                    Then $b_1 = y$ and $b_2 = y$ by \cref{pair_eq_iff}.
                                    Hence $b_1 = b_2$.
                                \caseOf{$a \neq k + 1$.}
                                    Then $(a,b_1)\in x$ by \cref{union_iff,singleton_iff,pair_eq_iff}.
                                    Then $(a,b_2)\in x$ by \cref{union_iff,singleton_iff,pair_eq_iff}.
                                    Thus $b_1 = b_2$ by \cref{rightunique,relation}.
                                \end{byCase}
                            \end{subproof}
                            Then $v$ is a function by \cref{rightunique}.
                            For all $i$ such that $i\in\dom{Y_1}$ we have $i\in\dom{v}$
                                by \cref{subseteq,union_iff,singleton_iff,dom_intro,real_naturals_leq_succ_elim,initial_segment_naturalsplus,indexed_product,funs_elim,dom_iff}.
                            Hence $\dom{Y_1}\subseteq\dom{v}$ by \cref{subseteq}.
                            Show that for all $i$ such that $i\in\dom{v}$ we have $i\in\dom{Y_1}$.
                            \begin{subproof}
                                Fix $i$ such that $i\in\dom{v}$.
                                Take $b$ such that $(i,b)\in v$ by \cref{dom_iff}.
                                Thus $(i,b)\in x$ or $(i,b) = (k + 1,y)$ by \cref{union_iff,singleton_iff}.
                                \begin{byCase}
                                \caseOf{$(i,b) = (k + 1,y)$.}
                                    Thus $i = k + 1$ by \cref{pair_eq_iff}.
                                    Thus $i\in J$.
                                    Thus $i\in\dom{Y_1}$ by \cref{subseteq}.
                                \caseOf{$(i,b)\in x$.}
                                    Thus $i\in\dom{x}$ by \cref{dom_intro}.
                                    Thus $i\in J_1$ by \cref{indexed_product,funs_elim}.
                                    Thus $i\in J$ by \cref{subseteq,initial_segment_naturalsplus}.
                                    Thus $i\in\dom{Y_1}$ by \cref{subseteq}.
                                \end{byCase}
                            \end{subproof}
                            Therefore $\dom{v}\subseteq\dom{Y_1}$ by \cref{subseteq}.
                            Hence $\dom{v} = \dom{Y_1}$ by \cref{subseteq_antisymmetric}.
                        Show that for all $i\in\dom{Y_1}$ we have $v(i)\in\unions{\ran{Y_1}}$.
                        \begin{subproof}
                            Fix $i$ such that $i\in\dom{Y_1}$.
                            Thus $i\in J$ by \cref{subseteq}.
                            \begin{byCase}
                            \caseOf{$i = k + 1$.}
                                Then $(k + 1,y)\in v$ by \cref{union_iff,singleton_iff}.
                                Hence $v(k + 1) = y$ by \cref{function_apply_intro}.
                                Thus $(k + 1,X_1)\in Y_1$ by \cref{function_apply_elim,subseteq}.
                                Thus $X_1\in\ran{Y_1}$ by \cref{ran_intro}.
                                Thus $y\in\unions{\ran{Y_1}}$ by \cref{unions_iff}.
                                Therefore $v(i)\in\unions{\ran{Y_1}}$.
                            \caseOf{$i \neq k + 1$.}
                                Thus $i\in J_1$ by \cref{real_naturals_leq_succ_elim,initial_segment_naturalsplus}.
                                Thus $i\in\dom{x}$ by \cref{subseteq,indexed_product,funs_elim}.
                                Thus $x(i)\in\apply{Y_0}{i}$ by \cref{indexed_product}.
                                Thus $(i,\apply{Y_1}{i})\in Y_0$.
                                Then $x(i)\in\apply{Y_1}{i}$ by \cref{function_apply_intro}.
                                Thus $(i,\apply{Y_1}{i})\in Y_1$ by \cref{function_apply_elim}.
                                Thus $\apply{Y_1}{i}\in\ran{Y_1}$ by \cref{ran_intro}.
                                Thus $x(i)\in\unions{\ran{Y_1}}$ by \cref{unions_iff}.
                                Thus $(i,x(i))\in v$ by \cref{function_apply_elim,union_iff}.
                                Thus $v(i) = x(i)$ by \cref{function_apply_intro}.
                                Hence $v(i)\in\unions{\ran{Y_1}}$.
                            \end{byCase}
                        \end{subproof}
                        Therefore $v\in\funs{\dom{Y_1}}{\unions{\ran{Y_1}}}$ by \cref{funs_intro}.
                    \end{subproof}
                    Then $v$ is right-unique and $v$ is a relation by \cref{funs,rels_is_relation}.
                    Show that for all $i\in\dom{Y_1}$ we have $v(i)\in\apply{Y_1}{i}$.
                    \begin{subproof}
                        Fix $i$ such that $i\in\dom{Y_1}$.
                        Thus $i\in J$ by \cref{subseteq}.
                        \begin{byCase}
                        \caseOf{$i = k + 1$.}
                            Thus $(k + 1,y)\in v$ by \cref{union_iff,singleton_iff}.
                            Thus $v(k + 1) = y$ by \cref{function_apply_intro}.
                            Thus $y\in X_1$ by \cref{subseteq}.
                            Hence $v(i)\in\apply{Y_1}{i}$.
