                        \caseOf{$i \neq k + 1$.}
                            Thus $i\in J_1$ by \cref{real_naturals_leq_succ_elim,initial_segment_naturalsplus}.
                            Thus $x(i)\in\apply{Y_0}{i}$ by \cref{indexed_product}.
                            Then $x(i)\in\apply{Y_1}{i}$ by \cref{function_apply_intro}.
                            Thus $(i,x(i))\in v$ by \cref{function_apply_elim,union_iff}.
                            Thus $v(i) = x(i)$ by \cref{function_apply_intro}.
                            Then $v(i)\in\apply{Y_1}{i}$.
                        \end{byCase}
                    \end{subproof}
                    Therefore $v\in\productFamily{Y_1}$ by \cref{indexed_product}.
                Hence $g(p)\in\productFamily{Y_1}$ by \cref{function_apply_intro}.
            \end{subproof}
            Therefore $g\in\funs{X_0\times X_1}{\productFamily{Y_1}}$ by \cref{funs_intro}.
        Then $g$ is right-unique and $g$ is a relation by \cref{funs,rels_is_relation}.
        Show that for all $i\in J$ we have $\piIndex{Y_1}{i}\circ g$ is continuous from $X_0\times X_1$
            to $\apply{Y_1}{i}$ with respect to $T_P$ and $\apply{T_1}{i}$.
        \begin{subproof}
            Fix $i$ such that $i\in J$.
            \begin{byCase}
            \caseOf{$i = k + 1$.}
                    Let $h = \piTwo{X_0}{X_1}$.
                    $h$ is continuous from $X_0\times X_1$ to $X_1$ with respect to $T_P$ and $T_{1p}$
                        by \cref{projection_two_continuous}.
                    Show that for all $p\in X_0\times X_1$ we have $\apply{\piIndex{Y_1}{i}\circ g}{p} = \apply{h}{p}$.
                    \begin{subproof}
                        Fix $p$ such that $p\in X_0\times X_1$.
                        Thus $g$ is a function to $\productFamily{Y_1}$ and $g$ is a function
                            by \cref{funs_elim,funs_is_function}.
                        Thus $g(p)\in\productFamily{Y_1}$
                            by \cref{funs,function_apply_elim,ran_intro,function_to_ran,subseteq}.
                        Thus $(p,\fst{p}\union\{(k + 1,\snd{p})\})\in g$.
                        Thus $g(p) = \fst{p}\union\{(k + 1,\snd{p})\}$ by \cref{function_apply_intro}.
                        Thus $(i,\snd{p})\in g(p)$ by \cref{union_iff,singleton_iff}.
                        Thus $g(p)\in\funs{\dom{Y_1}}{\unions{\ran{Y_1}}}$
                            and $g(p)$ is a function by \cref{indexed_product,funs_is_function}.
                        Thus $\apply{g(p)}{i} = \snd{p}$ by \cref{function_apply_intro}.
                        Thus $(g(p),\snd{p})\in\piIndex{Y_1}{i}$ by \cref{pair_eq_iff,projection_map_indexed}.
                        Thus $(p,\snd{p})\in\piIndex{Y_1}{i}\circ g$ by \cref{circ_iff}.
                        Thus $\piIndex{Y_1}{i}\in\funs{\productFamily{Y_1}}{\apply{Y_1}{i}}$
                            and $\piIndex{Y_1}{i}$ is a function by \cref{projection_map_indexed_function,funs_is_function}.
                        Thus $\piIndex{Y_1}{i}\circ g$ is a function by \cref{function_circ}.
                        Thus $\apply{\piIndex{Y_1}{i}\circ g}{p} = \snd{p}$ by \cref{function_apply_intro}.
                        Thus $h\in\funs{X_0\times X_1}{X_1}$ and $h$ is a function by \cref{continuous,funs_is_function}.
                        Take $x,y$ such that $p = (x,y)$ by \cref{times_elem_is_tuple}.
                        Thus $(x,y)\in X_0\times X_1$.
                        Thus $x\in X_0$ and $y\in X_1$ by \cref{times_tuple_elim}.
                        Thus $\snd{p} = y$ by \cref{snd_eq}.
                        Thus $(p,\snd{p})\in h$ by \cref{pair_eq_iff,projection_second}.
                        Therefore $\apply{\piIndex{Y_1}{i}\circ g}{p} = \apply{h}{p}$
                            by \cref{function_apply_intro}.
                    \end{subproof}
                    $\piIndex{Y_1}{i}\circ g$ is a function and $\dom{\piIndex{Y_1}{i}\circ g} = X_0\times X_1$
                        by \cref{projection_map_indexed_function,funs_is_function,function_circ,dom_iff,circ_iff,dom_intro,funs,subseteq,function_apply_elim,ran_intro,funs_elim,function_to_ran,projection_map_indexed,subseteq_antisymmetric}.
                    $h$ is a function and $\dom{h} = X_0\times X_1$ by \cref{continuous,funs_is_function,funs_elim}.
                    Hence $\piIndex{Y_1}{i}\circ g = h$ by \cref{funs_elim,subseteq,fun_subseteq,subseteq_antisymmetric}.
                    Thus $\piIndex{Y_1}{i}\circ g$ is continuous from $X_0\times X_1$ to $\apply{Y_1}{i}$ with respect to $T_P$ and $\apply{T_1}{i}$
                        by \cref{continuous_eq_subst}.
                \caseOf{$i \neq k + 1$.}
                    Then $i\in J_1$ by \cref{real_naturals_leq_succ_elim,initial_segment_naturalsplus}.
                    Let $h_0 = \piOne{X_0}{X_1}$.
                    $h_0$ is continuous from $X_0\times X_1$ to $X_0$ with respect to $T_P$ and $T_X$
                        by \cref{projection_one_continuous}.
                    $\piIndex{Y_0}{i}$ is continuous from $X_0$ to $\apply{Y_0}{i}$ with respect to $T_X$ and $\apply{T_0}{i}$
                        by \cref{projection_map_indexed_continuous,subseteq,subseteq_antisymmetric}.
                    Let $h = \piIndex{Y_0}{i}\circ h_0$.
                    $h$ is continuous from $X_0\times X_1$ to $\apply{Y_0}{i}$ with respect to $T_P$ and $\apply{T_0}{i}$
                        by \cref{continuous_composite}.
                    Show that for all $p\in X_0\times X_1$ we have $\apply{\piIndex{Y_1}{i}\circ g}{p} = \apply{h}{p}$.
                    \begin{subproof}
                            Fix $p$ such that $p\in X_0\times X_1$.
                            Thus $(p,\fst{p}\union\{(k + 1,\snd{p})\})\in g$.
                            Thus $g(p) = \fst{p}\union\{(k + 1,\snd{p})\}$ by \cref{function_apply_intro}.
                            Let $x = \fst{p}$.
                            Take $a,b$ such that $p = (a,b)$ and $a\in X_0$ and $b\in X_1$ by \cref{times_elem_is_tuple}.
                            Thus $\fst{p} = a$ by \cref{fst_eq}.
                            Thus $x = a$.
                            Thus $x\in X_0$.
                            Thus $(p,x)\in h_0$ by \cref{pair_eq_iff,projection_first}.
                            Thus $(x,\apply{x}{i})\in\piIndex{Y_0}{i}$ by \cref{projection_map_indexed}.
                            Thus $(p,\apply{x}{i})\in\piIndex{Y_0}{i}\circ h_0$ by \cref{circ_iff}.
                            Thus $h\in\funs{X_0\times X_1}{\apply{Y_0}{i}}$ and $h$ is a function
                                by \cref{continuous,funs_is_function}.
                            Thus $\apply{h}{p} = \apply{x}{i}$ by \cref{function_apply_intro}.
                            Thus $g$ is a function to $\productFamily{Y_1}$ and $g$ is a function
                                by \cref{funs_elim,funs_is_function}.
                            Thus $g(p)\in\productFamily{Y_1}$
                                by \cref{funs,function_apply_elim,ran_intro,function_to_ran,subseteq}.
                            Thus $g(p)\in\funs{\dom{Y_1}}{\unions{\ran{Y_1}}}$
                                and $g(p)$ is a function by \cref{indexed_product,funs_is_function}.
                            Thus $x\in\funs{\dom{Y_0}}{\unions{\ran{Y_0}}}$ and $x$ is a function
                                by \cref{indexed_product,funs_is_function}.
                            Thus $i\in\dom{x}$ by \cref{funs_elim,subseteq}.
                            Thus $(i,\apply{x}{i})\in x$ by \cref{function_apply_elim}.
                            Thus $(i,\apply{x}{i})\in g(p)$ by \cref{union_iff}.
                            Thus $\apply{g(p)}{i} = \apply{x}{i}$ by \cref{function_apply_intro}.
                            Thus $(g(p),\apply{x}{i})\in\piIndex{Y_1}{i}$ by \cref{projection_map_indexed}.
                            Thus $(p,\apply{x}{i})\in\piIndex{Y_1}{i}\circ g$ by \cref{circ_iff}.
                            Thus $\piIndex{Y_1}{i}\in\funs{\productFamily{Y_1}}{\apply{Y_1}{i}}$ and
                                $\piIndex{Y_1}{i}$ is a function by \cref{projection_map_indexed_function,funs_is_function}.
                            Thus $\piIndex{Y_1}{i}\circ g$ is a function by \cref{function_circ}.
                            Therefore $\apply{\piIndex{Y_1}{i}\circ g}{p} = \apply{h}{p}$
                                by \cref{function_apply_intro}.
                    \end{subproof}
                    $\piIndex{Y_1}{i}\circ g$ is a function and $\dom{\piIndex{Y_1}{i}\circ g} = X_0\times X_1$
                        by \cref{projection_map_indexed_function,funs_is_function,function_circ,dom_iff,circ_iff,dom_intro,funs,subseteq,function_apply_elim,ran_intro,funs_elim,function_to_ran,projection_map_indexed,subseteq_antisymmetric}.
                    $h$ is a function and $\dom{h} = X_0\times X_1$ by \cref{continuous,funs_is_function,funs_elim}.
                    Hence $\piIndex{Y_1}{i}\circ g = h$ by \cref{funs_elim,subseteq,fun_subseteq,subseteq_antisymmetric}.
                    Hence $h$ is continuous from $X_0\times X_1$ to $\apply{Y_1}{i}$ with respect to $T_P$ and $\apply{T_1}{i}$
                        by \cref{function_apply_intro,continuous_eq_codomain}.
                    Then $h\in\funs{X_0\times X_1}{\apply{Y_1}{i}}$ and $h$ is a function
                        by \cref{continuous,funs_is_function}.
                    Thus $\piIndex{Y_1}{i}\in\funs{\productFamily{Y_1}}{\apply{Y_1}{i}}$ and
                        $\piIndex{Y_1}{i}$ is a function by \cref{projection_map_indexed_function,funs_is_function}.
                    Hence $\piIndex{Y_1}{i}\circ g$ is a function by \cref{function_circ}.
                    Therefore $\piIndex{Y_1}{i}\circ g$ is continuous from $X_0\times X_1$ to $\apply{Y_1}{i}$ with respect to $T_P$ and $\apply{T_1}{i}$
                        by \cref{continuous_eq_subst}.
            \end{byCase}
        \end{subproof}
        $J$ is inhabited.
        $T_P$ is a topology on $X_0\times X_1$.
        Hence $g$ is continuous from $X_0\times X_1$ to $\productFamily{Y_1}$ with respect to $T_P$ and $\productTopologyIndexed{T_1}{Y_1}$
            by \cref{continuous_map_into_indexed_product}.
        Show that for all $z\in\productFamily{Y_1}$ there exists $p\in X_0\times X_1$ such that $g(p) = z$.
        \begin{subproof}
                Fix $z$ such that $z\in\productFamily{Y_1}$.
                Thus $z$ is a function and $\dom{z} = \dom{Y_1}$ by \cref{indexed_product,funs_elim}.
                Let $x = \{(i,z(i)) \mid i\in J_1\}$.
                $x$ is a relation by \cref{relation}.
                        For all $a,b_1,b_2$ such that $(a,b_1)\in x$ and $(a,b_2)\in x$ we have $b_1 = b_2$
                            by \cref{pair_eq_iff}.
                        Thus $x$ is a function by \cref{rightunique}.
                        Thus $\dom{x} = \dom{Y_0}$ by \cref{dom_intro,dom_iff,pair_eq_iff,subseteq,subseteq_antisymmetric}.
                        For all $i$ such that $i\in\dom{Y_0}$ we have $x(i)\in\unions{\ran{Y_0}}$
                            by \cref{subseteq,function_apply_intro,indexed_product,ran_intro,unions_iff}.
                        Thus $x\in\funs{\dom{Y_0}}{\unions{\ran{Y_0}}}$ and $x$ is a function
                            by \cref{funs_intro,funs_is_function}.
                        For all $i\in\dom{Y_0}$ we have $x(i)\in\apply{Y_0}{i}$
                            by \cref{subseteq,function_apply_intro,indexed_product}.
                Thus $x\in\productFamily{Y_0}$ by \cref{indexed_product}.
                Hence $x\in X_0$ by \cref{subseteq}.
                Let $y = z(k + 1)$.
                Then $y\in X_1$ by \cref{indexed_product,subseteq}.
                Let $p = (x,y)$.
                Hence $p\in X_0\times X_1$ by \cref{times_tuple_intro}.
                Show that for all $i\in\dom{z}$ we have $\apply{g(p)}{i} = z(i)$.
                    \begin{subproof}
                        Fix $i$ such that $i\in\dom{z}$.
                        Thus $i\in J$ by \cref{subseteq}.
                        Thus $p\in\dom{g}$ by \cref{funs}.
                        Hence $g(p)\in\productFamily{Y_1}$
                            by \cref{funs_elim,funs,function_apply_elim,ran_intro,function_to_ran,subseteq}.
                        Thus $g(p)\in\funs{\dom{Y_1}}{\unions{\ran{Y_1}}}$
                            and $g(p)$ is a function by \cref{indexed_product,funs_is_function}.
                        Thus $(p,\fst{p}\union\{(k + 1,\snd{p})\})\in g$.
                        Thus $g(p) = \fst{p}\union\{(k + 1,\snd{p})\}$ by \cref{function_apply_intro}.
                        Thus $g(p) = x\union\{(k + 1,y)\}$ by \cref{fst_eq,snd_eq}.
                        \begin{byCase}
                        \caseOf{$i = k + 1$.}
                            Thus $(k + 1,y)\in\{(k + 1,y)\}$ by \cref{singleton_intro}.
                            Thus $(k + 1,y)\in g(p)$ by \cref{union_iff}.
                            Thus $\apply{g(p)}{k + 1} = y$ by \cref{function_apply_intro}.
                            Thus $\apply{g(p)}{i} = z(i)$.
                        \caseOf{$i \neq k + 1$.}
                            Thus $i\in J_1$ by \cref{real_naturals_leq_succ_elim,initial_segment_naturalsplus}.
                            Thus $(i,z(i))\in x$.
                            Thus $(i,z(i))\in g(p)$ by \cref{union_iff}.
                            Thus $\apply{g(p)}{i} = z(i)$ by \cref{function_apply_intro}.
                        \end{byCase}
                    \end{subproof}
                Thus $g$ is a function to $\productFamily{Y_1}$ by \cref{funs_elim}.
                Thus $p\in\dom{g}$ by \cref{funs}.
                Hence $g(p)\in\productFamily{Y_1}$
                    by \cref{funs_elim,funs,function_apply_elim,ran_intro,function_to_ran,subseteq}.
                Thus $g(p)$ is a function and $\dom{g(p)} = \dom{Y_1}$ by \cref{indexed_product,funs_elim}.
                Therefore there exists $q\in X_0\times X_1$ such that $g(q) = z$
                    by \cref{subseteq,fun_subseteq,subseteq_antisymmetric}.
            \end{subproof}
        Therefore $g\in\surj{X_0\times X_1}{\productFamily{Y_1}}$ by \cref{surj}.
        Hence $\img{g}{X_0\times X_1} = \productFamily{Y_1}$ by \cref{surj,funs_elim,img_dom,funs_surj_iff}.
        Let $A = \img{g}{X_0\times X_1}$.
        Let $T_Q = \productTopologyIndexed{T_1}{Y_1}$.
        Let $T_A = \subspaceTopology{T_Q}{A}$.
        Then $T_A = \subspaceTopology{T_Q}{\productFamily{Y_1}}$.
        $T_Q$ is a topology on $\productFamily{Y_1}$
            by \cref{product_subbasis_finite_intersections_basis,basis_for_topology,basis_topology_is_topology}.
        Therefore $T_A = T_Q$
            by \cref{subspace_topology,topology_on,subseteq,powerset_elim,inter_absorb_supseteq_left,subseteq_antisymmetric}.
        Hence $\productFamily{Y_1}$ is compact with respect to $T_Q$ by \cref{continuous_image_compact}.
    \end{subproof}
    Hence $\finiteIndexedProductCompactProp{k + 1}$ by \cref{finite_indexed_product_compact_property}.
\end{proof}
Lemma 262
compact_indexed_product_initial_segment

Suppose $n\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\naturalsPlus$.
Let $J = \initialSegment{n}$.
Assume $Y$ is a function.
Assume $T_Y$ is a function.
Assume $\dom{Y} = J$.
Assume $\dom{T_Y} = J$.
Assume that for all $i\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} J$ we have $\apply{T_Y}{i}$ is a topology on $\apply{Y}{i}$.
Assume that for all $i\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} J$ we have $\apply{Y}{i}$ is compact with respect to $\apply{T_Y}{i}$.
Then $\productFamily{Y}$ is compact with respect to $\productTopologyIndexed{T_Y}{Y}$.

LaTeX source 
\begin{lemma}
\label{compact_indexed_product_initial_segment}
    Suppose $n\in\naturalsPlus$.
    Let $J = \initialSegment{n}$.
    Assume $Y$ is a function.
    Assume $T_Y$ is a function.
    Assume $\dom{Y} = J$.
    Assume $\dom{T_Y} = J$.
    Assume that for all $i\in J$ we have $\apply{T_Y}{i}$ is a topology on $\apply{Y}{i}$.
    Assume that for all $i\in J$ we have $\apply{Y}{i}$ is compact with respect to $\apply{T_Y}{i}$.
    Then $\productFamily{Y}$ is compact with respect to $\productTopologyIndexed{T_Y}{Y}$.
\end{lemma}
Proof

Let $A = \{m\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\naturalsPlus \mid \finiteIndexedProductCompactProp{m}\}$.
$A\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-subseteq}{\subseteq}\naturalsPlus$ by Definition 188.

Subclaim: Show that $1\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} A$.

It suffices to show that $1\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\naturalsPlus$ and $\finiteIndexedProductCompactProp{1}$.

Subclaim: Show that for all $Y_1,T_1$ such that $Y_1$ is a function and $T_1$ is a function and $\dom{Y_1} = \initialSegment{1}$ and $\dom{T_1} = \initialSegment{1}$ and for all $i\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\dom{Y_1}$ we have $\apply{T_1}{i}$ is a topology on $\apply{Y_1}{i}$ and $\apply{Y_1}{i}$ is compact with respect to $\apply{T_1}{i}$, we have $\productFamily{Y_1}$ is compact with respect to $\productTopologyIndexed{T_1}{Y_1}$.

Fix $Y_1$.
Fix $T_1$.
Assume $Y_1$ is a function.
Assume $T_1$ is a function.
Assume $\dom{Y_1} = \initialSegment{1}$.
Assume $\dom{T_1} = \initialSegment{1}$.
Assume that for all $i\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\dom{Y_1}$ we have $\apply{T_1}{i}$ is a topology on $\apply{Y_1}{i}$ and $\apply{Y_1}{i}$ is compact with respect to $\apply{T_1}{i}$.
Let $J_1 = \initialSegment{1}$.
Then $1\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} J_1$ by Axiom 32, Definition 281.
For all $i\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} J_1$ we have $i < 1$ or $i = 1$ by Definition 281.
For all $i\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} J_1$ we have $1\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-leq}{\leq} i$ by Definition 281, Axiom 33.
Thus for all $i\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} J_1$ we have $i = 1$.
Therefore $J_1 = \{1,1\}$ by Proposition 3, Definition 188, Proposition 19.
Let $X = \apply{Y_1}{1}$.
Let $T_X = \apply{T_1}{1}$.

Subclaim: Show that $T_X$ is a topology on $X$ and $X$ is compact with respect to $T_X$.

Follows by Definition 188.

Let $g = \{(x,\{(1,x)\}) \mid x\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} X\}$.
$g$ is a relation by Definition 202.
For all $x,u,v$ such that $(x,u)\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} g$ and $(x,v)\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} g$ we have $u = v$ by Axiom 7.
Hence $g$ is a function by Definition 241.
Hence $\dom{g} = X$ by Proposition 212, Proposition 211, Axiom 7, Definition 188, Proposition 3.

Subclaim: Show that for all $x\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\dom{g}$ we have $g(x)\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\productFamily{Y_1}$.

Fix $x$ such that $x\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\dom{g}$.
Take $y$ such that $(x,y)\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} g$ by Proposition 211.
Take $x_0\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} X$ such that $(x,y) = (x_0,\{(1,x_0)\})$.
Thus $y = \{(1,x_0)\}$ by Axiom 7.
Thus $y$ is a relation by Proposition 22, Definition 202.
For all $a,b_1,b_2$ such that $(a,b_1)\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} y$ and $(a,b_2)\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} y$ we have $b_1 = b_2$ by Proposition 22, Axiom 7.
Then $y$ is a function by Definition 241.
Therefore $\dom{y} = \dom{Y_1}$ by Definition 188, Proposition 22, Proposition 212, Proposition 211, Axiom 7, Proposition 3.
For all $i$ such that $i\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\dom{Y_1}$ we have $y(i)\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\unions{\ran{Y_1}}$ by Definition 188, Proposition 22, Proposition 358, Proposition 360, Proposition 221, Axiom 5.
Hence $y\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\funs{\dom{Y_1}}{\unions{\ran{Y_1}}}$ by Proposition 370.
For all $i$ such that $i\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\dom{Y_1}$ we have $y(i)\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\apply{Y_1}{i}$ by Definition 188, Proposition 22, Proposition 371, Proposition 358.
Therefore $y\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\productFamily{Y_1}$ by Definition 69.
Hence $g(x)\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\productFamily{Y_1}$ by Proposition 358.

Therefore $g\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\funs{X}{\productFamily{Y_1}}$ by Proposition 370.
Let $T_P = \productTopologyIndexed{T_1}{Y_1}$.
$J_1$ is inhabited.

Subclaim: Show that for all $i\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} J_1$ we have $\piIndex{Y_1}{i}\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-circ}{\circ} g$ is continuous from $X$ to $\apply{Y_1}{i}$ with respect to $T_X$ and $\apply{T_1}{i}$.

Fix $i$ such that $i\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} J_1$.
Then $i = 1$.
Let $j = \identity{X}$.
$j$ is continuous from $X$ to $X$ with respect to $T_X$ and $T_X$ by Lemma 204.

Subclaim: Show that for all $x\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} X$ we have $\apply{\piIndex{Y_1}{i}\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-circ}{\circ} g}{x} = \apply{j}{x}$.

Fix $x$ such that $x\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} X$.
Thus $g$ is a function to $\productFamily{Y_1}$ and $g$ is a function and $\dom{g} = X$ by Proposition 371, Proposition 368.
Thus $g(x)\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\productFamily{Y_1}$ by Definition 188, Definition 245, Proposition 360, Proposition 221, Proposition 366.
Thus $(x,\{(1,x)\})\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} g$.
Thus $g(x) = \{(1,x)\}$ by Proposition 358.
Thus $g(x)\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\funs{\dom{Y_1}}{\unions{\ran{Y_1}}}$ and $g(x)$ is a function by Definition 69, Proposition 371.
Thus $(1,x)\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} g(x)$ by Proposition 22.
Hence $\apply{g(x)}{i} = x$ by Proposition 358.
Thus $(g(x),x)\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\piIndex{Y_1}{i}$ by Axiom 7, Definition 72.
Thus $(x,x)\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\piIndex{Y_1}{i}\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-circ}{\circ} g$ by Proposition 269.
Thus $\piIndex{Y_1}{i}\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\funs{\productFamily{Y_1}}{\apply{Y_1}{i}}$ and $\piIndex{Y_1}{i}$ is a function by Lemma 108, Proposition 368.
Thus $\piIndex{Y_1}{i}\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-circ}{\circ} g$ is a function by Proposition 384.
Therefore $\apply{\piIndex{Y_1}{i}\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-circ}{\circ} g}{x} = \apply{j}{x}$ by Proposition 358, Proposition 417.

$\piIndex{Y_1}{i}\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-circ}{\circ} g$ is a function and $\dom{\piIndex{Y_1}{i}\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-circ}{\circ} g} = X$ by Proposition 371, Lemma 108, Proposition 368, Proposition 384, Proposition 211, Proposition 269, Proposition 212, Definition 188, Proposition 360, Proposition 221, Proposition 366, Definition 72, Proposition 3.
$j$ is a function and $\dom{j} = X$ by Proposition 413, Proposition 309.
Therefore $\piIndex{Y_1}{i}\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-circ}{\circ} g = j$ by Definition 188, Proposition 362, Proposition 3.
Hence $\piIndex{Y_1}{i}\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-circ}{\circ} g$ is continuous from $X$ to $\apply{Y_1}{i}$ with respect to $T_X$ and $\apply{T_1}{i}$.

Therefore $g$ is continuous from $X$ to $\productFamily{Y_1}$ with respect to $T_X$ and $T_P$ by Lemma 110.

Subclaim: Show that for all $y\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\productFamily{Y_1}$ there exists $x\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} X$ such that $g(x) = y$.

Fix $y$ such that $y\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\productFamily{Y_1}$.
Then $y$ is a function and $\dom{y} = \dom{Y_1}$ by Definition 69, Proposition 371.
Hence $1\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\dom{y}$ by Definition 188, Axiom 32, Definition 281.
Take $x\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} X$ such that $x = y(1)$.
Thus $(x,\{(1,x)\})\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} g$.
For all $i$ such that $i\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\dom{y}$ we have $\apply{g(x)}{i} = y(i)$ by Definition 188, Proposition 371, Proposition 358, Proposition 22, Proposition 360, Proposition 221, Proposition 366, Definition 69.
Thus $g(x)$ is a function and $\dom{g(x)} = \dom{Y_1}$ by Definition 188, Definition 245, Proposition 368, Proposition 360, Proposition 221, Proposition 366, Definition 69, Proposition 371.
Therefore there exists $z\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} X$ such that $g(z) = y$ by Definition 188, Proposition 362, Proposition 3.

Therefore $g\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\surj{X}{\productFamily{Y_1}}$ by Definition 247.
Hence $\img{g}{X} = \productFamily{Y_1}$ by Definition 247, Proposition 371, Proposition 250, Corollary 15.
Let $A_0 = \img{g}{X}$.
Let $T_A = \subspaceTopology{T_P}{A_0}$.
Then $T_A = \subspaceTopology{T_P}{\productFamily{Y_1}}$.
$T_P$ is a topology on $\productFamily{Y_1}$ by Lemma 94, Definition 15, Lemma 1.
Therefore $T_A = T_P$ by Definition 48, Definition 1, Definition 188, Proposition 156, Proposition 70, Proposition 3.
Hence $\productFamily{Y_1}$ is compact with respect to $T_P$ by Theorem 56.

Hence $\finiteIndexedProductCompactProp{1}$ by Definition 153.
Hence $1\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} A$ by Axiom 32.

For all $k$ such that $k\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} A$ we have $k\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\naturalsPlus$ and $\finiteIndexedProductCompactProp{k}$.
Thus for all $k\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} A$ we have $k + 1\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} A$ by Axiom 35, Lemma 261.
Hence $n\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} A$ by Theorem 89.
Therefore $\productFamily{Y}$ is compact with respect to $\productTopologyIndexed{T_Y}{Y}$ by Definition 188, Definition 153.

LaTeX source 
\begin{proof}
    Let $A = \{m\in\naturalsPlus \mid \finiteIndexedProductCompactProp{m}\}$.
    $A\subseteq\naturalsPlus$ by \cref{subseteq}.
    Show that $1\in A$.
    \begin{subproof}
        It suffices to show that $1\in\naturalsPlus$ and $\finiteIndexedProductCompactProp{1}$.
        Show that for all $Y_1,T_1$ such that $Y_1$ is a function and $T_1$ is a function and
            $\dom{Y_1} = \initialSegment{1}$ and $\dom{T_1} = \initialSegment{1}$ and
            for all $i\in\dom{Y_1}$ we have $\apply{T_1}{i}$ is a topology on $\apply{Y_1}{i}$ and
                $\apply{Y_1}{i}$ is compact with respect to $\apply{T_1}{i}$,
            we have $\productFamily{Y_1}$ is compact with respect to $\productTopologyIndexed{T_1}{Y_1}$.
        \begin{subproof}
            Fix $Y_1$.
            Fix $T_1$.
            Assume $Y_1$ is a function.
            Assume $T_1$ is a function.
            Assume $\dom{Y_1} = \initialSegment{1}$.
            Assume $\dom{T_1} = \initialSegment{1}$.
            Assume that for all $i\in\dom{Y_1}$ we have $\apply{T_1}{i}$ is a topology on $\apply{Y_1}{i}$ and
                $\apply{Y_1}{i}$ is compact with respect to $\apply{T_1}{i}$.
            Let $J_1 = \initialSegment{1}$.
            Then $1\in J_1$ by \cref{real_one_in_naturals,initial_segment_naturalsplus}.
            For all $i\in J_1$ we have $i < 1$ or $i = 1$ by \cref{initial_segment_naturalsplus}.
            For all $i\in J_1$ we have $1\leq i$ by \cref{initial_segment_naturalsplus,real_one_leq_naturalsplus}.
            Thus for all $i\in J_1$ we have $i = 1$.
            Therefore $J_1 = \{1,1\}$ by \cref{subseteq_antisymmetric,subseteq,upair_iff}.
            Let $X = \apply{Y_1}{1}$.
            Let $T_X = \apply{T_1}{1}$.
            Show that $T_X$ is a topology on $X$ and $X$ is compact with respect to $T_X$.
            \begin{subproof}
                Follows by \cref{subseteq}.
            \end{subproof}
            Let $g = \{(x,\{(1,x)\}) \mid x\in X\}$.
            $g$ is a relation by \cref{relation}.
            For all $x,u,v$ such that $(x,u)\in g$ and $(x,v)\in g$ we have $u = v$ by \cref{pair_eq_iff}.
            Hence $g$ is a function by \cref{rightunique}.
            Hence $\dom{g} = X$ by \cref{dom_intro,dom_iff,pair_eq_iff,subseteq,subseteq_antisymmetric}.
            Show that for all $x\in\dom{g}$ we have $g(x)\in\productFamily{Y_1}$.
            \begin{subproof}
                Fix $x$ such that $x\in\dom{g}$.
                Take $y$ such that $(x,y)\in g$ by \cref{dom_iff}.
                Take $x_0\in X$ such that $(x,y) = (x_0,\{(1,x_0)\})$.
                Thus $y = \{(1,x_0)\}$ by \cref{pair_eq_iff}.
                Thus $y$ is a relation by \cref{singleton_iff,relation}.
                For all $a,b_1,b_2$ such that $(a,b_1)\in y$ and $(a,b_2)\in y$ we have $b_1 = b_2$
                    by \cref{singleton_iff,pair_eq_iff}.
                Then $y$ is a function by \cref{rightunique}.
                Therefore $\dom{y} = \dom{Y_1}$
                    by \cref{subseteq,singleton_iff,dom_intro,dom_iff,pair_eq_iff,subseteq_antisymmetric}.
                For all $i$ such that $i\in\dom{Y_1}$ we have $y(i)\in\unions{\ran{Y_1}}$
                    by \cref{subseteq,singleton_iff,function_apply_intro,function_apply_elim,ran_intro,unions_iff}.
                Hence $y\in\funs{\dom{Y_1}}{\unions{\ran{Y_1}}}$ by \cref{funs_intro}.
                For all $i$ such that $i\in\dom{Y_1}$ we have $y(i)\in\apply{Y_1}{i}$
                    by \cref{subseteq,singleton_iff,funs_elim,function_apply_intro}.
                Therefore $y\in\productFamily{Y_1}$ by \cref{indexed_product}.
                Hence $g(x)\in\productFamily{Y_1}$ by \cref{function_apply_intro}.
            \end{subproof}
            Therefore $g\in\funs{X}{\productFamily{Y_1}}$ by \cref{funs_intro}.
            Let $T_P = \productTopologyIndexed{T_1}{Y_1}$.
        $J_1$ is inhabited.
        Show that for all $i\in J_1$ we have $\piIndex{Y_1}{i}\circ g$ is continuous from $X$ to $\apply{Y_1}{i}$ with respect to $T_X$ and $\apply{T_1}{i}$.
        \begin{subproof}
            Fix $i$ such that $i\in J_1$.
            Then $i = 1$.
            Let $j = \identity{X}$.
            $j$ is continuous from $X$ to $X$ with respect to $T_X$ and $T_X$ by \cref{identity_continuous}.
                    Show that for all $x\in X$ we have $\apply{\piIndex{Y_1}{i}\circ g}{x} = \apply{j}{x}$.
                    \begin{subproof}
                        Fix $x$ such that $x\in X$.
                        Thus $g$ is a function to $\productFamily{Y_1}$ and $g$ is a function and $\dom{g} = X$
                            by \cref{funs_elim,funs_is_function}.
                        Thus $g(x)\in\productFamily{Y_1}$ by \cref{subseteq,funs,function_apply_elim,ran_intro,function_to_ran}.
                        Thus $(x,\{(1,x)\})\in g$.
                        Thus $g(x) = \{(1,x)\}$ by \cref{function_apply_intro}.
                        Thus $g(x)\in\funs{\dom{Y_1}}{\unions{\ran{Y_1}}}$ and $g(x)$ is a function
                            by \cref{indexed_product,funs_elim}.
                        Thus $(1,x)\in g(x)$ by \cref{singleton_iff}.
                        Hence $\apply{g(x)}{i} = x$ by \cref{function_apply_intro}.
                        Thus $(g(x),x)\in\piIndex{Y_1}{i}$ by \cref{pair_eq_iff,projection_map_indexed}.
                        Thus $(x,x)\in\piIndex{Y_1}{i}\circ g$ by \cref{circ_iff}.
                        Thus $\piIndex{Y_1}{i}\in\funs{\productFamily{Y_1}}{\apply{Y_1}{i}}$
                            and $\piIndex{Y_1}{i}$ is a function by \cref{projection_map_indexed_function,funs_is_function}.
                        Thus $\piIndex{Y_1}{i}\circ g$ is a function by \cref{function_circ}.
                        Therefore $\apply{\piIndex{Y_1}{i}\circ g}{x} = \apply{j}{x}$
                            by \cref{function_apply_intro,id_apply}.
                    \end{subproof}
            $\piIndex{Y_1}{i}\circ g$ is a function and $\dom{\piIndex{Y_1}{i}\circ g} = X$
                by \cref{funs_elim,projection_map_indexed_function,funs_is_function,function_circ,dom_iff,circ_iff,dom_intro,subseteq,function_apply_elim,ran_intro,function_to_ran,projection_map_indexed,subseteq_antisymmetric}.
            $j$ is a function and $\dom{j} = X$ by \cref{id_is_function,id_dom}.
            Therefore $\piIndex{Y_1}{i}\circ g = j$ by \cref{subseteq,fun_subseteq,subseteq_antisymmetric}.
            Hence $\piIndex{Y_1}{i}\circ g$ is continuous from $X$ to $\apply{Y_1}{i}$ with respect to $T_X$ and $\apply{T_1}{i}$.
        \end{subproof}
        Therefore $g$ is continuous from $X$ to $\productFamily{Y_1}$ with respect to $T_X$ and $T_P$
            by \cref{continuous_map_into_indexed_product}.
        Show that for all $y\in\productFamily{Y_1}$ there exists $x\in X$ such that $g(x) = y$.
        \begin{subproof}
            Fix $y$ such that $y\in\productFamily{Y_1}$.
            Then $y$ is a function and $\dom{y} = \dom{Y_1}$ by \cref{indexed_product,funs_elim}.
            Hence $1\in\dom{y}$ by \cref{subseteq,real_one_in_naturals,initial_segment_naturalsplus}.
            Take $x\in X$ such that $x = y(1)$.
            Thus $(x,\{(1,x)\})\in g$.
            For all $i$ such that $i\in\dom{y}$ we have $\apply{g(x)}{i} = y(i)$
                by \cref{subseteq,funs_elim,function_apply_intro,singleton_iff,function_apply_elim,ran_intro,function_to_ran,indexed_product}.
            Thus $g(x)$ is a function and $\dom{g(x)} = \dom{Y_1}$
                by \cref{subseteq,funs,funs_is_function,function_apply_elim,ran_intro,function_to_ran,indexed_product,funs_elim}.
            Therefore there exists $z\in X$ such that $g(z) = y$
                by \cref{subseteq,fun_subseteq,subseteq_antisymmetric}.
        \end{subproof}
        Therefore $g\in\surj{X}{\productFamily{Y_1}}$ by \cref{surj}.
        Hence $\img{g}{X} = \productFamily{Y_1}$ by \cref{surj,funs_elim,img_dom,funs_surj_iff}.
        Let $A_0 = \img{g}{X}$.
        Let $T_A = \subspaceTopology{T_P}{A_0}$.
        Then $T_A = \subspaceTopology{T_P}{\productFamily{Y_1}}$.
        $T_P$ is a topology on $\productFamily{Y_1}$
            by \cref{product_subbasis_finite_intersections_basis,basis_for_topology,basis_topology_is_topology}.
        Therefore $T_A = T_P$
            by \cref{subspace_topology,topology_on,subseteq,powerset_elim,inter_absorb_supseteq_left,subseteq_antisymmetric}.
        Hence $\productFamily{Y_1}$ is compact with respect to $T_P$ by \cref{continuous_image_compact}.
    \end{subproof}
        Hence $\finiteIndexedProductCompactProp{1}$ by \cref{finite_indexed_product_compact_property}.
        Hence $1\in A$ by \cref{real_one_in_naturals}.
    \end{subproof}
    For all $k$ such that $k\in A$ we have $k\in\naturalsPlus$ and $\finiteIndexedProductCompactProp{k}$.
    Thus for all $k\in A$ we have $k + 1\in A$
        by \cref{real_naturals_closed_succ,finite_indexed_product_compact_property_succ}.
    Hence $n\in A$ by \cref{real_naturals_induction}.
    Therefore $\productFamily{Y}$ is compact with respect to $\productTopologyIndexed{T_Y}{Y}$
        by \cref{subseteq,finite_indexed_product_compact_property}.
\end{proof}
Lemma 263
square_metric_topology_equals_euclidean

Suppose $n\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\naturalsPlus$.
Let $J = \initialSegment{n}$.
Let $X = \realsPower{J}$.
Let $T_E = \metricTopology{\euclideanMetric{n}}{X}$.
Let $T_S = \metricTopology{\squareMetric{n}}{X}$.
Then $T_E = T_S$.

LaTeX source 
\begin{lemma}
\label{square_metric_topology_equals_euclidean}
    Suppose $n\in\naturalsPlus$.
    Let $J = \initialSegment{n}$.
    Let $X = \realsPower{J}$.
    Let $T_E = \metricTopology{\euclideanMetric{n}}{X}$.
    Let $T_S = \metricTopology{\squareMetric{n}}{X}$.
    Then $T_E = T_S$.
\end{lemma}
Proof

Let $X_0 = X$.
Let $d = \euclideanMetric{n}$.
Let $d_1 = \squareMetric{n}$.
$d$ is a metric on $X_0$ and $d_1$ is a metric on $X_0$ by Theorem 18, Lemma 163.

Subclaim: Show that for all $x\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} X_0$ we have for all $\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-epsilon}{\epsilon}\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\reals$ such that $0 < \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-epsilon}{\epsilon}$ there exists $\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-delta}{\delta}\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\reals$ such that $0 < \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-delta}{\delta}$ and $\metricBall{d}{X_0}{x}{\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-delta}{\delta}} \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-subseteq}{\subseteq} \metricBall{d_1}{X_0}{x}{\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-epsilon}{\epsilon}}$.

Fix $x$ such that $x\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} X_0$.
Fix $\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-epsilon}{\epsilon}$ such that $\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-epsilon}{\epsilon}\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\reals$ and $0 < \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-epsilon}{\epsilon}$.
Let $\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-delta}{\delta} = \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-epsilon}{\epsilon}$.

Subclaim: Show that $0 < \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-delta}{\delta}$ and $\metricBall{d}{X_0}{x}{\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-delta}{\delta}} \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-subseteq}{\subseteq} \metricBall{d_1}{X_0}{x}{\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-epsilon}{\epsilon}}$.

Follows by Definition 188, Definition 82, Lemma 160, Lemma 132, Lemma 136, Lemma 161, Lemma 128, Lemma 6, Lemma 7, Axiom 44, Proposition 358.

Hence $T_E$ is finer than $T_S$ on $X_0$ by Lemma 124.

Subclaim: Show that for all $x\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} X_0$ we have for all $\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-epsilon}{\epsilon}\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\reals$ such that $0 < \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-epsilon}{\epsilon}$ there exists $\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-delta}{\delta}\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\reals$ such that $0 < \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-delta}{\delta}$ and $\metricBall{d_1}{X_0}{x}{\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-delta}{\delta}} \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-subseteq}{\subseteq} \metricBall{d}{X_0}{x}{\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-epsilon}{\epsilon}}$.

Fix $x$ such that $x\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} X_0$.
Fix $\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-epsilon}{\epsilon}$ such that $\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-epsilon}{\epsilon}\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\reals$ and $0 < \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-epsilon}{\epsilon}$.
$0 < 1$ and $0\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\reals$ and $1\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\reals$ and $n\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\reals$ by Theorem 114, Axiom 24, Axiom 31, Axiom 32, Definition 188.
Hence $0 < n$ by Axiom 33, Lemma 8, Axiom 44.
Then $\sqrt{n}\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\reals$ and $\sqrt{n} \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-geq}{\geq} 0$ and $\sqrt{n} \rmul \sqrt{n} = n$ by Axiom 51.
Hence $\sqrt{n}$ is positive.
Let $\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-delta}{\delta} = \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-epsilon}{\epsilon} \rmul \inv{\sqrt{n}}$.
$\sqrt{n} \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-neq}{\neq} 0$ and $\inv{\sqrt{n}}\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\reals$ and $\inv{\sqrt{n}} > 0$ by Lemma 404, Axiom 29, Theorem 115.
Therefore $\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-delta}{\delta}\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\reals$ and $\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-delta}{\delta} > 0$ by Axiom 21, Theorem 106.

Subclaim: Show that for all $y$ such that $y\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\metricBall{d_1}{X_0}{x}{\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-delta}{\delta}}$ we have $y\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\metricBall{d}{X_0}{x}{\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-epsilon}{\epsilon}}$.

Fix $y$ such that $y\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\metricBall{d_1}{X_0}{x}{\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-delta}{\delta}}$.
Thus $y\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} X_0$ and $d_1((x,y)) < \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-delta}{\delta}$ by Definition 82.
Take $m\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\reals$ such that $((x,y),m)\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} d_1$ by Lemma 132.
Take $r\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\reals$ such that $((x,y),r)\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} d$ by Lemma 160.
Hence $m < \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-delta}{\delta}$ by Lemma 128, Proposition 358, Lemma 7.
Then $r \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-leq}{\leq} \sqrt{n} \rmul m$ by Lemma 136.
$m \rmul \sqrt{n} < \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-delta}{\delta} \rmul \sqrt{n}$ by Axiom 46.
Hence $\sqrt{n} \rmul m < \sqrt{n} \rmul \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-delta}{\delta}$ by Axiom 27.
Then $\sqrt{n} \rmul \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-delta}{\delta} = \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-epsilon}{\epsilon}$ by Axiom 26, Axiom 27, Axiom 29, Axiom 28.
Hence $\sqrt{n} \rmul m < \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-epsilon}{\epsilon}$ by Lemma 8.
Therefore $d((x,y)) < \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-epsilon}{\epsilon}$ by Lemma 161, Axiom 21, Lemma 6, Lemma 7, Axiom 44, Proposition 358.
Hence $y\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\metricBall{d}{X_0}{x}{\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-epsilon}{\epsilon}}$ by Definition 82.

Therefore $\metricBall{d_1}{X_0}{x}{\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-delta}{\delta}} \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-subseteq}{\subseteq} \metricBall{d}{X_0}{x}{\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-epsilon}{\epsilon}}$ by Definition 188.

Hence $T_S$ is finer than $T_E$ on $X_0$ by Lemma 124.
Hence $T_E = T_S$ by Definition 8, Proposition 3.

LaTeX source 
\begin{proof}
    Let $X_0 = X$.
    Let $d = \euclideanMetric{n}$.
    Let $d_1 = \squareMetric{n}$.
    $d$ is a metric on $X_0$ and $d_1$ is a metric on $X_0$ by \cref{euclidean_metric_is_metric,square_metric_is_metric}.
    Show that for all $x\in X_0$ we have
        for all $\epsilon\in\reals$ such that $0 < \epsilon$ there exists $\delta\in\reals$
            such that $0 < \delta$ and
            $\metricBall{d}{X_0}{x}{\delta} \subseteq \metricBall{d_1}{X_0}{x}{\epsilon}$.
    \begin{subproof}
        Fix $x$ such that $x\in X_0$.
        Fix $\epsilon$ such that $\epsilon\in\reals$ and $0 < \epsilon$.
        Let $\delta = \epsilon$.
        Show that $0 < \delta$ and
            $\metricBall{d}{X_0}{x}{\delta} \subseteq \metricBall{d_1}{X_0}{x}{\epsilon}$.
        \begin{subproof}
            Follows by \cref{subseteq,metric_ball,euclidean_metric_value_exists,square_metric_value_exists,euclidean_square_metric_bounds,euclidean_metric_function,square_metric_function,real_leq_split,real_lt_subst_left,real_lt_trans,function_apply_intro}.
        \end{subproof}
    \end{subproof}
    Hence $T_E$ is finer than $T_S$ on $X_0$ by \cref{metric_topology_finer_iff}.
    Show that for all $x\in X_0$ we have
        for all $\epsilon\in\reals$ such that $0 < \epsilon$ there exists $\delta\in\reals$
            such that $0 < \delta$ and
            $\metricBall{d_1}{X_0}{x}{\delta} \subseteq \metricBall{d}{X_0}{x}{\epsilon}$.
    \begin{subproof}
        Fix $x$ such that $x\in X_0$.
        Fix $\epsilon$ such that $\epsilon\in\reals$ and $0 < \epsilon$.
        $0 < 1$ and $0\in\reals$ and $1\in\reals$ and $n\in\reals$
            by \cref{real_zero_lt_one,real_add_ident,real_naturals_subset_reals,real_one_in_naturals,subseteq}.
        Hence $0 < n$ by \cref{real_one_leq_naturalsplus,real_lt_subst_right,real_lt_trans}.
        Then $\sqrt{n}\in\reals$ and $\sqrt{n} \geq 0$ and $\sqrt{n} \rmul \sqrt{n} = n$ by \cref{positive_square_root}.
        Hence $\sqrt{n}$ is positive.
        Let $\delta = \epsilon \rmul \inv{\sqrt{n}}$.
        $\sqrt{n} \neq 0$ and $\inv{\sqrt{n}}\in\reals$ and $\inv{\sqrt{n}} > 0$
            by \cref{real_pos_nonzero,real_mul_inverse,real_inv_pos}.
        Therefore $\delta\in\reals$ and $\delta > 0$ by \cref{real_mul_closed,real_mul_pos_pos_gt}.
        Show that for all $y$ such that $y\in\metricBall{d_1}{X_0}{x}{\delta}$ we have
            $y\in\metricBall{d}{X_0}{x}{\epsilon}$.
        \begin{subproof}
            Fix $y$ such that $y\in\metricBall{d_1}{X_0}{x}{\delta}$.
            Thus $y\in X_0$ and $d_1((x,y)) < \delta$ by \cref{metric_ball}.
            Take $m\in\reals$ such that $((x,y),m)\in d_1$ by \cref{square_metric_value_exists}.
            Take $r\in\reals$ such that $((x,y),r)\in d$ by \cref{euclidean_metric_value_exists}.
            Hence $m < \delta$ by \cref{square_metric_function,function_apply_intro,real_lt_subst_left}.
            Then $r \leq \sqrt{n} \rmul m$ by \cref{euclidean_square_metric_bounds}.
            $m \rmul \sqrt{n} < \delta \rmul \sqrt{n}$ by \cref{real_lt_mul_pos}.
            Hence $\sqrt{n} \rmul m < \sqrt{n} \rmul \delta$ by \cref{real_mul_comm}.
            Then $\sqrt{n} \rmul \delta = \epsilon$ by
                \cref{real_mul_assoc,real_mul_comm,real_mul_inverse,real_mul_ident}.
            Hence $\sqrt{n} \rmul m < \epsilon$ by \cref{real_lt_subst_right}.
            Therefore $d((x,y)) < \epsilon$ by
                \cref{euclidean_metric_function,real_mul_closed,real_leq_split,real_lt_subst_left,real_lt_trans,function_apply_intro}.
            Hence $y\in\metricBall{d}{X_0}{x}{\epsilon}$ by \cref{metric_ball}.
        \end{subproof}
        Therefore $\metricBall{d_1}{X_0}{x}{\delta} \subseteq \metricBall{d}{X_0}{x}{\epsilon}$ by \cref{subseteq}.
    \end{subproof}
    Hence $T_S$ is finer than $T_E$ on $X_0$ by \cref{metric_topology_finer_iff}.
    Hence $T_E = T_S$ by \cref{finer_topology,subseteq_antisymmetric}.
\end{proof}
Lemma 264
reals_standard_indexed_families_data

Suppose $n\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\naturalsPlus$.
Let $J = \initialSegment{n}$.
Let $X_1 = \{(i,\reals) \mid i\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} J\}$.
Let $T_1 = \{(i,\standardTopologyR) \mid i\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} J\}$.
Then $X_1$ is a function and $T_1$ is a function and $\dom{X_1} = J$ and $\dom{T_1} = J$.

LaTeX source 
\begin{lemma}
\label{reals_standard_indexed_families_data}
    Suppose $n\in\naturalsPlus$.
    Let $J = \initialSegment{n}$.
    Let $X_1 = \{(i,\reals) \mid i\in J\}$.
    Let $T_1 = \{(i,\standardTopologyR) \mid i\in J\}$.
    Then $X_1$ is a function and $T_1$ is a function and $\dom{X_1} = J$ and $\dom{T_1} = J$.
\end{lemma}
Proof

For all $i,y_1,y_2$ such that $(i,y_1)\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} X_1$ and $(i,y_2)\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} X_1$ we have $y_1 = y_2$.

Subclaim: Show that $X_1$ is a function.

Follows by Definition 202, Definition 241.

For all $i,y_1,y_2$ such that $(i,y_1)\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} T_1$ and $(i,y_2)\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} T_1$ we have $y_1 = y_2$.

Subclaim: Show that $T_1$ is a function.

Follows by Definition 202, Definition 241.

Subclaim: Show that $\dom{X_1} = J$.

Follows by Proposition 212, Proposition 211, Axiom 7, Definition 188, Proposition 3.

Subclaim: Show that $\dom{T_1} = J$.

Follows by Proposition 212, Proposition 211, Axiom 7, Definition 188, Proposition 3.

LaTeX source 
\begin{proof}
    For all $i,y_1,y_2$ such that $(i,y_1)\in X_1$ and $(i,y_2)\in X_1$ we have $y_1 = y_2$.
    Show that $X_1$ is a function.
    \begin{subproof}
        Follows by \cref{relation,rightunique}.
    \end{subproof}
    For all $i,y_1,y_2$ such that $(i,y_1)\in T_1$ and $(i,y_2)\in T_1$ we have $y_1 = y_2$.
    Show that $T_1$ is a function.
    \begin{subproof}
        Follows by \cref{relation,rightunique}.
    \end{subproof}
    Show that $\dom{X_1} = J$.
    \begin{subproof}
        Follows by \cref{dom_intro,dom_iff,pair_eq_iff,subseteq,subseteq_antisymmetric}.
    \end{subproof}
    Show that $\dom{T_1} = J$.
    \begin{subproof}
        Follows by \cref{dom_intro,dom_iff,pair_eq_iff,subseteq,subseteq_antisymmetric}.
    \end{subproof}
\end{proof}
Theorem 61
compact_reals_power_iff_closed_bounded

Suppose $n\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\naturalsPlus$.
Let $J = \initialSegment{n}$.
Let $X = \realsPower{J}$.
Let $d = \euclideanMetric{n}$.
Let $\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-rho}{\rho} = \squareMetric{n}$.
Let $T = \metricTopology{\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-rho}{\rho}}{X}$.
Suppose $A\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-subseteq}{\subseteq} X$.
Let $T_A = \subspaceTopology{T}{A}$.
Then $A$ is compact with respect to $T_A$ iff $A$ is closed in $X$ with respect to $T$ and $A$ is bounded in $X$ with respect to $d$ or $A$ is bounded in $X$ with respect to $\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-rho}{\rho}$.

LaTeX source 
\begin{theorem}
\label{compact_reals_power_iff_closed_bounded}
    Suppose $n\in\naturalsPlus$.
    Let $J = \initialSegment{n}$.
    Let $X = \realsPower{J}$.
    Let $d = \euclideanMetric{n}$.
    Let $\rho = \squareMetric{n}$.
    Let $T = \metricTopology{\rho}{X}$.
    Suppose $A\subseteq X$.
    Let $T_A = \subspaceTopology{T}{A}$.
    Then $A$ is compact with respect to $T_A$ iff
        $A$ is closed in $X$ with respect to $T$ and
        $A$ is bounded in $X$ with respect to $d$ or $A$ is bounded in $X$ with respect to $\rho$.
\end{theorem}
Proof

Let $X_0 = X$.
Let $d_1 = \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-rho}{\rho}$.

Subclaim: Show that if $A$ is compact with respect to $T_A$ then $A$ is closed in $X$ with respect to $T$ and $A$ is bounded in $X$ with respect to $d$ or $A$ is bounded in $X$ with respect to $\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-rho}{\rho}$.

Assume $A$ is compact with respect to $T_A$.
$d_1$ is a metric on $X$ by Lemma 163.
$T$ is a topology on $X$ by Lemma 1, Definition 84, Lemma 111, Definition 15.
Let $T_S = \metricTopology{d_1}{X_0}$.
Let $X_1 = \{(i,\reals) \mid i\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} J\}$.
Let $T_1 = \{(i,\standardTopologyR) \mid i\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} J\}$.
Let $T_0 = \productTopologyIndexed{T_1}{X_1}$.
$d$ is a metric on $X_0$ and $d_1$ is a metric on $X_0$ by Theorem 18, Lemma 163.
Hence $J$ is inhabited by Axiom 32, Axiom 33, Definition 281.
$X_1$ is a function and $T_1$ is a function and $\dom{X_1} = J$ and $\dom{T_1} = J$ by Lemma 264.
For all $\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-alpha}{\alpha}\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\dom{X_1}$ we have $\apply{X_1}{\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-alpha}{\alpha}} = \reals$ and $\apply{T_1}{\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-alpha}{\alpha}} = \standardTopologyR$ by Definition 188, Proposition 358.
$\realOrderTopology = \standardTopologyR$ and $\realOrderTopology = \basisTopology{\orderBasis{\realOrderRel}{\reals}}{\reals}$ by Lemma 230, Definition 135.
$\realOrderRel$ is a simple order relation on $\reals$ by Lemma 218.
$\reals$ has more than one element by Definition 133, Lemma 220.
Hence $\realOrderTopology$ is the order topology on $\reals$ with respect to $\realOrderRel$ by Definition 36.
Therefore for all $\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-alpha}{\alpha}\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\dom{X_1}$ we have $\apply{X_1}{\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-alpha}{\alpha}}$ is Hausdorff with respect to $\apply{T_1}{\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-alpha}{\alpha}}$ by Theorem 12, Lemma 230.
Hence $X_0$ is Hausdorff with respect to $T_0$ by Lemma 105, Lemma 126.
Therefore $T_S = T_0$ by Lemma 263, Theorem 19.
Then $X$ is Hausdorff with respect to $T$.
Therefore $A$ is closed in $X$ with respect to $T$ by Theorem 55.

Subclaim: Show that $A$ is bounded in $X$ with respect to $\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-rho}{\rho}$.
Case. $A = \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-emptyset}{\emptyset}$.

Let $M = 0$.
For all $a_1,a_2\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} A$ we have $\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-rho}{\rho}((a_1,a_2)) \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-leq}{\leq} M$ by Proposition 12, Proposition 4, Axiom 3.
Thus $A$ is bounded in $X$ with respect to $\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-rho}{\rho}$ by Axiom 24, Definition 89.

Case. $A \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-neq}{\neq} \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-emptyset}{\emptyset}$.

$A$ is inhabited by Proposition 10.
Let $C = \{\metricBall{\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-rho}{\rho}}{X}{a}{1} \mid a\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} A\}$.
For all $U$ such that $U\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} C$ we have $U$ is open in $X$ with respect to $T$ by Axiom 31, Axiom 32, Definition 188, Theorem 114, Definition 82, Proposition 155, Definition 83, Lemma 111, Lemma 2, Definition 84, Definition 15, Definition 3.
Hence $C$ is a covering of $A$ by Definition 188, Definition 147, Lemma 163, Definition 78, Definition 81, Theorem 114, Lemma 7, Definition 82, Axiom 5.
$A$ is a subspace of $X$ with respect to $T$ by Definition 49.
Thus there exists $B$ such that $B\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-subseteq}{\subseteq} C$ and $B$ is finite and $B$ is a covering of $A$ by Lemma 248.
Let $R = \{w\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} B\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-times}{\times} A \mid \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-exists}{\exists} U\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} B.\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-exists}{\exists} a\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} A. w = (U,a) \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-land}{\land} U = \metricBall{\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-rho}{\rho}}{X}{a}{1}\}$.
Thus $R$ is a relation by Definition 202.
For all $U\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} B$ there exists $a$ such that $U\mathrel{R} a$.
Take $g$ such that $g$ is a function on $B$ and for all $U\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} B$ we have $U\mathrel{R}\apply{g}{U}$ by Axiom 1.
Let $F = \img{g}{B}$.
$F$ is finite by Lemma 109.
Hence $F\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-subseteq}{\subseteq} A$ by Definition 188, Proposition 247, Proposition 358, Definition 188, Proposition 114.
Take $a_0$ such that $a_0\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} A$.
Thus $a_0\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\unions{B}$ by Definition 147, Definition 188.
Take $U_0\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} B$ such that $a_0\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} U_0$ by Axiom 5.
Take $f_0$ such that $f_0\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} F$ by Definition 188, Proposition 360, Proposition 247.
Let $h = \{(f,\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-rho}{\rho}((a_0,f))) \mid f\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} F\}$.
$h$ is a relation by Definition 202.
For all $x,y_1,y_2$ such that $(x,y_1)\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} h$ and $(x,y_2)\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} h$ we have $y_1 = y_2$ by Axiom 7.
Hence $h$ is right-unique by Definition 241.
For all $f$ such that $f\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} F$ we have $f\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\dom{h}$.
For all $f$ such that $f\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\dom{h}$ we have $f\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} F$ by Proposition 211, Axiom 7.
Therefore $\dom{h} = F$ by Definition 188, Proposition 3.
Hence $h$ is a function on $F$.
Let $D = \img{h}{F}$.
$D$ is finite by Lemma 109.
For all $r$ such that $r\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} D$ we have $r\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\reals$ by Proposition 247, Definition 188, Proposition 115, Definition 81, Proposition 373, Lemma 163, Axiom 7.
Hence $D\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-subseteq}{\subseteq}\reals$ by Definition 188.
Take $f_1$ such that $f_1\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} F$.
Thus $(f_1,\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-rho}{\rho}((a_0,f_1)))\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} h$.
Therefore $D\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-neq}{\neq}\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-emptyset}{\emptyset}$ by Proposition 247, Proposition 10, Lemma 219.
Take $M_0\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} D$ such that $M_0$ is a maximum of $D$ by Lemma 422.
Let $M = (M_0 + 1) + (M_0 + 1)$.

Subclaim: Show that for all $x,y\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} A$ we have $\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-rho}{\rho}((x,y)) \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-leq}{\leq} M$.

Fix $x$.
Fix $y$ such that $x\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} A$ and $y\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} A$.
Take $U_x\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} B$ such that $x\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} U_x$ by Definition 147, Definition 188, Axiom 5.
Take $U_y\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} B$ such that $y\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} U_y$ by Definition 147, Definition 188, Axiom 5.
Let $f_x = g(U_x)$.
Let $f_y = g(U_y)$.
$U_x\mathrel{R} f_x$ by Proposition 360.
$U_y\mathrel{R} f_y$ by Proposition 360.
Thus $(U_x,f_x)\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} R$.
Take $U_2, a_2$ such that $U_2\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} B$ and $a_2\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} A$ and $(U_x,f_x) = (U_2,a_2)$ and $U_2 = \metricBall{\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-rho}{\rho}}{X}{a_2}{1}$.
Thus $U_x = \metricBall{\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-rho}{\rho}}{X}{f_x}{1}$ by Axiom 7.
Thus $(U_y,f_y)\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} R$.
Take $U_1, a_1$ such that $U_1\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} B$ and $a_1\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} A$ and $(U_y,f_y) = (U_1,a_1)$ and $U_1 = \metricBall{\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-rho}{\rho}}{X}{a_1}{1}$.
Thus $U_y = \metricBall{\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-rho}{\rho}}{X}{f_y}{1}$ by Axiom 7.
Hence $x\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} \metricBall{\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-rho}{\rho}}{X}{f_x}{1}$.
Thus $x\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} X$ and $\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-rho}{\rho}((f_x,x)) < 1$ by Definition 82.
Thus $f_x\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} A$ and $f_x\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} X$ by Definition 188, Proposition 114.
Therefore $\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-rho}{\rho}((x,f_x)) < 1$ by Definition 79, Definition 81, Lemma 163, Lemma 7.
Hence $y\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} \metricBall{\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-rho}{\rho}}{X}{f_y}{1}$.
Thus $y\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} X$ and $\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-rho}{\rho}((f_y,y)) < 1$ by Definition 82.
Thus $f_y\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} A$ and $f_y\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} X$ by Definition 188, Proposition 114.
Therefore $\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-rho}{\rho}((y,f_y)) < 1$ by Definition 79, Definition 81, Lemma 163, Lemma 7.
Then $f_x\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} F$ and $f_y\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} F$ by Definition 188, Proposition 360, Proposition 247.
Hence $h(f_x)\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} D$ and $h(f_y)\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} D$ by Definition 188, Proposition 360, Proposition 247.
Therefore $h(f_x) \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-leq}{\leq} M_0$ and $h(f_y) \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-leq}{\leq} M_0$ by Definition 273.
Then $\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-rho}{\rho}((a_0,f_x)) \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-leq}{\leq} M_0$ and $\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-rho}{\rho}((a_0,f_y)) \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-leq}{\leq} M_0$ by Proposition 358.

Subclaim: Show that $\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-rho}{\rho}((x,y)) \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-leq}{\leq} \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-rho}{\rho}((x,f_x)) + \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-rho}{\rho}((f_x,a_0)) + \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-rho}{\rho}((a_0,f_y)) + \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-rho}{\rho}((f_y,y))$.

$x\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} X$ and $y\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} X$ and $f_x\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} X$ and $f_y\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} X$ and $a_0\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} X$ by Definition 188.
Thus $\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-rho}{\rho}((x,y)) \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-leq}{\leq} \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-rho}{\rho}((x,f_x)) + \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-rho}{\rho}((f_x,y))$ by Lemma 163, Definition 81, Definition 80.
Thus $\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-rho}{\rho}((f_x,y)) \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-leq}{\leq} \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-rho}{\rho}((f_x,a_0)) + \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-rho}{\rho}((a_0,y))$ by Lemma 163, Definition 81, Definition 80.
Thus $\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-rho}{\rho}((a_0,y)) \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-leq}{\leq} \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-rho}{\rho}((a_0,f_y)) + \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-rho}{\rho}((f_y,y))$ by Lemma 163, Definition 81, Definition 80.
Thus $x\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} X$ and $f_x\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} A$ and $f_x\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} X$ and $f_y\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} A$ by Proposition 4.
Thus $f_y\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} X$ and $y\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} X$ and $a_0\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} A$ and $a_0\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} X$ by Proposition 4.
$(x,f_x)\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} X\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-times}{\times} X$ by Proposition 115.
$(f_x,y)\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} X\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-times}{\times} X$ by Proposition 115.
$(f_x,a_0)\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} X\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-times}{\times} X$ by Proposition 115.
$(a_0,y)\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} X\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-times}{\times} X$ by Proposition 115.
$(a_0,f_y)\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} X\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-times}{\times} X$ by Proposition 115.
$(f_y,y)\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} X\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-times}{\times} X$ by Proposition 115.
$(x,y)\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} X\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-times}{\times} X$ by Proposition 115.
Thus $\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-rho}{\rho}((x,y))\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\reals$ by Definition 81, Proposition 373.
Thus $\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-rho}{\rho}((x,f_x))\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\reals$ by Definition 81, Proposition 373.
Thus $\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-rho}{\rho}((f_x,y))\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\reals$ by Definition 81, Proposition 373.
Thus $\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-rho}{\rho}((f_x,a_0))\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\reals$ by Definition 81, Proposition 373.
Thus $\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-rho}{\rho}((a_0,y))\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\reals$ by Definition 81, Proposition 373.
Thus $\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-rho}{\rho}((a_0,f_y))\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\reals$ by Definition 81, Proposition 373.
Thus $\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-rho}{\rho}((f_y,y))\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\reals$ by Definition 81, Proposition 373.
Let $r_1 = \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-rho}{\rho}((x,f_x))$.
Let $r_2 = \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-rho}{\rho}((f_x,y))$.
Let $r_3 = \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-rho}{\rho}((f_x,a_0))$.
Let $r_4 = \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-rho}{\rho}((a_0,y))$.
Thus $r_1\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\reals$ and $r_2\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\reals$ and $r_3\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\reals$ and $r_4\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\reals$.
Thus $r_3 + r_4\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\reals$ by Axiom 20.
Thus $r_1 + r_2\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\reals$ by Axiom 20.
Thus $r_1 + (r_3 + r_4)\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\reals$ by Axiom 20.
$r_2 \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-leq}{\leq} r_3 + r_4$.
Hence $r_2 + r_1 \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-leq}{\leq} (r_3 + r_4) + r_1$ by Lemma 408.
Then $r_1 + r_2 = r_2 + r_1$ by Axiom 23.
$(r_3 + r_4) + r_1 = r_1 + (r_3 + r_4)$ by Axiom 23.
Therefore $r_1 + r_2 \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-leq}{\leq} (r_3 + r_4) + r_1$ by Lemma 407.
Hence $r_1 + r_2 \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-leq}{\leq} r_1 + (r_3 + r_4)$ by Lemma 407.
Then $\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-rho}{\rho}((x,y)) \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-leq}{\leq} r_1 + (r_3 + r_4)$ by Lemma 407.
Let $u = \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-rho}{\rho}((a_0,f_y)) + \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-rho}{\rho}((f_y,y))$.
Thus $u\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\reals$ by Axiom 20.
Thus $r_1 + r_3\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\reals$ by Axiom 20.
Thus $(r_1 + r_3) + u\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\reals$ by Axiom 20.
$r_4 \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-leq}{\leq} u$.
Therefore $r_4 + (r_1 + r_3) \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-leq}{\leq} u + (r_1 + r_3)$ by Lemma 408.
Thus $(r_1 + r_3) + r_4 = r_4 + (r_1 + r_3)$ by Axiom 23.
Thus $u + (r_1 + r_3) = (r_1 + r_3) + u$ by Axiom 23.
Hence $(r_1 + r_3) + r_4 \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-leq}{\leq} u + (r_1 + r_3)$ by Lemma 407.
Then $(r_1 + r_3) + r_4 \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-leq}{\leq} (r_1 + r_3) + u$ by Lemma 407.
$r_1 + (r_3 + r_4) = (r_1 + r_3) + r_4$ by Axiom 22.
Therefore $r_1 + (r_3 + r_4) \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-leq}{\leq} (r_1 + r_3) + u$ by Lemma 407.
Hence $\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-rho}{\rho}((x,y)) \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-leq}{\leq} (r_1 + r_3) + u$ by Lemma 407.
Therefore $\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-rho}{\rho}((x,y)) \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-leq}{\leq} \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-rho}{\rho}((x,f_x)) + \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-rho}{\rho}((f_x,a_0)) + \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-rho}{\rho}((a_0,f_y)) + \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-rho}{\rho}((f_y,y))$ by Axiom 22, Axiom 23.

Subclaim: Show that $\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-rho}{\rho}((x,y)) \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-leq}{\leq} M$.

Let $s_1 = \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-rho}{\rho}((x,f_x))$.
Let $s_2 = \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-rho}{\rho}((f_x,a_0))$.
Let $s_3 = \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-rho}{\rho}((a_0,f_y))$.
Let $s_4 = \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-rho}{\rho}((f_y,y))$.
Thus $x\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} X$ and $y\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} X$ and $f_x\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} A$ and $f_x\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} X$ and $f_y\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} A$ and $f_y\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} X$ and $a_0\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} A$ and $a_0\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} X$ by Proposition 4.
$(x,y)\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} X\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-times}{\times} X$ by Proposition 115.
$(x,f_x)\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} X\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-times}{\times} X$ by Proposition 115.
$(f_x,a_0)\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} X\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-times}{\times} X$ by Proposition 115.
$(a_0,f_y)\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} X\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-times}{\times} X$ by Proposition 115.
$(f_y,y)\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} X\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-times}{\times} X$ by Proposition 115.
Thus $s_1\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\reals$ and $s_2\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\reals$ and $s_3\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\reals$ and $s_4\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\reals$ by Definition 81, Proposition 373.
$s_1 + s_2\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\reals$ and $s_3 + s_4\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\reals$ by Axiom 20.
$1\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\reals$ by Axiom 31, Axiom 32, Definition 188.
Hence $s_2 = \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-rho}{\rho}((a_0,f_x))$ by Definition 79, Definition 81, Lemma 163.
Therefore $s_1 \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-leq}{\leq} 1$ by Lemma 146.
Then $s_4 \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-leq}{\leq} 1$ by Definition 79, Definition 81, Lemma 163, Lemma 7, Lemma 146.
Hence $s_2 \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-leq}{\leq} M_0$ and $s_3 \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-leq}{\leq} M_0$.
Thus $M_0\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\reals$ by Definition 273, Definition 188.
$M_0 + 1\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\reals$ by Axiom 20.
$(s_1 + s_2) + (s_3 + s_4)\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\reals$ by Axiom 20.
$(M_0 + 1) + (M_0 + 1)\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\reals$ by Axiom 20.
Therefore $\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-rho}{\rho}((x,y))\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\reals$ by Definition 81, Proposition 373.
$\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-rho}{\rho}((x,y)) \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-leq}{\leq} s_1 + s_2 + s_3 + s_4$.
Hence $s_1 + s_2 + s_3 + s_4 = (s_1 + s_2) + (s_3 + s_4)$ by Axiom 22.
Therefore $\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-rho}{\rho}((x,y)) \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-leq}{\leq} (s_1 + s_2) + (s_3 + s_4)$ by Lemma 407.
Then $(s_1 + s_2) + (s_3 + s_4) \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-leq}{\leq} (M_0 + 1) + (M_0 + 1)$ by Lemma 256.
Hence $\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-rho}{\rho}((x,y)) \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-leq}{\leq} (M_0 + 1) + (M_0 + 1)$ by Lemma 407.
Therefore $\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-rho}{\rho}((x,y)) \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-leq}{\leq} M$.

Hence $M\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\reals$ by Definition 273, Definition 188, Axiom 31, Axiom 32, Axiom 20.
Therefore $A$ is bounded in $X$ with respect to $\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-rho}{\rho}$ by Definition 89.

LaTeX source 
\begin{byCase}
            \caseOf{$A = \emptyset$.}
                Let $M = 0$.
                For all $a_1,a_2\in A$ we have $\rho((a_1,a_2)) \leq M$
                    by \cref{subseteq_emptyset_iff,elem_subseteq,emptyset}.
                Thus $A$ is bounded in $X$ with respect to $\rho$ by \cref{real_add_ident,metric_bounded}.
            \caseOf{$A \neq \emptyset$.}
                $A$ is inhabited by \cref{nonempty_inhabited}.
                Let $C = \{\metricBall{\rho}{X}{a}{1} \mid a\in A\}$.
                For all $U$ such that $U\in C$ we have $U$ is open in $X$ with respect to $T$
                    by \cref{real_naturals_subset_reals,real_one_in_naturals,subseteq,real_zero_lt_one,metric_ball,powerset_intro,metric_basis,metric_basis_is_basis,basis_elem_open_in_generated,metric_topology,basis_for_topology,open_in}.
                Hence $C$ is a covering of $A$
                    by \cref{subseteq,covering,square_metric_is_metric,metric_zero_characterization,metric_on,real_zero_lt_one,real_lt_subst_left,metric_ball,unions_iff}.
                $A$ is a subspace of $X$ with respect to $T$ by \cref{subspace_of}.
                Thus there exists $B$ such that
                    $B\subseteq C$ and
                    $B$ is finite and
                    $B$ is a covering of $A$
                    by \cref{compact_subspace_open_covering}.
                Let $R = \{w\in B\times A \mid \exists U\in B.\exists a\in A. w = (U,a) \land U = \metricBall{\rho}{X}{a}{1}\}$.
                Thus $R$ is a relation by \cref{relation}.
                For all $U\in B$ there exists $a$ such that $U\mathrel{R} a$.
                Take $g$ such that $g$ is a function on $B$ and for all $U\in B$ we have $U\mathrel{R}\apply{g}{U}$
                    by \cref{choice_function}.
                Let $F = \img{g}{B}$.
                $F$ is finite by \cref{finite_image_function}.
                Hence $F\subseteq A$ by \cref{subseteq,img_iff,function_apply_intro,subseteq,times_tuple_elim}.
                Take $a_0$ such that $a_0\in A$.
                Thus $a_0\in\unions{B}$ by \cref{covering,subseteq}.
                Take $U_0\in B$ such that $a_0\in U_0$ by \cref{unions_iff}.
                Take $f_0$ such that $f_0\in F$ by \cref{subseteq,function_apply_elim,img_iff}.
                Let $h = \{(f,\rho((a_0,f))) \mid f\in F\}$.
                $h$ is a relation by \cref{relation}.
                For all $x,y_1,y_2$ such that $(x,y_1)\in h$ and $(x,y_2)\in h$ we have $y_1 = y_2$
                    by \cref{pair_eq_iff}.
                Hence $h$ is right-unique by \cref{rightunique}.
                For all $f$ such that $f\in F$ we have $f\in\dom{h}$.
                For all $f$ such that $f\in\dom{h}$ we have $f\in F$ by \cref{dom_iff,pair_eq_iff}.
                Therefore $\dom{h} = F$ by \cref{subseteq,subseteq_antisymmetric}.
                Hence $h$ is a function on $F$.
                Let $D = \img{h}{F}$.
                $D$ is finite by \cref{finite_image_function}.
                For all $r$ such that $r\in D$ we have $r\in\reals$
                    by \cref{img_iff,subseteq,times_tuple_intro,metric_on,funs_type_apply,square_metric_is_metric,pair_eq_iff}.
                Hence $D\subseteq\reals$ by \cref{subseteq}.
                Take $f_1$ such that $f_1\in F$.
                Thus $(f_1,\rho((a_0,f_1)))\in h$.
                Therefore $D\neq\emptyset$ by \cref{img_iff,nonempty_inhabited,inhabited_nonempty}.
                Take $M_0\in D$ such that $M_0$ is a maximum of $D$ by \cref{finite_real_maximum}.
                Let $M = (M_0 + 1) + (M_0 + 1)$.
                Show that for all $x,y\in A$ we have $\rho((x,y)) \leq M$.
                \begin{subproof}
                    Fix $x$.
                    Fix $y$ such that $x\in A$ and $y\in A$.
                    Take $U_x\in B$ such that $x\in U_x$ by \cref{covering,subseteq,unions_iff}.
                    Take $U_y\in B$ such that $y\in U_y$ by \cref{covering,subseteq,unions_iff}.
                    Let $f_x = g(U_x)$.
                    Let $f_y = g(U_y)$.
                    $U_x\mathrel{R} f_x$ by \cref{function_apply_elim}.
                    $U_y\mathrel{R} f_y$ by \cref{function_apply_elim}.
                    Thus $(U_x,f_x)\in R$.
                    Take $U_2, a_2$ such that
                        $U_2\in B$ and $a_2\in A$ and
                        $(U_x,f_x) = (U_2,a_2)$ and $U_2 = \metricBall{\rho}{X}{a_2}{1}$.
                    Thus $U_x = \metricBall{\rho}{X}{f_x}{1}$ by \cref{pair_eq_iff}.
                    Thus $(U_y,f_y)\in R$.
                    Take $U_1, a_1$ such that
                        $U_1\in B$ and $a_1\in A$ and
                        $(U_y,f_y) = (U_1,a_1)$ and $U_1 = \metricBall{\rho}{X}{a_1}{1}$.
                    Thus $U_y = \metricBall{\rho}{X}{f_y}{1}$ by \cref{pair_eq_iff}.
                    Hence $x\in \metricBall{\rho}{X}{f_x}{1}$.
                    Thus $x\in X$ and $\rho((f_x,x)) < 1$ by \cref{metric_ball}.
                    Thus $f_x\in A$ and $f_x\in X$ by \cref{subseteq,times_tuple_elim}.
                    Therefore $\rho((x,f_x)) < 1$
                        by \cref{metric_symmetric,metric_on,square_metric_is_metric,real_lt_subst_left}.
                    Hence $y\in \metricBall{\rho}{X}{f_y}{1}$.
                    Thus $y\in X$ and $\rho((f_y,y)) < 1$ by \cref{metric_ball}.
                    Thus $f_y\in A$ and $f_y\in X$ by \cref{subseteq,times_tuple_elim}.
                    Therefore $\rho((y,f_y)) < 1$
                        by \cref{metric_symmetric,metric_on,square_metric_is_metric,real_lt_subst_left}.
                    Then $f_x\in F$ and $f_y\in F$ by \cref{subseteq,function_apply_elim,img_iff}.
                    Hence $h(f_x)\in D$ and $h(f_y)\in D$ by \cref{subseteq,function_apply_elim,img_iff}.
                    Therefore $h(f_x) \leq M_0$ and $h(f_y) \leq M_0$ by \cref{real_maximum}.
                    Then $\rho((a_0,f_x)) \leq M_0$ and $\rho((a_0,f_y)) \leq M_0$ by \cref{function_apply_intro}.
                    Show that $\rho((x,y)) \leq \rho((x,f_x)) + \rho((f_x,a_0)) + \rho((a_0,f_y)) + \rho((f_y,y))$.
                    \begin{subproof}
                        $x\in X$ and $y\in X$ and $f_x\in X$ and $f_y\in X$ and $a_0\in X$ by \cref{subseteq}.
                        Thus $\rho((x,y)) \leq \rho((x,f_x)) + \rho((f_x,y))$
                            by \cref{square_metric_is_metric,metric_on,metric_triangle_inequality}.
                        Thus $\rho((f_x,y)) \leq \rho((f_x,a_0)) + \rho((a_0,y))$
                            by \cref{square_metric_is_metric,metric_on,metric_triangle_inequality}.
                        Thus $\rho((a_0,y)) \leq \rho((a_0,f_y)) + \rho((f_y,y))$
                            by \cref{square_metric_is_metric,metric_on,metric_triangle_inequality}.
                        Thus $x\in X$ and $f_x\in A$ and $f_x\in X$ and $f_y\in A$ by \cref{elem_subseteq}.
                        Thus $f_y\in X$ and $y\in X$ and $a_0\in A$ and $a_0\in X$ by \cref{elem_subseteq}.
                        $(x,f_x)\in X\times X$ by \cref{times_tuple_intro}.
                        $(f_x,y)\in X\times X$ by \cref{times_tuple_intro}.
                        $(f_x,a_0)\in X\times X$ by \cref{times_tuple_intro}.
                        $(a_0,y)\in X\times X$ by \cref{times_tuple_intro}.
                        $(a_0,f_y)\in X\times X$ by \cref{times_tuple_intro}.
                        $(f_y,y)\in X\times X$ by \cref{times_tuple_intro}.
                        $(x,y)\in X\times X$ by \cref{times_tuple_intro}.
                        Thus $\rho((x,y))\in\reals$ by \cref{metric_on,funs_type_apply}.
                        Thus $\rho((x,f_x))\in\reals$ by \cref{metric_on,funs_type_apply}.
                        Thus $\rho((f_x,y))\in\reals$ by \cref{metric_on,funs_type_apply}.
                        Thus $\rho((f_x,a_0))\in\reals$ by \cref{metric_on,funs_type_apply}.
                        Thus $\rho((a_0,y))\in\reals$ by \cref{metric_on,funs_type_apply}.
