                        Thus $\rho((a_0,f_y))\in\reals$ by \cref{metric_on,funs_type_apply}.
                        Thus $\rho((f_y,y))\in\reals$ by \cref{metric_on,funs_type_apply}.
                        Let $r_1 = \rho((x,f_x))$.
                        Let $r_2 = \rho((f_x,y))$.
                        Let $r_3 = \rho((f_x,a_0))$.
                        Let $r_4 = \rho((a_0,y))$.
                        Thus $r_1\in\reals$ and $r_2\in\reals$ and $r_3\in\reals$ and $r_4\in\reals$.
                        Thus $r_3 + r_4\in\reals$ by \cref{real_add_closed}.
                        Thus $r_1 + r_2\in\reals$ by \cref{real_add_closed}.
                        Thus $r_1 + (r_3 + r_4)\in\reals$ by \cref{real_add_closed}.
                        $r_2 \leq r_3 + r_4$.
                        Hence $r_2 + r_1 \leq (r_3 + r_4) + r_1$ by \cref{real_leq_add}.
                        Then $r_1 + r_2 = r_2 + r_1$ by \cref{real_add_comm}.
                        $(r_3 + r_4) + r_1 = r_1 + (r_3 + r_4)$ by \cref{real_add_comm}.
                        Therefore $r_1 + r_2 \leq (r_3 + r_4) + r_1$ by \cref{real_leq_trans}.
                        Hence $r_1 + r_2 \leq r_1 + (r_3 + r_4)$ by \cref{real_leq_trans}.
                        Then $\rho((x,y)) \leq r_1 + (r_3 + r_4)$ by \cref{real_leq_trans}.
                        Let $u = \rho((a_0,f_y)) + \rho((f_y,y))$.
                        Thus $u\in\reals$ by \cref{real_add_closed}.
                        Thus $r_1 + r_3\in\reals$ by \cref{real_add_closed}.
                        Thus $(r_1 + r_3) + u\in\reals$ by \cref{real_add_closed}.
                        $r_4 \leq u$.
                        Therefore $r_4 + (r_1 + r_3) \leq u + (r_1 + r_3)$ by \cref{real_leq_add}.
                        Thus $(r_1 + r_3) + r_4 = r_4 + (r_1 + r_3)$ by \cref{real_add_comm}.
                        Thus $u + (r_1 + r_3) = (r_1 + r_3) + u$ by \cref{real_add_comm}.
                        Hence $(r_1 + r_3) + r_4 \leq u + (r_1 + r_3)$ by \cref{real_leq_trans}.
                        Then $(r_1 + r_3) + r_4 \leq (r_1 + r_3) + u$ by \cref{real_leq_trans}.
                        $r_1 + (r_3 + r_4) = (r_1 + r_3) + r_4$ by \cref{real_add_assoc}.
                        Therefore $r_1 + (r_3 + r_4) \leq (r_1 + r_3) + u$ by \cref{real_leq_trans}.
                        Hence $\rho((x,y)) \leq (r_1 + r_3) + u$ by \cref{real_leq_trans}.
                        Therefore $\rho((x,y)) \leq \rho((x,f_x)) + \rho((f_x,a_0)) + \rho((a_0,f_y)) + \rho((f_y,y))$
                            by \cref{real_add_assoc,real_add_comm}.
                    \end{subproof}
                    Show that $\rho((x,y)) \leq M$.
                    \begin{subproof}
                        Let $s_1 = \rho((x,f_x))$.
                        Let $s_2 = \rho((f_x,a_0))$.
                        Let $s_3 = \rho((a_0,f_y))$.
                        Let $s_4 = \rho((f_y,y))$.
                        Thus $x\in X$ and $y\in X$ and $f_x\in A$ and $f_x\in X$ and $f_y\in A$ and $f_y\in X$ and $a_0\in A$ and $a_0\in X$
                            by \cref{elem_subseteq}.
                        $(x,y)\in X\times X$ by \cref{times_tuple_intro}.
                        $(x,f_x)\in X\times X$ by \cref{times_tuple_intro}.
                        $(f_x,a_0)\in X\times X$ by \cref{times_tuple_intro}.
                        $(a_0,f_y)\in X\times X$ by \cref{times_tuple_intro}.
                        $(f_y,y)\in X\times X$ by \cref{times_tuple_intro}.
                        Thus $s_1\in\reals$ and $s_2\in\reals$ and $s_3\in\reals$ and $s_4\in\reals$ by \cref{metric_on,funs_type_apply}.
                        $s_1 + s_2\in\reals$ and $s_3 + s_4\in\reals$ by \cref{real_add_closed}.
                        $1\in\reals$ by \cref{real_naturals_subset_reals,real_one_in_naturals,subseteq}.
                        Hence $s_2 = \rho((a_0,f_x))$ by \cref{metric_symmetric,metric_on,square_metric_is_metric}.
                        Therefore $s_1 \leq 1$ by \cref{real_lt_imp_leq}.
                        Then $s_4 \leq 1$ by \cref{metric_symmetric,metric_on,square_metric_is_metric,real_lt_subst_left,real_lt_imp_leq}.
                        Hence $s_2 \leq M_0$ and $s_3 \leq M_0$.
                        Thus $M_0\in\reals$ by \cref{real_maximum,subseteq}.
                        $M_0 + 1\in\reals$ by \cref{real_add_closed}.
                        $(s_1 + s_2) + (s_3 + s_4)\in\reals$ by \cref{real_add_closed}.
                        $(M_0 + 1) + (M_0 + 1)\in\reals$ by \cref{real_add_closed}.
                        Therefore $\rho((x,y))\in\reals$ by \cref{metric_on,funs_type_apply}.
                        $\rho((x,y)) \leq s_1 + s_2 + s_3 + s_4$.
                        Hence $s_1 + s_2 + s_3 + s_4 = (s_1 + s_2) + (s_3 + s_4)$ by \cref{real_add_assoc}.
                        Therefore $\rho((x,y)) \leq (s_1 + s_2) + (s_3 + s_4)$ by \cref{real_leq_trans}.
                        Then $(s_1 + s_2) + (s_3 + s_4) \leq (M_0 + 1) + (M_0 + 1)$ by \cref{real_four_sum_bound}.
                        Hence $\rho((x,y)) \leq (M_0 + 1) + (M_0 + 1)$ by \cref{real_leq_trans}.
                        Therefore $\rho((x,y)) \leq M$.
                    \end{subproof}
                \end{subproof}
                Hence $M\in\reals$ by \cref{real_maximum,subseteq,real_naturals_subset_reals,real_one_in_naturals,real_add_closed}.
                Therefore $A$ is bounded in $X$ with respect to $\rho$ by \cref{metric_bounded}.
            
\end{byCase}

Hence $A$ is bounded in $X$ with respect to $d$ or $A$ is bounded in $X$ with respect to $\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-rho}{\rho}$.

Subclaim: Show that if $A$ is closed in $X$ with respect to $T$ and $A$ is bounded in $X$ with respect to $d$ or $A$ is bounded in $X$ with respect to $\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-rho}{\rho}$ then $A$ is compact with respect to $T_A$.

Assume $A$ is closed in $X$ with respect to $T$ and $A$ is bounded in $X$ with respect to $d$ or $A$ is bounded in $X$ with respect to $\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-rho}{\rho}$.

Case. $A = \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-emptyset}{\emptyset}$.

For all $C$ such that $C$ is an open covering of $A$ with respect to $T_A$ there exists $B$ such that $B\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-subseteq}{\subseteq} C$ and $B$ is finite and $B$ is a covering of $A$ by Lemma 393, Proposition 11, Definition 147.
Therefore $A$ is compact with respect to $T_A$ by Definition 149.

Case. $A \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-neq}{\neq} \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-emptyset}{\emptyset}$.

$A$ is inhabited by Proposition 10.

Subclaim: Show that $A$ is bounded in $X$ with respect to $\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-rho}{\rho}$.
Case. $A$ is bounded in $X$ with respect to $\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-rho}{\rho}$.

Thus $A$ is bounded in $X$ with respect to $\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-rho}{\rho}$.

Case. $A$ is bounded in $X$ with respect to $d$.

Take $M\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\reals$ such that for all $a_1,a_2\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} A$ we have $d((a_1,a_2)) \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-leq}{\leq} M$ by Definition 89.
For all $a_1,a_2\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} A$ we have $\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-rho}{\rho}((a_1,a_2)) \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-leq}{\leq} M$ by Proposition 4, Lemma 160, Lemma 132, Proposition 115, Lemma 136, Lemma 161, Lemma 128, Proposition 358, Lemma 407.
Hence $A$ is bounded in $X$ with respect to $\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-rho}{\rho}$ by Lemma 163, Definition 89.

LaTeX source 
\begin{byCase}
                \caseOf{$A$ is bounded in $X$ with respect to $\rho$.}
                    Thus $A$ is bounded in $X$ with respect to $\rho$.
                \caseOf{$A$ is bounded in $X$ with respect to $d$.}
                    Take $M\in\reals$ such that for all $a_1,a_2\in A$ we have $d((a_1,a_2)) \leq M$
                        by \cref{metric_bounded}.
                    For all $a_1,a_2\in A$ we have $\rho((a_1,a_2)) \leq M$
                        by \cref{elem_subseteq,euclidean_metric_value_exists,square_metric_value_exists,times_tuple_intro,euclidean_square_metric_bounds,euclidean_metric_function,square_metric_function,function_apply_intro,real_leq_trans}.
                    Hence $A$ is bounded in $X$ with respect to $\rho$ by \cref{square_metric_is_metric,metric_bounded}.
                
\end{byCase}

Take $M\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\reals$ such that for all $a_1,a_2\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} A$ we have $\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-rho}{\rho}((a_1,a_2)) \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-leq}{\leq} M$ by Definition 89.
Take $x_0$ such that $x_0\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} A$.
Let $Y = \{(i,\intervalclosed{x_0(i) - M}{x_0(i) + M}) \mid i\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} J\}$.
For all $i,u,v$ such that $(i,u)\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} Y$ and $(i,v)\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} Y$ we have $u = v$.
Hence $Y$ is a function by Definition 202, Definition 241.
Then $\dom{Y} = J$ by Proposition 212, Proposition 211, Axiom 7, Definition 188, Proposition 3.

Subclaim: Show that for all $x$ such that $x\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} A$ we have $x\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} \productFamily{Y}$.

Fix $x$ such that $x\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} A$.

Subclaim: Show that for all $i\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} J$ we have $x(i)\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} \intervalclosed{x_0(i) - M}{x_0(i) + M}$.

Fix $i$ such that $i\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} J$.
Thus $x_0\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\realsPower{J}$ and $x\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\realsPower{J}$ by Proposition 4.
Thus $x_0(i)\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\reals$ and $x(i)\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\reals$ by Definition 93, Definition 68, Proposition 373.
$\neg{x(i)}\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\reals$ by Axiom 25.
$x_0(i) - x(i) = x_0(i) + \neg{x(i)}$ by Definition 263.
Thus $x_0(i) - x(i)\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\reals$ by Axiom 20.
Thus $\abs{x_0(i) - x(i)}\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\reals$ by Lemma 415.
Take $m\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\reals$ such that $((x_0,x),m)\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-rho}{\rho}$ by Lemma 132.
Thus $m\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\coordDiffSet{n}{x_0}{x}$ and for all $t\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\coordDiffSet{n}{x_0}{x}$ we have $t \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-leq}{\leq} m$ by Definition 99, Axiom 7.
Thus $\abs{x_0(i) - x(i)} \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-leq}{\leq} m$ by Definition 98.
Thus $m \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-leq}{\leq} M$ by Lemma 128, Proposition 358, Lemma 407.
Thus $\abs{x_0(i) - x(i)} \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-leq}{\leq} M$ by Lemma 407.
Thus $\abs{x(i) - x_0(i)} \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-leq}{\leq} M$ by Theorem 121, Lemma 407.
Thus $M$ is nonnegative by Theorem 119, Axiom 24, Lemma 407.
Thus $x_0(i) - M \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-leq}{\leq} x(i)$ by Theorem 130.
Thus $x(i) \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-leq}{\leq} x_0(i) + M$ by Theorem 131.
Thus $x(i)\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\intervalclosed{x_0(i) - M}{x_0(i) + M}$ by Definition 275.

Thus $x$ is a function to $\reals$ and $\dom{x} = \dom{Y}$ by Definition 188, Definition 93, Definition 68, Proposition 371.
For all $i\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\dom{Y}$ we have $x(i)\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\unions{\ran{Y}}$ by Definition 188, Proposition 221, Axiom 5.
Hence $x\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\funs{\dom{Y}}{\unions{\ran{Y}}}$ by Proposition 370.
For all $i\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\dom{Y}$ we have $x(i)\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\apply{Y}{i}$ by Proposition 211, Axiom 7, Proposition 358.
Hence $x\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\productFamily{Y}$ by Definition 69.

Hence $A\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-subseteq}{\subseteq} \productFamily{Y}$ by Definition 188.
Let $T_Y = \{(i,\subspaceTopology{\standardTopologyR}{\intervalclosed{x_0(i) - M}{x_0(i) + M}}) \mid i\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} J\}$.
For all $i,u,v$ such that $(i,u)\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} T_Y$ and $(i,v)\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} T_Y$ we have $u = v$.
Therefore $T_Y$ is a function by Definition 202, Definition 241.
Hence $\dom{T_Y} = J$ by Proposition 212, Proposition 211, Axiom 7, Definition 188, Proposition 3.

Subclaim: Show that for all $i\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} J$ we have $\intervalclosed{x_0(i) - M}{x_0(i) + M}$ is compact with respect to $\subspaceTopology{\standardTopologyR}{\intervalclosed{x_0(i) - M}{x_0(i) + M}}$.

Fix $i$ such that $i\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} J$.
Thus $x_0(i)\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\reals$ by Proposition 4, Definition 93, Definition 68, Proposition 373.
$0\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\reals$ by Axiom 24.
$\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-rho}{\rho}$ is a metric on $X$ by Lemma 163.
Hence $\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-rho}{\rho}$ is nonnegative on $X$ by Definition 81.
Thus $x_0\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} X$ by Proposition 4.
$(x_0,x_0)\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} X\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-times}{\times} X$ by Proposition 115.
Thus $\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-rho}{\rho}((x_0,x_0))\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\reals$ by Definition 81, Proposition 373.
Thus $\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-rho}{\rho}((x_0,x_0)) \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-geq}{\geq} 0$ by Definition 77.
Thus $\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-rho}{\rho}((x_0,x_0)) \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-leq}{\leq} M$.
Therefore $0 \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-leq}{\leq} M$ by Lemma 407.
$\neg{M}\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\reals$ and $\neg{0}\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\reals$ by Axiom 25.
$0 + \neg{0} = 0$ and $0 + \neg{0} = \neg{0}$ by Axiom 25, Axiom 24.
Hence $\neg{0} = 0$.
Therefore $\neg{M} \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-leq}{\leq} 0$ by Lemma 406, Lemma 407.
Hence $\neg{M} \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-leq}{\leq} M$ by Lemma 407.
Therefore $\neg{M} + x_0(i) \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-leq}{\leq} M + x_0(i)$ by Lemma 408.
$x_0(i) + \neg{M} = \neg{M} + x_0(i)$ and $x_0(i) + M = M + x_0(i)$ by Axiom 23.
Then $x_0(i) + \neg{M} \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-leq}{\leq} x_0(i) + M$ by Lemma 407.
Thus $x_0(i) + M\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\reals$ by Axiom 20.
$x_0(i) - M = x_0(i) + \neg{M}$ by Definition 263.
Thus $x_0(i) - M\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\reals$ by Axiom 20.
Thus $x_0(i) - M \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-leq}{\leq} x_0(i) + M$ by Definition 263.
Hence $\intervalclosed{x_0(i) - M}{x_0(i) + M}$ is compact with respect to $\subspaceTopology{\standardTopologyR}{\intervalclosed{x_0(i) - M}{x_0(i) + M}}$ by Lemma 257.

For all $i\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} J$ we have $\apply{T_Y}{i}$ is a topology on $\apply{Y}{i}$ by Definition 188, Proposition 358, Lemma 18, Lemma 1, Definition 18, Definition 275, Lemma 51.
For all $i\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} J$ we have $\apply{Y}{i}$ is compact with respect to $\apply{T_Y}{i}$ by Definition 188, Proposition 358.
Therefore $\productFamily{Y}$ is compact with respect to $\productTopologyIndexed{T_Y}{Y}$ by Lemma 262.
Let $T_{P0} = \productTopologyIndexed{T_Y}{Y}$.
Let $T_{Y0} = \subspaceTopology{T}{\productFamily{Y}}$.
Let $X_1 = \{(i,\reals) \mid i\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} J\}$.
Let $T_1 = \{(i,\standardTopologyR) \mid i\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} J\}$.
Let $T_0 = \productTopologyIndexed{T_1}{X_1}$.
Hence $T = T_0$ by Theorem 19, Lemma 263.
$X_1$ is a function and $T_1$ is a function and $\dom{X_1} = J$ and $\dom{T_1} = J$ by Lemma 264.
Therefore $\dom{X_1}$ is inhabited by Axiom 32, Axiom 33, Definition 281, Definition 188.
For all $i\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\dom{X_1}$ we have $\apply{Y}{i}\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-subseteq}{\subseteq}\apply{X_1}{i}$ by Definition 188, Proposition 358, Definition 275, Proposition 6.
For all $i\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\dom{X_1}$ we have $\apply{T_1}{i}$ is a topology on $\apply{X_1}{i}$ by Definition 188, Proposition 358, Lemma 18, Lemma 1, Definition 18.
Hence $\productFamily{Y}$ is a subspace of $\productFamily{X_1}$ with respect to $T$ by Lemma 103.
Let $j = \identity{\productFamily{Y}}$.
Therefore $\dom{Y}$ is inhabited by Axiom 32, Axiom 33, Definition 281, Definition 188.
For all $i\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\dom{Y}$ we have $\apply{T_Y}{i}$ is a topology on $\apply{Y}{i}$ by Definition 188, Proposition 358, Lemma 18, Lemma 1, Definition 18, Definition 275, Lemma 51.
Therefore $T_{P0}$ is a topology on $\productFamily{Y}$ by Lemma 94, Definition 15, Lemma 1.
Then $\productFamily{X_1} = X$ by Lemma 126.
Hence $\productFamily{Y}\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-subseteq}{\subseteq} X$ by Definition 49, Proposition 6.
$j\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\funs{\productFamily{Y}}{X}$ by Proposition 418, Proposition 372.

Subclaim: Show that for all $i\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\dom{X_1}$ we have $\piIndex{X_1}{i}\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-circ}{\circ} j$ is continuous from $\productFamily{Y}$ to $\apply{X_1}{i}$ with respect to $T_{P0}$ and $\apply{T_1}{i}$.

Fix $i$ such that $i\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\dom{X_1}$.
Let $g = \piIndex{Y}{i}$.
Let $k = \identity{\apply{Y}{i}}$.
$k$ is continuous from $\apply{Y}{i}$ to $\apply{X_1}{i}$ with respect to $\apply{T_Y}{i}$ and $\apply{T_1}{i}$ by Definition 188, Definition 49, Proposition 358, Lemma 81.
$g$ is continuous from $\productFamily{Y}$ to $\apply{Y}{i}$ with respect to $T_{P0}$ and $\apply{T_Y}{i}$ by Lemma 258.
Therefore $k\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-circ}{\circ} g$ is continuous from $\productFamily{Y}$ to $\apply{X_1}{i}$ with respect to $T_{P0}$ and $\apply{T_1}{i}$ by Lemma 82.

Subclaim: Show that $k\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-circ}{\circ} g = \piIndex{X_1}{i}\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-circ}{\circ} j$.

For all $y,z$ if $y\mathrel{k\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-circ}{\circ} g} z$ then $y\mathrel{\piIndex{X_1}{i}\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-circ}{\circ} j} z$ by Proposition 269, Proposition 305, Definition 72, Axiom 7, Definition 49, Definition 188.
For all $y,z$ if $y\mathrel{\piIndex{X_1}{i}\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-circ}{\circ} j} z$ then $y\mathrel{k\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-circ}{\circ} g} z$ by Proposition 269, Proposition 305, Definition 72, Axiom 7, Definition 188, Definition 69.
Follows by set extensionality.

Hence $\piIndex{X_1}{i}\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-circ}{\circ} j$ is continuous from $\productFamily{Y}$ to $\apply{X_1}{i}$ with respect to $T_{P0}$ and $\apply{T_1}{i}$ by Lemma 259.

Therefore $j$ is continuous from $\productFamily{Y}$ to $\productFamily{X_1}$ with respect to $T_{P0}$ and $T_0$ by Lemma 110.
Then $j$ is continuous from $\productFamily{Y}$ to $X$ with respect to $T_{P0}$ and $T$ by Lemma 260.

Subclaim: Show that for all $U$ such that $U\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} T_{Y0}$ we have $U\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} T_{P0}$.

Fix $U$ such that $U\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} T_{Y0}$.
Take $V\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} T$ such that $U = \productFamily{Y}\inter V$ by Definition 48.
Let $j_0 = \identity{\productFamily{Y}}$.
Thus $V$ is open in $X$ with respect to $T$ by Definition 62, Definition 3.
Hence $\preimg{j_0}{V}$ is open in $\productFamily{Y}$ with respect to $T_{P0}$ by Definition 62.
Therefore $\preimg{j_0}{V} = \productFamily{Y}\inter V$ by Proposition 258, Proposition 305, Proposition 61, Definition 192, Definition 188, Proposition 3.
Then $U\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} T_{P0}$ by Definition 3.

Hence $T_{Y0}\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-subseteq}{\subseteq} T_{P0}$ by Definition 188.
For all $C$ such that $C$ is an open covering of $\productFamily{Y}$ with respect to $T_{Y0}$ there exists $B$ such that $B\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-subseteq}{\subseteq} C$ and $B$ is finite and $B$ is a covering of $\productFamily{Y}$ by Definition 148, Definition 3, Definition 188, Definition 149.
Therefore $\productFamily{Y}$ is compact with respect to $T_{Y0}$ by Definition 149.
Hence $T$ is a topology on $X$ by Lemma 163, Lemma 111, Lemma 1, Definition 84, Definition 15.
Hence $\productFamily{Y}\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-subseteq}{\subseteq} X$ by Definition 69, Proposition 371, Definition 188, Proposition 358, Definition 275, Proposition 370, Definition 68, Definition 93.
Hence $\productFamily{Y}$ is a subspace of $X$ with respect to $T$ by Definition 49.
Then $A = A\inter\productFamily{Y}$ by Definition 188, Proposition 69.
Hence $A$ is closed in $\productFamily{Y}$ with respect to $T_{Y0}$ by Theorem 3, Definition 49.
Therefore $T$ is a topology on $X$ and $\productFamily{Y}\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-subseteq}{\subseteq} X$ by Definition 49.
Then $T_{Y0}$ is a topology on $\productFamily{Y}$ by Lemma 51.
Hence $A$ is compact with respect to $T_A$ by Theorem 54, Lemma 203.

LaTeX source 
\begin{byCase}
            \caseOf{$A = \emptyset$.}
                For all $C$ such that $C$ is an open covering of $A$ with respect to $T_A$ there exists $B$ such that
                    $B\subseteq C$ and $B$ is finite and $B$ is a covering of $A$
                    by \cref{emptyset_is_finite,emptyset_subseteq,covering}.
            Therefore $A$ is compact with respect to $T_A$ by \cref{compact}.
        \caseOf{$A \neq \emptyset$.}
            $A$ is inhabited by \cref{nonempty_inhabited}.
            Show that $A$ is bounded in $X$ with respect to $\rho$.
            \begin{subproof}
                \begin{byCase}
                \caseOf{$A$ is bounded in $X$ with respect to $\rho$.}
                    Thus $A$ is bounded in $X$ with respect to $\rho$.
                \caseOf{$A$ is bounded in $X$ with respect to $d$.}
                    Take $M\in\reals$ such that for all $a_1,a_2\in A$ we have $d((a_1,a_2)) \leq M$
                        by \cref{metric_bounded}.
                    For all $a_1,a_2\in A$ we have $\rho((a_1,a_2)) \leq M$
                        by \cref{elem_subseteq,euclidean_metric_value_exists,square_metric_value_exists,times_tuple_intro,euclidean_square_metric_bounds,euclidean_metric_function,square_metric_function,function_apply_intro,real_leq_trans}.
                    Hence $A$ is bounded in $X$ with respect to $\rho$ by \cref{square_metric_is_metric,metric_bounded}.
                \end{byCase}
            \end{subproof}
            Take $M\in\reals$ such that for all $a_1,a_2\in A$ we have $\rho((a_1,a_2)) \leq M$
                by \cref{metric_bounded}.
            Take $x_0$ such that $x_0\in A$.
            Let $Y = \{(i,\intervalclosed{x_0(i) - M}{x_0(i) + M}) \mid i\in J\}$.
            For all $i,u,v$ such that $(i,u)\in Y$ and $(i,v)\in Y$ we have $u = v$.
            Hence $Y$ is a function by \cref{relation,rightunique}.
            Then $\dom{Y} = J$ by \cref{dom_intro,dom_iff,pair_eq_iff,subseteq,subseteq_antisymmetric}.
            Show that for all $x$ such that $x\in A$ we have $x\in \productFamily{Y}$.
            \begin{subproof}
                Fix $x$ such that $x\in A$.
                Show that for all $i\in J$ we have $x(i)\in \intervalclosed{x_0(i) - M}{x_0(i) + M}$.
                \begin{subproof}
                    Fix $i$ such that $i\in J$.
                    Thus $x_0\in\realsPower{J}$ and $x\in\realsPower{J}$ by \cref{elem_subseteq}.
                    Thus $x_0(i)\in\reals$ and $x(i)\in\reals$ by \cref{reals_power,tuple_power,funs_type_apply}.
                    $\neg{x(i)}\in\reals$ by \cref{real_add_inverse}.
                    $x_0(i) - x(i) = x_0(i) + \neg{x(i)}$ by \cref{real_sub}.
                    Thus $x_0(i) - x(i)\in\reals$ by \cref{real_add_closed}.
                    Thus $\abs{x_0(i) - x(i)}\in\reals$ by \cref{real_abs_in_reals}.
                    Take $m\in\reals$ such that $((x_0,x),m)\in \rho$ by \cref{square_metric_value_exists}.
                    Thus $m\in\coordDiffSet{n}{x_0}{x}$ and for all $t\in\coordDiffSet{n}{x_0}{x}$ we have $t \leq m$
                        by \cref{square_metric,pair_eq_iff}.
                    Thus $\abs{x_0(i) - x(i)} \leq m$ by \cref{coord_diff_set}.
                    Thus $m \leq M$ by \cref{square_metric_function,function_apply_intro,real_leq_trans}.
                    Thus $\abs{x_0(i) - x(i)} \leq M$ by \cref{real_leq_trans}.
                    Thus $\abs{x(i) - x_0(i)} \leq M$ by \cref{real_abs_sub_swap,real_leq_trans}.
                    Thus $M$ is nonnegative by \cref{real_abs_nonnegative,real_add_ident,real_leq_trans}.
                    Thus $x_0(i) - M \leq x(i)$ by \cref{real_abs_sub_leq_imp_left_bound}.
                    Thus $x(i) \leq x_0(i) + M$ by \cref{real_abs_sub_leq_imp_right_bound}.
                    Thus $x(i)\in\intervalclosed{x_0(i) - M}{x_0(i) + M}$ by \cref{intervalclosed}.
                \end{subproof}
                Thus $x$ is a function to $\reals$ and $\dom{x} = \dom{Y}$ by \cref{subseteq,reals_power,tuple_power,funs_elim}.
                For all $i\in\dom{Y}$ we have $x(i)\in\unions{\ran{Y}}$ by \cref{subseteq,ran_intro,unions_iff}.
                Hence $x\in\funs{\dom{Y}}{\unions{\ran{Y}}}$ by \cref{funs_intro}.
                For all $i\in\dom{Y}$ we have $x(i)\in\apply{Y}{i}$ by \cref{dom_iff,pair_eq_iff,function_apply_intro}.
                Hence $x\in\productFamily{Y}$ by \cref{indexed_product}.
            \end{subproof}
            Hence $A\subseteq \productFamily{Y}$ by \cref{subseteq}.
            Let $T_Y = \{(i,\subspaceTopology{\standardTopologyR}{\intervalclosed{x_0(i) - M}{x_0(i) + M}}) \mid i\in J\}$.
            For all $i,u,v$ such that $(i,u)\in T_Y$ and $(i,v)\in T_Y$ we have $u = v$.
            Therefore $T_Y$ is a function by \cref{relation,rightunique}.
            Hence $\dom{T_Y} = J$ by \cref{dom_intro,dom_iff,pair_eq_iff,subseteq,subseteq_antisymmetric}.
            
            Show that for all $i\in J$ we have $\intervalclosed{x_0(i) - M}{x_0(i) + M}$ is compact
                with respect to $\subspaceTopology{\standardTopologyR}{\intervalclosed{x_0(i) - M}{x_0(i) + M}}$.
            \begin{subproof}
                Fix $i$ such that $i\in J$.
                Thus $x_0(i)\in\reals$ by \cref{elem_subseteq,reals_power,tuple_power,funs_type_apply}.
                $0\in\reals$ by \cref{real_add_ident}.
                $\rho$ is a metric on $X$ by \cref{square_metric_is_metric}.
                Hence $\rho$ is nonnegative on $X$ by \cref{metric_on}.
                Thus $x_0\in X$ by \cref{elem_subseteq}.
                $(x_0,x_0)\in X\times X$ by \cref{times_tuple_intro}.
                Thus $\rho((x_0,x_0))\in\reals$ by \cref{metric_on,funs_type_apply}.
                Thus $\rho((x_0,x_0)) \geq 0$ by \cref{metric_nonnegative}.
                Thus $\rho((x_0,x_0)) \leq M$.
                Therefore $0 \leq M$ by \cref{real_leq_trans}.
                $\neg{M}\in\reals$ and $\neg{0}\in\reals$ by \cref{real_add_inverse}.
                $0 + \neg{0} = 0$ and $0 + \neg{0} = \neg{0}$ by \cref{real_add_inverse,real_add_ident}.
                Hence $\neg{0} = 0$.
                Therefore $\neg{M} \leq 0$ by \cref{real_leq_neg,real_leq_trans}.
                Hence $\neg{M} \leq M$ by \cref{real_leq_trans}.
                Therefore $\neg{M} + x_0(i) \leq M + x_0(i)$ by \cref{real_leq_add}.
                $x_0(i) + \neg{M} = \neg{M} + x_0(i)$ and $x_0(i) + M = M + x_0(i)$ by \cref{real_add_comm}.
                Then $x_0(i) + \neg{M} \leq x_0(i) + M$ by \cref{real_leq_trans}.
                Thus $x_0(i) + M\in\reals$ by \cref{real_add_closed}.
                $x_0(i) - M = x_0(i) + \neg{M}$ by \cref{real_sub}.
                Thus $x_0(i) - M\in\reals$ by \cref{real_add_closed}.
                Thus $x_0(i) - M \leq x_0(i) + M$ by \cref{real_sub}.
                Hence $\intervalclosed{x_0(i) - M}{x_0(i) + M}$ is compact
                    with respect to $\subspaceTopology{\standardTopologyR}{\intervalclosed{x_0(i) - M}{x_0(i) + M}}$
                    by \cref{real_intervalclosed_compact_standard}.
            \end{subproof}
            For all $i\in J$ we have $\apply{T_Y}{i}$ is a topology on $\apply{Y}{i}$
                by \cref{subseteq,function_apply_intro,standard_basis_is_basis,basis_topology_is_topology,standard_topology_reals,intervalclosed,subspace_topology_is_topology}.
            For all $i\in J$ we have $\apply{Y}{i}$ is compact with respect to $\apply{T_Y}{i}$
                by \cref{subseteq,function_apply_intro}.
            Therefore $\productFamily{Y}$ is compact with respect to $\productTopologyIndexed{T_Y}{Y}$ by \cref{compact_indexed_product_initial_segment}.
            Let $T_{P0} = \productTopologyIndexed{T_Y}{Y}$.
            Let $T_{Y0} = \subspaceTopology{T}{\productFamily{Y}}$.
            Let $X_1 = \{(i,\reals) \mid i\in J\}$.
            Let $T_1 = \{(i,\standardTopologyR) \mid i\in J\}$.
            Let $T_0 = \productTopologyIndexed{T_1}{X_1}$.
            Hence $T = T_0$ by \cref{euclidean_metric_product_topology,square_metric_topology_equals_euclidean}.
            $X_1$ is a function and $T_1$ is a function and $\dom{X_1} = J$ and $\dom{T_1} = J$
                by \cref{reals_standard_indexed_families_data}.
            Therefore $\dom{X_1}$ is inhabited by \cref{real_one_in_naturals,real_one_leq_naturalsplus,initial_segment_naturalsplus,subseteq}.
            For all $i\in\dom{X_1}$ we have $\apply{Y}{i}\subseteq\apply{X_1}{i}$
                by \cref{subseteq,function_apply_intro,intervalclosed,subseteq_transitive}.
            For all $i\in\dom{X_1}$ we have $\apply{T_1}{i}$ is a topology on $\apply{X_1}{i}$
                by \cref{subseteq,function_apply_intro,standard_basis_is_basis,basis_topology_is_topology,standard_topology_reals}.
            Hence $\productFamily{Y}$ is a subspace of $\productFamily{X_1}$ with respect to $T$ by \cref{product_subspace_product}.
            Let $j = \identity{\productFamily{Y}}$.
            Therefore $\dom{Y}$ is inhabited by \cref{real_one_in_naturals,real_one_leq_naturalsplus,initial_segment_naturalsplus,subseteq}.
            For all $i\in\dom{Y}$ we have $\apply{T_Y}{i}$ is a topology on $\apply{Y}{i}$
                by \cref{subseteq,function_apply_intro,standard_basis_is_basis,basis_topology_is_topology,standard_topology_reals,intervalclosed,subspace_topology_is_topology}.
            Therefore $T_{P0}$ is a topology on $\productFamily{Y}$
                by \cref{product_subbasis_finite_intersections_basis,basis_for_topology,basis_topology_is_topology}.
            Then $\productFamily{X_1} = X$ by \cref{product_family_reals_power}.
            Hence $\productFamily{Y}\subseteq X$ by \cref{subspace_of,subseteq_transitive}.
            $j\in\funs{\productFamily{Y}}{X}$ by \cref{id_elem_funs,funs_weaken_codom}.
            Show that for all $i\in\dom{X_1}$ we have $\piIndex{X_1}{i}\circ j$ is continuous from $\productFamily{Y}$
                to $\apply{X_1}{i}$ with respect to $T_{P0}$ and $\apply{T_1}{i}$.
            \begin{subproof}
                Fix $i$ such that $i\in\dom{X_1}$.
                Let $g = \piIndex{Y}{i}$.
                Let $k = \identity{\apply{Y}{i}}$.
                $k$ is continuous from $\apply{Y}{i}$ to $\apply{X_1}{i}$ with respect to $\apply{T_Y}{i}$ and $\apply{T_1}{i}$
                    by \cref{subseteq,subspace_of,function_apply_intro,inclusion_continuous}.
                $g$ is continuous from $\productFamily{Y}$ to $\apply{Y}{i}$ with respect to $T_{P0}$ and $\apply{T_Y}{i}$
                    by \cref{projection_map_indexed_continuous}.
                Therefore $k\circ g$ is continuous from $\productFamily{Y}$ to $\apply{X_1}{i}$ with respect to $T_{P0}$ and $\apply{T_1}{i}$
                    by \cref{continuous_composite}.
                Show that $k\circ g = \piIndex{X_1}{i}\circ j$.
                \begin{subproof}
                    For all $y,z$ if $y\mathrel{k\circ g} z$ then $y\mathrel{\piIndex{X_1}{i}\circ j} z$
                        by \cref{circ_iff,id_iff,projection_map_indexed,pair_eq_iff,subspace_of,subseteq}.
                    For all $y,z$ if $y\mathrel{\piIndex{X_1}{i}\circ j} z$ then $y\mathrel{k\circ g} z$
                        by \cref{circ_iff,id_iff,projection_map_indexed,pair_eq_iff,subseteq,indexed_product}.
                    Follows by set extensionality.
                \end{subproof}
                Hence $\piIndex{X_1}{i}\circ j$ is continuous from $\productFamily{Y}$ to $\apply{X_1}{i}$ with respect to $T_{P0}$ and $\apply{T_1}{i}$
                    by \cref{continuous_eq_subst}.
            \end{subproof}
            Therefore $j$ is continuous from $\productFamily{Y}$ to $\productFamily{X_1}$ with respect to $T_{P0}$ and $T_0$
                by \cref{continuous_map_into_indexed_product}.
            Then $j$ is continuous from $\productFamily{Y}$ to $X$ with respect to $T_{P0}$ and $T$ by \cref{continuous_eq_codomain}.
            Show that for all $U$ such that $U\in T_{Y0}$ we have $U\in T_{P0}$.
            \begin{subproof}
                Fix $U$ such that $U\in T_{Y0}$.
                Take $V\in T$ such that $U = \productFamily{Y}\inter V$ by \cref{subspace_topology}.
                Let $j_0 = \identity{\productFamily{Y}}$.
                Thus $V$ is open in $X$ with respect to $T$ by \cref{continuous,open_in}.
                Hence $\preimg{j_0}{V}$ is open in $\productFamily{Y}$ with respect to $T_{P0}$ by \cref{continuous}.
                Therefore $\preimg{j_0}{V} = \productFamily{Y}\inter V$
                    by \cref{preimg_iff,id_iff,inter_intro,inter,subseteq,subseteq_antisymmetric}.
                Then $U\in T_{P0}$ by \cref{open_in}.
            \end{subproof}
            Hence $T_{Y0}\subseteq T_{P0}$ by \cref{subseteq}.
            For all $C$ such that $C$ is an open covering of $\productFamily{Y}$ with respect to $T_{Y0}$ there exists $B$ such that
                $B\subseteq C$ and $B$ is finite and $B$ is a covering of $\productFamily{Y}$
                by \cref{open_covering,open_in,subseteq,compact}.
            Therefore $\productFamily{Y}$ is compact with respect to $T_{Y0}$ by \cref{compact}.
            Hence $T$ is a topology on $X$ by \cref{square_metric_is_metric,metric_basis_is_basis,basis_topology_is_topology,metric_topology,basis_for_topology}.
            Hence $\productFamily{Y}\subseteq X$
                by \cref{indexed_product,funs_elim,subseteq,function_apply_intro,intervalclosed,funs_intro,tuple_power,reals_power}.
            Hence $\productFamily{Y}$ is a subspace of $X$ with respect to $T$ by \cref{subspace_of}.
            Then $A = A\inter\productFamily{Y}$ by \cref{subseteq,inter_absorb_supseteq_right}.
            Hence $A$ is closed in $\productFamily{Y}$ with respect to $T_{Y0}$ by \cref{closed_in_subspace_iff,subspace_of}.
            Therefore $T$ is a topology on $X$ and $\productFamily{Y}\subseteq X$ by \cref{subspace_of}.
            Then $T_{Y0}$ is a topology on $\productFamily{Y}$ by \cref{subspace_topology_is_topology}.
            Hence $A$ is compact with respect to $T_A$ by \cref{closed_subspace_compact,subspace_subspace_topology}.
        
\end{byCase}
LaTeX source 
\begin{proof}
    Let $X_0 = X$.
    Let $d_1 = \rho$.
    Show that if $A$ is compact with respect to $T_A$ then
        $A$ is closed in $X$ with respect to $T$ and
        $A$ is bounded in $X$ with respect to $d$ or $A$ is bounded in $X$ with respect to $\rho$.
    \begin{subproof}
        Assume $A$ is compact with respect to $T_A$.
        $d_1$ is a metric on $X$ by \cref{square_metric_is_metric}.
        $T$ is a topology on $X$ by \cref{basis_topology_is_topology,metric_topology,metric_basis_is_basis,basis_for_topology}.
        Let $T_S = \metricTopology{d_1}{X_0}$.
        Let $X_1 = \{(i,\reals) \mid i\in J\}$.
        Let $T_1 = \{(i,\standardTopologyR) \mid i\in J\}$.
        Let $T_0 = \productTopologyIndexed{T_1}{X_1}$.
        $d$ is a metric on $X_0$ and $d_1$ is a metric on $X_0$ by \cref{euclidean_metric_is_metric,square_metric_is_metric}.
        Hence $J$ is inhabited by \cref{real_one_in_naturals,real_one_leq_naturalsplus,initial_segment_naturalsplus}.
        $X_1$ is a function and $T_1$ is a function and $\dom{X_1} = J$ and $\dom{T_1} = J$
            by \cref{reals_standard_indexed_families_data}.
        For all $\alpha\in\dom{X_1}$ we have $\apply{X_1}{\alpha} = \reals$ and $\apply{T_1}{\alpha} = \standardTopologyR$
            by \cref{subseteq,function_apply_intro}.
        $\realOrderTopology = \standardTopologyR$ and $\realOrderTopology = \basisTopology{\orderBasis{\realOrderRel}{\reals}}{\reals}$
            by \cref{real_order_topology_eq_standard,real_order_topology}.
        $\realOrderRel$ is a simple order relation on $\reals$ by \cref{real_order_relation_simple}.
        $\reals$ has more than one element by \cref{linear_continuum,reals_linear_continuum}.
        Hence $\realOrderTopology$ is the order topology on $\reals$ with respect to $\realOrderRel$ by \cref{order_topology}.
        Therefore for all $\alpha\in\dom{X_1}$ we have $\apply{X_1}{\alpha}$ is Hausdorff with respect to $\apply{T_1}{\alpha}$
            by \cref{order_topology_hausdorff,real_order_topology_eq_standard}.
        Hence $X_0$ is Hausdorff with respect to $T_0$ by \cref{product_hausdorff_product,product_family_reals_power}.
        Therefore $T_S = T_0$ by \cref{square_metric_topology_equals_euclidean,euclidean_metric_product_topology}.
        Then $X$ is Hausdorff with respect to $T$.
        Therefore $A$ is closed in $X$ with respect to $T$ by \cref{compact_subspace_closed}.
        Show that $A$ is bounded in $X$ with respect to $\rho$.
        \begin{subproof}
            \begin{byCase}
            \caseOf{$A = \emptyset$.}
                Let $M = 0$.
                For all $a_1,a_2\in A$ we have $\rho((a_1,a_2)) \leq M$
                    by \cref{subseteq_emptyset_iff,elem_subseteq,emptyset}.
                Thus $A$ is bounded in $X$ with respect to $\rho$ by \cref{real_add_ident,metric_bounded}.
            \caseOf{$A \neq \emptyset$.}
                $A$ is inhabited by \cref{nonempty_inhabited}.
                Let $C = \{\metricBall{\rho}{X}{a}{1} \mid a\in A\}$.
                For all $U$ such that $U\in C$ we have $U$ is open in $X$ with respect to $T$
                    by \cref{real_naturals_subset_reals,real_one_in_naturals,subseteq,real_zero_lt_one,metric_ball,powerset_intro,metric_basis,metric_basis_is_basis,basis_elem_open_in_generated,metric_topology,basis_for_topology,open_in}.
                Hence $C$ is a covering of $A$
                    by \cref{subseteq,covering,square_metric_is_metric,metric_zero_characterization,metric_on,real_zero_lt_one,real_lt_subst_left,metric_ball,unions_iff}.
                $A$ is a subspace of $X$ with respect to $T$ by \cref{subspace_of}.
                Thus there exists $B$ such that
                    $B\subseteq C$ and
                    $B$ is finite and
                    $B$ is a covering of $A$
                    by \cref{compact_subspace_open_covering}.
                Let $R = \{w\in B\times A \mid \exists U\in B.\exists a\in A. w = (U,a) \land U = \metricBall{\rho}{X}{a}{1}\}$.
                Thus $R$ is a relation by \cref{relation}.
                For all $U\in B$ there exists $a$ such that $U\mathrel{R} a$.
                Take $g$ such that $g$ is a function on $B$ and for all $U\in B$ we have $U\mathrel{R}\apply{g}{U}$
                    by \cref{choice_function}.
                Let $F = \img{g}{B}$.
                $F$ is finite by \cref{finite_image_function}.
                Hence $F\subseteq A$ by \cref{subseteq,img_iff,function_apply_intro,subseteq,times_tuple_elim}.
                Take $a_0$ such that $a_0\in A$.
                Thus $a_0\in\unions{B}$ by \cref{covering,subseteq}.
                Take $U_0\in B$ such that $a_0\in U_0$ by \cref{unions_iff}.
                Take $f_0$ such that $f_0\in F$ by \cref{subseteq,function_apply_elim,img_iff}.
                Let $h = \{(f,\rho((a_0,f))) \mid f\in F\}$.
                $h$ is a relation by \cref{relation}.
                For all $x,y_1,y_2$ such that $(x,y_1)\in h$ and $(x,y_2)\in h$ we have $y_1 = y_2$
                    by \cref{pair_eq_iff}.
                Hence $h$ is right-unique by \cref{rightunique}.
                For all $f$ such that $f\in F$ we have $f\in\dom{h}$.
                For all $f$ such that $f\in\dom{h}$ we have $f\in F$ by \cref{dom_iff,pair_eq_iff}.
                Therefore $\dom{h} = F$ by \cref{subseteq,subseteq_antisymmetric}.
                Hence $h$ is a function on $F$.
                Let $D = \img{h}{F}$.
                $D$ is finite by \cref{finite_image_function}.
                For all $r$ such that $r\in D$ we have $r\in\reals$
                    by \cref{img_iff,subseteq,times_tuple_intro,metric_on,funs_type_apply,square_metric_is_metric,pair_eq_iff}.
                Hence $D\subseteq\reals$ by \cref{subseteq}.
                Take $f_1$ such that $f_1\in F$.
                Thus $(f_1,\rho((a_0,f_1)))\in h$.
                Therefore $D\neq\emptyset$ by \cref{img_iff,nonempty_inhabited,inhabited_nonempty}.
                Take $M_0\in D$ such that $M_0$ is a maximum of $D$ by \cref{finite_real_maximum}.
                Let $M = (M_0 + 1) + (M_0 + 1)$.
                Show that for all $x,y\in A$ we have $\rho((x,y)) \leq M$.
                \begin{subproof}
                    Fix $x$.
                    Fix $y$ such that $x\in A$ and $y\in A$.
                    Take $U_x\in B$ such that $x\in U_x$ by \cref{covering,subseteq,unions_iff}.
                    Take $U_y\in B$ such that $y\in U_y$ by \cref{covering,subseteq,unions_iff}.
                    Let $f_x = g(U_x)$.
                    Let $f_y = g(U_y)$.
                    $U_x\mathrel{R} f_x$ by \cref{function_apply_elim}.
                    $U_y\mathrel{R} f_y$ by \cref{function_apply_elim}.
                    Thus $(U_x,f_x)\in R$.
                    Take $U_2, a_2$ such that
                        $U_2\in B$ and $a_2\in A$ and
                        $(U_x,f_x) = (U_2,a_2)$ and $U_2 = \metricBall{\rho}{X}{a_2}{1}$.
                    Thus $U_x = \metricBall{\rho}{X}{f_x}{1}$ by \cref{pair_eq_iff}.
                    Thus $(U_y,f_y)\in R$.
                    Take $U_1, a_1$ such that
                        $U_1\in B$ and $a_1\in A$ and
                        $(U_y,f_y) = (U_1,a_1)$ and $U_1 = \metricBall{\rho}{X}{a_1}{1}$.
                    Thus $U_y = \metricBall{\rho}{X}{f_y}{1}$ by \cref{pair_eq_iff}.
                    Hence $x\in \metricBall{\rho}{X}{f_x}{1}$.
                    Thus $x\in X$ and $\rho((f_x,x)) < 1$ by \cref{metric_ball}.
                    Thus $f_x\in A$ and $f_x\in X$ by \cref{subseteq,times_tuple_elim}.
                    Therefore $\rho((x,f_x)) < 1$
                        by \cref{metric_symmetric,metric_on,square_metric_is_metric,real_lt_subst_left}.
                    Hence $y\in \metricBall{\rho}{X}{f_y}{1}$.
                    Thus $y\in X$ and $\rho((f_y,y)) < 1$ by \cref{metric_ball}.
                    Thus $f_y\in A$ and $f_y\in X$ by \cref{subseteq,times_tuple_elim}.
                    Therefore $\rho((y,f_y)) < 1$
                        by \cref{metric_symmetric,metric_on,square_metric_is_metric,real_lt_subst_left}.
                    Then $f_x\in F$ and $f_y\in F$ by \cref{subseteq,function_apply_elim,img_iff}.
                    Hence $h(f_x)\in D$ and $h(f_y)\in D$ by \cref{subseteq,function_apply_elim,img_iff}.
                    Therefore $h(f_x) \leq M_0$ and $h(f_y) \leq M_0$ by \cref{real_maximum}.
                    Then $\rho((a_0,f_x)) \leq M_0$ and $\rho((a_0,f_y)) \leq M_0$ by \cref{function_apply_intro}.
                    Show that $\rho((x,y)) \leq \rho((x,f_x)) + \rho((f_x,a_0)) + \rho((a_0,f_y)) + \rho((f_y,y))$.
                    \begin{subproof}
                        $x\in X$ and $y\in X$ and $f_x\in X$ and $f_y\in X$ and $a_0\in X$ by \cref{subseteq}.
                        Thus $\rho((x,y)) \leq \rho((x,f_x)) + \rho((f_x,y))$
                            by \cref{square_metric_is_metric,metric_on,metric_triangle_inequality}.
                        Thus $\rho((f_x,y)) \leq \rho((f_x,a_0)) + \rho((a_0,y))$
                            by \cref{square_metric_is_metric,metric_on,metric_triangle_inequality}.
                        Thus $\rho((a_0,y)) \leq \rho((a_0,f_y)) + \rho((f_y,y))$
                            by \cref{square_metric_is_metric,metric_on,metric_triangle_inequality}.
                        Thus $x\in X$ and $f_x\in A$ and $f_x\in X$ and $f_y\in A$ by \cref{elem_subseteq}.
                        Thus $f_y\in X$ and $y\in X$ and $a_0\in A$ and $a_0\in X$ by \cref{elem_subseteq}.
                        $(x,f_x)\in X\times X$ by \cref{times_tuple_intro}.
                        $(f_x,y)\in X\times X$ by \cref{times_tuple_intro}.
                        $(f_x,a_0)\in X\times X$ by \cref{times_tuple_intro}.
                        $(a_0,y)\in X\times X$ by \cref{times_tuple_intro}.
                        $(a_0,f_y)\in X\times X$ by \cref{times_tuple_intro}.
                        $(f_y,y)\in X\times X$ by \cref{times_tuple_intro}.
                        $(x,y)\in X\times X$ by \cref{times_tuple_intro}.
                        Thus $\rho((x,y))\in\reals$ by \cref{metric_on,funs_type_apply}.
                        Thus $\rho((x,f_x))\in\reals$ by \cref{metric_on,funs_type_apply}.
                        Thus $\rho((f_x,y))\in\reals$ by \cref{metric_on,funs_type_apply}.
                        Thus $\rho((f_x,a_0))\in\reals$ by \cref{metric_on,funs_type_apply}.
                        Thus $\rho((a_0,y))\in\reals$ by \cref{metric_on,funs_type_apply}.
                        Thus $\rho((a_0,f_y))\in\reals$ by \cref{metric_on,funs_type_apply}.
                        Thus $\rho((f_y,y))\in\reals$ by \cref{metric_on,funs_type_apply}.
                        Let $r_1 = \rho((x,f_x))$.
                        Let $r_2 = \rho((f_x,y))$.
                        Let $r_3 = \rho((f_x,a_0))$.
                        Let $r_4 = \rho((a_0,y))$.
                        Thus $r_1\in\reals$ and $r_2\in\reals$ and $r_3\in\reals$ and $r_4\in\reals$.
                        Thus $r_3 + r_4\in\reals$ by \cref{real_add_closed}.
                        Thus $r_1 + r_2\in\reals$ by \cref{real_add_closed}.
                        Thus $r_1 + (r_3 + r_4)\in\reals$ by \cref{real_add_closed}.
                        $r_2 \leq r_3 + r_4$.
                        Hence $r_2 + r_1 \leq (r_3 + r_4) + r_1$ by \cref{real_leq_add}.
                        Then $r_1 + r_2 = r_2 + r_1$ by \cref{real_add_comm}.
                        $(r_3 + r_4) + r_1 = r_1 + (r_3 + r_4)$ by \cref{real_add_comm}.
                        Therefore $r_1 + r_2 \leq (r_3 + r_4) + r_1$ by \cref{real_leq_trans}.
                        Hence $r_1 + r_2 \leq r_1 + (r_3 + r_4)$ by \cref{real_leq_trans}.
                        Then $\rho((x,y)) \leq r_1 + (r_3 + r_4)$ by \cref{real_leq_trans}.
                        Let $u = \rho((a_0,f_y)) + \rho((f_y,y))$.
                        Thus $u\in\reals$ by \cref{real_add_closed}.
                        Thus $r_1 + r_3\in\reals$ by \cref{real_add_closed}.
                        Thus $(r_1 + r_3) + u\in\reals$ by \cref{real_add_closed}.
                        $r_4 \leq u$.
                        Therefore $r_4 + (r_1 + r_3) \leq u + (r_1 + r_3)$ by \cref{real_leq_add}.
                        Thus $(r_1 + r_3) + r_4 = r_4 + (r_1 + r_3)$ by \cref{real_add_comm}.
                        Thus $u + (r_1 + r_3) = (r_1 + r_3) + u$ by \cref{real_add_comm}.
                        Hence $(r_1 + r_3) + r_4 \leq u + (r_1 + r_3)$ by \cref{real_leq_trans}.
                        Then $(r_1 + r_3) + r_4 \leq (r_1 + r_3) + u$ by \cref{real_leq_trans}.
                        $r_1 + (r_3 + r_4) = (r_1 + r_3) + r_4$ by \cref{real_add_assoc}.
                        Therefore $r_1 + (r_3 + r_4) \leq (r_1 + r_3) + u$ by \cref{real_leq_trans}.
                        Hence $\rho((x,y)) \leq (r_1 + r_3) + u$ by \cref{real_leq_trans}.
                        Therefore $\rho((x,y)) \leq \rho((x,f_x)) + \rho((f_x,a_0)) + \rho((a_0,f_y)) + \rho((f_y,y))$
                            by \cref{real_add_assoc,real_add_comm}.
                    \end{subproof}
                    Show that $\rho((x,y)) \leq M$.
                    \begin{subproof}
                        Let $s_1 = \rho((x,f_x))$.
                        Let $s_2 = \rho((f_x,a_0))$.
                        Let $s_3 = \rho((a_0,f_y))$.
                        Let $s_4 = \rho((f_y,y))$.
                        Thus $x\in X$ and $y\in X$ and $f_x\in A$ and $f_x\in X$ and $f_y\in A$ and $f_y\in X$ and $a_0\in A$ and $a_0\in X$
                            by \cref{elem_subseteq}.
                        $(x,y)\in X\times X$ by \cref{times_tuple_intro}.
                        $(x,f_x)\in X\times X$ by \cref{times_tuple_intro}.
                        $(f_x,a_0)\in X\times X$ by \cref{times_tuple_intro}.
                        $(a_0,f_y)\in X\times X$ by \cref{times_tuple_intro}.
                        $(f_y,y)\in X\times X$ by \cref{times_tuple_intro}.
                        Thus $s_1\in\reals$ and $s_2\in\reals$ and $s_3\in\reals$ and $s_4\in\reals$ by \cref{metric_on,funs_type_apply}.
                        $s_1 + s_2\in\reals$ and $s_3 + s_4\in\reals$ by \cref{real_add_closed}.
                        $1\in\reals$ by \cref{real_naturals_subset_reals,real_one_in_naturals,subseteq}.
                        Hence $s_2 = \rho((a_0,f_x))$ by \cref{metric_symmetric,metric_on,square_metric_is_metric}.
                        Therefore $s_1 \leq 1$ by \cref{real_lt_imp_leq}.
                        Then $s_4 \leq 1$ by \cref{metric_symmetric,metric_on,square_metric_is_metric,real_lt_subst_left,real_lt_imp_leq}.
                        Hence $s_2 \leq M_0$ and $s_3 \leq M_0$.
                        Thus $M_0\in\reals$ by \cref{real_maximum,subseteq}.
                        $M_0 + 1\in\reals$ by \cref{real_add_closed}.
                        $(s_1 + s_2) + (s_3 + s_4)\in\reals$ by \cref{real_add_closed}.
                        $(M_0 + 1) + (M_0 + 1)\in\reals$ by \cref{real_add_closed}.
                        Therefore $\rho((x,y))\in\reals$ by \cref{metric_on,funs_type_apply}.
                        $\rho((x,y)) \leq s_1 + s_2 + s_3 + s_4$.
                        Hence $s_1 + s_2 + s_3 + s_4 = (s_1 + s_2) + (s_3 + s_4)$ by \cref{real_add_assoc}.
                        Therefore $\rho((x,y)) \leq (s_1 + s_2) + (s_3 + s_4)$ by \cref{real_leq_trans}.
                        Then $(s_1 + s_2) + (s_3 + s_4) \leq (M_0 + 1) + (M_0 + 1)$ by \cref{real_four_sum_bound}.
                        Hence $\rho((x,y)) \leq (M_0 + 1) + (M_0 + 1)$ by \cref{real_leq_trans}.
                        Therefore $\rho((x,y)) \leq M$.
                    \end{subproof}
                \end{subproof}
                Hence $M\in\reals$ by \cref{real_maximum,subseteq,real_naturals_subset_reals,real_one_in_naturals,real_add_closed}.
                Therefore $A$ is bounded in $X$ with respect to $\rho$ by \cref{metric_bounded}.
            \end{byCase}
        \end{subproof}
        Hence $A$ is bounded in $X$ with respect to $d$ or $A$ is bounded in $X$ with respect to $\rho$.
    \end{subproof}
    Show that if
        $A$ is closed in $X$ with respect to $T$ and
        $A$ is bounded in $X$ with respect to $d$ or $A$ is bounded in $X$ with respect to $\rho$
        then $A$ is compact with respect to $T_A$.
    \begin{subproof}
        Assume $A$ is closed in $X$ with respect to $T$ and
            $A$ is bounded in $X$ with respect to $d$ or $A$ is bounded in $X$ with respect to $\rho$.
        \begin{byCase}
            \caseOf{$A = \emptyset$.}
                For all $C$ such that $C$ is an open covering of $A$ with respect to $T_A$ there exists $B$ such that
                    $B\subseteq C$ and $B$ is finite and $B$ is a covering of $A$
                    by \cref{emptyset_is_finite,emptyset_subseteq,covering}.
            Therefore $A$ is compact with respect to $T_A$ by \cref{compact}.
        \caseOf{$A \neq \emptyset$.}
            $A$ is inhabited by \cref{nonempty_inhabited}.
            Show that $A$ is bounded in $X$ with respect to $\rho$.
            \begin{subproof}
                \begin{byCase}
                \caseOf{$A$ is bounded in $X$ with respect to $\rho$.}
                    Thus $A$ is bounded in $X$ with respect to $\rho$.
                \caseOf{$A$ is bounded in $X$ with respect to $d$.}
                    Take $M\in\reals$ such that for all $a_1,a_2\in A$ we have $d((a_1,a_2)) \leq M$
                        by \cref{metric_bounded}.
                    For all $a_1,a_2\in A$ we have $\rho((a_1,a_2)) \leq M$
                        by \cref{elem_subseteq,euclidean_metric_value_exists,square_metric_value_exists,times_tuple_intro,euclidean_square_metric_bounds,euclidean_metric_function,square_metric_function,function_apply_intro,real_leq_trans}.
                    Hence $A$ is bounded in $X$ with respect to $\rho$ by \cref{square_metric_is_metric,metric_bounded}.
                \end{byCase}
            \end{subproof}
            Take $M\in\reals$ such that for all $a_1,a_2\in A$ we have $\rho((a_1,a_2)) \leq M$
                by \cref{metric_bounded}.
            Take $x_0$ such that $x_0\in A$.
            Let $Y = \{(i,\intervalclosed{x_0(i) - M}{x_0(i) + M}) \mid i\in J\}$.
            For all $i,u,v$ such that $(i,u)\in Y$ and $(i,v)\in Y$ we have $u = v$.
            Hence $Y$ is a function by \cref{relation,rightunique}.
            Then $\dom{Y} = J$ by \cref{dom_intro,dom_iff,pair_eq_iff,subseteq,subseteq_antisymmetric}.
            Show that for all $x$ such that $x\in A$ we have $x\in \productFamily{Y}$.
            \begin{subproof}
                Fix $x$ such that $x\in A$.
                Show that for all $i\in J$ we have $x(i)\in \intervalclosed{x_0(i) - M}{x_0(i) + M}$.
                \begin{subproof}
                    Fix $i$ such that $i\in J$.
                    Thus $x_0\in\realsPower{J}$ and $x\in\realsPower{J}$ by \cref{elem_subseteq}.
                    Thus $x_0(i)\in\reals$ and $x(i)\in\reals$ by \cref{reals_power,tuple_power,funs_type_apply}.
                    $\neg{x(i)}\in\reals$ by \cref{real_add_inverse}.
                    $x_0(i) - x(i) = x_0(i) + \neg{x(i)}$ by \cref{real_sub}.
                    Thus $x_0(i) - x(i)\in\reals$ by \cref{real_add_closed}.
                    Thus $\abs{x_0(i) - x(i)}\in\reals$ by \cref{real_abs_in_reals}.
                    Take $m\in\reals$ such that $((x_0,x),m)\in \rho$ by \cref{square_metric_value_exists}.
                    Thus $m\in\coordDiffSet{n}{x_0}{x}$ and for all $t\in\coordDiffSet{n}{x_0}{x}$ we have $t \leq m$
                        by \cref{square_metric,pair_eq_iff}.
                    Thus $\abs{x_0(i) - x(i)} \leq m$ by \cref{coord_diff_set}.
                    Thus $m \leq M$ by \cref{square_metric_function,function_apply_intro,real_leq_trans}.
                    Thus $\abs{x_0(i) - x(i)} \leq M$ by \cref{real_leq_trans}.
                    Thus $\abs{x(i) - x_0(i)} \leq M$ by \cref{real_abs_sub_swap,real_leq_trans}.
                    Thus $M$ is nonnegative by \cref{real_abs_nonnegative,real_add_ident,real_leq_trans}.
                    Thus $x_0(i) - M \leq x(i)$ by \cref{real_abs_sub_leq_imp_left_bound}.
                    Thus $x(i) \leq x_0(i) + M$ by \cref{real_abs_sub_leq_imp_right_bound}.
                    Thus $x(i)\in\intervalclosed{x_0(i) - M}{x_0(i) + M}$ by \cref{intervalclosed}.
                \end{subproof}
                Thus $x$ is a function to $\reals$ and $\dom{x} = \dom{Y}$ by \cref{subseteq,reals_power,tuple_power,funs_elim}.
                For all $i\in\dom{Y}$ we have $x(i)\in\unions{\ran{Y}}$ by \cref{subseteq,ran_intro,unions_iff}.
                Hence $x\in\funs{\dom{Y}}{\unions{\ran{Y}}}$ by \cref{funs_intro}.
                For all $i\in\dom{Y}$ we have $x(i)\in\apply{Y}{i}$ by \cref{dom_iff,pair_eq_iff,function_apply_intro}.
                Hence $x\in\productFamily{Y}$ by \cref{indexed_product}.
            \end{subproof}
            Hence $A\subseteq \productFamily{Y}$ by \cref{subseteq}.
            Let $T_Y = \{(i,\subspaceTopology{\standardTopologyR}{\intervalclosed{x_0(i) - M}{x_0(i) + M}}) \mid i\in J\}$.
            For all $i,u,v$ such that $(i,u)\in T_Y$ and $(i,v)\in T_Y$ we have $u = v$.
            Therefore $T_Y$ is a function by \cref{relation,rightunique}.
            Hence $\dom{T_Y} = J$ by \cref{dom_intro,dom_iff,pair_eq_iff,subseteq,subseteq_antisymmetric}.
            
            Show that for all $i\in J$ we have $\intervalclosed{x_0(i) - M}{x_0(i) + M}$ is compact
                with respect to $\subspaceTopology{\standardTopologyR}{\intervalclosed{x_0(i) - M}{x_0(i) + M}}$.
            \begin{subproof}
                Fix $i$ such that $i\in J$.
                Thus $x_0(i)\in\reals$ by \cref{elem_subseteq,reals_power,tuple_power,funs_type_apply}.
                $0\in\reals$ by \cref{real_add_ident}.
                $\rho$ is a metric on $X$ by \cref{square_metric_is_metric}.
                Hence $\rho$ is nonnegative on $X$ by \cref{metric_on}.
                Thus $x_0\in X$ by \cref{elem_subseteq}.
                $(x_0,x_0)\in X\times X$ by \cref{times_tuple_intro}.
                Thus $\rho((x_0,x_0))\in\reals$ by \cref{metric_on,funs_type_apply}.
                Thus $\rho((x_0,x_0)) \geq 0$ by \cref{metric_nonnegative}.
                Thus $\rho((x_0,x_0)) \leq M$.
                Therefore $0 \leq M$ by \cref{real_leq_trans}.
                $\neg{M}\in\reals$ and $\neg{0}\in\reals$ by \cref{real_add_inverse}.
                $0 + \neg{0} = 0$ and $0 + \neg{0} = \neg{0}$ by \cref{real_add_inverse,real_add_ident}.
                Hence $\neg{0} = 0$.
                Therefore $\neg{M} \leq 0$ by \cref{real_leq_neg,real_leq_trans}.
                Hence $\neg{M} \leq M$ by \cref{real_leq_trans}.
                Therefore $\neg{M} + x_0(i) \leq M + x_0(i)$ by \cref{real_leq_add}.
                $x_0(i) + \neg{M} = \neg{M} + x_0(i)$ and $x_0(i) + M = M + x_0(i)$ by \cref{real_add_comm}.
                Then $x_0(i) + \neg{M} \leq x_0(i) + M$ by \cref{real_leq_trans}.
                Thus $x_0(i) + M\in\reals$ by \cref{real_add_closed}.
                $x_0(i) - M = x_0(i) + \neg{M}$ by \cref{real_sub}.
                Thus $x_0(i) - M\in\reals$ by \cref{real_add_closed}.
                Thus $x_0(i) - M \leq x_0(i) + M$ by \cref{real_sub}.
                Hence $\intervalclosed{x_0(i) - M}{x_0(i) + M}$ is compact
                    with respect to $\subspaceTopology{\standardTopologyR}{\intervalclosed{x_0(i) - M}{x_0(i) + M}}$
                    by \cref{real_intervalclosed_compact_standard}.
            \end{subproof}
            For all $i\in J$ we have $\apply{T_Y}{i}$ is a topology on $\apply{Y}{i}$
                by \cref{subseteq,function_apply_intro,standard_basis_is_basis,basis_topology_is_topology,standard_topology_reals,intervalclosed,subspace_topology_is_topology}.
            For all $i\in J$ we have $\apply{Y}{i}$ is compact with respect to $\apply{T_Y}{i}$
                by \cref{subseteq,function_apply_intro}.
            Therefore $\productFamily{Y}$ is compact with respect to $\productTopologyIndexed{T_Y}{Y}$ by \cref{compact_indexed_product_initial_segment}.
            Let $T_{P0} = \productTopologyIndexed{T_Y}{Y}$.
            Let $T_{Y0} = \subspaceTopology{T}{\productFamily{Y}}$.
            Let $X_1 = \{(i,\reals) \mid i\in J\}$.
            Let $T_1 = \{(i,\standardTopologyR) \mid i\in J\}$.
            Let $T_0 = \productTopologyIndexed{T_1}{X_1}$.
            Hence $T = T_0$ by \cref{euclidean_metric_product_topology,square_metric_topology_equals_euclidean}.
            $X_1$ is a function and $T_1$ is a function and $\dom{X_1} = J$ and $\dom{T_1} = J$
                by \cref{reals_standard_indexed_families_data}.
            Therefore $\dom{X_1}$ is inhabited by \cref{real_one_in_naturals,real_one_leq_naturalsplus,initial_segment_naturalsplus,subseteq}.
            For all $i\in\dom{X_1}$ we have $\apply{Y}{i}\subseteq\apply{X_1}{i}$
                by \cref{subseteq,function_apply_intro,intervalclosed,subseteq_transitive}.
            For all $i\in\dom{X_1}$ we have $\apply{T_1}{i}$ is a topology on $\apply{X_1}{i}$
                by \cref{subseteq,function_apply_intro,standard_basis_is_basis,basis_topology_is_topology,standard_topology_reals}.
            Hence $\productFamily{Y}$ is a subspace of $\productFamily{X_1}$ with respect to $T$ by \cref{product_subspace_product}.
            Let $j = \identity{\productFamily{Y}}$.
            Therefore $\dom{Y}$ is inhabited by \cref{real_one_in_naturals,real_one_leq_naturalsplus,initial_segment_naturalsplus,subseteq}.
            For all $i\in\dom{Y}$ we have $\apply{T_Y}{i}$ is a topology on $\apply{Y}{i}$
                by \cref{subseteq,function_apply_intro,standard_basis_is_basis,basis_topology_is_topology,standard_topology_reals,intervalclosed,subspace_topology_is_topology}.
            Therefore $T_{P0}$ is a topology on $\productFamily{Y}$
                by \cref{product_subbasis_finite_intersections_basis,basis_for_topology,basis_topology_is_topology}.
            Then $\productFamily{X_1} = X$ by \cref{product_family_reals_power}.
            Hence $\productFamily{Y}\subseteq X$ by \cref{subspace_of,subseteq_transitive}.
            $j\in\funs{\productFamily{Y}}{X}$ by \cref{id_elem_funs,funs_weaken_codom}.
            Show that for all $i\in\dom{X_1}$ we have $\piIndex{X_1}{i}\circ j$ is continuous from $\productFamily{Y}$
                to $\apply{X_1}{i}$ with respect to $T_{P0}$ and $\apply{T_1}{i}$.
            \begin{subproof}
                Fix $i$ such that $i\in\dom{X_1}$.
                Let $g = \piIndex{Y}{i}$.
                Let $k = \identity{\apply{Y}{i}}$.
                $k$ is continuous from $\apply{Y}{i}$ to $\apply{X_1}{i}$ with respect to $\apply{T_Y}{i}$ and $\apply{T_1}{i}$
                    by \cref{subseteq,subspace_of,function_apply_intro,inclusion_continuous}.
                $g$ is continuous from $\productFamily{Y}$ to $\apply{Y}{i}$ with respect to $T_{P0}$ and $\apply{T_Y}{i}$
                    by \cref{projection_map_indexed_continuous}.
                Therefore $k\circ g$ is continuous from $\productFamily{Y}$ to $\apply{X_1}{i}$ with respect to $T_{P0}$ and $\apply{T_1}{i}$
                    by \cref{continuous_composite}.
                Show that $k\circ g = \piIndex{X_1}{i}\circ j$.
                \begin{subproof}
                    For all $y,z$ if $y\mathrel{k\circ g} z$ then $y\mathrel{\piIndex{X_1}{i}\circ j} z$
                        by \cref{circ_iff,id_iff,projection_map_indexed,pair_eq_iff,subspace_of,subseteq}.
                    For all $y,z$ if $y\mathrel{\piIndex{X_1}{i}\circ j} z$ then $y\mathrel{k\circ g} z$
                        by \cref{circ_iff,id_iff,projection_map_indexed,pair_eq_iff,subseteq,indexed_product}.
                    Follows by set extensionality.
                \end{subproof}
                Hence $\piIndex{X_1}{i}\circ j$ is continuous from $\productFamily{Y}$ to $\apply{X_1}{i}$ with respect to $T_{P0}$ and $\apply{T_1}{i}$
                    by \cref{continuous_eq_subst}.
            \end{subproof}
            Therefore $j$ is continuous from $\productFamily{Y}$ to $\productFamily{X_1}$ with respect to $T_{P0}$ and $T_0$
                by \cref{continuous_map_into_indexed_product}.
            Then $j$ is continuous from $\productFamily{Y}$ to $X$ with respect to $T_{P0}$ and $T$ by \cref{continuous_eq_codomain}.
            Show that for all $U$ such that $U\in T_{Y0}$ we have $U\in T_{P0}$.
            \begin{subproof}
                Fix $U$ such that $U\in T_{Y0}$.
                Take $V\in T$ such that $U = \productFamily{Y}\inter V$ by \cref{subspace_topology}.
                Let $j_0 = \identity{\productFamily{Y}}$.
                Thus $V$ is open in $X$ with respect to $T$ by \cref{continuous,open_in}.
                Hence $\preimg{j_0}{V}$ is open in $\productFamily{Y}$ with respect to $T_{P0}$ by \cref{continuous}.
                Therefore $\preimg{j_0}{V} = \productFamily{Y}\inter V$
                    by \cref{preimg_iff,id_iff,inter_intro,inter,subseteq,subseteq_antisymmetric}.
                Then $U\in T_{P0}$ by \cref{open_in}.
            \end{subproof}
            Hence $T_{Y0}\subseteq T_{P0}$ by \cref{subseteq}.
            For all $C$ such that $C$ is an open covering of $\productFamily{Y}$ with respect to $T_{Y0}$ there exists $B$ such that
                $B\subseteq C$ and $B$ is finite and $B$ is a covering of $\productFamily{Y}$
                by \cref{open_covering,open_in,subseteq,compact}.
            Therefore $\productFamily{Y}$ is compact with respect to $T_{Y0}$ by \cref{compact}.
            Hence $T$ is a topology on $X$ by \cref{square_metric_is_metric,metric_basis_is_basis,basis_topology_is_topology,metric_topology,basis_for_topology}.
            Hence $\productFamily{Y}\subseteq X$
                by \cref{indexed_product,funs_elim,subseteq,function_apply_intro,intervalclosed,funs_intro,tuple_power,reals_power}.
            Hence $\productFamily{Y}$ is a subspace of $X$ with respect to $T$ by \cref{subspace_of}.
            Then $A = A\inter\productFamily{Y}$ by \cref{subseteq,inter_absorb_supseteq_right}.
            Hence $A$ is closed in $\productFamily{Y}$ with respect to $T_{Y0}$ by \cref{closed_in_subspace_iff,subspace_of}.
            Therefore $T$ is a topology on $X$ and $\productFamily{Y}\subseteq X$ by \cref{subspace_of}.
            Then $T_{Y0}$ is a topology on $\productFamily{Y}$ by \cref{subspace_topology_is_topology}.
            Hence $A$ is compact with respect to $T_A$ by \cref{closed_subspace_compact,subspace_subspace_topology}.
        \end{byCase}
    \end{subproof}
\end{proof}
Lemma 265
finite_inhabited_largest_element

Assume $R$ is a simple order relation on $X$.
Suppose $F\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-subseteq}{\subseteq} X$.
Suppose $F$ is finite.
Suppose $F$ is inhabited.
Then there exists $b$ such that $b$ is a largest element of $F$ with respect to $R$.

LaTeX source 
\begin{lemma}
\label{finite_inhabited_largest_element}
    Assume $R$ is a simple order relation on $X$.
    Suppose $F\subseteq X$.
    Suppose $F$ is finite.
    Suppose $F$ is inhabited.
    Then there exists $b$ such that $b$ is a largest element of $F$ with respect to $R$.
\end{lemma}
Proof

Take $k$ such that $k$ is a natural number and there exists a bijection from $k$ to $F$ by Definition 261.
Take $f$ such that $f$ is a bijection from $k$ to $F$.
Let $A = \{ n\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\naturals \mid \text{for all $G,g$ such that $g$ is a bijection from $n$ to $G$ and $G\subseteq X$ and $G$ is inhabited there exists $b$ such that $b$ is a largest element of $G$ with respect to $R$}\}$.

Subclaim: Show that for all $G,g$ such that $g$ is a bijection from $\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-emptyset}{\emptyset}$ to $G$ and $G\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-subseteq}{\subseteq} X$ and $G$ is inhabited there exists $b$ such that $b$ is a largest element of $G$ with respect to $R$.

Follows by Definition 248, Definition 247, Axiom 3.

Hence $\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-emptyset}{\emptyset}\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} A$.

Subclaim: Show that for all $n\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} A$ we have $\suc{n}\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} A$.

Fix $n\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} A$.

Subclaim: Show that for all $G,g$ such that $g$ is a bijection from $\suc{n}$ to $G$ and $G\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-subseteq}{\subseteq} X$ and $G$ is inhabited there exists $b$ such that $b$ is a largest element of $G$ with respect to $R$.

Fix $G$.
Fix $g$ such that $g$ is a bijection from $\suc{n}$ to $G$ and $G\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-subseteq}{\subseteq} X$ and $G$ is inhabited.
$g$ is a function and $\dom{g} = \suc{n}$ by Proposition 402, Proposition 403.
Thus $n\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\dom{g}$ by Proposition 424.
Let $a = g(n)$.
Let $G_1 = \img{g}{n}$.
Thus $G_1\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-subseteq}{\subseteq} G$ and $G_1\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-subseteq}{\subseteq} X$ by Definition 251, Definition 188, Proposition 248, Proposition 250, Definition 248, Corollary 14, Proposition 6.
Thus $a\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} G$ by Proposition 360, Proposition 220, Definition 248, Corollary 14.

Subclaim: Show that for all $z$ such that $z\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} G$ we have $z\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} G_1\union\{a\}$.

Fix $z\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} G$.
Thus there exists $x\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\suc{n}$ such that $g(x)=z$ by Definition 248, Definition 247.
$x\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} n$ or $x = n$ by Definition 251.

Case. $x\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} n$.

$(x,z)\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} g$ by Proposition 360.
Thus $z\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} G_1$ by Proposition 247.
Thus $z\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} G_1\union\{a\}$ by Proposition 38.

Case. $x = n$.

Thus $z = a$.
Thus $z\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} G_1\union\{a\}$ by Proposition 39, Proposition 20.

LaTeX source 
\begin{byCase}
                \caseOf{$x\in n$.}
                    $(x,z)\in g$ by \cref{function_apply_elim}.
                    Thus $z\in G_1$ by \cref{img_iff}.
                    Thus $z\in G_1\union\{a\}$ by \cref{union_intro_left}.
                \caseOf{$x = n$.}
                    Thus $z = a$.
                    Thus $z\in G_1\union\{a\}$ by \cref{union_intro_right,singleton_intro}.
                
\end{byCase}

Hence $G\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-subseteq}{\subseteq} G_1\union\{a\}$ by Definition 188.
Therefore $G_1\union\{a\}\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-subseteq}{\subseteq} G$ by Proposition 25, Proposition 44.
Then $G = G_1\union\{a\}$ by Proposition 3.
Let $h = \restrl{g}{n}$.
$h$ is a function and $\dom{h} = \dom{g}\inter n$ by Proposition 395, Corollary 10.
For all $x$ such that $x\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} n$ we have $x\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\dom{g}$ by Definition 251, Proposition 403.
Hence $n\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-subseteq}{\subseteq}\dom{g}$ by Definition 188.
Therefore $\dom{h} = n$ by Proposition 70.
For all $x\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\dom{h}$ we have $h(x)\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} G_1$ by Proposition 360, Proposition 275, Proposition 4, Proposition 247.
Hence $h\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\funs{n}{G_1}$ by Proposition 370.
For all $y\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} G_1$ there exists $x\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} n$ such that $h(x) = y$ by Proposition 247, Proposition 361, Proposition 396.
Therefore $h\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\surj{n}{G_1}$ by Definition 247.
Hence $h$ is injective by Proposition 396, Definition 248, Proposition 398.
Hence $h$ is a bijection from $n$ to $G_1$ by Definition 248.

Case. $n = \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-emptyset}{\emptyset}$.

Thus $a$ is a largest element of $G$ with respect to $R$ by Proposition 257, Proposition 49, Proposition 41 [Commutativity of union], Proposition 20, Proposition 22, Definition 30.

Case. $n\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-neq}{\neq} \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-emptyset}{\emptyset}$.

Hence $G_1$ is inhabited by Proposition 10, Definition 251, Proposition 403, Proposition 360, Proposition 247.
Take $b$ such that $b$ is a largest element of $G_1$ with respect to $R$.
Therefore $b\mathrel{R}a$ or $a\mathrel{R}b$ or $b = a$ by Definition 30, Proposition 4, Definition 28, Definition 230.

Case. $b\mathrel{R}a$ or $b = a$.

For all $y\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} G$ we have $y\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} G_1$ or $y = a$ by Axiom 6, Proposition 22.
For all $y\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} G_1$ we have $y\mathrel{R}b$ or $y = b$ by Definition 30.
Hence for all $y\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} G$ we have $y\mathrel{R}a$ or $y = a$ by Definition 28, Definition 227 [Transitivity].
Hence $a$ is a largest element of $G$ with respect to $R$ by Proposition 39, Proposition 20, Definition 30.

Case. $a\mathrel{R}b$.

For all $y\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} G$ we have $y\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} G_1$ or $y = a$ by Axiom 6, Proposition 22.
Hence for all $y\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} G$ we have $y\mathrel{R}b$ or $y = b$ by Definition 30.
Therefore $b$ is a largest element of $G$ with respect to $R$ by Proposition 4, Definition 30.

LaTeX source 
\begin{byCase}
                \caseOf{$b\mathrel{R}a$ or $b = a$.}
                    For all $y\in G$ we have $y\in G_1$ or $y = a$ by \cref{union_iff,singleton_iff}.
                    For all $y\in G_1$ we have $y\mathrel{R}b$ or $y = b$ by \cref{largest_element}.
                    Hence for all $y\in G$ we have $y\mathrel{R}a$ or $y = a$
                        by \cref{simple_order_relation,transitive}.
                    Hence $a$ is a largest element of $G$ with respect to $R$
                        by \cref{union_intro_right,singleton_intro,largest_element}.
                \caseOf{$a\mathrel{R}b$.}
                    For all $y\in G$ we have $y\in G_1$ or $y = a$ by \cref{union_iff,singleton_iff}.
                    Hence for all $y\in G$ we have $y\mathrel{R}b$ or $y = b$ by \cref{largest_element}.
                    Therefore $b$ is a largest element of $G$ with respect to $R$ by \cref{elem_subseteq,largest_element}.
                
\end{byCase}
LaTeX source 
\begin{byCase}
            \caseOf{$n = \emptyset$.}
                Thus $a$ is a largest element of $G$ with respect to $R$
                    by \cref{img_emptyset,union_emptyset,union_comm,singleton_intro,singleton_iff,largest_element}.
            \caseOf{$n\neq \emptyset$.}
                Hence $G_1$ is inhabited by \cref{nonempty_inhabited,suc,bijections_dom,function_apply_elim,img_iff}.
                Take $b$ such that $b$ is a largest element of $G_1$ with respect to $R$.
                Therefore $b\mathrel{R}a$ or $a\mathrel{R}b$ or $b = a$
                    by \cref{largest_element,elem_subseteq,simple_order_relation,connex}.
                \begin{byCase}
                \caseOf{$b\mathrel{R}a$ or $b = a$.}
                    For all $y\in G$ we have $y\in G_1$ or $y = a$ by \cref{union_iff,singleton_iff}.
                    For all $y\in G_1$ we have $y\mathrel{R}b$ or $y = b$ by \cref{largest_element}.
                    Hence for all $y\in G$ we have $y\mathrel{R}a$ or $y = a$
                        by \cref{simple_order_relation,transitive}.
                    Hence $a$ is a largest element of $G$ with respect to $R$
                        by \cref{union_intro_right,singleton_intro,largest_element}.
                \caseOf{$a\mathrel{R}b$.}
                    For all $y\in G$ we have $y\in G_1$ or $y = a$ by \cref{union_iff,singleton_iff}.
                    Hence for all $y\in G$ we have $y\mathrel{R}b$ or $y = b$ by \cref{largest_element}.
                    Therefore $b$ is a largest element of $G$ with respect to $R$ by \cref{elem_subseteq,largest_element}.
                \end{byCase}
            
\end{byCase}

Hence $\suc{n}\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} A$.

Hence $A$ is an inductive set.
Therefore $k\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} A$ by Axiom 15, Definition 188.
Then for all $G,g$ such that $g$ is a bijection from $k$ to $G$ and $G\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-subseteq}{\subseteq} X$ and $G$ is inhabited there exists $b$ such that $b$ is a largest element of $G$ with respect to $R$.
Hence there exists $b$ such that $b$ is a largest element of $F$ with respect to $R$.

LaTeX source 
\begin{proof}
    Take $k$ such that $k$ is a natural number and there exists a bijection from $k$ to $F$ by \cref{finite}.
    Take $f$ such that $f$ is a bijection from $k$ to $F$.
    Let $A = \{ n\in\naturals \mid \text{for all $G,g$ such that $g$ is a bijection from $n$ to $G$ and $G\subseteq X$ and $G$ is inhabited
        there exists $b$ such that $b$ is a largest element of $G$ with respect to $R$}\}$.
    Show that for all $G,g$ such that $g$ is a bijection from $\emptyset$ to $G$ and $G\subseteq X$ and $G$ is inhabited
        there exists $b$ such that $b$ is a largest element of $G$ with respect to $R$.
    \begin{subproof}
        Follows by \cref{bijections,surj,emptyset}.
    \end{subproof}
    Hence $\emptyset\in A$.
    Show that for all $n\in A$ we have $\suc{n}\in A$.
    \begin{subproof}
        Fix $n\in A$.
        Show that for all $G,g$ such that $g$ is a bijection from $\suc{n}$ to $G$ and $G\subseteq X$ and $G$ is inhabited
            there exists $b$ such that $b$ is a largest element of $G$ with respect to $R$.
        \begin{subproof}
            Fix $G$.
            Fix $g$ such that $g$ is a bijection from $\suc{n}$ to $G$ and $G\subseteq X$ and $G$ is inhabited.
            $g$ is a function and $\dom{g} = \suc{n}$ by \cref{bijection_is_function,bijections_dom}.
            Thus $n\in\dom{g}$ by \cref{suc_intro_self}.
            Let $a = g(n)$.
            Let $G_1 = \img{g}{n}$.
            Thus $G_1\subseteq G$ and $G_1\subseteq X$
                by \cref{suc,subseteq,img_subseteq,img_dom,bijections,ran_of_surj,subseteq_transitive}.
            Thus $a\in G$ by \cref{function_apply_elim,ran_iff,bijections,ran_of_surj}.
            Show that for all $z$ such that $z\in G$ we have $z\in G_1\union\{a\}$.
            \begin{subproof}
                Fix $z\in G$.
                Thus there exists $x\in\suc{n}$ such that $g(x)=z$ by \cref{bijections,surj}.
                $x\in n$ or $x = n$ by \cref{suc}.
                \begin{byCase}
                \caseOf{$x\in n$.}
