                    $(x,z)\in g$ by \cref{function_apply_elim}.
                    Thus $z\in G_1$ by \cref{img_iff}.
                    Thus $z\in G_1\union\{a\}$ by \cref{union_intro_left}.
                \caseOf{$x = n$.}
                    Thus $z = a$.
                    Thus $z\in G_1\union\{a\}$ by \cref{union_intro_right,singleton_intro}.
                \end{byCase}
            \end{subproof}
            Hence $G\subseteq G_1\union\{a\}$ by \cref{subseteq}.
            Therefore $G_1\union\{a\}\subseteq G$ by \cref{singleton_subset_intro,union_subsets_is_subset}.
            Then $G = G_1\union\{a\}$ by \cref{subseteq_antisymmetric}.
            Let $h = \restrl{g}{n}$.
            $h$ is a function and $\dom{h} = \dom{g}\inter n$ by \cref{restrl_of_function_is_function,dom_of_restrl_eq_inter}.
            For all $x$ such that $x\in n$ we have $x\in\dom{g}$ by \cref{suc,bijections_dom}.
            Hence $n\subseteq\dom{g}$ by \cref{subseteq}.
            Therefore $\dom{h} = n$ by \cref{inter_absorb_supseteq_left}.
            For all $x\in\dom{h}$ we have $h(x)\in G_1$
                by \cref{function_apply_elim,restrl_subseteq,elem_subseteq,img_iff}.
            Hence $h\in\funs{n}{G_1}$ by \cref{funs_intro}.
            For all $y\in G_1$ there exists $x\in n$ such that $h(x) = y$
                by \cref{img_iff,function_apply_iff,restrl_of_function_apply}.
            Therefore $h\in\surj{n}{G_1}$ by \cref{surj}.
            Hence $h$ is injective by \cref{restrl_of_function_apply,bijections,injective_function}.
            Hence $h$ is a bijection from $n$ to $G_1$ by \cref{bijections}.
            \begin{byCase}
            \caseOf{$n = \emptyset$.}
                Thus $a$ is a largest element of $G$ with respect to $R$
                    by \cref{img_emptyset,union_emptyset,union_comm,singleton_intro,singleton_iff,largest_element}.
            \caseOf{$n\neq \emptyset$.}
                Hence $G_1$ is inhabited by \cref{nonempty_inhabited,suc,bijections_dom,function_apply_elim,img_iff}.
                Take $b$ such that $b$ is a largest element of $G_1$ with respect to $R$.
                Therefore $b\mathrel{R}a$ or $a\mathrel{R}b$ or $b = a$
                    by \cref{largest_element,elem_subseteq,simple_order_relation,connex}.
                \begin{byCase}
                \caseOf{$b\mathrel{R}a$ or $b = a$.}
                    For all $y\in G$ we have $y\in G_1$ or $y = a$ by \cref{union_iff,singleton_iff}.
                    For all $y\in G_1$ we have $y\mathrel{R}b$ or $y = b$ by \cref{largest_element}.
                    Hence for all $y\in G$ we have $y\mathrel{R}a$ or $y = a$
                        by \cref{simple_order_relation,transitive}.
                    Hence $a$ is a largest element of $G$ with respect to $R$
                        by \cref{union_intro_right,singleton_intro,largest_element}.
                \caseOf{$a\mathrel{R}b$.}
                    For all $y\in G$ we have $y\in G_1$ or $y = a$ by \cref{union_iff,singleton_iff}.
                    Hence for all $y\in G$ we have $y\mathrel{R}b$ or $y = b$ by \cref{largest_element}.
                    Therefore $b$ is a largest element of $G$ with respect to $R$ by \cref{elem_subseteq,largest_element}.
                \end{byCase}
            \end{byCase}
        \end{subproof}
        Hence $\suc{n}\in A$.
    \end{subproof}
    Hence $A$ is an inductive set.
    Therefore $k\in A$ by \cref{num_naturals_smallest_inductive_set,subseteq}.
    Then for all $G,g$ such that $g$ is a bijection from $k$ to $G$ and $G\subseteq X$ and $G$ is inhabited
        there exists $b$ such that $b$ is a largest element of $G$ with respect to $R$.
    Hence there exists $b$ such that $b$ is a largest element of $F$ with respect to $R$.
\end{proof}
Lemma 266
finite_inhabited_smallest_element

Assume $R$ is a simple order relation on $X$.
Suppose $F\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-subseteq}{\subseteq} X$.
Suppose $F$ is finite.
Suppose $F$ is inhabited.
Then there exists $a$ such that $a$ is a smallest element of $F$ with respect to $R$.

LaTeX source 
\begin{lemma}
\label{finite_inhabited_smallest_element}
    Assume $R$ is a simple order relation on $X$.
    Suppose $F\subseteq X$.
    Suppose $F$ is finite.
    Suppose $F$ is inhabited.
    Then there exists $a$ such that $a$ is a smallest element of $F$ with respect to $R$.
\end{lemma}
Proof

Then there exists $a$ such that $a$ is a smallest element of $F$ with respect to $R$ by Definition 28, Proposition 202, Proposition 198, Definition 227 [Transitivity], Definition 226, Definition 230, Lemma 265, Definition 30, Definition 29.

LaTeX source 
\begin{proof}
    Then there exists $a$ such that $a$ is a smallest element of $F$ with respect to $R$
        by \cref{simple_order_relation,converse_type,converse_iff,transitive,asymmetric,connex,finite_inhabited_largest_element,largest_element,smallest_element}.
\end{proof}
Theorem 62
extreme_value_theorem

Assume $T_X$ is a topology on $X$.
Assume $T_Y$ is the order topology on $Y$ with respect to $R$.
Suppose $f$ is continuous from $X$ to $Y$ with respect to $T_X$ and $T_Y$.
Suppose $X$ is compact with respect to $T_X$.
Suppose $X\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-neq}{\neq}\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-emptyset}{\emptyset}$.
Then there exist $c,d\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} X$ such that $\apply{f}{c}$ is a smallest element of $\img{f}{X}$ with respect to $R$ and $\apply{f}{d}$ is a largest element of $\img{f}{X}$ with respect to $R$.

LaTeX source 
\begin{theorem}
\label{extreme_value_theorem}
    Assume $T_X$ is a topology on $X$.
    Assume $T_Y$ is the order topology on $Y$ with respect to $R$.
    Suppose $f$ is continuous from $X$ to $Y$ with respect to $T_X$ and $T_Y$.
    Suppose $X$ is compact with respect to $T_X$.
    Suppose $X\neq\emptyset$.
    Then there exist $c,d\in X$ such that
        $\apply{f}{c}$ is a smallest element of $\img{f}{X}$ with respect to $R$ and
        $\apply{f}{d}$ is a largest element of $\img{f}{X}$ with respect to $R$.
\end{theorem}
Proof

Let $A = \img{f}{X}$.
Let $T_A = \subspaceTopology{T_Y}{A}$.
Hence $A$ is compact with respect to $T_A$ by Theorem 56.
$T_Y = \basisTopology{\orderBasis{R}{Y}}{Y}$ and $R$ is a simple order relation on $Y$ and $Y$ has more than one element by Definition 36.
Therefore $T_Y$ is a topology on $Y$ by Definition 36, Lemma 38, Lemma 1.
Then $f$ is a function to $Y$ and $\dom{f} = X$ by Definition 62, Proposition 371.
Hence $A\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-subseteq}{\subseteq} Y$ by Proposition 250, Proposition 366.
Therefore $A$ is a subspace of $Y$ with respect to $T_Y$ by Definition 49.
Then $A$ is inhabited by Proposition 10, Definition 62, Proposition 371, Proposition 360, Proposition 247.

Subclaim: Show that there exists $M$ such that $M$ is a largest element of $A$ with respect to $R$.

Suppose not.
Let $C = \{U \mid \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-exists}{\exists} a\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} A. U = \openrayleft{R}{Y}{a}\}$.
For all $y$ such that $y\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} A$ we have $y\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\unions{C}$ by Definition 30, Definition 188, Definition 36, Definition 28, Definition 230, Definition 38, Axiom 5.
Hence $C$ is a covering of $A$ by Definition 188, Definition 147.
Take $B$ such that $B\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-subseteq}{\subseteq} C$ and $B$ is finite and $B$ is a covering of $A$ by Definition 188, Lemma 71, Lemma 248, Definition 149.
Thus $B$ is inhabited by Proposition 10, Definition 147, Definition 188, Axiom 5.
Let $R_B = \{w\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} B\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-times}{\times} A \mid \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-exists}{\exists} U\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} B.\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-exists}{\exists} a\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} A. w = (U,a) \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-land}{\land} U = \openrayleft{R}{Y}{a}\}$.
For all $w$ such that $w\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} R_B$ there exist $U,a$ such that $w = (U,a)$.
Therefore $R_B$ is a relation by Definition 202.
For all $U\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} B$ there exists $a$ such that $U\mathrel{R_B}a$.
Take $g$ such that $g$ is a function on $B$ and for all $U\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} B$ we have $U\mathrel{R_B} \apply{g}{U}$ by Axiom 19.
Let $E = \img{g}{B}$.
Therefore $E\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-subseteq}{\subseteq} A$ by Proposition 247, Proposition 358, Definition 202, Axiom 7, Definition 188.
Then $E$ is finite and $E$ is inhabited by Lemma 109, Proposition 10, Proposition 360, Proposition 247.
Therefore $E\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-subseteq}{\subseteq} Y$ by Proposition 6.
Take $a_0$ such that $a_0$ is a largest element of $E$ with respect to $R$ by Lemma 265.
For all $U\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} B$ we have $a_0\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-notin}{\notin} U$ by Axiom 1, Definition 202, Axiom 7, Proposition 360, Proposition 247, Definition 30, Definition 38, Definition 28, Definition 36, Definition 226.
Hence $a_0\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-notin}{\notin}\unions{B}$ by Axiom 5.
Therefore $a_0\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\unions{B}$ by Definition 30, Proposition 4, Definition 147, Definition 188.
Contradiction.

Subclaim: Show that there exists $m$ such that $m$ is a smallest element of $A$ with respect to $R$.

Suppose not.
Let $C = \{U \mid \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-exists}{\exists} a\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} A. U = \openrayright{R}{Y}{a}\}$.
For all $y$ such that $y\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} A$ we have $y\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\unions{C}$ by Definition 29, Definition 188, Definition 36, Definition 28, Definition 230, Definition 37, Axiom 5.
Hence $C$ is a covering of $A$ by Definition 188, Definition 147.
Take $B$ such that $B\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-subseteq}{\subseteq} C$ and $B$ is finite and $B$ is a covering of $A$ by Definition 188, Lemma 70, Lemma 248, Definition 149.
Thus $B$ is inhabited by Proposition 10, Definition 147, Definition 188, Axiom 5.
Let $R_B = \{w\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} B\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-times}{\times} A \mid \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-exists}{\exists} U\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} B.\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-exists}{\exists} a\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} A. w = (U,a) \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-land}{\land} U = \openrayright{R}{Y}{a}\}$.
For all $w$ such that $w\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} R_B$ there exist $U,a$ such that $w = (U,a)$.
Therefore $R_B$ is a relation by Definition 202.
For all $U\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} B$ there exists $a$ such that $U\mathrel{R_B}a$.
Take $g$ such that $g$ is a function on $B$ and for all $U\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} B$ we have $U\mathrel{R_B} \apply{g}{U}$ by Axiom 19.
Let $E = \img{g}{B}$.
Therefore $E\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-subseteq}{\subseteq} A$ by Proposition 247, Proposition 358, Definition 202, Axiom 7, Definition 188.
Then $E$ is finite and $E$ is inhabited by Lemma 109, Proposition 10, Proposition 360, Proposition 247.
Therefore $E\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-subseteq}{\subseteq} Y$ by Proposition 6.
Take $a_0$ such that $a_0$ is a smallest element of $E$ with respect to $R$ by Lemma 266.
For all $U\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} B$ we have $a_0\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-notin}{\notin} U$ by Axiom 1, Definition 202, Axiom 7, Proposition 360, Proposition 247, Definition 29, Definition 37, Definition 28, Definition 36, Definition 226.
Hence $a_0\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-notin}{\notin}\unions{B}$ by Axiom 5.
Therefore $a_0\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\unions{B}$ by Definition 29, Proposition 4, Definition 147, Definition 188.
Contradiction.

Take $m$ such that $m$ is a smallest element of $A$ with respect to $R$.
Take $c\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} X$ such that $f(c) = m$ by Definition 29, Proposition 247, Proposition 358.
Take $M$ such that $M$ is a largest element of $A$ with respect to $R$.
Take $d\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} X$ such that $f(d) = M$ by Definition 30, Proposition 247, Proposition 358.
Therefore $\apply{f}{c}$ is a smallest element of $A$ with respect to $R$ and $\apply{f}{d}$ is a largest element of $A$ with respect to $R$ by Definition 29, Definition 30.

LaTeX source 
\begin{proof}
    Let $A = \img{f}{X}$.
    Let $T_A = \subspaceTopology{T_Y}{A}$.
    Hence $A$ is compact with respect to $T_A$ by \cref{continuous_image_compact}.
    $T_Y = \basisTopology{\orderBasis{R}{Y}}{Y}$ and $R$ is a simple order relation on $Y$
        and $Y$ has more than one element by \cref{order_topology}.
    Therefore $T_Y$ is a topology on $Y$ by \cref{order_topology,order_basis_is_basis,basis_topology_is_topology}.
    Then $f$ is a function to $Y$ and $\dom{f} = X$ by \cref{continuous,funs_elim}.
    Hence $A\subseteq Y$ by \cref{img_dom,function_to_ran}.
    Therefore $A$ is a subspace of $Y$ with respect to $T_Y$ by \cref{subspace_of}.
    Then $A$ is inhabited by \cref{nonempty_inhabited,continuous,funs_elim,function_apply_elim,img_iff}.
    Show that there exists $M$ such that $M$ is a largest element of $A$ with respect to $R$.
    \begin{subproof}
        Suppose not.
        Let $C = \{U \mid \exists a\in A. U = \openrayleft{R}{Y}{a}\}$.
        For all $y$ such that $y\in A$ we have $y\in\unions{C}$
            by \cref{largest_element,subseteq,order_topology,simple_order_relation,connex,openray_left,unions_iff}.
        Hence $C$ is a covering of $A$ by \cref{subseteq,covering}.
        Take $B$ such that $B\subseteq C$ and $B$ is finite and $B$ is a covering of $A$
            by \cref{subseteq,openrayleft_open_in_order_topology,compact_subspace_open_covering,compact}.
        Thus $B$ is inhabited by \cref{nonempty_inhabited,covering,subseteq,unions_iff}.
        Let $R_B = \{w\in B\times A \mid \exists U\in B.\exists a\in A. w = (U,a) \land U = \openrayleft{R}{Y}{a}\}$.
        For all $w$ such that $w\in R_B$ there exist $U,a$ such that $w = (U,a)$.
        Therefore $R_B$ is a relation by \cref{relation}.
        For all $U\in B$ there exists $a$ such that $U\mathrel{R_B}a$.
        Take $g$ such that $g$ is a function on $B$ and for all $U\in B$ we have $U\mathrel{R_B} \apply{g}{U}$
            by \cref{axiom_of_choice}.
        Let $E = \img{g}{B}$.
        Therefore $E\subseteq A$ by \cref{img_iff,function_apply_intro,relation,pair_eq_iff,subseteq}.
        Then $E$ is finite and $E$ is inhabited by \cref{finite_image_function,nonempty_inhabited,function_apply_elim,img_iff}.
        Therefore $E\subseteq Y$ by \cref{subseteq_transitive}.
        Take $a_0$ such that $a_0$ is a largest element of $E$ with respect to $R$
            by \cref{finite_inhabited_largest_element}.
        For all $U\in B$ we have $a_0\notin U$
            by \cref{choice_function,relation,pair_eq_iff,function_apply_elim,img_iff,largest_element,openray_left,simple_order_relation,order_topology,asymmetric}.
        Hence $a_0\notin\unions{B}$ by \cref{unions_iff}.
        Therefore $a_0\in\unions{B}$ by \cref{largest_element,elem_subseteq,covering,subseteq}.
        Contradiction.
    \end{subproof}
    Show that there exists $m$ such that $m$ is a smallest element of $A$ with respect to $R$.
    \begin{subproof}
        Suppose not.
        Let $C = \{U \mid \exists a\in A. U = \openrayright{R}{Y}{a}\}$.
        For all $y$ such that $y\in A$ we have $y\in\unions{C}$
            by \cref{smallest_element,subseteq,order_topology,simple_order_relation,connex,openray_right,unions_iff}.
        Hence $C$ is a covering of $A$ by \cref{subseteq,covering}.
        Take $B$ such that $B\subseteq C$ and $B$ is finite and $B$ is a covering of $A$
            by \cref{subseteq,openrayright_open_in_order_topology,compact_subspace_open_covering,compact}.
        Thus $B$ is inhabited by \cref{nonempty_inhabited,covering,subseteq,unions_iff}.
        Let $R_B = \{w\in B\times A \mid \exists U\in B.\exists a\in A. w = (U,a) \land U = \openrayright{R}{Y}{a}\}$.
        For all $w$ such that $w\in R_B$ there exist $U,a$ such that $w = (U,a)$.
        Therefore $R_B$ is a relation by \cref{relation}.
        For all $U\in B$ there exists $a$ such that $U\mathrel{R_B}a$.
        Take $g$ such that $g$ is a function on $B$ and for all $U\in B$ we have $U\mathrel{R_B} \apply{g}{U}$
            by \cref{axiom_of_choice}.
        Let $E = \img{g}{B}$.
        Therefore $E\subseteq A$ by \cref{img_iff,function_apply_intro,relation,pair_eq_iff,subseteq}.
        Then $E$ is finite and $E$ is inhabited by \cref{finite_image_function,nonempty_inhabited,function_apply_elim,img_iff}.
        Therefore $E\subseteq Y$ by \cref{subseteq_transitive}.
        Take $a_0$ such that $a_0$ is a smallest element of $E$ with respect to $R$
            by \cref{finite_inhabited_smallest_element}.
        For all $U\in B$ we have $a_0\notin U$
            by \cref{choice_function,relation,pair_eq_iff,function_apply_elim,img_iff,smallest_element,openray_right,simple_order_relation,order_topology,asymmetric}.
        Hence $a_0\notin\unions{B}$ by \cref{unions_iff}.
        Therefore $a_0\in\unions{B}$ by \cref{smallest_element,elem_subseteq,covering,subseteq}.
        Contradiction.
    \end{subproof}
    Take $m$ such that $m$ is a smallest element of $A$ with respect to $R$.
    Take $c\in X$ such that $f(c) = m$ by \cref{smallest_element,img_iff,function_apply_intro}.
    Take $M$ such that $M$ is a largest element of $A$ with respect to $R$.
    Take $d\in X$ such that $f(d) = M$ by \cref{largest_element,img_iff,function_apply_intro}.
    Therefore $\apply{f}{c}$ is a smallest element of $A$ with respect to $R$ and
        $\apply{f}{d}$ is a largest element of $A$ with respect to $R$ by \cref{smallest_element,largest_element}.
\end{proof}
Definition 154
point_distance_set

$\pointDistanceSet{d}{A}{x} = \{d((x,a)) \mid a\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} A\}$.

LaTeX source 
\begin{definition}
\label{point_distance_set}
    $\pointDistanceSet{d}{A}{x} = \{d((x,a)) \mid a\in A\}$.
\end{definition}
Definition 155
point_set_distance

$r$ is the distance from $x$ to $A$ in $X$ with respect to $d$ iff $x\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} X$ and $A\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-subseteq}{\subseteq} X$ and $r\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\reals$ and $r$ is an infimum of $\pointDistanceSet{d}{A}{x}$.

LaTeX source 
\begin{definition}
\label{point_set_distance}
    $r$ is the distance from $x$ to $A$ in $X$ with respect to $d$ iff
        $x\in X$ and $A\subseteq X$ and $r\in\reals$ and
        $r$ is an infimum of $\pointDistanceSet{d}{A}{x}$.
\end{definition}
Lemma 267
point_set_distance_continuous

Suppose $d$ is a metric on $X$.
Suppose $A\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-subseteq}{\subseteq} X$.
Suppose $A\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-neq}{\neq}\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-emptyset}{\emptyset}$.
Let $T = \metricTopology{d}{X}$.
Then there exists $f$ such that $f$ is a function from $X$ to $\reals$ and for all $x\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} X$ we have $f(x)$ is the distance from $x$ to $A$ in $X$ with respect to $d$ and $f$ is continuous from $X$ to $\reals$ with respect to $T$ and $\standardTopologyR$.

LaTeX source 
\begin{lemma}
\label{point_set_distance_continuous}
    Suppose $d$ is a metric on $X$.
    Suppose $A\subseteq X$.
    Suppose $A\neq\emptyset$.
    Let $T = \metricTopology{d}{X}$.
    Then there exists $f$ such that
        $f$ is a function from $X$ to $\reals$ and
        for all $x\in X$ we have $f(x)$ is the distance from $x$ to $A$ in $X$ with respect to $d$ and
        $f$ is continuous from $X$ to $\reals$ with respect to $T$ and $\standardTopologyR$.
\end{lemma}
Proof

Let $R = \{w\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} X\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-times}{\times}\reals \mid \text{there exists $x\in X$ such that there exists $r\in\reals$ such that $w = (x,r)$ and $r$ is the distance from $x$ to $A$ in $X$ with respect to $d$}\}$.
For all $w$ such that $w\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} R$ there exist $x,r$ such that $w = (x,r)$.
Hence $R$ is a relation by Definition 202.

Subclaim: Show that for all $x\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} X$ there exists $r$ such that $x\mathrel{R} r$.

Fix $x\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} X$.
Let $S = \pointDistanceSet{d}{A}{x}$.
Thus $S\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-subseteq}{\subseteq}\reals$ by Definition 154, Definition 188, Proposition 115, Definition 81, Proposition 373.
Therefore $S\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-neq}{\neq}\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-emptyset}{\emptyset}$ by Proposition 10, Definition 154, Axiom 3.
Thus $0$ is a lower bound of $S$ and $S$ is bounded below by Definition 154, Definition 188, Definition 81, Definition 77, Axiom 24, Definition 268, Definition 269.
Take $r$ such that $r$ is an infimum of $S$ by Theorem 140.
Thus $r$ is the distance from $x$ to $A$ in $X$ with respect to $d$ by Definition 272, Definition 268, Definition 155.
Hence $(x,r)\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} R$.
Therefore $x\mathrel{R} r$ by Definition 202.

Take $f$ such that $f$ is a function on $X$ and for all $x\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} X$ we have $x\mathrel{R} f(x)$ by Axiom 19.
For all $x\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} X$ we have $f(x)$ is the distance from $x$ to $A$ in $X$ with respect to $d$ and $f(x)\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\reals$ by Definition 202, Axiom 7, Definition 155.
Hence $f$ is a function from $X$ to $\reals$ by Proposition 370, Definition 245.
Let $T_Y = \metricTopology{\standardMetricR}{\reals}$.

Subclaim: Show that for all $x\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} X$ we have for all $\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-epsilon}{\epsilon}\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\reals$ such that $0 < \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-epsilon}{\epsilon}$ there exists $\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-delta}{\delta}\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\reals$ such that $0 < \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-delta}{\delta}$ and for all $y\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} X$ if $d((x,y)) < \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-delta}{\delta}$ then $\apply{\standardMetricR}{(f(x),f(y))} < \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-epsilon}{\epsilon}$.

Fix $x\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} X$.
Fix $\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-epsilon}{\epsilon}$ such that $\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-epsilon}{\epsilon}\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\reals$ and $0 < \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-epsilon}{\epsilon}$.
Let $\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-delta}{\delta} = \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-epsilon}{\epsilon}$.
Then $0 < \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-delta}{\delta}$.

Subclaim: Show that for all $y\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} X$ if $d((x,y)) < \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-delta}{\delta}$ then $\apply{\standardMetricR}{(f(x),f(y))} < \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-epsilon}{\epsilon}$.

Fix $y\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} X$.
Assume $d((x,y)) < \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-delta}{\delta}$.
Thus $d((x,y)) < \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-epsilon}{\epsilon}$ by Lemma 8.
Let $S_x = \pointDistanceSet{d}{A}{x}$.
Let $S_y = \pointDistanceSet{d}{A}{y}$.
$f(x)$ is an infimum of $S_x$ and $f(y)$ is an infimum of $S_y$ by Definition 155.
Therefore $f(x)$ is a lower bound of $S_x$ and $f(y)$ is a lower bound of $S_y$ by Definition 272.

Subclaim: Show that $f(x) \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-geq}{\geq} f(y) - d((x,y))$.

Let $b = f(y) - d((x,y))$.
$f(y)\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\reals$ by Definition 155.
$(x,y)\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} X\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-times}{\times} X$ by Proposition 115.
$d\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\funs{X\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-times}{\times} X}{\reals}$ by Definition 81.
Thus $d((x,y))\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\reals$ by Proposition 373.
$b = f(y) + \neg{(d((x,y)))}$ by Definition 263.
$\neg{(d((x,y)))}\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\reals$ by Axiom 25.
Thus $b\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\reals$ by Axiom 20.

Subclaim: Show that for all $s\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} S_x$ we have $b \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-leq}{\leq} s$.

Fix $s\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} S_x$.
Take $a\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} A$ such that $s = d((x,a))$ by Definition 154.
Thus $a\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} X$ by Definition 188.
Thus $d((y,x)) = d((x,y))$ by Definition 81, Definition 79.
Thus $d((y,x)) + d((x,a)) \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-geq}{\geq} d((y,a))$ by Definition 81, Definition 80.
Hence $d((y,a)) \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-leq}{\leq} d((y,x)) + d((x,a))$.
Therefore $d((y,a)) \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-leq}{\leq} d((x,y)) + d((x,a))$.
$(y,a)\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} X\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-times}{\times} X$ and $(x,y)\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} X\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-times}{\times} X$ and $(x,a)\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} X\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-times}{\times} X$ by Proposition 115.
Thus $d((y,a))\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\reals$ and $d((x,y))\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\reals$ and $d((x,a))\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\reals$ by Proposition 373.
Thus $d((x,y)) + d((x,a))\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\reals$ by Axiom 20.
Thus $f(y) \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-leq}{\leq} d((y,a))$ by Definition 155, Definition 268, Definition 154.
Thus $f(y) \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-leq}{\leq} d((x,y)) + d((x,a))$ by Lemma 407.
Let $u = d((x,y))$.
Let $v = d((x,a))$.
Then $f(y) \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-leq}{\leq} u + v$ by Lemma 407.
Hence $f(y) + \neg{u} \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-leq}{\leq} (u + v) + \neg{u}$ by Lemma 408.
Therefore $u\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\reals$ and $v\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\reals$.
$u + \neg{u} = 0$ by Axiom 25.
$(u + v) + \neg{u} = v + (u + \neg{u})$ by Axiom 22, Axiom 23.
Thus $(u + v) + \neg{u} = v + 0$ by Axiom 25.
$0\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\reals$ by Axiom 24.
$v + 0 = 0 + v$ by Axiom 23.
$0 + v = v$ by Axiom 24.
Then $(u + v) + \neg{u} = v$ by Axiom 23, Axiom 24.
Hence $f(y) + \neg{u} \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-leq}{\leq} v$ by Lemma 407.
Therefore $f(y) - u \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-leq}{\leq} v$ by Definition 263.
Then $f(y) - d((x,y)) \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-leq}{\leq} d((x,a))$.
Hence $b \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-leq}{\leq} s$.

Therefore $b$ is a lower bound of $S_x$ by Definition 268, Definition 188.
Then $f(x) \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-geq}{\geq} b$ by Definition 272.

Subclaim: Show that $f(y) \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-geq}{\geq} f(x) - d((x,y))$.

Let $b = f(x) - d((x,y))$.
$f(x)\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\reals$ and $(x,y)\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} X\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-times}{\times} X$ and $d\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\funs{X\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-times}{\times} X}{\reals}$ by Definition 155, Proposition 115, Definition 81.
Thus $d((x,y))\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\reals$ by Proposition 373.
$b = f(x) + \neg{(d((x,y)))}$ by Definition 263.
$\neg{(d((x,y)))}\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\reals$ by Axiom 25.
Thus $b\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\reals$ by Axiom 20.

Subclaim: Show that for all $s\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} S_y$ we have $b \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-leq}{\leq} s$.

Fix $s\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} S_y$.
Take $a\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} A$ such that $s = d((y,a))$ by Definition 154.
Thus $a\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} X$ by Definition 188.
Thus $d((x,y)) = d((y,x))$ by Definition 81, Definition 79.
Thus $d((x,y)) + d((y,a)) \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-geq}{\geq} d((x,a))$ by Definition 81, Definition 80.
Hence $d((x,a)) \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-leq}{\leq} d((x,y)) + d((y,a))$.
$(x,a)\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} X\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-times}{\times} X$ and $(x,y)\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} X\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-times}{\times} X$ and $(y,a)\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} X\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-times}{\times} X$ by Proposition 115.
Thus $d((x,a))\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\reals$ and $d((x,y))\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\reals$ and $d((y,a))\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\reals$ by Proposition 373.
Thus $d((x,y)) + d((y,a))\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\reals$ by Axiom 20.
Thus $f(x) \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-leq}{\leq} d((x,a))$ by Definition 155, Definition 268, Definition 154.
Thus $f(x) \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-leq}{\leq} d((x,y)) + d((y,a))$ by Lemma 407.
Let $u = d((x,y))$.
Let $v = d((y,a))$.
Then $f(x) \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-leq}{\leq} u + v$ by Lemma 407.
Hence $f(x) + \neg{u} \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-leq}{\leq} (u + v) + \neg{u}$ by Lemma 408.
Therefore $u\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\reals$ and $v\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\reals$.
$u + \neg{u} = 0$ by Axiom 25.
$(u + v) + \neg{u} = v + (u + \neg{u})$ by Axiom 22, Axiom 23.
Thus $(u + v) + \neg{u} = v + 0$ by Axiom 25.
$0\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\reals$ by Axiom 24.
$v + 0 = 0 + v$ by Axiom 23.
$0 + v = v$ by Axiom 24.
Then $(u + v) + \neg{u} = v$ by Axiom 23, Axiom 24.
Hence $f(x) + \neg{u} \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-leq}{\leq} v$ by Lemma 407.
Therefore $f(x) - u \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-leq}{\leq} v$ by Definition 263.
Then $f(x) - d((x,y)) \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-leq}{\leq} d((y,a))$.
Hence $b \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-leq}{\leq} s$.

Therefore $b$ is a lower bound of $S_y$ by Definition 268, Definition 188.
Then $f(y) \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-geq}{\geq} b$ by Definition 272.

Thus $f(x) - d((x,y)) \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-leq}{\leq} f(y)$.
$f(x)\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\reals$ and $f(y)\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\reals$ by Definition 155.
$(x,y)\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} X\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-times}{\times} X$ and $d\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\funs{X\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-times}{\times} X}{\reals}$ by Proposition 115, Definition 81.
Thus $d((x,y))\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\reals$ by Proposition 373.
$\neg{(d((x,y)))}\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\reals$ by Axiom 25.
Hence $f(x) + \neg{(d((x,y)))}\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\reals$ by Axiom 20.
Therefore $f(x) - d((x,y))\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\reals$ by Definition 263.
Then $(f(x) - d((x,y))) + d((x,y)) \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-leq}{\leq} f(y) + d((x,y))$ by Lemma 408.
Let $s_0 = (f(x) - d((x,y))) + d((x,y))$.
$f(x) - d((x,y)) = f(x) + \neg{(d((x,y)))}$ by Definition 263.
Thus $s_0 = f(x) + (\neg{(d((x,y)))} + d((x,y)))$ by Axiom 22.
$\neg{(d((x,y)))} + d((x,y)) = 0$ by Axiom 25, Axiom 23.
Thus $s_0 = f(x) + 0$ by Axiom 22.
$f(x) + 0 = 0 + f(x)$ by Definition 155, Axiom 24, Axiom 23.
$0 + f(x) = f(x)$ by Definition 155, Axiom 24.
Hence $s_0 = f(x)$ by Axiom 23, Axiom 24.
Then $f(x) \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-leq}{\leq} s_0$.
Therefore $f(x) \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-leq}{\leq} f(y) + d((x,y))$ by Lemma 407.
Then $f(y) - d((x,y)) \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-leq}{\leq} f(x)$.
$f(y)\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\reals$ and $f(x)\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\reals$ by Definition 155.
$(x,y)\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} X\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-times}{\times} X$ and $d\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\funs{X\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-times}{\times} X}{\reals}$ by Proposition 115, Definition 81.
Thus $d((x,y))\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\reals$ by Proposition 373.
$\neg{(d((x,y)))}\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\reals$ by Axiom 25.
Hence $f(y) + \neg{(d((x,y)))}\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\reals$ by Axiom 20.
Therefore $f(y) - d((x,y))\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\reals$ by Definition 263.
Thus $(f(y) - d((x,y))) + d((x,y)) \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-leq}{\leq} f(x) + d((x,y))$ by Lemma 408.
Let $s_1 = (f(y) - d((x,y))) + d((x,y))$.
$f(y) - d((x,y)) = f(y) + \neg{(d((x,y)))}$ by Definition 263.
Thus $s_1 = f(y) + (\neg{(d((x,y)))} + d((x,y)))$ by Axiom 22.
$\neg{(d((x,y)))} + d((x,y)) = 0$ by Axiom 25, Axiom 23.
Thus $s_1 = f(y) + 0$ by Axiom 22.
$f(y) + 0 = 0 + f(y)$ by Definition 155, Axiom 24, Axiom 23.
$0 + f(y) = f(y)$ by Definition 155, Axiom 24.
Hence $s_1 = f(y)$ by Axiom 23, Axiom 24.
Then $f(y) \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-leq}{\leq} s_1$.
Therefore $f(y) \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-leq}{\leq} f(x) + d((x,y))$ by Lemma 407.
$f(x)\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\reals$ and $f(y)\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\reals$ by Definition 155.
$(x,y)\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} X\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-times}{\times} X$ and $d\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\funs{X\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-times}{\times} X}{\reals}$ by Proposition 115, Definition 81.
Thus $d((x,y))\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\reals$ by Proposition 373.
$\neg{(f(y))}\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\reals$ by Axiom 25.
Thus $f(x) + \neg{(f(y))}\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\reals$ by Axiom 20.
$f(x) - f(y)\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\reals$ by Definition 263.
$\neg{(f(x) - f(y))}\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\reals$ by Axiom 25.
Thus $f(y) + d((x,y))\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\reals$ by Axiom 20.
Thus $f(x) + \neg{(f(y))} \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-leq}{\leq} (f(y) + d((x,y))) + \neg{(f(y))}$ by Lemma 408.
$(f(y) + d((x,y))) + \neg{(f(y))} = f(y) + (d((x,y)) + \neg{(f(y))})$ by Axiom 22.
$d((x,y)) + \neg{(f(y))} = \neg{(f(y))} + d((x,y))$ by Axiom 23.
$f(y) + (\neg{(f(y))} + d((x,y))) = (f(y) + \neg{(f(y))}) + d((x,y))$ by Axiom 22.
$f(y) + \neg{(f(y))} = 0$ by Axiom 25.
$0\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\reals$ by Axiom 24.
Therefore $(f(y) + d((x,y))) + \neg{(f(y))} = d((x,y))$ by Axiom 24, Axiom 22, Axiom 23.
Then $f(x) + \neg{(f(y))} \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-leq}{\leq} d((x,y))$ by Lemma 407.
Hence $f(x) - f(y) \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-leq}{\leq} d((x,y))$ by Definition 263.
Thus $f(x) + d((x,y))\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\reals$ and $\neg{(f(x))}\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\reals$ by Axiom 20, Axiom 25.
Thus $f(y) + \neg{(f(x))} \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-leq}{\leq} (f(x) + d((x,y))) + \neg{(f(x))}$ by Lemma 408.
$(f(x) + d((x,y))) + \neg{(f(x))} = f(x) + (d((x,y)) + \neg{(f(x))})$ by Axiom 22.
$d((x,y)) + \neg{(f(x))} = \neg{(f(x))} + d((x,y))$ by Axiom 23.
$f(x) + (\neg{(f(x))} + d((x,y))) = (f(x) + \neg{(f(x))}) + d((x,y))$ by Axiom 22.
$f(x) + \neg{(f(x))} = 0$ by Axiom 25.
$0\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\reals$ by Axiom 24.
Therefore $(f(x) + d((x,y))) + \neg{(f(x))} = d((x,y))$ by Axiom 24, Axiom 22, Axiom 23.
Then $f(y) + \neg{(f(x))} \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-leq}{\leq} d((x,y))$ by Lemma 407.
Hence $f(y) - f(x) \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-leq}{\leq} d((x,y))$ by Definition 263.
Thus $f(y) - f(x) = \neg{(f(x) - f(y))}$ by Lemma 414.
Then $\neg{(f(x) - f(y))} \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-leq}{\leq} d((x,y))$.
Therefore $\abs{(f(x) - f(y))} \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-leq}{\leq} d((x,y))$ by Theorem 123.
$f(x)\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\reals$ and $f(y)\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\reals$ by Definition 155.
$\neg{(f(y))}\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\reals$ by Axiom 25.
Thus $f(x) + \neg{(f(y))}\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\reals$ by Axiom 20.
$f(x) - f(y)\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\reals$ by Definition 263.
$(f(x),f(y))\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\reals\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-times}{\times}\reals$ by Proposition 115.
$\abs{(f(x) - f(y))}\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\reals$ by Lemma 415.
Let $p = (f(x),f(y))$.
$\fst{p} = f(x)$ and $\snd{p} = f(y)$ by Axiom 11, Axiom 12.
Then $\abs{(\fst{p} - \snd{p})} = \abs{(f(x) - f(y))}$.
Hence $(p,\abs{(f(x) - f(y))})\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\standardMetricR$ by Definition 86.
Therefore $\apply{\standardMetricR}{(f(x),f(y))} = \abs{(f(x) - f(y))}$ by Lemma 115, Definition 81, Proposition 371, Proposition 358.
Then $\apply{\standardMetricR}{(f(x),f(y))} \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-leq}{\leq} d((x,y))$.
$(x,y)\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} X\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-times}{\times} X$ by Proposition 115.
$d\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\funs{X\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-times}{\times} X}{\reals}$ by Definition 81.
Thus $d((x,y))\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\reals$ and $\apply{\standardMetricR}{(f(x),f(y))}\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\reals$ by Proposition 373.
Hence $\apply{\standardMetricR}{(f(x),f(y))} < \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-epsilon}{\epsilon}$ by Lemma 6, Axiom 44, Lemma 7.

Hence $f$ is continuous from $X$ to $\reals$ with respect to $T$ and $T_Y$ by Lemma 115, Theorem 24.
Therefore $f$ is continuous from $X$ to $\reals$ with respect to $T$ and $\standardTopologyR$ by Lemma 117, Lemma 260.

LaTeX source 
\begin{proof}
    Let $R = \{w\in X\times\reals \mid \text{there exists $x\in X$ such that there exists $r\in\reals$ such that
        $w = (x,r)$ and $r$ is the distance from $x$ to $A$ in $X$ with respect to $d$}\}$.
    For all $w$ such that $w\in R$ there exist $x,r$ such that $w = (x,r)$.
    Hence $R$ is a relation by \cref{relation}.
    Show that for all $x\in X$ there exists $r$ such that $x\mathrel{R} r$.
    \begin{subproof}
        Fix $x\in X$.
        Let $S = \pointDistanceSet{d}{A}{x}$.
        Thus $S\subseteq\reals$ by \cref{point_distance_set,subseteq,times_tuple_intro,metric_on,funs_type_apply}.
        Therefore $S\neq\emptyset$ by \cref{nonempty_inhabited,point_distance_set,emptyset}.
        Thus $0$ is a lower bound of $S$ and $S$ is bounded below
            by \cref{point_distance_set,subseteq,metric_on,metric_nonnegative,real_add_ident,real_lower_bound,real_bounded_below}.
        Take $r$ such that $r$ is an infimum of $S$ by \cref{real_infimum_exists}.
        Thus $r$ is the distance from $x$ to $A$ in $X$ with respect to $d$
            by \cref{real_infimum,real_lower_bound,point_set_distance}.
        Hence $(x,r)\in R$.
        Therefore $x\mathrel{R} r$ by \cref{relation}.
    \end{subproof}
    Take $f$ such that $f$ is a function on $X$ and for all $x\in X$ we have $x\mathrel{R} f(x)$
        by \cref{axiom_of_choice}.
    For all $x\in X$ we have $f(x)$ is the distance from $x$ to $A$ in $X$ with respect to $d$ and $f(x)\in\reals$
        by \cref{relation,pair_eq_iff,point_set_distance}.
    Hence $f$ is a function from $X$ to $\reals$ by \cref{funs_intro,funs}.
    Let $T_Y = \metricTopology{\standardMetricR}{\reals}$.
    Show that for all $x\in X$ we have
        for all $\epsilon\in\reals$ such that $0 < \epsilon$
        there exists $\delta\in\reals$ such that $0 < \delta$ and
        for all $y\in X$ if $d((x,y)) < \delta$ then
            $\apply{\standardMetricR}{(f(x),f(y))} < \epsilon$.
    \begin{subproof}
        Fix $x\in X$.
        Fix $\epsilon$ such that $\epsilon\in\reals$ and $0 < \epsilon$.
        Let $\delta = \epsilon$.
        Then $0 < \delta$.
        Show that for all $y\in X$ if $d((x,y)) < \delta$ then
            $\apply{\standardMetricR}{(f(x),f(y))} < \epsilon$.
        \begin{subproof}
            Fix $y\in X$.
            Assume $d((x,y)) < \delta$.
            Thus $d((x,y)) < \epsilon$ by \cref{real_lt_subst_right}.
            Let $S_x = \pointDistanceSet{d}{A}{x}$.
            Let $S_y = \pointDistanceSet{d}{A}{y}$.
                $f(x)$ is an infimum of $S_x$ and $f(y)$ is an infimum of $S_y$ by \cref{point_set_distance}.
                Therefore $f(x)$ is a lower bound of $S_x$ and $f(y)$ is a lower bound of $S_y$ by \cref{real_infimum}.
                Show that $f(x) \geq f(y) - d((x,y))$.
                \begin{subproof}
                    Let $b = f(y) - d((x,y))$.
                    $f(y)\in\reals$ by \cref{point_set_distance}.
                    $(x,y)\in X\times X$ by \cref{times_tuple_intro}.
                    $d\in\funs{X\times X}{\reals}$ by \cref{metric_on}.
                    Thus $d((x,y))\in\reals$ by \cref{funs_type_apply}.
                    $b = f(y) + \neg{(d((x,y)))}$ by \cref{real_sub}.
                    $\neg{(d((x,y)))}\in\reals$ by \cref{real_add_inverse}.
                    Thus $b\in\reals$ by \cref{real_add_closed}.
                    Show that for all $s\in S_x$ we have $b \leq s$.
                    \begin{subproof}
                        Fix $s\in S_x$.
                        Take $a\in A$ such that $s = d((x,a))$ by \cref{point_distance_set}.
                        Thus $a\in X$ by \cref{subseteq}.
                        Thus $d((y,x)) = d((x,y))$ by \cref{metric_on,metric_symmetric}.
                        Thus $d((y,x)) + d((x,a)) \geq d((y,a))$ by \cref{metric_on,metric_triangle_inequality}.
                        Hence $d((y,a)) \leq d((y,x)) + d((x,a))$.
                        Therefore $d((y,a)) \leq d((x,y)) + d((x,a))$.
                        $(y,a)\in X\times X$ and $(x,y)\in X\times X$ and $(x,a)\in X\times X$
                            by \cref{times_tuple_intro}.
                        Thus $d((y,a))\in\reals$ and $d((x,y))\in\reals$ and $d((x,a))\in\reals$
                            by \cref{funs_type_apply}.
                        Thus $d((x,y)) + d((x,a))\in\reals$ by \cref{real_add_closed}.
                        Thus $f(y) \leq d((y,a))$ by \cref{point_set_distance,real_lower_bound,point_distance_set}.
                        Thus $f(y) \leq d((x,y)) + d((x,a))$ by \cref{real_leq_trans}.
                        Let $u = d((x,y))$.
                        Let $v = d((x,a))$.
                        Then $f(y) \leq u + v$ by \cref{real_leq_trans}.
                        Hence $f(y) + \neg{u} \leq (u + v) + \neg{u}$ by \cref{real_leq_add}.
                        Therefore $u\in\reals$ and $v\in\reals$.
                        $u + \neg{u} = 0$ by \cref{real_add_inverse}.
                        $(u + v) + \neg{u} = v + (u + \neg{u})$ by \cref{real_add_assoc,real_add_comm}.
                        Thus $(u + v) + \neg{u} = v + 0$ by \cref{real_add_inverse}.
                        $0\in\reals$ by \cref{real_add_ident}.
                        $v + 0 = 0 + v$ by \cref{real_add_comm}.
                        $0 + v = v$ by \cref{real_add_ident}.
                        Then $(u + v) + \neg{u} = v$ by \cref{real_add_comm,real_add_ident}.
                        Hence $f(y) + \neg{u} \leq v$ by \cref{real_leq_trans}.
                        Therefore $f(y) - u \leq v$ by \cref{real_sub}.
                        Then $f(y) - d((x,y)) \leq d((x,a))$.
                        Hence $b \leq s$.
                    \end{subproof}
                    Therefore $b$ is a lower bound of $S_x$ by \cref{real_lower_bound,subseteq}.
                    Then $f(x) \geq b$ by \cref{real_infimum}.
                \end{subproof}
                Show that $f(y) \geq f(x) - d((x,y))$.
                \begin{subproof}
                    Let $b = f(x) - d((x,y))$.
                    $f(x)\in\reals$ and $(x,y)\in X\times X$ and $d\in\funs{X\times X}{\reals}$
                        by \cref{point_set_distance,times_tuple_intro,metric_on}.
                    Thus $d((x,y))\in\reals$ by \cref{funs_type_apply}.
                    $b = f(x) + \neg{(d((x,y)))}$ by \cref{real_sub}.
                    $\neg{(d((x,y)))}\in\reals$ by \cref{real_add_inverse}.
                    Thus $b\in\reals$ by \cref{real_add_closed}.
                    Show that for all $s\in S_y$ we have $b \leq s$.
                    \begin{subproof}
                        Fix $s\in S_y$.
                        Take $a\in A$ such that $s = d((y,a))$ by \cref{point_distance_set}.
                        Thus $a\in X$ by \cref{subseteq}.
                        Thus $d((x,y)) = d((y,x))$ by \cref{metric_on,metric_symmetric}.
                        Thus $d((x,y)) + d((y,a)) \geq d((x,a))$ by \cref{metric_on,metric_triangle_inequality}.
                        Hence $d((x,a)) \leq d((x,y)) + d((y,a))$.
                        $(x,a)\in X\times X$ and $(x,y)\in X\times X$ and $(y,a)\in X\times X$
                            by \cref{times_tuple_intro}.
                        Thus $d((x,a))\in\reals$ and $d((x,y))\in\reals$ and $d((y,a))\in\reals$
                            by \cref{funs_type_apply}.
                        Thus $d((x,y)) + d((y,a))\in\reals$ by \cref{real_add_closed}.
                        Thus $f(x) \leq d((x,a))$ by \cref{point_set_distance,real_lower_bound,point_distance_set}.
                        Thus $f(x) \leq d((x,y)) + d((y,a))$ by \cref{real_leq_trans}.
                        Let $u = d((x,y))$.
                        Let $v = d((y,a))$.
                        Then $f(x) \leq u + v$ by \cref{real_leq_trans}.
                        Hence $f(x) + \neg{u} \leq (u + v) + \neg{u}$ by \cref{real_leq_add}.
                        Therefore $u\in\reals$ and $v\in\reals$.
                        $u + \neg{u} = 0$ by \cref{real_add_inverse}.
                        $(u + v) + \neg{u} = v + (u + \neg{u})$ by \cref{real_add_assoc,real_add_comm}.
                        Thus $(u + v) + \neg{u} = v + 0$ by \cref{real_add_inverse}.
                        $0\in\reals$ by \cref{real_add_ident}.
                        $v + 0 = 0 + v$ by \cref{real_add_comm}.
                        $0 + v = v$ by \cref{real_add_ident}.
                        Then $(u + v) + \neg{u} = v$ by \cref{real_add_comm,real_add_ident}.
                        Hence $f(x) + \neg{u} \leq v$ by \cref{real_leq_trans}.
                        Therefore $f(x) - u \leq v$ by \cref{real_sub}.
                        Then $f(x) - d((x,y)) \leq d((y,a))$.
                        Hence $b \leq s$.
                    \end{subproof}
                    Therefore $b$ is a lower bound of $S_y$ by \cref{real_lower_bound,subseteq}.
                    Then $f(y) \geq b$ by \cref{real_infimum}.
                \end{subproof}
                Thus $f(x) - d((x,y)) \leq f(y)$.
                $f(x)\in\reals$ and $f(y)\in\reals$ by \cref{point_set_distance}.
                $(x,y)\in X\times X$ and $d\in\funs{X\times X}{\reals}$
                    by \cref{times_tuple_intro,metric_on}.
                Thus $d((x,y))\in\reals$ by \cref{funs_type_apply}.
                $\neg{(d((x,y)))}\in\reals$ by \cref{real_add_inverse}.
                Hence $f(x) + \neg{(d((x,y)))}\in\reals$ by \cref{real_add_closed}.
                Therefore $f(x) - d((x,y))\in\reals$ by \cref{real_sub}.
                Then $(f(x) - d((x,y))) + d((x,y)) \leq f(y) + d((x,y))$ by \cref{real_leq_add}.
                Let $s_0 = (f(x) - d((x,y))) + d((x,y))$.
                $f(x) - d((x,y)) = f(x) + \neg{(d((x,y)))}$ by \cref{real_sub}.
                Thus $s_0 = f(x) + (\neg{(d((x,y)))} + d((x,y)))$ by \cref{real_add_assoc}.
                $\neg{(d((x,y)))} + d((x,y)) = 0$ by \cref{real_add_inverse,real_add_comm}.
                Thus $s_0 = f(x) + 0$ by \cref{real_add_assoc}.
                $f(x) + 0 = 0 + f(x)$ by \cref{point_set_distance,real_add_ident,real_add_comm}.
                $0 + f(x) = f(x)$ by \cref{point_set_distance,real_add_ident}.
                Hence $s_0 = f(x)$ by \cref{real_add_comm,real_add_ident}.
                Then $f(x) \leq s_0$.
                Therefore $f(x) \leq f(y) + d((x,y))$ by \cref{real_leq_trans}.
                Then $f(y) - d((x,y)) \leq f(x)$.
                $f(y)\in\reals$ and $f(x)\in\reals$ by \cref{point_set_distance}.
                $(x,y)\in X\times X$ and $d\in\funs{X\times X}{\reals}$
                    by \cref{times_tuple_intro,metric_on}.
                Thus $d((x,y))\in\reals$ by \cref{funs_type_apply}.
                $\neg{(d((x,y)))}\in\reals$ by \cref{real_add_inverse}.
                Hence $f(y) + \neg{(d((x,y)))}\in\reals$ by \cref{real_add_closed}.
                Therefore $f(y) - d((x,y))\in\reals$ by \cref{real_sub}.
                Thus $(f(y) - d((x,y))) + d((x,y)) \leq f(x) + d((x,y))$ by \cref{real_leq_add}.
                Let $s_1 = (f(y) - d((x,y))) + d((x,y))$.
                $f(y) - d((x,y)) = f(y) + \neg{(d((x,y)))}$ by \cref{real_sub}.
                Thus $s_1 = f(y) + (\neg{(d((x,y)))} + d((x,y)))$ by \cref{real_add_assoc}.
                $\neg{(d((x,y)))} + d((x,y)) = 0$ by \cref{real_add_inverse,real_add_comm}.
                Thus $s_1 = f(y) + 0$ by \cref{real_add_assoc}.
                $f(y) + 0 = 0 + f(y)$ by \cref{point_set_distance,real_add_ident,real_add_comm}.
                $0 + f(y) = f(y)$ by \cref{point_set_distance,real_add_ident}.
                Hence $s_1 = f(y)$ by \cref{real_add_comm,real_add_ident}.
                Then $f(y) \leq s_1$.
                Therefore $f(y) \leq f(x) + d((x,y))$ by \cref{real_leq_trans}.
                $f(x)\in\reals$ and $f(y)\in\reals$ by \cref{point_set_distance}.
                $(x,y)\in X\times X$ and $d\in\funs{X\times X}{\reals}$
                    by \cref{times_tuple_intro,metric_on}.
                Thus $d((x,y))\in\reals$ by \cref{funs_type_apply}.
                $\neg{(f(y))}\in\reals$ by \cref{real_add_inverse}.
                Thus $f(x) + \neg{(f(y))}\in\reals$ by \cref{real_add_closed}.
                $f(x) - f(y)\in\reals$ by \cref{real_sub}.
                $\neg{(f(x) - f(y))}\in\reals$ by \cref{real_add_inverse}.
                Thus $f(y) + d((x,y))\in\reals$ by \cref{real_add_closed}.
                Thus $f(x) + \neg{(f(y))} \leq (f(y) + d((x,y))) + \neg{(f(y))}$ by \cref{real_leq_add}.
                $(f(y) + d((x,y))) + \neg{(f(y))} = f(y) + (d((x,y)) + \neg{(f(y))})$ by \cref{real_add_assoc}.
                $d((x,y)) + \neg{(f(y))} = \neg{(f(y))} + d((x,y))$ by \cref{real_add_comm}.
                $f(y) + (\neg{(f(y))} + d((x,y))) = (f(y) + \neg{(f(y))}) + d((x,y))$ by \cref{real_add_assoc}.
                $f(y) + \neg{(f(y))} = 0$ by \cref{real_add_inverse}.
                $0\in\reals$ by \cref{real_add_ident}.
                Therefore $(f(y) + d((x,y))) + \neg{(f(y))} = d((x,y))$ by \cref{real_add_ident,real_add_assoc,real_add_comm}.
                Then $f(x) + \neg{(f(y))} \leq d((x,y))$ by \cref{real_leq_trans}.
                Hence $f(x) - f(y) \leq d((x,y))$ by \cref{real_sub}.
                Thus $f(x) + d((x,y))\in\reals$ and $\neg{(f(x))}\in\reals$
                    by \cref{real_add_closed,real_add_inverse}.
                Thus $f(y) + \neg{(f(x))} \leq (f(x) + d((x,y))) + \neg{(f(x))}$ by \cref{real_leq_add}.
                $(f(x) + d((x,y))) + \neg{(f(x))} = f(x) + (d((x,y)) + \neg{(f(x))})$ by \cref{real_add_assoc}.
                $d((x,y)) + \neg{(f(x))} = \neg{(f(x))} + d((x,y))$ by \cref{real_add_comm}.
                $f(x) + (\neg{(f(x))} + d((x,y))) = (f(x) + \neg{(f(x))}) + d((x,y))$ by \cref{real_add_assoc}.
                $f(x) + \neg{(f(x))} = 0$ by \cref{real_add_inverse}.
                $0\in\reals$ by \cref{real_add_ident}.
                Therefore $(f(x) + d((x,y))) + \neg{(f(x))} = d((x,y))$ by \cref{real_add_ident,real_add_assoc,real_add_comm}.
                Then $f(y) + \neg{(f(x))} \leq d((x,y))$ by \cref{real_leq_trans}.
                Hence $f(y) - f(x) \leq d((x,y))$ by \cref{real_sub}.
                Thus $f(y) - f(x) = \neg{(f(x) - f(y))}$ by \cref{real_sub_swap_neg}.
                Then $\neg{(f(x) - f(y))} \leq d((x,y))$.
                Therefore $\abs{(f(x) - f(y))} \leq d((x,y))$ by \cref{real_abs_leq_if_pm_leq}.
                $f(x)\in\reals$ and $f(y)\in\reals$ by \cref{point_set_distance}.
                $\neg{(f(y))}\in\reals$ by \cref{real_add_inverse}.
                Thus $f(x) + \neg{(f(y))}\in\reals$ by \cref{real_add_closed}.
                $f(x) - f(y)\in\reals$ by \cref{real_sub}.
                $(f(x),f(y))\in\reals\times\reals$ by \cref{times_tuple_intro}.
                $\abs{(f(x) - f(y))}\in\reals$ by \cref{real_abs_in_reals}.
                Let $p = (f(x),f(y))$.
                $\fst{p} = f(x)$ and $\snd{p} = f(y)$ by \cref{fst_eq,snd_eq}.
                Then $\abs{(\fst{p} - \snd{p})} = \abs{(f(x) - f(y))}$.
                Hence $(p,\abs{(f(x) - f(y))})\in\standardMetricR$ by \cref{standard_metric_r}.
                Therefore $\apply{\standardMetricR}{(f(x),f(y))} = \abs{(f(x) - f(y))}$
                    by \cref{standard_metric_is_metric,metric_on,funs_elim,function_apply_intro}.
                Then $\apply{\standardMetricR}{(f(x),f(y))} \leq d((x,y))$.
                $(x,y)\in X\times X$ by \cref{times_tuple_intro}.
                $d\in\funs{X\times X}{\reals}$ by \cref{metric_on}.
                Thus $d((x,y))\in\reals$ and $\apply{\standardMetricR}{(f(x),f(y))}\in\reals$
                    by \cref{funs_type_apply}.
                Hence $\apply{\standardMetricR}{(f(x),f(y))} < \epsilon$
                    by \cref{real_leq_split,real_lt_trans,real_lt_subst_left}.
        \end{subproof}
    \end{subproof}
    Hence $f$ is continuous from $X$ to $\reals$ with respect to $T$ and $T_Y$
        by \cref{standard_metric_is_metric,metric_continuity_eps_delta}.
    Therefore $f$ is continuous from $X$ to $\reals$ with respect to $T$ and $\standardTopologyR$
        by \cref{standard_metric_topology,continuous_eq_codomain}.
\end{proof}

from §27 Item 6: Lebesgue number lemma

Lemma 268
lebesgue_number_lemma

Suppose $d$ is a metric on $X$.
Let $T = \metricTopology{d}{X}$.
Suppose $A$ is an open covering of $X$ with respect to $T$.
Suppose $X\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-neq}{\neq}\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-emptyset}{\emptyset}$.
Suppose $X$ is compact with respect to $T$.
Then there exists $\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-delta}{\delta}\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\reals$ such that $0 < \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-delta}{\delta}$ and for all $B$ such that $B\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-subseteq}{\subseteq} X$ and for all $x,y\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} B$ we have $d((x,y)) < \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-delta}{\delta}$ there exists $U\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} A$ such that $B\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-subseteq}{\subseteq} U$.

LaTeX source 
\begin{lemma}
\label{lebesgue_number_lemma}
    Suppose $d$ is a metric on $X$.
    Let $T = \metricTopology{d}{X}$.
    Suppose $A$ is an open covering of $X$ with respect to $T$.
    Suppose $X\neq\emptyset$.
    Suppose $X$ is compact with respect to $T$.
    Then there exists $\delta\in\reals$ such that $0 < \delta$ and
        for all $B$ such that $B\subseteq X$ and
            for all $x,y\in B$ we have $d((x,y)) < \delta$
        there exists $U\in A$ such that $B\subseteq U$.
\end{lemma}
Proof

Omitted.

LaTeX source 
\begin{proof}
    Omitted. 
\end{proof}
Definition 156
lebesgue_number

$\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-delta}{\delta}$ is a Lebesgue number for $A$ in $X$ with respect to $d$ iff $d$ is a metric on $X$ and $A$ is an open covering of $X$ with respect to $\metricTopology{d}{X}$ and $X\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-neq}{\neq}\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-emptyset}{\emptyset}$ and $\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-delta}{\delta}\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\reals$ and $0 < \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-delta}{\delta}$ and for all $B$ such that $B\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-subseteq}{\subseteq} X$ and for all $x,y\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} B$ we have $d((x,y)) < \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-delta}{\delta}$ there exists $U\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} A$ such that $B\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-subseteq}{\subseteq} U$.

LaTeX source 
\begin{definition}
\label{lebesgue_number}
    $\delta$ is a Lebesgue number for $A$ in $X$ with respect to $d$ iff
        $d$ is a metric on $X$ and
        $A$ is an open covering of $X$ with respect to $\metricTopology{d}{X}$ and
        $X\neq\emptyset$ and
        $\delta\in\reals$ and $0 < \delta$ and
        for all $B$ such that $B\subseteq X$ and
            for all $x,y\in B$ we have $d((x,y)) < \delta$
        there exists $U\in A$ such that $B\subseteq U$.
\end{definition}
Definition 157
uniformly_continuous

$f$ is uniformly continuous from $X$ to $Y$ with respect to $d_X$ and $d_Y$ iff $f\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\funs{X}{Y}$ and $d_X$ is a metric on $X$ and $d_Y$ is a metric on $Y$ and for all $\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-epsilon}{\epsilon}\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\reals$ such that $0 < \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-epsilon}{\epsilon}$ there exists $\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-delta}{\delta}\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\reals$ such that $0 < \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-delta}{\delta}$ and for all $x_0,x_1\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} X$ such that $d_X((x_0,x_1)) < \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-delta}{\delta}$ we have $d_Y((\apply{f}{x_0},\apply{f}{x_1})) < \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-epsilon}{\epsilon}$.

LaTeX source 
\begin{definition}
\label{uniformly_continuous}
    $f$ is uniformly continuous from $X$ to $Y$ with respect to $d_X$ and $d_Y$ iff
        $f\in\funs{X}{Y}$ and
        $d_X$ is a metric on $X$ and
        $d_Y$ is a metric on $Y$ and
        for all $\epsilon\in\reals$ such that $0 < \epsilon$
            there exists $\delta\in\reals$ such that $0 < \delta$ and
                for all $x_0,x_1\in X$ such that $d_X((x_0,x_1)) < \delta$
                    we have $d_Y((\apply{f}{x_0},\apply{f}{x_1})) < \epsilon$.
\end{definition}
Theorem 63
uniform_continuity_theorem

Suppose $d_X$ is a metric on $X$.
Suppose $d_Y$ is a metric on $Y$.
Let $T_X = \metricTopology{d_X}{X}$.
Let $T_Y = \metricTopology{d_Y}{Y}$.
Suppose $f$ is continuous from $X$ to $Y$ with respect to $T_X$ and $T_Y$.
Suppose $X$ is compact with respect to $T_X$.
Then $f$ is uniformly continuous from $X$ to $Y$ with respect to $d_X$ and $d_Y$.

LaTeX source 
\begin{theorem}
\label{uniform_continuity_theorem}
    Suppose $d_X$ is a metric on $X$.
    Suppose $d_Y$ is a metric on $Y$.
    Let $T_X = \metricTopology{d_X}{X}$.
    Let $T_Y = \metricTopology{d_Y}{Y}$.
    Suppose $f$ is continuous from $X$ to $Y$ with respect to $T_X$ and $T_Y$.
    Suppose $X$ is compact with respect to $T_X$.
    Then $f$ is uniformly continuous from $X$ to $Y$ with respect to $d_X$ and $d_Y$.
\end{theorem}
Proof

$f\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\funs{X}{Y}$ by Definition 62, Definition 245.

Subclaim: Show that for all $\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-epsilon}{\epsilon}\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\reals$ such that $0 < \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-epsilon}{\epsilon}$ there exists $\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-delta}{\delta}\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\reals$ such that $0 < \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-delta}{\delta}$ and for all $x_0,x_1\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} X$ such that $d_X((x_0,x_1)) < \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-delta}{\delta}$ we have $d_Y((\apply{f}{x_0},\apply{f}{x_1})) < \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-epsilon}{\epsilon}$.

Fix $\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-epsilon}{\epsilon}\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\reals$.
Assume $0 < \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-epsilon}{\epsilon}$.
Let $\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-epsilon}{\epsilon}_0 = \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-epsilon}{\epsilon} / (1 + 1)$.
$1\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\reals$ by Axiom 28.
$0\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\reals$ by Axiom 24.
$0 < 1$ by Theorem 114.
Hence $1 < 1 + 1$ by Axiom 45, Axiom 24.
Therefore $(1 + 1)\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\reals$ and $0 < 1 + 1$ by Axiom 20, Axiom 44.
Then $(1 + 1)\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-neq}{\neq} 0$ by Lemma 404.
Hence $\inv{(1 + 1)}\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\reals$ and $\inv{(1 + 1)} > 0$ by Axiom 29, Theorem 115.
$\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-epsilon}{\epsilon}_0 = \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-epsilon}{\epsilon} \rmul \inv{(1 + 1)}$ and $\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-epsilon}{\epsilon}_0\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\reals$ by Definition 264, Axiom 21.
Hence $0 < \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-epsilon}{\epsilon}_0$ by Definition 264, Theorem 92, Axiom 46, Lemma 7.

Case. $X = \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-emptyset}{\emptyset}$.

Let $\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-delta}{\delta}_0 = \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-epsilon}{\epsilon}_0$.
$\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-delta}{\delta}_0\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\reals$ and $0 < \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-delta}{\delta}_0$.

Subclaim: Show that for all $x_0,x_1$ such that $x_0\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} X$ and $x_1\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} X$ and $d_X((x_0,x_1)) < \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-delta}{\delta}_0$ we have $d_Y((\apply{f}{x_0},\apply{f}{x_1})) < \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-epsilon}{\epsilon}$.

Trivial.

Hence there exists $\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-delta}{\delta}\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\reals$ such that $0 < \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-delta}{\delta}$ and for all $x_0,x_1\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} X$ such that $d_X((x_0,x_1)) < \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-delta}{\delta}$ we have $d_Y((\apply{f}{x_0},\apply{f}{x_1})) < \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-epsilon}{\epsilon}$.

Case. $X \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-neq}{\neq} \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-emptyset}{\emptyset}$.

Let $R = \{w\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} X\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-times}{\times}\reals \mid \text{$0 < \snd{w}$ and for all $y\in X$ if $d_X((\fst{w},y)) < \snd{w}$ then $d_Y((\apply{f}{\fst{w}},\apply{f}{y})) < \epsilon_0$}\}$.
Hence $R$ is a relation by Proposition 121, Definition 202.
For all $x\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} X$ there exists $\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-delta}{\delta}\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\reals$ such that $x\mathrel{R}\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-delta}{\delta}$ by Theorem 24, Proposition 115, Axiom 11, Axiom 12, Definition 202.
Take $g$ such that $g$ is a function on $X$ and for all $x\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} X$ we have $x\mathrel{R}\apply{g}{x}$ by Axiom 1.
Let $A = \{U\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\pow{X} \mid \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-exists}{\exists} x\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} X. U = \metricBall{d_X}{X}{x}{\apply{g}{x}}\}$.

Hence for all $x\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} X$ we have $0 < \apply{g}{x}$ by Axiom 1, Axiom 11, Axiom 12, Definition 202.
Therefore for all $x\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} X$ we have $x\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\unions{A}$ by Definition 78, Definition 81, Lemma 7, Definition 82, Definition 188, Proposition 155, Axiom 5.
For all $U$ such that $U\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} A$ we have $U$ is open in $X$ with respect to $T_X$ by Axiom 1, Axiom 11, Axiom 12, Proposition 114, Definition 82, Definition 188, Proposition 155, Definition 83, Lemma 111, Lemma 2, Definition 15, Definition 84, Definition 3, Lemma 1.
Therefore $A$ is an open covering of $X$ with respect to $T_X$ by Definition 188, Definition 147, Definition 148.

Take $\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-delta}{\delta}_1\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\reals$ such that $0 < \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-delta}{\delta}_1$ and for all $B$ such that $B\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-subseteq}{\subseteq} X$ and for all $x,y\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} B$ we have $d_X((x,y)) < \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-delta}{\delta}_1$ there exists $U\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} A$ such that $B\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-subseteq}{\subseteq} U$ by Lemma 268.

Subclaim: Show that for all $x_0,x_1$ such that $x_0\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} X$ and $x_1\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} X$ and $d_X((x_0,x_1)) < \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-delta}{\delta}_1$ we have $d_Y((\apply{f}{x_0},\apply{f}{x_1})) < \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-epsilon}{\epsilon}$.

Fix $x_0$.
Fix $x_1$.
Assume $x_0\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} X$ and $x_1\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} X$ and $d_X((x_0,x_1)) < \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-delta}{\delta}_1$.
Let $B = \{x_0,x_1\}$.
$B\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-subseteq}{\subseteq} X$ by Proposition 19, Definition 188.

Subclaim: Show that for all $x\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} B$ we have for all $y\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} B$ we have $d_X((x,y)) < \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-delta}{\delta}_1$.

Fix $x$.
Assume $x\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} B$.
Fix $y$.
Assume $y\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} B$.
$x = x_0$ or $x = x_1$ by Proposition 19.
Then $y = x_0$ or $y = x_1$ by Proposition 19.

Case. $x = x_0$.
Case. $y = x_0$.

Thus $d_X((x,y)) < \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-delta}{\delta}_1$ by Definition 78, Definition 81, Lemma 7.

Case. $y \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-neq}{\neq} x_0$.

Then $y = x_1$.
$d_X((x,y)) < \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-delta}{\delta}_1$.

LaTeX source 
\begin{byCase}
                        \caseOf{$y = x_0$.}
                            Thus $d_X((x,y)) < \delta_1$ by \cref{metric_zero_characterization,metric_on,real_lt_subst_left}.
                        \caseOf{$y \neq x_0$.}
                            Then $y = x_1$.
                            $d_X((x,y)) < \delta_1$.
                        
\end{byCase}
Case. $x \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-neq}{\neq} x_0$.

Then $x = x_1$.

Case. $y = x_0$.

$d_X((x_1,x_0)) = d_X((x_0,x_1))$ by Definition 79, Definition 81.
$d_X((x,y)) < \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-delta}{\delta}_1$.

Case. $y \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-neq}{\neq} x_0$.

Then $y = x_1$.
Therefore $d_X((x,y)) < \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-delta}{\delta}_1$ by Definition 78, Definition 81, Lemma 7.

LaTeX source 
\begin{byCase}
                        \caseOf{$y = x_0$.}
                            $d_X((x_1,x_0)) = d_X((x_0,x_1))$ by \cref{metric_symmetric,metric_on}.
                            $d_X((x,y)) < \delta_1$.
                        \caseOf{$y \neq x_0$.}
                            Then $y = x_1$.
                            Therefore $d_X((x,y)) < \delta_1$ by \cref{metric_zero_characterization,metric_on,real_lt_subst_left}.
                        
\end{byCase}
LaTeX source 
\begin{byCase}
                    \caseOf{$x = x_0$.}
                        \begin{byCase}
                        \caseOf{$y = x_0$.}
                            Thus $d_X((x,y)) < \delta_1$ by \cref{metric_zero_characterization,metric_on,real_lt_subst_left}.
                        \caseOf{$y \neq x_0$.}
                            Then $y = x_1$.
                            $d_X((x,y)) < \delta_1$.
                        \end{byCase}
                    \caseOf{$x \neq x_0$.}
                        Then $x = x_1$.
                        \begin{byCase}
                        \caseOf{$y = x_0$.}
                            $d_X((x_1,x_0)) = d_X((x_0,x_1))$ by \cref{metric_symmetric,metric_on}.
                            $d_X((x,y)) < \delta_1$.
                        \caseOf{$y \neq x_0$.}
                            Then $y = x_1$.
                            Therefore $d_X((x,y)) < \delta_1$ by \cref{metric_zero_characterization,metric_on,real_lt_subst_left}.
                        \end{byCase}
                    
\end{byCase}

Take $U\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} A$ such that $B\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-subseteq}{\subseteq} U$.
Take $x\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} X$ such that $U = \metricBall{d_X}{X}{x}{\apply{g}{x}}$.
Thus $d_X((x,x_0)) < \apply{g}{x}$ and $d_X((x,x_1)) < \apply{g}{x}$ by Proposition 16, Proposition 17, Definition 188, Definition 82.
Hence $(x,\apply{g}{x})\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} R$ by Axiom 1, Definition 202.
For all $y\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} X$ if $d_X((x,y)) < \apply{g}{x}$ then $d_Y((\apply{f}{x},\apply{f}{y})) < \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-epsilon}{\epsilon}_0$ by Axiom 11, Axiom 12.
Therefore $d_Y((\apply{f}{x},\apply{f}{x_0})) < \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-epsilon}{\epsilon}_0$ and $d_Y((\apply{f}{x},\apply{f}{x_1})) < \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-epsilon}{\epsilon}_0$.
$\apply{f}{x_0}\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} Y$ and $\apply{f}{x}\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} Y$ and $\apply{f}{x_1}\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} Y$ by Proposition 373.
Hence $d_Y((\apply{f}{x_0},\apply{f}{x})) < \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-epsilon}{\epsilon}_0$ by Definition 79, Definition 81, Lemma 7.
Let $s = d_Y((\apply{f}{x_0},\apply{f}{x})) + d_Y((\apply{f}{x},\apply{f}{x_1}))$.
Then $d_Y((\apply{f}{x_0},\apply{f}{x_1})) \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-leq}{\leq} s$ by Definition 81, Definition 80.
$d_Y\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\funs{Y\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-times}{\times} Y}{\reals}$ by Definition 81.
$(\apply{f}{x_0},\apply{f}{x})\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} Y\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-times}{\times} Y$ and $(\apply{f}{x},\apply{f}{x_1})\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} Y\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-times}{\times} Y$ by Proposition 115.
Hence $d_Y((\apply{f}{x_0},\apply{f}{x}))\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\reals$ and $d_Y((\apply{f}{x},\apply{f}{x_1}))\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\reals$ by Proposition 373.
Therefore $s < \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-epsilon}{\epsilon}_0 + \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-epsilon}{\epsilon}_0$ by Theorem 102.
Hence $d_Y((\apply{f}{x_0},\apply{f}{x_1})) < \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-epsilon}{\epsilon}_0 + \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-epsilon}{\epsilon}_0$ by Proposition 115, Proposition 373, Axiom 20, Lemma 6, Axiom 44, Lemma 7.

$\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-epsilon}{\epsilon}_0 = \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-epsilon}{\epsilon} \rmul \inv{(1 + 1)}$ by Definition 264.
Hence $\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-epsilon}{\epsilon}_0 + \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-epsilon}{\epsilon}_0 = (\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-epsilon}{\epsilon} \rmul \inv{(1 + 1)}) + (\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-epsilon}{\epsilon} \rmul \inv{(1 + 1)})$.
$(\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-epsilon}{\epsilon} + \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-epsilon}{\epsilon}) \rmul \inv{(1 + 1)} = (\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-epsilon}{\epsilon} \rmul \inv{(1 + 1)}) + (\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-epsilon}{\epsilon} \rmul \inv{(1 + 1)})$ by Lemma 398.
Therefore $\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-epsilon}{\epsilon}_0 + \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-epsilon}{\epsilon}_0 = (\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-epsilon}{\epsilon} + \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-epsilon}{\epsilon}) \rmul \inv{(1 + 1)}$.
$\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-epsilon}{\epsilon} \rmul (1 + 1) = (\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-epsilon}{\epsilon} \rmul 1) + (\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-epsilon}{\epsilon} \rmul 1)$ by Axiom 30.
Hence $\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-epsilon}{\epsilon} \rmul 1 = \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-epsilon}{\epsilon}$ by Axiom 28, Axiom 27.
Therefore $\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-epsilon}{\epsilon} \rmul (1 + 1) = \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-epsilon}{\epsilon} + \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-epsilon}{\epsilon}$.
Then $(\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-epsilon}{\epsilon} + \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-epsilon}{\epsilon}) \rmul \inv{(1 + 1)} = (\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-epsilon}{\epsilon} \rmul (1 + 1)) \rmul \inv{(1 + 1)}$.
$(\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-epsilon}{\epsilon} \rmul (1 + 1)) \rmul \inv{(1 + 1)} = \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-epsilon}{\epsilon} \rmul ((1 + 1) \rmul \inv{(1 + 1)})$ by Axiom 26.
$(1 + 1) \rmul \inv{(1 + 1)} = 1$ by Axiom 29.
Hence $\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-epsilon}{\epsilon}_0 + \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-epsilon}{\epsilon}_0 = \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-epsilon}{\epsilon} \rmul 1$.
Therefore $\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-epsilon}{\epsilon}_0 + \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-epsilon}{\epsilon}_0 = \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-epsilon}{\epsilon}$ by Axiom 28.
Therefore $d_Y((\apply{f}{x_0},\apply{f}{x_1})) < \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-epsilon}{\epsilon}$ by Lemma 8.

Hence there exists $\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-delta}{\delta}\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in}\reals$ such that $0 < \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-delta}{\delta}$ and for all $x_0,x_1\class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-in}{\in} X$ such that $d_X((x_0,x_1)) < \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-delta}{\delta}$ we have $d_Y((\apply{f}{x_0},\apply{f}{x_1})) < \class{latex-symbol-tip latex-symbol-id-epsilon}{\epsilon}$.

LaTeX source 
\begin{byCase}
        \caseOf{$X = \emptyset$.}
            Let $\delta_0 = \epsilon_0$.
            $\delta_0\in\reals$ and $0 < \delta_0$.
            Show that for all $x_0,x_1$ such that $x_0\in X$ and $x_1\in X$ and $d_X((x_0,x_1)) < \delta_0$
                we have $d_Y((\apply{f}{x_0},\apply{f}{x_1})) < \epsilon$.
            \begin{subproof}
                Trivial.
            \end{subproof}
            Hence there exists $\delta\in\reals$ such that $0 < \delta$ and
                for all $x_0,x_1\in X$ such that $d_X((x_0,x_1)) < \delta$
                    we have $d_Y((\apply{f}{x_0},\apply{f}{x_1})) < \epsilon$.
        \caseOf{$X \neq \emptyset$.}
            Let $R = \{w\in X\times\reals \mid \text{$0 < \snd{w}$ and
                for all $y\in X$ if $d_X((\fst{w},y)) < \snd{w}$ then
                $d_Y((\apply{f}{\fst{w}},\apply{f}{y})) < \epsilon_0$}\}$.
            Hence $R$ is a relation by \cref{times_elem_is_tuple,relation}.
            For all $x\in X$ there exists $\delta\in\reals$ such that $x\mathrel{R}\delta$
                by \cref{metric_continuity_eps_delta,times_tuple_intro,fst_eq,snd_eq,relation}.
            Take $g$ such that $g$ is a function on $X$ and for all $x\in X$ we have $x\mathrel{R}\apply{g}{x}$
                by \cref{choice_function}.
            Let $A = \{U\in\pow{X} \mid \exists x\in X. U = \metricBall{d_X}{X}{x}{\apply{g}{x}}\}$.

            Hence for all $x\in X$ we have $0 < \apply{g}{x}$ by \cref{choice_function,fst_eq,snd_eq,relation}.
            Therefore for all $x\in X$ we have $x\in\unions{A}$
                by \cref{metric_zero_characterization,metric_on,real_lt_subst_left,metric_ball,subseteq,powerset_intro,unions_iff}.
            For all $U$ such that $U\in A$ we have $U$ is open in $X$ with respect to $T_X$
                by \cref{choice_function,fst_eq,snd_eq,times_tuple_elim,metric_ball,subseteq,powerset_intro,metric_basis,metric_basis_is_basis,basis_elem_open_in_generated,basis_for_topology,metric_topology,open_in,basis_topology_is_topology}.
            Therefore $A$ is an open covering of $X$ with respect to $T_X$ by \cref{subseteq,covering,open_covering}.

            Take $\delta_1\in\reals$ such that $0 < \delta_1$ and
                for all $B$ such that $B\subseteq X$ and
                    for all $x,y\in B$ we have $d_X((x,y)) < \delta_1$
                there exists $U\in A$ such that $B\subseteq U$
                by \cref{lebesgue_number_lemma}.

            Show that for all $x_0,x_1$ such that $x_0\in X$ and $x_1\in X$ and $d_X((x_0,x_1)) < \delta_1$
                we have $d_Y((\apply{f}{x_0},\apply{f}{x_1})) < \epsilon$.
            \begin{subproof}
                Fix $x_0$.
                Fix $x_1$.
                Assume $x_0\in X$ and $x_1\in X$ and $d_X((x_0,x_1)) < \delta_1$.
                Let $B = \{x_0,x_1\}$.
                $B\subseteq X$ by \cref{upair_iff,subseteq}.
                Show that for all $x\in B$ we have for all $y\in B$ we have $d_X((x,y)) < \delta_1$.
                \begin{subproof}
                    Fix $x$.
                    Assume $x\in B$.
                    Fix $y$.
                    Assume $y\in B$.
                    $x = x_0$ or $x = x_1$ by \cref{upair_iff}.
                    Then $y = x_0$ or $y = x_1$ by \cref{upair_iff}.
                    \begin{byCase}
                    \caseOf{$x = x_0$.}
